project.goal: Replace paper logbooks with FMCSA-compliant digital HOS tracking
project.target_users: Commercial truck drivers and fleet safety managers
project.timeline: 6-month delivery with FMCSA certification milestone at month 4

frontend.approach: Offline-first with SQLite local store and background sync

backend.framework: Express with Joi validation

security.auth: Cognito with MFA for managers, PIN for drivers

data.local: SQLite on device for offline logs

devops.environments: dev, staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: HOS compliance is the core value. The 11-hour driving and 14-hour on-duty rule enforcement must be mathematically precise—accumulated time calculations need to account for split sleeper berth provisions and restarts correctly or drivers face federal violations.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Offline-first architecture is non-negotiable given rural route connectivity. SQLite on device serves as the authoritative log source during offline periods; sync conflicts must favor the device record per FMCSA rules. ELD Bluetooth integration requires a native module for React Native.
reasoner_reviews.security: Driver duty status data is a federal record under 49 CFR 395. Immutability of certified logs is required—no updates after driver signature. Audit trail must capture every edit attempt with timestamp, user, and reason.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: FMCSA ELD certification requires passing 18 specific test scenarios. The sub-1-second ELD sync constraint means the Bluetooth handshake and server acknowledgment must complete within that window, which constrains retry logic design.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan addressed the offline sync conflict resolution gap. The remaining issue is that the HOS violation calculation engine has not been validated against FMCSA's 49 CFR 395.3 adverse driving conditions exception, which extends driving limits by 2 hours.

frontend.app_structure: React Native app with feature modules: duty_status, eld_sync, log_viewer, fleet_dashboard, auth. Offline-first with WatermelonDB on device for reactive local data
frontend.pages_and_flows: Driver: Status Change screen with GPS capture, Daily Log view with certification action, ELD Sync status indicator. Manager: Fleet Status map, Driver Logs review, HOS Violation alerts
frontend.state_management: Redux Toolkit slices per feature; RTK Query for API sync; background task queue for pending ELD sync events
frontend.frontend_risks: Bluetooth ELD pairing reliability varies by device model; must handle pairing failures gracefully with manual fallback entry

backend.service_design: Express app with domain modules: hos_engine (rule calculations), eld_sync (device protocol), duty_events (CRUD + immutability), fleet_reporting (analytics aggregations)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful v1 endpoints; webhook callbacks for ELD device acknowledgments; SSE for real-time fleet status push to manager dashboard
backend.failure_handling: ELD sync queue with at-least-once delivery; duplicate detection via idempotency keys; hos_engine uses event-sourcing pattern so all duty transitions are immutable
backend.backend_risks: HOS rule engine complexity: 60/70-hour 7/8-day calculations, split sleeper berth, adverse conditions exceptions all require separate calculation paths

security.auth_design: Cognito user pools: fleet_manager role with MFA; driver role with PIN auth plus biometric on device; service accounts for ELD devices via signed certificates
security.authorization_model: RBAC: drivers can only read/certify their own logs; fleet managers see all drivers in their carrier account; carrier_admin manages account roster
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every duty status change logged with GPS coordinates, timestamp, and method (automatic/manual/edit). Certified logs written to immutable append-only table. Edit history retained per 49 CFR 395.22(i)
security.security_risks: Drivers may attempt to manipulate logs to avoid HOS violations; system must detect and flag any manual override attempts for fleet manager review

data.entities: drivers (id, carrier_id, license_number, home_terminal, cognito_sub, created_at), duty_status_events (id, driver_id, status ENUM off_duty/sleeper_berth/driving/on_duty, start_ts, end_ts, gps_lat, gps_lng, source ENUM automatic/manual/edit, certified_at), eld_logs (id, driver_id, device_serial, data_transfer_payload_json, sync_ts, fmcsa_transfer_id), hos_violations (id, driver_id, violation_type, triggered_at, hours_over, resolved), vehicles (id, carrier_id, vin, eld_device_serial, license_plate), carrier_accounts (id, dot_number, name, timezone), inspection_reports (id, driver_id, vehicle_id, inspection_type, defects_json, outcome, inspected_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for all server-side records; SQLite via WatermelonDB on device for offline log capture; Redis pub/sub for real-time duty status broadcast to fleet manager SSE stream
data.retention_and_deletion: ELD logs retained 6 months on device per FMCSA; server retains 6 months hot, archived to S3 Glacier for remaining period; driver PII retained per employment plus 1 year then purged

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate in us-east-1 and us-west-2 active-active; ALB routing; RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis cluster mode
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, Jest unit tests for HOS engine, integration tests against containerized Postgres, build Docker, push ECR, rolling ECS deploy. Mobile: Expo EAS build pipeline targeting iOS and Android
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on all Fargate tasks; custom metrics: eld_sync_latency_ms, hos_violation_count_by_rule, duty_event_lag_seconds; PagerDuty for sync failure rate > 5%
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS service rollback to previous task definition on failed health check; database migrations use up/down scripts with rollback tested in staging

previous_plan.title: HOS Digital Logbook - ELD Compliance Platform v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: React Native offline-first ELD app with Node.js backend on AWS Fargate. Handles duty status transitions, GPS verification, ELD Bluetooth sync, and automated HOS violation detection for FMCSA compliance.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Offline-first React Native app with WatermelonDB local store; device is authoritative source during offline periods. Node.js Express backend on ECS Fargate handles sync, HOS rule evaluation, and fleet reporting. PostgreSQL 16 for persistent records.
previous_plan.data_model: drivers, duty_status_events, eld_logs, hos_violations, vehicles, carrier_accounts tables with device-authoritative sync strategy.
previous_plan.workflows: Duty status change captures GPS, writes to local SQLite, syncs to server. Daily certification locks log and writes to immutable table. Fleet manager receives SSE updates.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 focuses on resolving ISS-001: the missing adverse driving conditions exception in the HOS engine. The fix requires a driver self-declaration flow in the mobile app and a conditional calculation path in the server-side rule engine. This is a critical FMCSA compliance gap. The architecture is otherwise sound; the plan should reinforce the fix in the HOS engine module, add the FMCSA certification test matrix to phased implementation, and maintain all prior strong sections unchanged.

fix_report.action_taken: Added adverse driving conditions exception module to HOS engine: driver triggers self-declaration via app UI when encountering qualifying conditions (snow/ice, reduced visibility, hazardous conditions). Engine extends daily driving limit by up to 2 hours, logs declaration event to duty_status_events with source=adverse_declaration, and records GPS location of declaration. Added 49 CFR 395.1(b) test cases to HOS engine Jest suite. Updated FMCSA certification test scenario matrix in phased implementation.
fix_report.expected_outcome: HOS engine passes FMCSA certification test scenario 14 (adverse driving conditions) and no false violations generated for drivers in qualifying conditions

target_output.title: HOS Digital Logbook - ELD Compliance Platform
target_output.executive_summary: An FMCSA ELD mandate-compliant mobile platform built with React Native and Node.js on AWS ECS Fargate. Enables commercial truck drivers to digitally track duty status with GPS verification, sync logs to ELD devices over Bluetooth, and automatically detect HOS violations including adverse driving conditions exceptions. Fleet safety managers monitor compliance in real time via a server-sent events dashboard.
target_output.architecture_overview: Offline-first React Native app stores all duty_status_events in a WatermelonDB SQLite store on device; device record is the authoritative source per FMCSA 49 CFR 395.22. A Node.js Express backend on ECS Fargate receives sync batches, runs the HOS rule engine against cumulative duty hours, and writes violation records to PostgreSQL 16. Redis 7 pub/sub broadcasts real-time duty status changes to fleet manager SSE streams. The ELD Bluetooth sync module handles FMCSA Data Transfer Specification v4.0 handshake with in-cab ELD devices. All GPS coordinates and timestamps are captured at the device and treated as tamper-evident per FMCSA requirements.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74 with TypeScript, WatermelonDB for offline SQLite, Redux Toolkit with RTK Query, Expo EAS build. Backend: Node.js 20, Express, Joi, custom HOS rule engine module. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 ElastiCache. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate active-active us-east-1/us-west-2, ALB, CloudWatch, Datadog APM. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Expo EAS for mobile builds.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Duty Status Engine: driver transitions between off_duty, sleeper_berth, driving, on_duty with GPS capture and automatic ELD push. HOS Rule Engine: evaluates 11-hour driving, 14-hour on-duty, 60/70-hour 7/8-day cycle, 30-minute rest break, split sleeper berth provisions, and adverse driving conditions extension. ELD Sync: Bluetooth FMCSA DTS v4.0 handshake with latency under 1 second. Daily Log Certification: driver reviews, signs, and locks log; immutable post-certification. Fleet Manager Dashboard: real-time SSE duty status map, HOS violation alerts, driver log review and co-driver assignment.
target_output.system_components: React Native app with WatermelonDB local store and background sync task queue. Express API server on ECS Fargate with HOS engine, ELD sync handler, and fleet reporting modules. PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ for duty records and carrier data. Redis 7 pub/sub for SSE fan-out to fleet dashboards. AWS Cognito for auth. S3 for ELD data archive. CloudWatch + Datadog for monitoring.
target_output.workflows: Duty Status Change: Driver selects new status on app; app captures GPS lat/lng and UTC timestamp; event written to WatermelonDB; background task attempts sync to server via HTTPS within 60 seconds; server HOS engine evaluates cumulative hours for violations; ELD device receives Bluetooth event acknowledgment within 1 second. Adverse Driving Declaration: Driver encounters qualifying conditions and triggers declaration in app; HOS engine extends daily driving window by up to 2 hours; declaration event written to duty_status_events with source=adverse_declaration and GPS coordinates; fleet manager receives alert. Daily Log Certification: Driver reviews 24-hour log, corrects any unresolved edits with written reason, signs log; server writes certified_at timestamp and locks record; any subsequent edit attempt creates a new edit_request record requiring fleet manager countersignature per 49 CFR 395.22(j).
target_output.data_model: drivers (id UUID, carrier_id FK, dot_driver_id, license_number, license_state, home_terminal_tz, cognito_sub, status ENUM active/inactive, created_at). duty_status_events (id UUID, driver_id FK, vehicle_id FK, status ENUM off_duty/sleeper_berth/driving/on_duty, start_ts TIMESTAMPTZ, end_ts TIMESTAMPTZ, gps_lat NUMERIC, gps_lng NUMERIC, odometer_miles, source ENUM automatic/manual/edit/adverse_declaration, edit_reason TEXT, certified_at TIMESTAMPTZ, sequence_number INT). eld_logs (id UUID, driver_id FK, device_serial, fmcsa_transfer_id, payload_json, sync_ts, acknowledged_at). hos_violations (id UUID, driver_id FK, violation_type ENUM driving_11h/on_duty_14h/cycle_70h/rest_break_30m, triggered_at, hours_over NUMERIC, adverse_exception_applied BOOL, resolved_at). vehicles (id UUID, carrier_id FK, vin, eld_device_serial, license_plate, jurisdiction). carrier_accounts (id UUID, dot_number, mc_number, name, home_terminal_tz). inspection_reports (id UUID, driver_id FK, vehicle_id FK, inspection_type ENUM pre_trip/post_trip/roadside, defects_json, outcome ENUM pass/fail/conditional, inspected_at).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/duty-events (batch sync from device). GET /api/v1/drivers/:id/logs?date= (daily log retrieval). POST /api/v1/drivers/:id/logs/:date/certify (log certification). GET /api/v1/fleet/status (SSE stream for real-time duty status). GET /api/v1/drivers/:id/hos-summary (current HOS balance). POST /api/v1/eld/transfer (FMCSA data transfer request). GET /api/v1/violations?carrier_id=&status=unresolved (fleet violation queue).
target_output.security_and_compliance: FMCSA 49 CFR 395.22 immutability: duty_status_events certified_at field is write-once; post-certification edits require fleet manager countersignature and create separate edit_request records. Driver PII pseudonymized in analytics with carrier-scoped identifiers. Cognito MFA enforced for fleet_manager and carrier_admin roles; PIN plus biometric for driver mobile auth. Certificate pinning in React Native app against AWS API Gateway certificate. ELD device authentication via signed x.509 certificates issued per device serial. GDPR: EU driver data stored in eu-west-1 replica; right-to-erasure applies to PII but duty logs retained per FMCSA minimum.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate active-active across us-east-1 and us-west-2 behind Route 53 latency-based routing. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ primary in us-east-1 with read replica in us-west-2. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode. GitHub Actions CI: Jest unit tests for HOS engine (including all 18 FMCSA certification scenarios), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check gate. Expo EAS pipeline for iOS and Android builds with TestFlight and Play Console beta distribution. Migrations: Flyway with tested rollback scripts. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on all Fargate tasks. Custom metrics: eld_sync_latency_ms (alarm >1000ms), duty_event_sync_lag_seconds (alarm >60s), hos_violation_rate_by_type, adverse_declaration_count_by_region. Structured JSON logs with driver_id and carrier_id correlation. PagerDuty P1 alerts for ELD sync failure rate >5% over 5 minutes or HOS engine error rate >0%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ~$2,400/month for 500-vehicle fleet. ECS auto-scaling on CPU 60% target handles post-shift sync bursts (7am-9am peak). Redis pub/sub scales SSE fan-out linearly. Multi-AZ Postgres at ~$600/month. Mobile builds via Expo EAS at usage-based cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (8 weeks): Core duty status tracking with GPS, HOS engine for 11h/14h/70h rules including adverse driving conditions exception, daily log view and certification, device-authoritative offline sync, FMCSA DTS v4.0 ELD Bluetooth sync. HOS engine unit test suite covering all 18 FMCSA certification scenarios. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Fleet manager dashboard with SSE real-time map, HOS violation alert queue, driver log review and countersignature workflow, FMCSA third-party ELD certification submission. Phase 3 (4 weeks): IFTA mileage reporting, TMS dispatch integration webhooks, analytics dashboards for fleet safety KPIs.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across React Native and backend. HOS rule engine is pure functional code with 100% unit test coverage; no side effects in rule calculations. All duty_status_events use UTC timestamps; timezone conversion only at display layer. Feature flags for adverse conditions exception (toggleable per carrier during FMCSA certification testing). PR requires HOS engine test run passing before merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ELD Bluetooth pairing failures on specific device models. Mitigation: manual duty entry fallback with flag for fleet manager review; maintain compatibility matrix per ELD device model. Risk: WatermelonDB schema migration on device during app update while driver is offline. Mitigation: non-destructive schema migrations only; migration tested on minimum supported OS versions. Tradeoff: device-authoritative sync increases server reconciliation complexity but is required by FMCSA; accepted. Risk: FMCSA rule changes requiring HOS engine updates mid-deployment. Mitigation: rule parameters configurable via database-driven rule table, not hardcoded constants.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Adverse driving conditions exception implemented as driver self-declaration with GPS logging per 49 CFR 395.1(b). Split sleeper berth calculation uses paired sleeper events model. FMCSA data transfer to roadside inspectors implemented via QR code and Bluetooth output modes. EU driver data isolated to eu-west-1 replica for GDPR compliance.

metadata.notes: Round 3 resolves critical HOS adverse conditions exception gap; FMCSA ELD compliance focus.

project.goal: High-throughput telematics ingestion platform for 50K+ fleet vehicles
project.target_users: Internal engineering and analytics teams

backend.framework: Custom gRPC services with EMQX MQTT broker
backend.api: gRPC internal APIs, no public REST

security.auth: Custom JWT + mTLS between services
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for MQTT, mTLS for gRPC

data.event_bus: Kafka MSK-equivalent on GCP Pub/Sub bridge

devops.environments: dev, staging, prod

reasoner_reviews.product: The ingestion platform scope is well-bounded: MQTT intake, normalization, ClickHouse writes, and Kafka fan-out cover the core pipeline. The 50K concurrent connection requirement is the dominant technical constraint driving all architectural choices.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: EMQX cluster on GKE is the correct choice for MQTT at this scale. Go normalization workers with stateless horizontal scaling are well-suited. The ClickHouse MergeTree partitioning by vehicle_id and event_date will be critical for query performance. Previous rounds identified missing backpressure signaling from ClickHouse write path back to the MQTT broker -- this must be addressed.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS between all services via Istio service mesh is the right baseline. JWT validation at the EMQX level prevents unauthorized device publishing. The previous audit flagged that device certificate rotation was not described -- this gap must be closed in round 3.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GCP Committed Use Discounts for GKE node pools can bring monthly spend under $8K at target throughput. The previous plan did not include a ClickHouse cold-tier migration job to GCS, which is needed to contain hot storage costs beyond 90 days.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan improved the normalization worker design but left the device certificate lifecycle and ClickHouse backpressure unaddressed. These are the two blocking issues from ISS-002 and ISS-003 that must be resolved in this round.

backend.service_design: Four Go services: EMQX auth webhook (validates device JWTs), normalization workers (parse CAN bus + GPS frames into vehicle_telemetry_events structs), Kafka producer (fan-out to consumer topics), and batch compaction service (ClickHouse optimize + GCS export)
backend.api_patterns: Internal gRPC APIs only: TelemetryIngestionService.Publish, StreamRegistryService.Register, ConsumerSubscriptionService.Subscribe. No public REST exposure.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly compaction job merges ClickHouse parts, exports partitions older than 90 days to GCS as Parquet, updates ingestion_partitions table with archive pointers
backend.failure_handling: Normalization workers publish to dead-letter Kafka topic on parse failure; circuit breaker halts ClickHouse writes if error rate exceeds 5% in 30s window, rerouting to Redis buffer queue
backend.scaling_notes: KEDA horizontal pod autoscaler on Kafka consumer lag metric; EMQX cluster scaled via GKE node pool autoscaler on connection count; ClickHouse write workers scale on ingest queue depth
backend.backend_risks: ClickHouse write amplification during MergeTree compaction can spike CPU; mitigated by scheduling compaction during off-peak hours with resource quotas

security.auth_design: EMQX webhook calls Go auth service to validate device JWT on CONNECT; devices receive short-lived JWTs (1h TTL) rotated by device management service via MQTT last-will pattern
security.secrets_management: GCP Secret Manager for Kafka credentials, ClickHouse passwords, and TLS certs; mounted as Kubernetes secrets with 24h rotation via External Secrets Operator
security.abuse_prevention: Per-device publish rate limiting at EMQX (max 10 msg/s per client_id); anomalous burst detection flags device_registrations for manual review
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All gRPC calls logged with request_id, source_service, and outcome to Loki; ClickHouse write failures and dead-letter events generate PagerDuty alerts
security.security_risks: Compromised device certificate could inject malicious telemetry; mitigated by device-level JWT binding to device serial number verified at auth webhook

data.entities: vehicle_telemetry_events (device_id, vehicle_id, event_type ENUM gps/can_bus/sensor, lat, lng, speed_kmh, engine_rpm, fuel_level_pct, odometer_km, event_ts, ingested_at), device_registrations (device_id, vehicle_id, carrier_id, cert_fingerprint, status, last_seen_at), ingestion_partitions (partition_key, vehicle_id, event_date, row_count, size_bytes, archive_gcs_uri), consumer_subscriptions (consumer_id, topic_filter, event_types[], created_at), backpressure_metrics (worker_id, queue_depth, lag_ms, sampled_at)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(event_ts) and ordered by (vehicle_id, event_ts) for time-range queries; Redis sorted sets for dedup fingerprints with 5-minute TTL
data.retention_and_deletion: 90-day hot retention in ClickHouse; nightly Airflow DAG exports older partitions to GCS as Parquet with Snappy compression; ClickHouse partitions dropped after export verified
data.data_risks: Dedup cache miss during Redis failover could allow duplicate events into ClickHouse; mitigated by ReplacingMergeTree dedup on (device_id, event_ts) as secondary guard

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster with separate node pools for EMQX (memory-optimized), normalization workers (compute-optimized), and ClickHouse (storage-optimized N2D). Istio service mesh for mTLS and traffic observability.

devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Go /metrics endpoints and EMQX stats; Grafana dashboards for ingest rate, queue depth, ClickHouse write latency, and consumer lag; Loki for structured Go logs
devops.devops_risks: GKE Autopilot pod eviction during scale-up can cause in-flight message loss; mitigated by normalization workers checkpointing Kafka offset only after successful ClickHouse write

target_output.title: Fleet Telematics Ingestion Platform v3
target_output.executive_summary: A Go-based, GKE-hosted telematics ingestion infrastructure handling 50,000+ concurrent MQTT device connections via EMQX cluster, normalizing GPS and CAN bus frames into ClickHouse within 500ms p99, and fanning out events to downstream consumers via Kafka. Round 3 resolves device certificate rotation (short-lived JWTs with Redis deny-list revocation) and ClickHouse cold-tier archival to GCS Parquet.
target_output.architecture_overview: EMQX MQTT broker cluster (3 nodes, GKE node pool) receives device CONNECT and PUBLISH events. Auth webhook (Go service) validates device JWT against Redis deny-list and then verifies signature. Accepted PUBLISH payloads enqueued to Kafka topic 'raw-telemetry'. Normalization workers (Go, KEDA-scaled on consumer lag) parse GPS NMEA and CAN bus J1939 frames into vehicle_telemetry_events protobuf structs and write to ClickHouse via native protocol. Kafka producer then routes normalized events to per-event-type consumer topics. Device management service handles JWT re-issuance every 55 minutes via encrypted MQTT command channel. Nightly compaction service exports partitions older than 90 days to GCS Parquet and drops hot partitions post-verification.
target_output.technology_stack: Go 1.22 for all services (auth webhook, normalization workers, compaction service, device management service). EMQX 5.x MQTT broker cluster. ClickHouse 24.x with MergeTree + ReplacingMergeTree. GCP Pub/Sub as Kafka-compatible event bus. Redis 7 for dedup fingerprints and device deny-list. GKE Autopilot with Istio service mesh. Terraform + Argo CD for GitOps IaC. Grafana + Prometheus + Loki for observability. Google Cloud Build for CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: MQTT Device Auth: EMQX webhook validates device JWT (deny-list check + signature). Event Normalization: GPS NMEA + CAN J1939 parsing to canonical vehicle_telemetry_events. Kafka Fan-out: per-event-type topics for analytics, alerting, and geofence consumers. Device Lifecycle Management: JWT issuance, 55-min rotation, deny-list revocation. Cold-Tier Archival: nightly GCS Parquet export with partition drop verification. Backpressure Control: circuit breaker halts ClickHouse writes at 5% error rate, reroutes to Redis buffer.
target_output.system_components: EMQX Cluster (3 GKE pods, memory-optimized N2 nodes, 50K+ concurrent connections), Auth Webhook Service (Go, 3 replicas behind ClusterIP), Normalization Workers (Go, KEDA HPA on raw-telemetry consumer lag, 5-50 pods), Kafka (GCP Pub/Sub with Kafka-compatible API, 8 partitions per topic), ClickHouse (3-node cluster, NVMe-backed GKE persistent volumes, MergeTree partitioned by month), Device Management Service (Go, issues and rotates device JWTs via MQTT command channel), Compaction Service (Go CronJob, 02:00 UTC, GCS Parquet export), Redis 7 (deny-list + dedup cache), GCS Archive Bucket (fleet-telemetry-archive, Nearline class).
target_output.workflows: Device JWT Rotation: Device management service publishes encrypted JWT-refresh command to device/{device_id}/commands MQTT topic 5 minutes before expiry; device retrieves new JWT, sends DISCONNECT + CONNECT with new token; auth webhook verifies new JWT and removes previous token from deny-list. Ingest Path: Device publishes to telemetry/{vehicle_id}/events; EMQX auth webhook validates JWT (deny-list + signature, <5ms p99); PUBLISH forwarded to raw-telemetry Kafka topic; normalization worker consumes, parses NMEA/J1939, writes vehicle_telemetry_events row to ClickHouse, commits Kafka offset; Kafka producer copies normalized event to typed consumer topics (gps-events, engine-events, sensor-events). Cold Archival: Cloud Scheduler triggers compaction service at 02:00 UTC; service queries ingestion_partitions for partitions with event_date < NOW()-90d and archive_gcs_uri IS NULL; exports each partition to GCS as Parquet with Snappy; compares exported row_count to ClickHouse partition row count; on match, runs ALTER TABLE DROP PARTITION and updates ingestion_partitions.archive_gcs_uri.
target_output.data_model: vehicle_telemetry_events (device_id UUID, vehicle_id UUID, event_type ENUM('gps','can_bus','sensor'), lat FLOAT64, lng FLOAT64, speed_kmh FLOAT32, engine_rpm UINT16, fuel_level_pct FLOAT32, odometer_km UINT32, event_ts DateTime64(3), ingested_at DateTime64(3)) ENGINE=ReplacingMergeTree ORDER BY (vehicle_id, event_ts) PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(event_ts). device_registrations (device_id UUID PK, vehicle_id UUID, carrier_id UUID, cert_fingerprint TEXT, jwt_issued_at TIMESTAMP, jwt_expires_at TIMESTAMP, status ENUM active/suspended/revoked, last_seen_at TIMESTAMP). ingestion_partitions (partition_key TEXT PK, vehicle_id UUID, event_date DATE, row_count BIGINT, size_bytes BIGINT, archive_gcs_uri TEXT NULLABLE, archived_at TIMESTAMP NULLABLE). consumer_subscriptions (consumer_id UUID PK, topic_filter TEXT, event_types TEXT[], created_at TIMESTAMP). backpressure_metrics (worker_id TEXT, queue_depth INT, lag_ms INT, sampled_at TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: Internal gRPC only. TelemetryIngestionService: Publish(TelemetryFrame) -> PublishAck, GetDeviceStatus(DeviceId) -> DeviceStatus. StreamRegistryService: Register(StreamConfig) -> StreamId, ListStreams() -> StreamList. DeviceManagementService: IssueJWT(DeviceId) -> JWTCredential, RevokeDevice(DeviceId) -> RevokeAck, RotateJWT(DeviceId) -> JWTCredential. All services behind Istio with mTLS; no public-internet endpoints. Prometheus /metrics on :9090 per service.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Device Auth: EMQX auth webhook validates device JWT in two steps -- first checks Redis deny-list (compromised/revoked devices blocked in <60s), then verifies JWT signature and claims (device_id, carrier_id, exp). Device Lifecycle: Device management service issues JWTs with 1h TTL; re-issuance triggered 5 min before expiry via encrypted MQTT command channel (AES-256-GCM payload, per-device key in GCP Secret Manager); compromised device revocation adds device_id to Redis deny-list and sets device_registrations.status=revoked. Service mesh: Istio enforces mTLS for all pod-to-pod gRPC calls. Secrets: GCP Secret Manager + External Secrets Operator with 24h rotation. Network: GCP VPC private service connect; no public load balancers. Audit: all gRPC calls structured-logged to Loki with request_id, source_service, and outcome.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Google Cloud Build CI: Go unit + integration tests (ClickHouse test container), go vet + staticcheck, Docker build + push to Artifact Registry, Terraform plan on infrastructure changes. Argo CD GitOps: PR merge triggers Argo CD sync to dev, manual promotion to staging (automated load test with k6 at 60K synthetic MQTT connections), manual promotion to prod. GKE Autopilot node pools: EMQX (e2-highmem-8, 3 nodes), normalization workers (c2-standard-8, autoscaled 2-20 nodes), ClickHouse (n2d-highmem-16 with 2TB NVMe, 3 nodes). Compaction CronJob: Cloud Scheduler triggers at 02:00 UTC, exports ClickHouse partitions >90 days to gs://fleet-telemetry-archive/YYYY/MM/ as Parquet, verifies row counts, drops hot partitions, updates ingestion_partitions table. Rollback: Argo CD rollback to prior Helm revision; ClickHouse schema changes additive-only via Flyway.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: emqx_connections_count, telemetry_ingest_rate_events_per_sec, normalization_worker_queue_depth, clickhouse_write_latency_p99_ms, kafka_consumer_lag_messages, compaction_job_rows_exported. Grafana dashboards: Ingest Pipeline Overview (rate, latency, error rate), Device Health (active connections, auth failures, JWT rotation success rate), Cold-Tier Archival (partitions exported, GCS size, drop verification pass rate). Loki alerts: queue_depth >80% -> PagerDuty P2, ingest_latency p99 >400ms -> PagerDuty P1, compaction_job failure -> PagerDuty P2, device auth failure spike >100/min -> Slack #fleet-ops.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Projected $6,800/month at 50K devices: GKE nodes $4,200, ClickHouse NVMe storage $1,400, GCP Pub/Sub $600, GCS Nearline archive $200, Redis $400. Committed Use Discounts on GKE node pools reduce compute by 28%. ClickHouse cold-tier export to GCS (at $0.01/GB/month) vs hot NVMe ($0.17/GB/month) saves ~$900/month beyond 90 days. KEDA autoscaling on normalization workers eliminates over-provisioning during off-peak hours (1 AM - 5 AM, fleet activity drops 70%).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Core Ingestion (complete): EMQX cluster, auth webhook, normalization workers, ClickHouse raw writes, Kafka fan-out, Prometheus + Grafana. Phase 2 - Backpressure + Stability (round 2, complete): circuit breaker on ClickHouse writes, KEDA autoscaling on Kafka lag, dead-letter topic for parse failures, staging load test at 60K synthetic connections. Phase 3 - Security + Archival Hardening (round 3, current): Device JWT rotation with MQTT command channel and Redis deny-list revocation (ISS-002), nightly GCS Parquet compaction job with partition drop verification (ISS-003), External Secrets Operator for 24h credential rotation, k6 chaos tests for broker failover.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules with pinned dependency versions. staticcheck + go vet in CI (zero warnings gate). All gRPC services must implement health.v1 HealthCheck for Kubernetes liveness probes. Table-driven unit tests for normalization parsers (GPS NMEA, CAN J1939 frame parsing). Integration tests use ClickHouse testcontainer. No direct ClickHouse schema changes in prod -- all via Flyway additive migrations reviewed by two engineers. IaC changes require Terraform plan review in PR before Argo CD sync.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EMQX broker node failure during high-traffic period drops active MQTT sessions. Mitigation: EMQX cluster session persistence with Redis shared state; devices reconnect within 30s via exponential backoff. Risk: ClickHouse MergeTree compaction CPU spike during business hours impacts ingest latency. Mitigation: compaction scheduled 02:00 UTC with GKE resource quota limiting compaction CPU to 4 cores. Tradeoff: GCP Pub/Sub as Kafka-compatible bus over native Kafka MSK reduces operational complexity but introduces 50ms additional latency vs native Kafka -- acceptable for sub-500ms SLA with 400ms budget remaining. Tradeoff: ReplacingMergeTree dedup as secondary guard adds storage overhead (~8%) but prevents duplicate events from Redis failover scenarios.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected GCP Pub/Sub with Kafka-compatible API over GCP-managed Kafka (Confluent) for 40% cost reduction. Confirmed ClickHouse ReplacingMergeTree over Kafka dedup for duplicate protection during Redis failover. Chose Cloud Scheduler + Go CronJob over Airflow for cold-tier export to avoid Airflow operational overhead for a single nightly job. Device JWT TTL set to 1h balancing security (short window) vs re-auth overhead (5-min pre-expiry rotation).

metadata.notes: High-scale fleet telematics ingestion on GKE; round 3 closes device cert rotation and GCS cold-tier archival issues.

project_goal.value: Open-source TypeScript SDK providing a unified client for subscribing to real-time vehicle telemetry streams from Samsara, Geotab, and Verizon Connect with a normalized VehicleTelemetryEvent model
project_goal.rationale: Fleet app developers need a single normalized API surface across multiple telematics providers

target_users.value: TypeScript and JavaScript developers building fleet management applications that consume vehicle telematics data
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are application developers, not end users

project_class.rationale: Distributed as npm package with public API surface; no hosted backend

capabilities.rationale: WebSocket stream management, provider adapters, and data normalization are core capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-provider abstraction with reconnection logic and stream multiplexing is non-trivial but scope-bounded

risk_level.rationale: Library with no hosted infrastructure; API key handling delegated to consuming application

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK processes telemetry in-memory only; no data stored or transmitted by the library itself

external_exposure.rationale: Published to npm registry as public open-source package

access_model.value: API key per provider passed by consuming application at runtime; SDK never stores credentials
access_model.rationale: Credential management delegated to consuming application environment

feature_scope.value: Provider adapters for Samsara, Geotab, Verizon Connect; normalized VehicleTelemetryEvent model; unified EventEmitter interface; exponential-backoff reconnection; in-memory ring buffer for missed events during outages; TypeScript type definitions
feature_scope.rationale: Core feature set for v1.0.0 release

mvp_scope.value: Samsara adapter with normalized GPS and engine events, unified emitter, reconnection logic, published npm package with README and usage examples
mvp_scope.rationale: Single provider proves the abstraction before adding Geotab and Verizon Connect

security_baseline.value: No credential storage in SDK; API keys accepted only as runtime config; supply-chain security via npm provenance attestation and Dependabot
security_baseline.rationale: Library security is about supply chain and safe credential-handling patterns

frontend_stack.value: N/A - library_sdk with no UI
frontend_stack.rationale: No frontend component; TypeScript package only

backend_stack.value: TypeScript 5.4 with Bun 1.x runtime for development and testing; compiled ESM+CJS output targets Node 18+ and Bun 1.x
backend_stack.rationale: Bun for fast local development; dual-format output for broad consumer compatibility

data_platform.value: No persistent data store; in-memory ring buffer for missed-event replay during reconnection windows
data_platform.rationale: Library does not persist data; consumers handle their own storage

hosting_target.value: npm registry as @fleet-sdk scoped public package; source on GitHub
hosting_target.rationale: Standard open-source distribution channel

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK retains no telemetry data; all event data flows through process memory only
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No retention obligations for a stateless library

future_scope.value: Adapters for Motive and Trimble; MQTT provider support; TypeScript codegen from provider OpenAPI specs
future_scope.rationale: Provider expansion roadmap post v1.0.0

constraints.value: Compiled bundle <100KB gzipped; runtime dependencies limited to ws and eventemitter3; Node 18+ and Bun 1.x compatibility
constraints.rationale: Lean dependency footprint to minimize consumer supply-chain risk

observability_baseline.value: Optional structured logger callback in config; SDK emits internal events (connected, disconnected, reconnecting, schema_warning) for consumer monitoring wiring
observability_baseline.rationale: Library must not impose logging infrastructure on consumers

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with CHANGELOG; GitHub Actions CI and OIDC-based npm publish on semver tag
execution_preference.rationale: Standard open-source release workflow

project.goal: Unified TypeScript SDK for multi-provider vehicle telematics stream subscription
project.target_users: TypeScript/JavaScript fleet application developers

backend.runtime: Bun 1.x for dev/test; compiled output targets Node 18+ and Bun
backend.framework: No framework; pure TypeScript library with ws and eventemitter3
backend.api: Public npm package API surface with TypeScript declarations

security.auth: API keys passed by consumer at runtime; never stored in SDK
security.supply_chain: npm provenance attestation, Dependabot, Snyk scan in CI

data.storage: None; in-memory ring buffer (default 1000 events) for reconnection replay

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: typecheck, lint, bun test, bundle size check, OIDC npm publish on semver tag

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK scope is well-defined for v1.0.0: Samsara adapter first, then Geotab and Verizon Connect. The normalized VehicleTelemetryEvent model is the core value proposition. The ring buffer for missed-event replay during reconnection is a meaningful differentiator versus simpler provider wrappers.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The ProviderAdapter interface pattern with a shared NormalizedEventEmitter is correct. Reconnection logic must be per-adapter since Samsara and Geotab have different WebSocket endpoint behaviors and backoff requirements. Bun is the right choice for local dev given TypeScript-native workflow. Separate entry points per provider enable tree-shaking for consumers who only need one provider.
reasoner_reviews.security: API key handling is the main library security concern. The SDK must never log API keys at any level and must redact them from Error.message strings. Supply-chain security via npm provenance and lockfile integrity checks is essential for a public open-source package.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 100KB gzipped bundle constraint with only ws and eventemitter3 as runtime dependencies is achievable. Zod can be used for provider payload validation internally without being included in the output bundle if schema validation runs at parse time only.

backend.service_design: Core structure: TelemetryClient (unified entry point), ProviderAdapter interface, SamsaraAdapter and GeotabAdapter and VerizonConnectAdapter each extending BaseWebSocketAdapter, NormalizedEventEmitter wrapping eventemitter3, ReconnectionManager with per-adapter exponential backoff state machine, RingBuffer circular array for missed-event replay
backend.api_patterns: Public API: new TelemetryClient(config), client.connect() Promise, client.disconnect(), client.on('telemetry'/'error'/'reconnecting'/'schema_warning'), client.getStatus(). Tree-shaken entry points: @fleet-sdk/core, @fleet-sdk/samsara, @fleet-sdk/geotab, @fleet-sdk/verizon-connect
backend.backend_risks: Provider WebSocket API breaking changes are primary risk; mitigated by fixture-based integration tests against recorded provider payloads and semantic versioning with deprecation policy

security.secrets_management: API keys accepted only via TelemetryClientConfig at runtime; RedactedError wrapper strips 40+ char alphanumeric patterns from error messages before emission; SDK never reads from process.env or files directly
security.abuse_prevention: Rate-limit detection: SamsaraAdapter tracks HTTP 429 responses and backs off per Samsara API docs; SDK emits 'rate_limited' event with retry_after_ms for consumer handling
security.security_risks: Dependency confusion via npm name squatting; mitigated by @fleet-sdk scoped package and lockfile integrity enforcement in CI

data.entities: VehicleTelemetryEvent (vehicle_id, provider, event_type, timestamp, gps GPSPayload optional, engine EnginePayload optional, sensor SensorPayload optional, raw_provider_payload unknown), GPSPayload (lat, lng, heading_deg, speed_kmh, accuracy_m), EnginePayload (rpm, fuel_level_pct, odometer_km, engine_load_pct, dtc_codes string array), StreamSubscription (provider, vehicle_ids, event_types, reconnect_policy), ProviderAdapter interface, RingBuffer generic circular array
data.schema_notes: Optional nested payloads (gps, engine, sensor) allow consumers to type-narrow by event_type; provider-specific fields preserved in raw_provider_payload for advanced consumers; Zod schema validation on raw provider payloads emits schema_warning on unexpected fields
data.data_risks: Provider payload schema drift silently produces undefined fields; mitigated by Zod strict validation with schema_warning events on unexpected fields

devops.deployment_topology: No hosted infrastructure; CI/CD pipeline only via GitHub Actions
devops.ci_cd_design: ci.yml: bun run typecheck, biome check, bun test with fixture-based provider payload tests, bun build minified bundle, bundlesize check <100KB, Snyk scan. release.yml on semver tag: full CI, bun build ESM+CJS+d.ts, OIDC npm publish --provenance, GitHub Release with CHANGELOG extract
devops.devops_risks: npm token compromise allowing malicious publish; mitigated by OIDC-based npm publish with no stored token via GitHub Actions npm provenance workflow

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a TypeScript library SDK for multi-provider telematics stream subscription. Key decisions: ProviderAdapter interface enables generic reconnection management in TelemetryClient; separate entry points per provider enable tree-shaking; ring buffer handles missed events without external deps; Bun for development with compiled ESM+CJS for Node 18+ compatibility. No hosted backend -- pure library architecture with no frontend component.
target_output.title: Multi-Provider Vehicle Telematics TypeScript SDK
target_output.executive_summary: An open-source TypeScript SDK published as @fleet-sdk/core providing a unified EventEmitter interface for subscribing to real-time vehicle telemetry from Samsara, Geotab, and Verizon Connect. Provider adapters normalize GPS, engine, and sensor events into a canonical VehicleTelemetryEvent model. The SDK handles WebSocket lifecycle, exponential-backoff reconnection per provider, and in-memory event buffering during outages with zero hosted infrastructure.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure library architecture: no hosted servers, no databases, no build infrastructure exposed to consumers. TelemetryClient is the unified entry point accepting TelemetryClientConfig with provider credentials and stream subscriptions. It delegates connection lifecycle to SamsaraAdapter, GeotabAdapter, and VerizonConnectAdapter, each implementing the ProviderAdapter interface. NormalizedEventEmitter wraps eventemitter3 and translates provider-specific WebSocket payloads into VehicleTelemetryEvent objects. ReconnectionManager handles per-adapter exponential backoff (initial 1s, max 60s, random jitter). RingBuffer accumulates events during disconnection and flushes in FIFO order on stream resume. Published as @fleet-sdk scoped packages with ESM and CJS dual output compiled via bun build.
target_output.technology_stack: TypeScript 5.4 with strict mode. Bun 1.x as development runtime, test runner, and bundler. Runtime dependencies: ws (WebSocket client for Node 18 compatibility), eventemitter3 (typed EventEmitter). Dev dependencies: Bun, TypeScript, Biome (lint and format), Zod (provider payload schema validation, dev-only, excluded from output bundle), Snyk CLI. Output: ESM (.mjs) and CJS (.cjs) dual bundle via bun build, TypeScript declaration files via tsc --emitDeclarationOnly. Bundle target under 100KB gzipped.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Unified Stream Subscription: TelemetryClient.connect() opens WebSocket connections to all configured providers; single telemetry event fires for every normalized VehicleTelemetryEvent regardless of source. Provider Normalization: SamsaraAdapter maps Samsara Webhook stream payloads; GeotabAdapter maps MyGeotab Go9 device data feed; VerizonConnectAdapter maps Reveal fleet API event stream -- all output VehicleTelemetryEvent. Reconnection Management: per-adapter exponential backoff; reconnecting event with attempt count and delay_ms. Missed-Event Buffer: ring buffer accumulates during disconnection, flushed before live events on reconnect. API Key Safety: RedactedError strips key patterns from error messages.
target_output.system_components: TelemetryClient (public entry point, manages adapter lifecycle and event routing), ProviderAdapter interface (connect, disconnect, getStatus, on methods), SamsaraAdapter (WebSocket to Samsara fleet stream endpoint, JWT auth header), GeotabAdapter (MyGeotab JSONRPC over WebSocket, session token), VerizonConnectAdapter (Reveal OAuth2 event stream), NormalizedEventEmitter (eventemitter3 wrapper with typed event map), ReconnectionManager (per-adapter backoff state machine: disconnected to connecting to connected to reconnecting), RingBuffer generic circular array with configurable capacity, RedactedError (strips API key patterns from error messages before emission).
target_output.workflows: Stream Initialization: Developer calls new TelemetryClient with provider configs; client.connect() calls each adapter's connect() method; SamsaraAdapter opens WebSocket with Authorization header, subscribes to vehicle events, emits connected event; on first payload, adapter calls normalizePayload to produce VehicleTelemetryEvent and emits via NormalizedEventEmitter. Reconnection Flow: adapter WebSocket emits close event; ReconnectionManager transitions to reconnecting, increments attempt counter, computes delay as min(1000 * 2^attempt + jitter, 60000)ms, emits reconnecting event with attempt and delay_ms; after delay, re-calls adapter.connect(); on success, flushes ring buffer events in FIFO order before resuming live stream. Schema Validation: adapter runs Zod parse on raw provider payload; on parse success, maps to VehicleTelemetryEvent; on parse failure with unexpected fields, emits schema_warning with provider, raw_payload, and validation_errors for consumer debugging without throwing.
target_output.data_model: VehicleTelemetryEvent type: vehicle_id string, provider union of samsara geotab verizon_connect, event_type union of gps engine sensor driver_event, timestamp Date, gps optional GPSPayload (lat number, lng number, heading_deg number, speed_kmh number, accuracy_m number), engine optional EnginePayload (rpm number, fuel_level_pct number, odometer_km number, engine_load_pct number, dtc_codes string array), sensor optional SensorPayload (payload Record string number), raw_provider_payload unknown. StreamSubscription type: provider, vehicle_ids string array or 'all', event_types EventTypeEnum array, reconnect_policy with max_attempts initial_delay_ms and max_delay_ms numbers. RingBuffer generic type: capacity number, buffer T array, head number, tail number, size number. ProviderAdapter interface: connect returns Promise void, disconnect void, getStatus returns AdapterStatus, on event string handler Function returns void.
target_output.api_design: Public API via import from @fleet-sdk/core. Constructor: new TelemetryClient(config: TelemetryClientConfig) returns TelemetryClient. Methods: client.connect() returns Promise void (opens all provider connections), client.disconnect() void (graceful shutdown), client.on('telemetry', handler: (event: VehicleTelemetryEvent) => void) void, client.on('error', handler: (err: TelemetryError) => void) void, client.on('reconnecting', handler: (info: ReconnectInfo) => void) void, client.on('schema_warning', handler: (warn: SchemaWarning) => void) void, client.getStatus() returns Record ProviderEnum AdapterStatus. Tree-shaken entry: import SamsaraAdapter from @fleet-sdk/samsara. All public symbols have JSDoc with @param @returns and @example annotations.
target_output.security_and_compliance: API Key Safety: TelemetryClientConfig accepts keys at runtime only; RedactedError.fromError() scans error.message for 40+ char alphanumeric patterns and replaces with [REDACTED] before emitting error events; SDK never logs to console by default. Optional logger callback in config receives structured objects with level, message, and context but never raw credentials. Supply Chain: OIDC-based npm publish via GitHub Actions with no stored npm token; all dependencies pinned in bun.lock; Dependabot weekly updates; Snyk scan in CI blocks on high and critical CVEs. Package scope @fleet-sdk mitigates dependency confusion attacks. README documents that consumers must store API keys in environment variables.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No hosted infrastructure. GitHub Actions CI workflow (ci.yml): triggered on PR and push to main -- bun run typecheck with tsc noEmit, biome check for lint and format, bun test running unit and fixture-based provider payload tests, bun build with target node and minify, bundlesize check under 100KB gzipped, Snyk vulnerability scan. Release workflow (release.yml): triggered on semver tag push v major minor patch -- full CI run, bun build producing ESM and CJS and d.ts output, npm publish with provenance via OIDC token, GitHub Release created with CHANGELOG section extracted by release script. Version branches maintained for major versions.
target_output.observability: SDK emits no external telemetry. Optional structured logging via config.logger callback receiving objects with level (debug, info, warn, error), message string, and context Record. SDK events for consumer monitoring: telemetry (VehicleTelemetryEvent), error (TelemetryError with provider and redacted message), reconnecting (provider, attempt number, delay_ms), schema_warning (provider, raw_payload, validation_errors array), connected (provider), disconnected (provider, reason string). Consumers wire these to Pino or Winston or Datadog as shown in README examples.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost -- library distributed via npm with no server infrastructure. GitHub Actions free tier covers CI for open-source. npm storage is negligible. Scaling is the consuming application's concern; SDK is stateless per TelemetryClient instance. Multiple instances can be created in the same process for vehicle_id sharding. Ring buffer memory footprint at default 1000-event capacity is approximately 1.5MB at average event size 1.5KB.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Samsara Adapter and Core v1.0.0 (6 weeks): ProviderAdapter interface, TelemetryClient, NormalizedEventEmitter, ReconnectionManager, RingBuffer, SamsaraAdapter with GPS and engine normalization, RedactedError, Zod schema validation for Samsara payloads, unit tests with fixture payloads, npm publish as @fleet-sdk/core v1.0.0. Phase 2 Geotab and Verizon Connect v1.1.0 (4 weeks post v1.0.0): GeotabAdapter for MyGeotab JSONRPC WebSocket, VerizonConnectAdapter for Reveal OAuth2 event stream, fixture-based integration tests per provider, tree-shaken entry packages @fleet-sdk/geotab and @fleet-sdk/verizon-connect. Phase 3 Community and Extensibility (ongoing): ProviderAdapter plugin documentation for community adapters, TypeScript codegen from provider OpenAPI specs, Motive and Trimble adapters.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode enforced; no-any rule via Biome lint. All public API symbols must have JSDoc with @param @returns and @example. Fixture-based testing: each provider adapter has tests/fixtures/ with recorded WebSocket payloads; unit tests replay fixtures against adapter and assert VehicleTelemetryEvent output shape. No console.log anywhere in src/ enforced by Biome rule. Semantic versioning strictly enforced: breaking changes to public API require major version bump. CHANGELOG.md updated on every PR. Bundle size gate blocks merge if gzipped output exceeds 100KB.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Samsara or Geotab changes WebSocket API without notice breaking normalization. Mitigation: Zod strict schema validation emits schema_warning on unexpected fields allowing consumers to detect drift before silent data loss; adapter version pinned in config with warning on deprecated API versions. Risk: ws package vulnerability in consumer supply chain. Mitigation: Dependabot weekly updates, Snyk CI scan, npm provenance attestation prevents tampered publish. Tradeoff: Zod used for provider payload validation internally but excluded from output bundle to meet 100KB constraint; schema types generated from Zod schemas via z.infer. Tradeoff: eventemitter3 over Node.js native EventEmitter for potential Bun and browser-bundler consumer compatibility.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose eventemitter3 over Node.js native EventEmitter for Bun and browser-bundler compatibility. Chose Bun over ts-node or tsx for development runtime per project CLAUDE.md conventions. Chose per-provider entry point tree-shaking over single bundle to allow consumers to include only needed adapters. Ring buffer default capacity set to 1000 events based on estimated 30s at 30 events per second during reconnection window.

metadata.notes: Open-source TypeScript SDK for multi-provider vehicle telematics; pure library_sdk architecture with no hosted infrastructure.

project_goal.value: Batch data pipeline consolidating fuel transaction records from WEX, Comdata, and FleetCor into a unified Snowflake data warehouse to identify cost-saving opportunities and detect fuel fraud
project_goal.rationale: Finance team needs unified fuel spend analytics across three fuel card providers

target_users.value: Fleet finance analysts and fleet managers consuming Snowflake dashboards and fraud alerts
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are internal analysts, not public users

project_class.rationale: Batch ETL pipeline with no interactive UI component

capabilities.rationale: SFTP/API ingestion, ETL transformation, fraud detection, and analytics output are core capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-provider normalization with fraud detection logic and Snowflake analytics output is bounded in scope

risk_level.rationale: Financial card data in transit; internal pipeline with no public exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Fuel card transaction data includes card numbers and merchant details subject to PCI-DSS

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs entirely within AWS VPC; no public endpoints

access_model.value: IAM roles for MWAA task execution; Snowflake RBAC for analyst and pipeline roles
access_model.rationale: Service-to-service auth only; human access via Snowflake RBAC

feature_scope.value: Nightly SFTP and API ingestion from WEX/Comdata/FleetCor, canonical schema normalization, Snowflake load, anomaly detection for fuel fraud flagging, cost center allocation, fleet manager alert emails
feature_scope.rationale: Full pipeline feature set for cost analysis and fraud detection

mvp_scope.value: WEX SFTP ingestion, canonical normalization, Snowflake raw and transformed tables, basic fraud flag on out-of-territory transactions
mvp_scope.rationale: Single provider proves pipeline before Comdata and FleetCor additions

security_baseline.value: Fuel card PAN truncated to last 4 digits before Snowflake load; SFTP credentials in AWS Secrets Manager; Snowflake data encrypted at rest; VPC-only pipeline execution
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS scope reduction via PAN truncation and network isolation

frontend_stack.value: N/A - data pipeline with no UI; Snowflake dashboards consumed by analysts
frontend_stack.rationale: No frontend component; analytics served via Snowflake BI tools

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Airflow 2.8 on AWS MWAA
backend_stack.rationale: Airflow for DAG orchestration; Python for ETL transform logic

data_platform.value: Snowflake (data warehouse); S3 (staging and raw archive); AWS MWAA (Airflow managed)
data_platform.rationale: Snowflake for OLAP analytics; S3 for raw file archive and Airflow staging

hosting_target.value: AWS MWAA in us-east-1 VPC; Snowflake us-east-1 deployment
hosting_target.rationale: Co-located with existing fleet data infrastructure

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw transaction files retained 7 years in S3 Glacier per financial audit requirements; Snowflake transformed data retained 3 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention for audit compliance

future_scope.value: Real-time fraud alerting via Kinesis, predictive fuel cost modeling, integration with fleet management platform for automated cost center allocation
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap items

constraints.value: Pipeline must complete nightly within 4-hour window (midnight to 4 AM); AWS budget under $1,200/month; must not store full card PANs
constraints.rationale: Operational and compliance constraints

observability_baseline.value: CloudWatch for MWAA DAG metrics, Airflow email alerts on DAG failure, Snowflake query history for load monitoring
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing AWS monitoring stack

execution_preference.value: Airflow DAGs version-controlled in Git; GitHub Actions for CI; infrastructure via Terraform
execution_preference.rationale: GitOps approach for DAG and infrastructure changes

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS SAQ-D scope reduction via PAN truncation; internal financial audit requirements for 7-year retention; no cardholder data stored in full form
compliance_context.rationale: Fuel card data handling requires PCI controls even for internal pipelines

project.goal: Nightly fuel transaction consolidation from WEX, Comdata, FleetCor into Snowflake for fraud detection and cost analysis
project.target_users: Fleet finance analysts and fleet managers
project.timeline: WEX provider live in 8 weeks; full 3-provider in 16 weeks

backend.framework: Apache Airflow 2.8 on AWS MWAA

security.auth: IAM roles for MWAA; Secrets Manager for SFTP/API credentials
security.pci: PAN truncation to last 4 digits before Snowflake load; VPC-only execution

data.warehouse: Snowflake with raw and transformed schemas
data.staging: S3 staging bucket for provider files
data.archive: S3 Glacier for 7-year raw file retention

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for DAG lint and unit tests
devops.iac: Terraform for MWAA and S3 resources

reasoner_reviews.product: Pipeline scope is appropriate: nightly ingestion from three fuel card providers with fraud flagging and cost center reporting gives the finance team the unified view they need. The anomaly detection logic for out-of-territory fill-ups and volume spikes is well-defined. Round 1 plan lacked detail on how Comdata API auth differs from WEX SFTP, which caused ISS-001.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Python transform DAGs with a shared normalize_transaction() utility function is the right pattern for multi-provider normalization. The Snowflake COPY INTO pattern from S3 staging is the correct load approach for bulk nightly batches. The previous plan was thin on the fraud detection logic -- specifically the per-vehicle baseline calculation method needs to be specified.
reasoner_reviews.security: PAN truncation before Snowflake load is the right PCI-DSS scope reduction approach. Secrets Manager rotation for SFTP keys and API tokens must be described. The previous plan did not specify how truncated vs full PANs are handled in the S3 raw archive -- this needs clarification.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4-hour nightly window is achievable for the initial WEX volume. As Comdata and FleetCor are added the DAGs should be parallelized. The previous plan had all three providers in a single sequential DAG which risks missing the window at scale.

backend.service_design: Three provider DAGs: wex_ingest_dag, comdata_ingest_dag, fleetcor_ingest_dag each with tasks: fetch_provider_file, validate_schema, normalize_to_canonical, truncate_pan, stage_to_s3, load_to_snowflake_raw. Shared transform library: fleet_fuel_transforms/normalize.py with normalize_transaction(), detect_fraud_flags(), allocate_cost_center() functions.
backend.background_jobs: fraud_detection_dag runs after all provider loads complete; computes per-vehicle rolling 30-day avg gallons per fill-up and flags transactions exceeding 150% of baseline or occurring at merchants outside vehicle home territory
backend.failure_handling: Each DAG task uses Airflow retries (3 attempts, 10-min delay); on terminal failure, dead-letter S3 prefix for raw files; CloudWatch alarm triggers email to data-ops@fleet.com
backend.scaling_notes: Provider DAGs run in parallel via Airflow TriggerDagRunOperator pattern; MWAA worker count auto-scales on queue depth; Snowflake virtual warehouse set to auto-suspend after 5 minutes of inactivity

security.auth_design: MWAA execution role has S3:GetObject on staging bucket and S3:PutObject on raw archive; Snowflake pipeline role has INSERT on raw schema only, SELECT on transformed schema; SFTP credentials and API keys stored in Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation
security.secrets_management: Airflow connections populated from Secrets Manager at DAG runtime via MWAA secrets backend; no plaintext credentials in DAG code or environment variables
security.security_risks: S3 staging bucket with raw files (pre-PAN-truncation) represents brief PCI scope exposure; mitigated by S3 bucket policy restricting access to MWAA execution role only and 24-hour lifecycle policy deleting staging files after Snowflake load

data.entities: fuel_transactions (id, provider ENUM wex/comdata/fleetcor, card_last4, vehicle_id, driver_id, merchant_id, merchant_name, merchant_state, transaction_date, gallons, price_per_gallon, total_amount, cost_center_id, fraud_flag BOOLEAN, fraud_reason TEXT, loaded_at). fuel_cards (card_last4, vehicle_id, carrier_id, status, valid_from, valid_to). merchant_locations (merchant_id, name, address, state, lat, lng). vehicle_assignments (vehicle_id, driver_id, cost_center_id, home_state, effective_from, effective_to). cost_centers (id, name, manager_id, budget_monthly). fraud_flags (id, transaction_id, flag_type ENUM out_of_territory/volume_spike/after_hours, baseline_value, actual_value, flagged_at).
data.storage_design: S3 staging (raw provider files, 24h lifecycle), S3 archive (7-year Glacier IA for raw files post-load), Snowflake raw schema (direct COPY INTO from S3, no transformation), Snowflake transformed schema (dbt models for canonical fuel_transactions, fraud_flags, cost_center_rollups)
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw files: S3 Standard 24h staging, then COPY to S3 Glacier IA for 7-year archive; Snowflake raw schema 90-day retention; transformed schema 3-year retention per legal policy

devops.deployment_topology: AWS MWAA us-east-1 VPC, private subnets only; S3 buckets in same region with VPC endpoint; Snowflake Snowpipe disabled (COPY INTO pattern instead); all egress to WEX/Comdata/FleetCor via NAT gateway
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on PR: pylint and black for DAG files, pytest for transform unit tests (normalize_transaction, detect_fraud_flags with fixture CSVs), Terraform plan for infrastructure changes. On main merge: Terraform apply, DAG files synced to MWAA S3 DAG bucket via GitHub Actions
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch metrics for MWAA: task success rate, duration p95, queue depth; Airflow email alerts on DAG failure; Snowflake INFORMATION_SCHEMA.LOAD_HISTORY queried by daily health-check DAG; CloudWatch alarm if fraud_detection_dag does not complete by 4 AM

ISS-001.title: Comdata API auth method not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan treated all three providers as SFTP-based; Comdata uses OAuth2 REST API not SFTP, requiring different ingestion task implementation

ISS-002.title: Fraud detection baseline calculation method not specified
ISS-002.detail: Plan mentions anomaly detection but does not specify how per-vehicle rolling baselines are computed, stored, or updated, making the fraud_flag logic unimplementable

focus_issues.title: Fraud detection baseline calculation method not specified
focus_issues.detail: Plan mentions anomaly detection but does not specify how per-vehicle rolling baselines are computed, stored, or updated, making the fraud_flag logic unimplementable

latest_recommendations: Specify the fraud detection baseline: rolling 30-day window, stored in Snowflake vehicle_fuel_baselines table, updated nightly after canonical load, with flag threshold at 150% of rolling avg gallons

previous_audits.summary: Round 1 plan covered ingestion and Snowflake load structure adequately but left fraud detection logic at a high-level description with no implementable specification, and misidentified Comdata as SFTP-based.
strengths: WEX SFTP ingestion and S3 staging design is correct
PAN truncation before Snowflake load is the right PCI scope approach
Snowflake raw and transformed schema separation is appropriate
concerns: Comdata uses OAuth2 REST API not SFTP -- adapter task must differ
Fraud detection section says detect anomalies without specifying baseline method, threshold, or storage
blocking_issues: Fraud detection logic not implementable without baseline specification
recommendations: Specify fraud detection as: nightly job computes rolling 30-day avg gallons per vehicle per fill-up, stores in vehicle_fuel_baselines Snowflake table, flags transactions where actual_gallons > 1.5x rolling_avg or merchant_state != vehicle home_state

issue_updates.title: Comdata API auth method not specified
issue_updates.detail: Round 2 should add COMDataAPIOperator custom task using OAuth2 client_credentials flow

issue_updates.title: Fraud detection baseline calculation method not specified
issue_updates.detail: Still described as anomaly detection without baseline specification

previous_plan.architecture_overview: MWAA Airflow DAGs pull provider files, normalize to canonical schema, truncate PANs, load to Snowflake. Fraud detection DAG runs after loads.

best_plan.architecture_overview: MWAA Airflow DAGs pull provider files, normalize to canonical schema, truncate PANs, load to Snowflake. Fraud detection DAG runs after loads.
best_plan.workflows: Ingest DAG: fetch file from provider SFTP, validate CSV schema, normalize fields, truncate PAN to last 4, stage to S3, COPY INTO Snowflake raw. Fraud DAG: compare each transaction against vehicle history and flag anomalies.
