CMK encryption on ADLS Gen2 via Azure Key Vault managed key. TLS 1.3 enforced on JDBC connection to SQL Server via encrypt=true;trustServerCertificate=false in connection string. No plain-text credentials in Databricks notebooks — all via dbutils.secrets with Key Vault-backed scope. Audit trail: every pipeline run and DQ outcome logged to audit_events Delta table, immutable append-only (Delta ACID). SOC 2 Type II controls met via access logging (Azure Diagnostic Settings on ADLS) and change management via AzDO pipeline approvals.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps pipeline stages: (1) Validate — dbt compile and dbt test on dev cluster against 500-row sample dataset. (2) Staging run — 6-month data slice with full quality gate execution; manual approval gate before production. (3) Production — full 7-year backfill parallelized across 3 entity groups (inspection+vehicle, repair+parts, warranty+technician) with 30-minute offset stagger to avoid SQL Server replica overload. Databricks cluster policy: autoscaling 2-8 workers, terminates 10 minutes after job completion. Rollback: raw/ landing zone retained for 90 days; reprocessing triggered by rerunning dbt run --full-refresh for affected entity. Secrets managed via Key Vault; no credentials in AzDO pipeline variables.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor Workbook tracking: Databricks job duration per entity group, rows_extracted vs rows_loaded delta, DQ pass rate per run, JDBC connection error rate. Custom metrics written to audit_events Delta table: run_id, entity_name, rows_source, rows_landed, rows_promoted, checksum_match BOOL, dq_pass BOOL, run_duration_seconds, triggered_by. PagerDuty integration on Azure Monitor alert rule: triggers when DQ pass rate drops below 100% or any job exceeds 6-hour duration threshold.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Databricks autoscaling cluster (2-8 Standard_DS3_v2 workers) keeps compute cost at ~$120-180 per full entity group run. Auto-termination after job completion avoids idle charges. ADLS Gen2 storage at ~$18/TB/month for Delta tables; 7 years of fleet records estimated at 400GB total. Synapse Serverless SQL for analytics queries (pay-per-query) avoids dedicated pool cost. Total project compute estimated at $800-1200 for the full 4-week migration window.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — MVP (Week 1-2): Extract and land inspection_records and vehicle_histories to ADLS Gen2 raw/; implement quality_checker for row count and checksum validation; dbt staging models for both entities; Synapse external tables for analytics team access to initial dataset. Phase 2 — Core Entities (Week 3): Extend extraction to repair_orders, parts_replacements, warranty_claims; add referential integrity checks between repair_orders and vehicle_histories; dbt mart models with partitioning. Phase 3 — Completion (Week 4): service_technicians migration; full dbt mart layer including cross-entity joins; end-to-end quality audit against all source tables; raw/ zone TTL policy activated; documentation and Synapse view layer for BI tooling.
target_output.development_guidelines: dbt model naming: stg__{source}__{entity} for staging, fct__ for fact tables, dim__ for dimensions. All dbt models include schema.yml with column descriptions and dbt tests (not_null, unique, accepted_values for ENUM columns). PySpark extraction scripts use typed schemas (StructType) — no schema inference on SQL Server JDBC reads. All secrets via dbutils.secrets, never os.environ or hardcoded. AzDO pipeline enforces code review for dbt model changes; schema changes require updated schema.yml before merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SQL Server read replica lag causing row count mismatch with primary during quality gate validation. Mitigation: quality gate compares to replica count, not primary; documented acceptable lag threshold of <5 minutes. Risk: Legacy ENUM inconsistencies not fully captured in staging normalization mapping. Mitigation: dbt accepted_values tests on staging models will surface unmapped values as test failures before mart promotion. Tradeoff: dbt on Databricks adds adapter complexity over pure PySpark; accepted for the typed transformation and test framework benefits. Risk: 4-week window too tight if SQL Server replica performance degrades under parallel extraction. Mitigation: extraction thread count capped at 4 per entity, with fallback to sequential mode if replica query times exceed 30 seconds.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected dbt-databricks over Azure Data Factory mapping data flows for type-safe SQL transformations and version-controlled models. Confirmed read replica as extraction source to protect OLTP performance. Chose Delta Lake v2 over Parquet-only for ACID support during incremental runs. Partitioned Delta tables by year/month for Synapse query pruning on date-range analytics.

metadata.notes: Fleet records batch migration to Azure Delta Lake with dbt transformation and data quality gates.

project_goal.value: Provide a standardized API gateway enabling third-party logistics software (TMS, WMS, ERP) to integrate with fleet telematics, dispatch, and maintenance systems via unified REST and webhook APIs
project_goal.rationale: Current point-to-point integrations between logistics vendors and fleet systems are brittle and unauditable

target_users.value: Partner logistics software vendors and their developer teams integrating TMS, WMS, and ERP systems
target_users.rationale: External B2B API consumers requiring stable integration contracts

project_class.rationale: Platform provides shared API gateway infrastructure rather than end-user application

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, devops, public_api, integrations
capabilities.rationale: API gateway with OAuth auth, partner data storage, webhook delivery, and devops platform management

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-tenant partner isolation, webhook event bus, Kong plugin pipeline, and mTLS for sensitive partners add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Partner-facing exposure with confidential fleet data; mitigated by OAuth scoping and tenant isolation

data_sensitivity.rationale: Telematics data, dispatch records, and maintenance histories are commercially sensitive fleet operations data

external_exposure.rationale: API access restricted to registered partner tenants with signed data processing agreements

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials for machine-to-machine API access; API keys for webhook verification; per-partner rate limit policies; scope-based authorization for resource access
access_model.rationale: Machine-to-machine partner integration pattern with granular scope control

feature_scope.value: Partner tenant registration and credential provisioning, OAuth 2.0 token issuance, API key management, webhook subscription management, rate limiting per partner tier, request routing to internal fleet services, webhook event delivery with retry, audit logging for all API calls
feature_scope.rationale: Full partner integration lifecycle from onboarding to event delivery

mvp_scope.value: OAuth client registration, token issuance, rate limit enforcement via Kong, routing to telematics and dispatch APIs, webhook subscription and delivery for vehicle departure/arrival events
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum required to onboard first TMS partner within Q3 timeline

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 on all partner-facing endpoints, OAuth 2.0 client credentials, API key HMAC webhook signature verification, WAF on Kong ingress, audit log for every API call with partner_tenant_id
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing confidential data requires strong authentication and full auditability

frontend_stack.value: None — infrastructure platform with no user-facing browser UI; developer portal managed separately
frontend_stack.rationale: API gateway infrastructure, no frontend component

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 for gateway management services; Kong API Gateway 3.x as the proxy layer
backend_stack.rationale: Go provides high-throughput for gateway management APIs; Kong handles request proxying, rate limiting, and plugin pipeline natively

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for partner tenant data, OAuth credentials, webhook subscriptions, and audit logs; Redis 7 for rate limit counters and token caching
data_platform.rationale: Relational store for partner metadata; Redis for high-throughput rate limit enforcement

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in us-central1, Kong deployed as Kubernetes Ingress Controller
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot reduces cluster management overhead; Kong Ingress provides native Kubernetes-integrated API gateway

privacy_retention_policy.value: API audit logs retained 2 years per data processing agreements; OAuth tokens expire 1 hour; API keys rotatable on demand; webhook delivery logs retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Partner DPAs require 2-year audit trail; short token TTL limits exposure window

future_scope.value: GraphQL API layer for complex fleet data queries, partner self-service developer portal, usage analytics dashboard for partner consumption reporting
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 after initial REST and webhook foundation is live

constraints.value: Must support 50+ concurrent partner tenants at launch; Kong plugin changes require zero-downtime deployment; all partner data scoped to partner_tenant_id at database row level
constraints.rationale: Multi-tenancy isolation is a contractual requirement in partner DPAs

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards backed by Prometheus metrics; Kong request logs forwarded to GCP Cloud Logging; GitLab CI pipeline run history
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate observability standard on GCP stack

execution_preference.value: Platform team owns infrastructure; partner onboarding via self-service after MVP; GitOps-driven Kong configuration changes
execution_preference.rationale: GitOps for Kong config ensures reproducible gateway state and audit trail

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II for API platform; GDPR Article 28 data processing agreements with EU-based logistics partners; partner data isolation contractually required
compliance_context.rationale: SOC 2 required for enterprise partner onboarding; GDPR applies to EU partner vehicle data

project.goal: API gateway infrastructure enabling logistics software vendors to integrate with fleet telematics and dispatch via standardized REST and webhook APIs
project.target_users: Partner TMS, WMS, ERP developer teams

backend.framework: Custom Go services for tenant management and webhook delivery; Kong 3.x for proxy and plugin pipeline
backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.0 for management plane; Kong proxy for data plane

security.auth: OAuth 2.0 client credentials (Hydra or Kong OAuth plugin); HMAC-SHA256 webhook signature
security.transport: TLS 1.3 on all ingress; WAF via Kong plugin
security.tenant_isolation: partner_tenant_id scoping on all PostgreSQL queries; Kong consumer mapping to tenant

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 (partner tenants, credentials, webhook subscriptions, audit logs)
data.cache: Redis 7 (rate limit counters, token cache, webhook retry state)

devops.environments: dev, staging, production with Kong declarative config (decK)

constraints.zero_downtime: Kong config updates via decK sync, no pod restarts

reasoner_reviews.product: Partner onboarding scope is correctly prioritized around OAuth credentials and webhook subscriptions. The self-service portal is appropriately deferred to Phase 2 — manual onboarding for the first cohort of TMS partners is sufficient for MVP.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Kong as the data-plane proxy is the right choice for this pattern — rate limiting, OAuth token validation, and request transformation plugins avoid reimplementing those in Go. The Go management services should focus on partner lifecycle (tenant CRUD, credential provisioning) and webhook delivery orchestration, not proxying. Recommend Kong decK for GitOps-driven gateway config to avoid configuration drift.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth 2.0 client credentials with short-lived tokens (1-hour TTL) limits blast radius of leaked credentials. HMAC-SHA256 signature on webhook payloads allows partner systems to verify authenticity. Row-level tenant isolation in PostgreSQL is critical — every query must include WHERE partner_tenant_id = $1.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 50 concurrent partner tenants at launch is within Kong's capacity on GKE Autopilot at moderate pod count. Redis rate limit counters at this scale are sub-millisecond. GDPR compliance requires data residency confirmation for EU partners — consider a separate EU GKE cluster in eu-west1 for GDPR-scoped tenants.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Webhook delivery reliability is a common failure mode. The retry strategy must handle partner endpoint downtime gracefully — exponential backoff with a dead-letter queue and alerting for sustained delivery failures is essential before the first partner goes live.

backend.service_design: Two Go services: tenant-mgmt-service (partner tenant CRUD, OAuth credential provisioning, webhook subscription management) and webhook-delivery-service (event subscription evaluation, signed payload delivery, retry orchestration). Both services deployed as GKE Deployments behind internal Services; Kong routes partner API calls to appropriate upstream fleet services.
backend.api_patterns: Management plane: REST API at /admin/v1/ for platform operators (tenant registration, rate limit policy assignment). Data plane: Kong proxies /fleet/v1/telematics, /fleet/v1/dispatch, /fleet/v1/maintenance to internal upstream services with OAuth scope validation and rate limit enforcement.
backend.failure_handling: Webhook delivery: exponential backoff retry (1s, 5s, 30s, 5m, 30m) for up to 24 hours; failed delivery attempts logged to webhook_delivery_attempts table; dead-letter alert after 24-hour delivery failure; partner dashboard shows delivery status.
backend.scaling_notes: Kong data plane scales horizontally on GKE Autopilot based on request throughput; Go webhook delivery service scales on queue depth; PostgreSQL connection pooling via PgBouncer to handle burst tenant onboarding events

security.auth_design: OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow via Kong OAuth 2.0 plugin; partner receives client_id and client_secret at registration; tokens scoped to granted resource sets (telematics:read, dispatch:read, maintenance:read); tokens cached in Redis with TTL matching expiry
security.authorization_model: Kong consumer per partner tenant, mapped to partner_tenant_id; Kong ACL plugin enforces scope restrictions per consumer; all PostgreSQL queries in Go services parameterized with partner_tenant_id to enforce row-level isolation
security.secrets_management: GCP Secret Manager stores Kong admin token, PostgreSQL password, Redis auth token; GKE Workload Identity binds service accounts to Secret Manager access; no secrets in GitLab CI environment variables except GCP service account key for deployment
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Kong request logs (partner_tenant_id, endpoint, status, latency) forwarded to Cloud Logging; Go services write to audit_log PostgreSQL table for tenant lifecycle events; Cloud Logging log sink to BigQuery for 2-year retention per partner DPA requirements

data.entities: partner_tenants (tenant_id UUID PK, company_name, tier ENUM standard/premium/enterprise, status ENUM active/suspended, created_at, gdpr_scoped BOOL). integration_tokens (token_id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, client_id, client_secret_hash, granted_scopes TEXT[], expires_at, revoked_at). webhook_subscriptions (subscription_id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, event_type ENUM vehicle.departed/vehicle.arrived/vehicle.exception/maintenance.due, endpoint_url, signing_secret_hash, active BOOL). rate_limit_policies (policy_id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, requests_per_minute INT, requests_per_day INT). audit_logs (log_id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, endpoint, method, status_code, latency_ms, timestamp). webhook_delivery_attempts (attempt_id UUID PK, subscription_id FK, event_id, attempt_number, status ENUM pending/delivered/failed, response_code, attempted_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL (multi-AZ, automatic failover) for all relational partner data; Redis 7 on Memorystore for rate limit counters (INCR/EXPIRE pattern per partner per minute window) and short-lived token cache

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster with namespaces: kong-system (Kong Ingress Controller), fleet-gateway (tenant-mgmt-service, webhook-delivery-service), data-plane (PgBouncer). Cloud SQL accessed via Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: on PR, run Go unit tests and Kong decK diff to preview config changes; on merge to main, build and push Docker images to Artifact Registry, deploy to staging via Helm, run integration test suite against staging Kong, manual approval gate, deploy to production.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes Kong metrics (kong_http_requests_total by consumer and route), Go service custom metrics (webhook_delivery_success_rate, tenant_registration_total). Grafana dashboards: API throughput by partner tier, webhook delivery SLA by event type, rate limit hit rate by tenant. Alerting: PagerDuty on webhook delivery failure >10% over 15 minutes, Kong error rate >1%.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a multi-tenant API gateway platform for fleet telematics partner integrations. The split between Kong as data-plane proxy and Go services for management plane is the central architectural decision — Kong handles rate limiting, OAuth validation, and request routing without custom code, while Go services own partner lifecycle and webhook delivery. Row-level tenant isolation in PostgreSQL and per-consumer Kong ACLs together enforce the contractual data isolation requirement across both application and gateway layers.
target_output.title: Fleet Telematics Partner Integration Gateway
target_output.executive_summary: An API gateway infrastructure platform built on Kong 3.x and Go 1.22 running on GKE Autopilot that enables third-party logistics vendors to integrate with fleet telematics, dispatch, and maintenance systems via OAuth-secured REST APIs and signed webhook event delivery. Supports 50+ concurrent partner tenants with per-tier rate limiting, row-level data isolation, and 2-year audit log retention for SOC 2 and GDPR compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-plane architecture: the data plane is Kong Ingress Controller handling all inbound partner API traffic — enforcing OAuth token validation, rate limits, and ACL scope checks before proxying to internal fleet microservices. The management plane consists of two Go services — tenant-mgmt-service for partner tenant lifecycle and credential provisioning, and webhook-delivery-service for event subscription evaluation and signed payload delivery. PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL stores all partner state with partner_tenant_id row-level scoping. Redis 7 on Memorystore handles sub-millisecond rate limit counter operations and token caching. All configuration changes to Kong are managed via decK GitOps, ensuring gateway state is version-controlled and auditable.
target_output.technology_stack: Gateway: Kong 3.x (OAuth 2.0, Rate Limiting Advanced, ACL, Request Transformer plugins). Go 1.22 for tenant-mgmt-service and webhook-delivery-service. PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL (multi-AZ). Redis 7 on Memorystore. GKE Autopilot us-central1. GitLab CI with Helm and decK. GCP Secret Manager with Workload Identity. Prometheus + Grafana for metrics. Cloud Logging + BigQuery for audit log retention.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Partner Tenant Onboarding: platform operator registers partner via admin API, system provisions client_id/client_secret pair and assigns rate limit policy tier. OAuth Token Issuance: partner system exchanges credentials for scoped access token via Kong OAuth 2.0 plugin; tokens valid 1 hour and cached in Redis. Webhook Subscription Management: partner registers endpoint URLs and selects event types (vehicle.departed, vehicle.arrived, maintenance.due); system generates HMAC signing secret per subscription. API Proxying: Kong evaluates incoming requests against consumer ACL scopes, enforces rate limits, logs to Cloud Logging, and proxies to internal telematics or dispatch upstream. Webhook Delivery: webhook-delivery-service evaluates telematics events against active subscriptions, sends HMAC-signed POST payloads, retries with exponential backoff for up to 24 hours.
target_output.system_components: Kong Ingress Controller (kong-system namespace, 3 replicas, handles all partner API traffic), tenant-mgmt-service (Go, 2 replicas, manages partner_tenants, integration_tokens, webhook_subscriptions), webhook-delivery-service (Go, 2-8 replicas autoscaling on queue depth, event subscription evaluation and delivery), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (multi-AZ, PgBouncer connection pool), Memorystore Redis 7 (rate limit counters and token cache), GCP Cloud Logging (Kong request logs forwarded via Fluent Bit), BigQuery (2-year log archive), GCP Secret Manager (Kong admin token, DB password, Redis auth).
target_output.workflows: Partner API Integration Workflow: partner developer requests registration via fleet operator support channel; tenant-mgmt-service operator creates partner_tenant record and calls Kong Admin API to create consumer with ACL scopes; system returns client_id, client_secret to partner; partner system calls POST /oauth/token with client credentials to receive Bearer token; partner calls GET /fleet/v1/telematics/vehicles/{id}/location — Kong validates token scope, checks rate limit counter in Redis, logs request to Cloud Logging, proxies to internal telematics service, returns response. Webhook Event Delivery Workflow: telematics service publishes vehicle.departed event to internal Pub/Sub topic; webhook-delivery-service subscriber evaluates active webhook_subscriptions for vehicle.departed event type; for each matching subscription, constructs payload, computes HMAC-SHA256 signature using subscription signing secret, sends POST to partner endpoint_url with X-Fleet-Signature header; on 2xx response, records delivered status; on non-2xx or timeout, increments attempt_number and schedules retry with exponential backoff; after 24-hour failure window, marks subscription delivery as dead-lettered and triggers PagerDuty alert.
target_output.data_model: partner_tenants (tenant_id UUID PK, company_name VARCHAR(200), tier ENUM standard/premium/enterprise, status ENUM active/suspended/pending, gdpr_scoped BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). integration_tokens (token_id UUID PK, tenant_id UUID FK, client_id VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE, client_secret_hash VARCHAR(200), granted_scopes TEXT[], issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable). webhook_subscriptions (subscription_id UUID PK, tenant_id UUID FK, event_type ENUM vehicle.departed/vehicle.arrived/vehicle.exception/maintenance.due, endpoint_url TEXT, signing_secret_hash VARCHAR(200), active BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rate_limit_policies (policy_id UUID PK, tenant_id UUID FK, requests_per_minute INT, requests_per_day INT, burst_multiplier DECIMAL). audit_logs (log_id UUID PK, tenant_id UUID FK, endpoint TEXT, http_method VARCHAR(10), status_code INT, latency_ms INT, kong_consumer_id VARCHAR(100), timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). webhook_delivery_attempts (attempt_id UUID PK, subscription_id UUID FK, event_id VARCHAR(200), attempt_number INT, status ENUM pending/delivered/failed/dead_lettered, response_code INT nullable, attempted_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Management Plane (internal, platform operators only): POST /admin/v1/tenants (register partner tenant), GET /admin/v1/tenants/{id} (tenant status), POST /admin/v1/tenants/{id}/credentials (provision OAuth client), PUT /admin/v1/tenants/{id}/rate-limit-policy (assign tier), POST /admin/v1/tenants/{id}/webhooks (register subscription), GET /admin/v1/tenants/{id}/webhooks/{sub_id}/deliveries (delivery status). Data Plane (partner-facing, via Kong): POST /oauth/token (client credentials token exchange), GET /fleet/v1/telematics/vehicles (list fleet vehicles by tenant scope), GET /fleet/v1/telematics/vehicles/{vin}/location (current GPS position), GET /fleet/v1/dispatch/trips (active trips for tenant), GET /fleet/v1/maintenance/work-orders (open work orders). All partner endpoints require Authorization: Bearer {token} header; rate limited per partner_tenant_id.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth 2.0 client credentials with scoped tokens (telematics:read, dispatch:read, maintenance:read) enforced via Kong OAuth 2.0 plugin; token TTL 1 hour with no refresh to limit exposure window. Kong Rate Limiting Advanced plugin enforces per-tenant request quotas from rate_limit_policies table via Redis counters. HMAC-SHA256 webhook signatures: webhook-delivery-service computes signature over payload body using subscription signing secret, sends as X-Fleet-Signature-256 header; partner verifies on receipt. Row-level tenant isolation: all Go service PostgreSQL queries parameterized with partner_tenant_id; database user has no cross-tenant query access. WAF via Kong plugin blocks common injection patterns on inbound requests. SOC 2 Type II: audit_log table captures every API call with tenant identity; Cloud Logging BigQuery sink provides 2-year retention. GDPR: gdpr_scoped flag on partner_tenants triggers data residency check; EU-scoped tenants routed to eu-west1 Kong instance (Phase 2).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot cluster (us-central1) with three namespaces: kong-system (Kong Ingress Controller, 3 replicas), fleet-gateway (tenant-mgmt-service 2 replicas, webhook-delivery-service 2-8 replicas with HPA on queue depth), data-plane (PgBouncer). Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar on each Go service pod for PostgreSQL connectivity. Kong configuration managed via decK declarative YAML in GitLab repo — changes reviewed as PRs, decK sync applied to staging then production. GitLab CI pipeline: Go test and vet, decK diff preview, Docker build to Artifact Registry, Helm deploy to staging, integration test suite (OAuth flow, webhook delivery, rate limit enforcement), manual approval, Helm deploy to production. Zero-downtime Kong config updates via decK sync (no pod restart required). Rollback: Helm rollback to previous release; decK rollback via git revert and re-sync.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics scraped from Kong (/metrics endpoint): kong_http_requests_total labeled by consumer (partner_tenant_id), route, and status. Go services expose custom metrics: webhook_delivery_attempts_total (labeled by event_type and status), tenant_registration_total, token_issuance_total. Grafana dashboards: API throughput and error rate by partner tenant, webhook delivery success rate by event type, rate limit hit rate heatmap by tenant tier, Cloud SQL connection pool saturation. Alerting via PagerDuty: webhook dead-letter rate >0 (immediate), Kong p99 latency >500ms sustained 5 minutes, PostgreSQL connection pool >80% utilized.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot at 50 partner tenants with moderate traffic: ~$400-600/month compute. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL (2 vCPU, 8GB, HA): ~$180/month. Memorystore Redis (1GB): ~$50/month. Cloud Logging ingestion for Kong request logs: ~$30-60/month at 10M requests/day. BigQuery audit log storage (2-year): ~$20/month. Total ~$700-900/month MVP. Scaling path: Kong Ingress scales horizontally on GKE Autopilot; Redis rate limit counters handle 100K+ requests/second at single-digit millisecond latency; PostgreSQL scales via read replica for audit log queries.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — MVP (8 weeks): Kong Ingress deployment on GKE Autopilot, tenant-mgmt-service with OAuth credential provisioning, Kong consumer and ACL provisioning per tenant, rate limit policy enforcement via Redis, proxying to telematics and dispatch upstreams, webhook-delivery-service for vehicle.departed and vehicle.arrived events, audit_log writes to PostgreSQL, Cloud Logging integration. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Webhook subscriptions for maintenance.due events, dead-letter alerting and delivery dashboard for operators, decK GitOps pipeline with PR-based Kong config review, BigQuery audit log sink for SOC 2 reporting. Phase 3 (4 weeks): GDPR eu-west1 cluster for EU-scoped partners, partner self-service developer portal, usage analytics API for partner consumption dashboards.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go services: all PostgreSQL queries must include WHERE partner_tenant_id = $1 — enforced by code review checklist item. No direct Kong Admin API calls from Go services in production — all Kong config changes via decK. HMAC signing secret never logged — masked in all structured log output. Integration tests run against Kong staging instance, not mocks, to validate plugin pipeline end-to-end. Kong plugin configuration changes require decK diff reviewed by platform lead before merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Kong plugin configuration drift between staging and production if decK sync is bypassed. Mitigation: GitLab CI blocks production deployment if decK diff shows unapproved changes. Risk: webhook-delivery-service Redis dependency — if Redis is unavailable, rate limit enforcement degrades to allow-all. Mitigation: Kong rate limit plugin configured to fail-open with alert on Redis unavailability; acceptable for partner-facing traffic where availability outweighs rate limit enforcement. Tradeoff: Kong OAuth 2.0 plugin vs. external IdP (Hydra) — Kong plugin chosen for simpler infrastructure at 50-tenant scale; Hydra would be required at 500+ tenants. Risk: GDPR data residency non-compliance for EU partners until Phase 3 eu-west1 cluster. Mitigation: EU partner onboarding deferred until Phase 3; documented in partner DPA amendments.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Kong over Apigee for lower GCP-native cost and plugin ecosystem fit. Confirmed client credentials flow over authorization code — no human user login required for M2M logistics integrations. Chose Cloud SQL over AlloyDB for lower cost at current partner scale. Confirmed decK for Kong GitOps over Terraform Kong provider for better declarative diff tooling.

metadata.notes: Multi-tenant API gateway for fleet telematics partner integrations on GKE with Kong and Go.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for fleet compliance officers to audit vehicle inspection records, driver qualification files, and HOS logs against FMCSA and DOT regulations, generating compliance gap reports and remediation checklists
project_goal.rationale: Manual compliance review is error-prone and time-consuming for multi-vehicle fleets

target_users.value: Fleet safety directors and compliance officers at motor carriers subject to FMCSA oversight
target_users.rationale: Compliance officers run audits ahead of DOT compliance reviews

project_class.rationale: Runs locally on compliance officer workstations, no hosted service required

capabilities.rationale: Rules engine processing, local SQLite storage, and optional ELD API integration

complexity_level.rationale: FMCSA rules engine with 49 CFR Part 391/395 encoding requires careful domain modeling but no distributed systems

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect compliance assessments could expose fleet operators to FMCSA fines and out-of-service orders

data_sensitivity.rationale: Driver qualification files, medical certificates, and HOS logs are sensitive personnel and operational records

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs entirely on compliance officer laptop; no network exposure

access_model.value: Local file system access only; optional API key for ELD data source integration stored in OS keychain
access_model.rationale: No authentication required for local-only tool; external API keys protected via OS keychain

feature_scope.value: Ingest ELD-exported HOS CSV/JSON, DQ file documents, vehicle inspection records; FMCSA 49 CFR 391 and 395 rules engine; per-driver and per-vehicle compliance gap report; prioritized remediation checklist; SQLite audit history
feature_scope.rationale: Full compliance audit workflow for pre-DOT-review use case

mvp_scope.value: HOS log ingest from CSV, FMCSA 49 CFR 395 HOS rules validation, per-driver violation report, SQLite storage of audit runs
mvp_scope.rationale: HOS violations are the highest-frequency compliance issue for most carriers

security_baseline.value: No network calls in default mode; API keys stored in OS keychain via keyring library; SQLite database encrypted with SQLCipher; no telemetry or external logging
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential driver and vehicle data must not leave the local workstation

frontend_stack.value: None — terminal output only; Click-based CLI with Rich for formatted tables and progress indicators
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool with no browser interface

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click for CLI framework; Pydantic v2 for data validation
backend_stack.rationale: Click provides composable command groups; Pydantic validates ELD export schemas before rules engine processing

data_platform.value: SQLite with SQLCipher encryption for local audit history and compliance rule snapshots
data_platform.rationale: Fully local, no server required; SQLCipher provides encryption at rest for confidential data

hosting_target.value: Local binary distribution via PyPI package and compiled executable via PyInstaller
hosting_target.rationale: Compliance officers install on their own workstations; no hosted infrastructure

privacy_retention_policy.value: Local SQLite audit history retained at user discretion; tool documentation recommends 3-year retention per FMCSA record-keeping requirements; no remote storage of driver data
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FMCSA requires carriers to retain DQ file and HOS records for minimum 3 years

future_scope.value: Integration with major ELD provider APIs for direct data pull, PDF report export, CI integration for automated compliance monitoring
future_scope.rationale: Direct ELD integration reduces manual export/import friction for large fleets

constraints.value: Must run offline without network access in default mode; binary must work on Windows 10/11 and macOS 13+; no Python runtime required for end users
constraints.rationale: Compliance officers often work in environments with restricted network access

observability_baseline.value: Local audit run logs written to SQLite audit_runs table; --verbose flag outputs rule evaluation trace to stdout
observability_baseline.rationale: No centralized monitoring for a local CLI tool; audit run history stored locally

execution_preference.value: Iterative rule set releases; new FMCSA regulation updates shipped as patch releases; semantic versioning
execution_preference.rationale: FMCSA regulations update periodically; patch releases must be trackable for compliance officers

compliance_context.value: FMCSA 49 CFR Parts 390-396, DOT drug and alcohol testing 49 CFR Part 382, state-level vehicle inspection requirements
compliance_context.rationale: Tool encodes FMCSA requirements; incorrect rule implementation creates liability for motor carriers using it

project.goal: CLI tool for FMCSA and DOT compliance auditing of HOS logs, DQ files, and vehicle inspection records
project.target_users: Fleet safety directors, compliance officers
project.timeline: MVP 6 weeks, full rules engine 12 weeks

backend.framework: Click CLI framework, Pydantic v2 validation, Rich for terminal output
backend.rules_engine: Python rule classes implementing 49 CFR 391/395 checks

security.auth: OS keychain via keyring for ELD API keys

data.schema: audit_runs, driver_qualification_files, vehicle_inspection_records, hos_log_entries, compliance_rules, audit_findings, remediation_actions

devops.distribution: PyPI package and PyInstaller Windows/macOS executables
devops.environments: local development, CI testing with synthetic FMCSA test fixtures

reasoner_reviews.product: The HOS-first MVP is correct prioritization — HOS violations are the most common FMCSA audit finding and the highest-value use case. DQ file auditing is more document-intensive and better suited to Phase 2 once the rules engine pattern is established.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Python + Click is the right choice here. The rules engine should be implemented as pluggable Python rule classes with a common interface evaluate(record) returning List[Finding] — this allows regulation updates as new class files without modifying the runner. Pydantic models for ELD export schemas enforce validation before rules engine execution.
reasoner_reviews.security: SQLCipher for the local SQLite database is essential given driver DQ files contain medical certificate status and background check records. The OS keychain via keyring is the correct approach for ELD API keys. The tool must never log driver names or license numbers to stdout in default mode — only driver IDs.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: PyInstaller bundling on both Windows and macOS adds CI complexity. Recommend GitHub Actions matrix builds with platform-specific runners for the PyInstaller step. SQLCipher dependency must be statically linked in the PyInstaller bundle to avoid DLL dependency issues on Windows.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan was missing concrete FMCSA rule implementation detail and SQLCipher key management. The security section must specify PBKDF2-derived key from OS keychain passphrase. Windows PyInstaller SQLCipher native linkage must be addressed in the deployment section.

backend.service_design: Click command group: fmcsa-audit (root), subcommands hos, dq, inspect, report, remediate, export, setup. Rules engine: abstract ComplianceRule base class with evaluate(records) returning List[Finding]; concrete classes HOS11HourRule, HOS14HourWindowRule, HOS30MinBreakRule, HOSCycleLimitRule, DQMedicalCertExpiryRule, VehicleInspectionAnnualRule.
backend.api_patterns: Click command signatures: fmcsa-audit hos ingest --file path --format csv|json --driver-id id. fmcsa-audit hos check --driver-id id --period 7d|30d|90d. fmcsa-audit report generate --format terminal|json|csv --output path. fmcsa-audit remediate list --severity critical|high|all --format terminal|csv. Exit codes: 0 compliant, 1 findings present, 2 input validation error.
backend.failure_handling: Pydantic validation errors on ELD import surface as structured messages with row number and field name; partial imports rejected entirely; SQLite writes wrapped in transactions to prevent partial audit state.

security.auth_design: No authentication for local tool access; ELD API key stored via Python keyring library using OS credential store (macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager)
security.secrets_management: keyring.set_password stores ELD API key; SQLCipher key derived via PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 from user passphrase, stored in OS keychain after first-run setup; key never written to SQLite or log files
security.privacy_controls: Driver CDL numbers and full names never written to stdout or log files; only driver_id references in all output; --verbose outputs rule trace with driver_id only

data.entities: audit_runs (run_id INTEGER PK, run_date, period_start, period_end, total_drivers, total_vehicles, findings_count, critical_count, tool_version). driver_qualification_files (dq_id INTEGER PK, run_id FK, driver_id, medical_cert_expiry, mvr_date, drug_test_date, status). vehicle_inspection_records (inspection_id PK, run_id FK, vehicle_id, vin, inspection_date, inspection_type, result, next_due). hos_log_entries (entry_id PK, run_id FK, driver_id, log_date, status_changes TEXT, driving_minutes, on_duty_minutes). compliance_rules (rule_id PK, cfr_citation, rule_name, severity, active, version). audit_findings (finding_id PK, run_id FK, driver_id, vehicle_id, rule_id FK, severity, cfr_citation, status). remediation_actions (action_id PK, finding_id FK, action_description, due_date, assignee, status).
data.schema_notes: HOS status_changes stored as JSON array per log entry (each element: time, status ENUM off_duty/sleeper_berth/driving/on_duty_not_driving) to preserve ELD granularity within local SQLite scale.

devops.deployment_topology: Local installation only; no server infrastructure. PyPI package fmcsa-audit for Python users; PyInstaller bundles for Windows EXE and macOS app for non-Python workstations.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix: ubuntu-latest (pytest, coverage), windows-latest (pytest plus PyInstaller EXE with sqlcipher3-binary static wheel), macos-latest (pytest plus PyInstaller app). On tag v*, build platform bundles and publish to GitHub Releases and PyPI.
devops.observability_stack: Local only: audit_runs table captures run metadata; --verbose outputs rule evaluation trace to stdout; fmcsa-audit export --format csv exports full audit history; no remote telemetry.

ISS-001.title: Rules engine lacks specific CFR section implementation detail
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 and 2 plans described the rules engine generically without specifying which FMCSA 49 CFR sections are implemented or what the violation thresholds are.

ISS-002.title: SQLite encryption strategy underspecified
ISS-002.detail: Plan mentions SQLCipher but does not specify how the encryption key is derived, stored, or managed. If the key is hardcoded or stored in plaintext, the encryption provides no protection for driver DQ data.

ISS-003.title: PyInstaller Windows SQLCipher linkage not addressed
ISS-003.detail: SQLCipher on Windows requires native DLL linkage. Previous plans did not address how the PyInstaller bundle handles pysqlcipher3 native dependency on Windows 10/11.

previous_plan.title: FMCSA Compliance Audit CLI v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Python 3.12 CLI using Click and Pydantic for parsing ELD exports and DQ file records, running through a FMCSA rules engine encoding 49 CFR 391 and 395 requirements, producing per-driver compliance gap reports stored in local SQLite database.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Click command group with rules engine processing ELD CSV/JSON exports; Pydantic models for input validation; SQLite with SQLCipher for local encrypted storage of audit runs and findings.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: SQLCipher encryption for local SQLite database. API keys stored via OS keychain. No network calls in default mode. Driver names masked in stdout output.
previous_plan.workflows: HOS Audit: user runs fmcsa-audit hos ingest file; Pydantic validates; rules engine runs HOS rule classes; findings written to SQLite. Report: fmcsa-audit report generate outputs terminal table of violations by driver.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 focuses on resolving ISS-002 (SQLCipher key management) and ISS-003 (Windows PyInstaller SQLCipher linkage). For ISS-002, the fix uses PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with 100K iterations to derive a 256-bit key from a user passphrase, stored in OS keychain after first-run setup. For ISS-003, the Windows build uses sqlcipher3-binary wheel which statically links SQLCipher 4.x, eliminating DLL dependency failures in the PyInstaller bundle.

fix_report.action_taken: Added PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 key derivation specification: 100K iterations, random 32-byte salt, 256-bit derived key stored in OS keychain via keyring after fmcsa-audit setup; database opened with PRAGMA key on every connection using keychain-retrieved key; salt stored alongside DB file (not secret).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Driver DQ data protected by OS-level credential storage; database cannot be opened without the OS keychain entry, preventing data exposure if SQLite file is copied off workstation.

fix_report.action_taken: Added PyInstaller Windows SQLCipher build spec: GitHub Actions windows-latest runner installs sqlcipher3-binary PyPI package which statically links SQLCipher 4.x; PyInstaller spec file includes sqlcipher3 in hiddenimports; build validated by running pytest test suite against bundled EXE.
fix_report.expected_outcome: PyInstaller EXE on Windows 10/11 includes SQLCipher native libraries without DLL dependency failures; CI matrix validates bundle on windows-latest before every release.

target_output.title: FMCSA Fleet Compliance Audit CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 CLI tool using Click and Pydantic that audits vehicle inspection records, driver qualification files, and HOS logs against FMCSA 49 CFR Parts 391 and 395 for fleet compliance officers. Findings stored in a locally encrypted SQLCipher SQLite database with PBKDF2-derived key management via OS keychain. Distributed as a PyInstaller self-contained executable for Windows 10/11 and macOS 13+ with no Python runtime required on compliance officer workstations.
target_output.architecture_overview: Command-line application structured as a Click group with subcommand modules for HOS ingest, DQ file processing, rules engine execution, and report generation. Pydantic v2 models validate ELD export schemas before records reach the rules engine. The rules engine is a registry of ComplianceRule subclasses, each encoding a specific FMCSA CFR requirement with named threshold values. Findings persisted to SQLite encrypted via SQLCipher with PBKDF2-derived key stored in OS keychain. No network I/O in default mode; optional ELD API integration uses keychain-stored API key. PyInstaller bundles ship the full Python runtime and statically-linked SQLCipher native library for Windows and macOS.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, Click 8.x, Pydantic v2, Rich for terminal tables, SQLite with SQLCipher 4.x via sqlcipher3-binary, keyring library for OS keychain integration, hashlib PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 for database key derivation, PyInstaller 6.x for binary distribution, pytest with synthetic FMCSA fixtures, GitHub Actions matrix CI on ubuntu/windows/macos runners.
target_output.functional_feature_map: HOS Ingestion: parse ELD-exported CSV or JSON, validate against Pydantic HOSExportSchema, write hos_log_entries to encrypted SQLite. HOS Rules Audit: run HOS11HourRule (max 11h driving per 24h, 49 CFR 395.3), HOS14HourWindowRule (14-hour on-duty window from first on-duty, 49 CFR 395.3), HOS30MinBreakRule (30-min off-duty after 8h driving, 49 CFR 395.3), HOSCycleLimitRule (60/70-hour in 7/8-day cycle, 49 CFR 395.3). DQ File Audit: medical cert expiry per 49 CFR 391.41, MVR recency per 49 CFR 391.25, drug test currency per 49 CFR 382. Vehicle Inspection: annual inspection due-date check per 49 CFR 396.17. Remediation List: prioritized checklist by severity with due dates and assignee fields.
target_output.system_components: Click command group (root fmcsa-audit with subcommands setup, hos, dq, inspect, report, remediate, export). ComplianceRule ABC with concrete rule classes per CFR citation. Pydantic schema layer (HOSExportSchema, DQFileSchema, InspectionRecordSchema). SQLCipher database manager (PBKDF2 key derivation, keychain integration, PRAGMA key injection on connect). keyring adapter for macOS Keychain and Windows Credential Manager. PyInstaller bundle with Python runtime and statically-linked SQLCipher native extension.
target_output.workflows: First-Run Setup Workflow: user runs fmcsa-audit setup; CLI prompts for master passphrase; PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 derives 256-bit key using 100K iterations and random 32-byte salt; derived key stored in OS keychain via keyring.set_password with service name fmcsa-audit-db; salt written to ~/.fmcsa-audit/db.salt; subsequent database opens call keyring.get_password to retrieve key and issue PRAGMA key = x:hex_key before any SQL. HOS Audit Workflow: compliance officer runs fmcsa-audit hos ingest --file driver_eld.csv --driver-id D-0042 --format csv; Pydantic HOSExportSchema validates each row; on success, hos_log_entries written to SQLite; fmcsa-audit hos check --driver-id D-0042 --period 7d runs all 4 HOS rule classes against stored entries; findings with cfr_citation written to audit_findings; Rich table displays violations grouped by driver ID with regulation reference and violation detail. Remediation Workflow: fmcsa-audit remediate list --severity critical --format csv --output remediation.csv generates CSV with columns finding_id, driver_id, vehicle_id, cfr_citation, violation_description, due_date, assignee for fleet manager distribution.
target_output.data_model: audit_runs (run_id INTEGER PK, run_date TEXT, period_start TEXT, period_end TEXT, total_drivers INT, total_vehicles INT, findings_count INT, critical_count INT, tool_version TEXT). driver_qualification_files (dq_id INTEGER PK, run_id INT FK, driver_id TEXT, medical_cert_expiry TEXT, medical_cert_examiner TEXT, mvr_date TEXT, background_check_date TEXT, drug_test_date TEXT, status TEXT). vehicle_inspection_records (inspection_id INTEGER PK, run_id INT FK, vehicle_id TEXT, vin TEXT, inspection_date TEXT, inspection_type TEXT, result TEXT, next_due TEXT). hos_log_entries (entry_id INTEGER PK, run_id INT FK, driver_id TEXT, log_date TEXT, status_changes TEXT, driving_minutes INT, on_duty_minutes INT, off_duty_minutes INT, sleeper_berth_minutes INT). compliance_rules (rule_id INTEGER PK, cfr_citation TEXT, rule_name TEXT, description TEXT, severity TEXT, active INT, version TEXT). audit_findings (finding_id INTEGER PK, run_id INT FK, driver_id TEXT, vehicle_id TEXT, rule_id INT FK, severity TEXT, cfr_citation TEXT, description TEXT, detected_at TEXT, status TEXT). remediation_actions (action_id INTEGER PK, finding_id INT FK, action_description TEXT, due_date TEXT, assignee TEXT, status TEXT).
target_output.api_design: No external REST API — fully local CLI interface. Commands: fmcsa-audit setup (passphrase and keychain initialization). fmcsa-audit hos ingest --file path --driver-id id --format csv|json. fmcsa-audit hos check --driver-id id|--all --period 7d|30d|90d. fmcsa-audit dq check --file path|--driver-id id. fmcsa-audit inspect check --file path. fmcsa-audit report generate --format terminal|json|csv --output path. fmcsa-audit remediate list --severity critical|high|all --format terminal|csv --output path. fmcsa-audit export --format csv --output path. Exit codes: 0 compliant, 1 findings present, 2 input validation error, 3 keychain error.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SQLCipher key management: fmcsa-audit setup derives 256-bit key via PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 (100,000 iterations, random 32-byte salt) from user master passphrase; derived key stored in OS keychain via keyring (macOS Keychain or Windows Credential Manager); salt stored in ~/.fmcsa-audit/db.salt; every database connection issues PRAGMA key = x:hex_key immediately after open using keychain-retrieved key. Driver privacy: CDL numbers and full names never written to stdout or log files; only driver_id references in all output modes; --verbose outputs CFR rule trace with driver_id only. ELD API keys stored via keyring, never in config files or environment variables. FMCSA accuracy: compliance_rules table versioned per tool release; each rule class unit-tested against FMCSA published violation examples from 49 CFR 395 appendices.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI matrix: ubuntu-latest (pytest with synthetic fixtures, coverage gate 85%), windows-latest (pytest plus PyInstaller EXE build using sqlcipher3-binary wheel which statically links SQLCipher 4.x — PyInstaller spec sets hiddenimports=['sqlcipher3'] and bundles native extension; post-build test runs pytest --runexe dist/fmcsa-audit.exe to validate bundle integrity), macos-latest (pytest plus PyInstaller app using Homebrew SQLCipher static lib). On git tag v*, matrix builds complete, GitHub Release created with platform EXEs and PyPI wheel artifacts. Install: pip install fmcsa-audit or download platform binary from GitHub Releases. No server infrastructure required.
target_output.observability: audit_runs SQLite table captures per-run metadata: run_date, period, total_drivers, findings_count, critical_count, tool_version. --verbose flag outputs ComplianceRule.evaluate() trace to stdout: rule name, driver_id, hours evaluated, pass/fail with CFR citation. fmcsa-audit export --format csv exports full audit history for fleet safety recordkeeping. No remote telemetry — some fleet environments restrict outbound network access entirely.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No hosted infrastructure cost. Distribution via PyPI (free) and GitHub Releases (free). Local SQLite handles hundreds of thousands of HOS log entries per driver per year without performance issues at single-workstation query rates. Rules engine processes a 30-day HOS export for 50 drivers in under 10 seconds on a modern laptop. PyInstaller bundle approximately 80-120MB including Python runtime and SQLCipher.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Click CLI skeleton, fmcsa-audit setup with PBKDF2 keychain initialization, hos ingest and hos check commands with 4 HOS rule classes for 49 CFR 395, SQLCipher database with key management, report generate command, GitHub Actions CI matrix with PyInstaller builds and bundle validation. Phase 2 (4 weeks): DQ file ingest and 49 CFR 391 rule classes (medical cert expiry, MVR recency, drug test currency), vehicle inspection annual check per 49 CFR 396.17, remediate list command with CSV export. Phase 3 (3 weeks): Optional ELD API integration for direct data pull, PDF report export, CI/CD integration mode with exit codes for compliance monitoring scripts.
target_output.development_guidelines: Every ComplianceRule subclass requires unit tests with passing record, boundary violation, and clear violation cases using synthetic FMCSA log data. CFR citation must appear in both rule class docstring and audit_findings record. Never log driver_id with CDL number together in any output mode. keyring.get_password must handle None return with clear fmcsa-audit setup reminder, not Python exception traceback. SQLCipher PRAGMA key issued immediately after connection open before any other SQL. sqlcipher3-binary wheel version pinned in requirements to prevent silent SQLCipher version changes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: FMCSA regulation updates require rule class updates and patch releases; compliance officers on outdated versions may get incorrect findings. Mitigation: compliance_rules table includes version field; tool warns when rule set version is more than 6 months old. Risk: PBKDF2 key derivation adds 500ms to database open on first unlock per session. Acceptable for compliance tool; documented in user guide. Risk: sqlcipher3-binary wheel compatibility with future Python 3.x minor versions on Windows. Mitigation: CI matrix pins Python 3.12.x; pre-release testing on 3.13 when available. Tradeoff: local SQLite over shared network database accepted for single-user compliance officer use case with no server infrastructure requirement. Risk: user forgetting master passphrase locks out audit history. Mitigation: documented recovery procedure recommends storing passphrase in corporate password manager.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected sqlcipher3-binary over building SQLCipher from source for Windows to avoid Visual Studio build toolchain on compliance officer laptops. Confirmed PBKDF2 over Argon2 — Python hashlib PBKDF2 requires no additional package, reducing PyInstaller bundle complexity. Chose Click over argparse for composable command groups with automatic help generation. Confirmed offline-first design with regulation rules encoded in Python classes updated via tool patch releases.

metadata.notes: Round 3 resolving SQLCipher key management and Windows PyInstaller linkage for FMCSA compliance audit CLI.

project_goal.value: Automation tool that scales preventive maintenance scheduling across a large mixed fleet by ingesting mileage and engine-hour telemetry and auto-generating work orders in a CMMS when vehicles approach service intervals
project_goal.rationale: Manual maintenance scheduling at fleet scale leads to missed intervals and warranty compliance failures

target_users.value: Fleet maintenance managers and CMMS administrators overseeing preventive maintenance programs
target_users.rationale: Primary beneficiaries are maintenance ops staff who currently manage intervals in spreadsheets

project_class.rationale: Automated scheduling and CMMS integration with no end-user UI — triggered by telemetry thresholds

capabilities.rationale: Telemetry polling jobs, PostgreSQL data storage, CMMS API integration, and interval analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-vehicle fleet scaling, Celery distributed task queues, CMMS API rate limit management, and dynamic worker scaling add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: CMMS duplicate work order creation and missed service intervals are operational risks; mitigated by idempotency keys and monitoring

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vehicle telemetry and maintenance records are internal operational data with no PII

external_exposure.rationale: All services run within private AWS VPC; CMMS API calls are outbound to vendor SaaS

access_model.value: IAM roles for ECS task execution; CMMS API credentials in AWS Secrets Manager; no human user authentication
access_model.rationale: Service-to-service automation with no interactive users

feature_scope.value: Hourly telemetry ingestion (mileage, engine hours per vehicle), maintenance interval threshold evaluation per vehicle, automated CMMS work order creation via API, priority scoring by interval urgency, analytics on scheduled vs overdue maintenance across fleet
feature_scope.rationale: Full automated PM scheduling lifecycle from telemetry to work order

mvp_scope.value: Telemetry ingestion for top 200 vehicles, oil change and tire rotation interval checks, CMMS work order creation for vehicles within 10% of due interval, basic overdue alert reporting
mvp_scope.rationale: 200-vehicle pilot validates CMMS integration and interval logic before full fleet rollout

security_baseline.value: IAM roles with least privilege, CMMS API credentials in Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation, TLS 1.3 on all outbound CMMS API calls, no telemetry data stored beyond 90-day rolling window
security_baseline.rationale: Internal tool with standard AWS security posture

frontend_stack.value: None — automation tool with no browser UI; operational visibility via CloudWatch dashboards
frontend_stack.rationale: Fully automated tool triggered by telemetry thresholds

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Celery 5.x for distributed task execution; FastAPI for internal health and admin endpoints
backend_stack.rationale: Celery handles distributed task queuing and dynamic worker scaling; FastAPI exposes health checks for ECS monitoring

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS for vehicle and interval state; Redis 7 on ElastiCache as Celery broker and result backend
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for persistent vehicle and maintenance state; Redis for Celery task queue and idempotency key cache

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1, separate task definitions for scheduler and worker services
hosting_target.rationale: Fargate eliminates EC2 instance management; separate task definitions allow independent scaling of scheduler and worker pools

privacy_retention_policy.value: Telemetry readings retained 90 days in PostgreSQL; work_orders and maintenance_intervals retained indefinitely for fleet maintenance history; archived to S3 after 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Fleet maintenance history required for warranty claim support and audit; telemetry retained only for interval evaluation window

future_scope.value: Predictive maintenance ML model using historical failure patterns, integration with OEM telematics APIs for direct data pull, parts inventory reservation at work order creation
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 after PM scheduling automation is stable across full fleet

constraints.value: CMMS API rate limit of 100 requests per minute; full fleet of 2000 vehicles must be evaluated hourly; work order creation must be idempotent to prevent duplicates on retry
constraints.rationale: CMMS vendor enforces rate limits; duplicate work orders cause maintenance dispatcher confusion

observability_baseline.value: CloudWatch metrics for Celery queue depth, work order creation success rate, and telemetry ingestion lag; CloudWatch alarms for missed hourly evaluation windows
observability_baseline.rationale: AWS-native monitoring for Fargate-hosted automation services

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure-as-code via Terraform; Celery worker scaling via ECS service autoscaling on queue depth
execution_preference.rationale: Terraform manages Fargate task definitions and RDS; ECS autoscaling handles fleet-wide maintenance campaign bursts

project.goal: Automated preventive maintenance scheduling by ingesting vehicle telemetry and triggering CMMS work orders at service intervals
project.target_users: Fleet maintenance managers, CMMS admins
project.timeline: MVP 200-vehicle pilot in 8 weeks, full 2000-vehicle fleet in 16 weeks

backend.framework: Celery 5.x for task queues, FastAPI for health endpoints
backend.integration: CMMS REST API via httpx with retry and rate limit handling

security.auth: IAM roles for ECS tasks
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for CMMS API credentials, 30-day rotation
security.transport: TLS 1.3 on all CMMS API calls

data.cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache as Celery broker

devops.environments: dev, staging (200-vehicle subset), production (2000 vehicles)

constraints.idempotency: Work order creation must not duplicate on retry

reasoner_reviews.product: The 200-vehicle pilot scoping is correct — validates CMMS API integration behavior and rate limit handling before scaling. Oil change and tire rotation are the highest-frequency maintenance events and the right MVP focus.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Celery with Redis on ElastiCache is well-suited for this workload pattern. The hourly telemetry evaluation for 2000 vehicles at 100 req/min CMMS rate limit requires careful queue depth management — the scheduler must spread work order creation tasks across the evaluation window rather than bursting all 2000 at once. ECS Fargate autoscaling on Celery queue depth handles seasonal maintenance campaign bursts cleanly.
reasoner_reviews.security: IAM role least privilege is adequate for this internal automation tool. CMMS credentials in Secrets Manager with rotation is correct. The main security risk is accidental duplicate work order creation — idempotency key validation in Redis before each CMMS API call is the critical control.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 2000 vehicles at 100 req/min CMMS limit means work order creation phase must span at least 20 minutes of the hourly window. Scheduler should distribute CMMS tasks with a rate limiter at 80 req/min to leave headroom for manual CMMS API usage.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan had a critical flaw: the technology stack section specified Node.js with Bull queue as the task execution layer, contradicting the contract's Python 3.12 with Celery backend_stack requirement. This is a contract violation that must be corrected in round 3.

backend.service_design: Two ECS services: pm-scheduler (FastAPI + Celery Beat, runs interval evaluation hourly) and pm-worker (Celery workers, executes telemetry ingestion and CMMS API calls). pm-scheduler evaluates all vehicles per scheduled_tasks, enqueues work_order_create tasks for eligible vehicles. pm-worker processes work_order_create tasks with Redis-backed idempotency key check before CMMS API call.
backend.business_modules: telemetry_ingestor.py (polls telematics API or reads CSV feed, writes telemetry_readings), interval_evaluator.py (compares current_mileage/engine_hours against maintenance_intervals thresholds, identifies vehicles within 10% of due), cmms_client.py (httpx-based CMMS API client with 80 req/min rate limiter using token bucket, idempotency key validation), work_order_router.py (priority scoring: overdue > within 10% > upcoming)
backend.failure_handling: Celery task retry with exponential backoff (3 retries, 60s/300s/900s) for CMMS API failures; CMMS 429 rate limit response triggers Celery rate_limit on worker; idempotency key stored in Redis with 24-hour TTL prevents duplicate work orders on retry; dead-letter queue for tasks exceeding retry limit with CloudWatch alarm.

security.auth_design: ECS task execution role with IAM permissions for RDS access via RDS IAM auth, ElastiCache access, and Secrets Manager read for CMMS credentials; no hardcoded credentials in task definitions
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager stores CMMS API key and RDS password; ECS task uses secrets injection via task definition secretsFrom; 30-day automatic rotation for CMMS credentials
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudWatch Logs for all ECS task output; work order creation events logged to work_order_audit PostgreSQL table with vehicle_id, interval_type, cmms_work_order_id, created_at, idempotency_key; duplicate prevention logged as prevented_duplicate events.

data.entities: vehicles (vehicle_id UUID PK, vin, make, model, year, fleet_group, active BOOL). maintenance_intervals (interval_id UUID PK, vehicle_id FK, service_type ENUM oil_change/tire_rotation/brake_inspection/transmission_service/annual_pm, mileage_interval INT, engine_hour_interval INT, last_service_mileage INT, last_service_engine_hours INT, last_service_date DATE). telemetry_readings (reading_id UUID PK, vehicle_id FK, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, odometer_miles INT, engine_hours DECIMAL, fuel_level_pct INT, source TEXT). work_orders (wo_id UUID PK, vehicle_id FK, interval_id FK, cmms_work_order_id TEXT, service_type, priority ENUM critical/high/medium, status ENUM pending/created/completed, idempotency_key TEXT UNIQUE, created_at). service_bays (bay_id UUID PK, location TEXT, bay_number INT, active BOOL). parts_inventory (part_id UUID PK, part_number TEXT, description TEXT, quantity_on_hand INT, reorder_threshold INT). scheduled_tasks (task_id UUID PK, vehicle_id FK, interval_id FK, next_evaluation TIMESTAMPTZ, last_run TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS (Multi-AZ) for all persistent data; Redis 7 ElastiCache for Celery broker, result backend, idempotency key cache (24-hour TTL), and rate limit token bucket state.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster with two services: pm-scheduler (1 task, Celery Beat + FastAPI health endpoint) and pm-worker (2-10 tasks, autoscaling on Celery queue depth via custom CloudWatch metric). RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in private subnet; ElastiCache Redis in private subnet. Terraform manages all infrastructure.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR, run pytest and mypy type checking; on merge to main, build Docker images for pm-scheduler and pm-worker, push to ECR, deploy to staging via Terraform apply in staging workspace with 200-vehicle subset, run integration tests against staging CMMS sandbox, manual approval, Terraform apply to production.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch metrics: Celery queue depth (celery_queue_length custom metric from worker heartbeat), CMMS work_order_create success rate, CMMS 429 rate limit hit rate, telemetry ingestion lag (minutes since last reading per vehicle). CloudWatch alarm: queue depth >500 for 10 minutes, CMMS success rate <95% over 30 minutes, any vehicle without telemetry reading for >2 hours.

ISS-001.detail: Round 2 plan technology_stack section described Node.js with Bull queue and a TypeScript scheduler service. The frozen contract specifies Python 3.12 with Celery as the backend_stack. This is a direct contract violation — all plan sections must use Python Celery, not Node.js Bull.

ISS-002.detail: Both prior rounds mentioned the 100 req/min CMMS rate limit but did not specify the rate limiting mechanism in the Celery worker or how the scheduler distributes work order creation tasks across the hourly window.

previous_plan.title: Fleet PM Automation Tool v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Automation tool using Node.js TypeScript scheduler with Bull queue on ECS Fargate to ingest vehicle telemetry and trigger CMMS work orders when maintenance intervals approach threshold.
