target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 resolves the critical ISS-001 contract violation by replacing all Node.js/Bull references with Python 3.12/Celery throughout every plan section. ISS-002 is addressed by specifying a token bucket rate limiter at 80 req/min in the Celery CMMS client and a task distribution strategy that spreads work order creation across the hourly evaluation window. The plan is intentionally flawed in the cost_and_scaling section which omits NAT Gateway egress costs for CMMS API calls — a common omission for internal tools that leads to budget surprises.

fix_report.action_taken: Completely rewrote technology_stack, system_components, deployment_and_operations, and all code-referencing sections to use Python 3.12, Celery 5.x, and Celery Beat — removing all Node.js, TypeScript, and Bull queue references that contradicted the frozen contract.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Plan now aligns with contracted Python 3.12 Celery backend; no contract violations in technology sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Added token bucket rate limiter at 80 req/min in cmms_client.py using Redis INCR/EXPIRE pattern; specified that pm-scheduler distributes work order creation tasks across the first 25 minutes of each hourly window with 10ms inter-task delay to stay within CMMS API limits.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CMMS API rate limit of 100 req/min never exceeded; 20 req/min headroom preserved for manual CMMS operations; no 429 errors under normal fleet evaluation load.

target_output.title: Fleet Preventive Maintenance Automation Tool
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 Celery-based automation tool running on AWS ECS Fargate that ingests hourly vehicle telemetry (mileage and engine hours) for a 2000-vehicle mixed fleet and auto-generates work orders in a CMMS when vehicles approach configured maintenance intervals. Celery Beat schedules hourly evaluation; Celery workers handle CMMS API calls with token bucket rate limiting at 80 req/min and Redis-backed idempotency to prevent duplicate work orders. PostgreSQL 16 on RDS stores vehicle and interval state; CloudWatch monitors queue depth and work order creation success rate.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven automation architecture with two ECS Fargate services. pm-scheduler runs Celery Beat for hourly interval evaluation and a FastAPI health endpoint: it queries PostgreSQL for all active vehicles, compares current telemetry against maintenance_intervals thresholds, and enqueues work_order_create Celery tasks for vehicles within 10% of service due. pm-worker runs Celery workers that process work_order_create tasks: each task checks a Redis idempotency key before calling the CMMS REST API via cmms_client.py with token bucket rate limiting at 80 req/min. Redis ElastiCache serves as both Celery broker and idempotency key cache. ECS service autoscaling scales pm-worker tasks based on Celery queue depth CloudWatch metric during fleet-wide maintenance campaigns.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, Celery 5.x (distributed task queue and Celery Beat scheduler), FastAPI (internal health and admin endpoints), httpx (async CMMS API client with retry and rate limiting), PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 on ElastiCache (Celery broker, result backend, idempotency key cache), AWS ECS Fargate, ECR, CloudWatch, Secrets Manager, Terraform for IaC, GitHub Actions CI/CD.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Telemetry Ingestion: hourly poll of telematics API or CSV feed, writes odometer_miles and engine_hours to telemetry_readings per vehicle. Interval Evaluation: compare current telemetry against maintenance_intervals thresholds; flag vehicles with remaining_mileage_pct < 10% or remaining_engine_hours_pct < 10% as due_soon; flag overdue vehicles for priority escalation. Work Order Creation: Celery task calls cmms_client create_work_order with vehicle_id, service_type, priority, and idempotency_key; validates idempotency key in Redis before API call; writes result to work_orders table. Priority Scoring: overdue intervals scored critical, within 10% scored high, upcoming (15-20%) scored medium. Fleet Analytics: daily CloudWatch metric showing scheduled_vs_overdue ratio per service_type across fleet groups.
target_output.system_components: pm-scheduler ECS service (1 task: Celery Beat for hourly evaluation cron, FastAPI /health and /admin/fleet-status endpoints), pm-worker ECS service (2-10 tasks: Celery workers processing work_order_create and telemetry_ingest task queues, autoscaling on celery_queue_length CloudWatch metric), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (vehicles, maintenance_intervals, telemetry_readings, work_orders tables), ElastiCache Redis 7 (Celery broker, result backend, idempotency key store), Secrets Manager (CMMS API key, RDS password), CloudWatch (custom metrics, alarms, dashboards).
target_output.workflows: Hourly Interval Evaluation Workflow: Celery Beat triggers evaluate_fleet task every hour; interval_evaluator.py queries PostgreSQL for all active vehicles with their latest telemetry_reading; for each vehicle, iterates maintenance_intervals and computes remaining_mileage = last_service_mileage + mileage_interval - current_odometer; vehicles with remaining_mileage < threshold * 0.10 are eligible for work order; scheduler enqueues work_order_create tasks with 10ms inter-task delay spread over 25 minutes to stay within CMMS rate limits. Work Order Creation Workflow: Celery worker picks up work_order_create task; checks Redis for idempotency key {vehicle_id}:{service_type}:{interval_month} with 24-hour TTL; if key exists, logs prevented_duplicate and returns; if not, calls cmms_client.create_work_order with CMMS API token from Secrets Manager; on 200 response, writes work_orders record and sets Redis idempotency key; on 429, Celery rate_limit('10/m') throttles worker and retries; on 5xx, exponential backoff retry up to 3 attempts.
target_output.data_model: vehicles (vehicle_id UUID PK, vin TEXT UNIQUE, make TEXT, model TEXT, year INT, fleet_group TEXT, active BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). maintenance_intervals (interval_id UUID PK, vehicle_id FK, service_type TEXT CHECK service_type IN ('oil_change','tire_rotation','brake_inspection','transmission_service','annual_pm'), mileage_interval INT, engine_hour_interval INT, last_service_mileage INT, last_service_engine_hours INT, last_service_date DATE). telemetry_readings (reading_id UUID PK, vehicle_id FK, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, odometer_miles INT, engine_hours DECIMAL, fuel_level_pct INT, source TEXT, INDEX on vehicle_id, recorded_at DESC). work_orders (wo_id UUID PK, vehicle_id FK, interval_id FK, cmms_work_order_id TEXT, service_type TEXT, priority TEXT, status TEXT, idempotency_key TEXT UNIQUE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). service_bays (bay_id UUID PK, location TEXT, bay_number INT, active BOOL). parts_inventory (part_id UUID PK, part_number TEXT, description TEXT, quantity_on_hand INT, reorder_threshold INT). scheduled_tasks (task_id UUID PK, vehicle_id FK, interval_id FK, next_evaluation TIMESTAMPTZ, last_run TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No external-facing REST API. Internal FastAPI endpoints on pm-scheduler: GET /health (ECS health check), GET /admin/fleet-status (summary of active vehicles, pending evaluations, queue depth). Celery task signatures: evaluate_fleet() triggered by Beat schedule every hour. telemetry_ingest(vehicle_id, source_url) for per-vehicle telemetry poll. work_order_create(vehicle_id, interval_id, service_type, priority, idempotency_key) for CMMS API call. CMMS API outbound: POST /work-orders with JSON body vehicle_identifier, service_code, priority, scheduled_date, notes.
target_output.security_and_compliance: ECS task execution IAM role with permissions: secretsmanager:GetSecretValue for CMMS and RDS secrets, rds-db:connect for IAM authentication, elasticache:Connect. No hardcoded credentials in Docker images or task definitions — all via Secrets Manager injection. TLS 1.3 enforced on all httpx calls to CMMS API via verify=True default. CMMS API token rotated every 30 days via Secrets Manager rotation Lambda. Work order idempotency: Redis key {vehicle_id}:{service_type}:{yyyymm} with 24-hour TTL prevents duplicate work orders from Celery task retries. CloudTrail logs all Secrets Manager GetSecretValue calls for audit trail.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform manages: ECS cluster, pm-scheduler and pm-worker task definitions, RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ, ElastiCache Redis, ECR repositories, CloudWatch log groups, IAM roles. GitHub Actions CI: pytest with mock CMMS API, mypy type checking, Docker build for pm-scheduler and pm-worker images, push to ECR. Staging deploy: Terraform apply in staging workspace with 200-vehicle subset and CMMS sandbox endpoint; integration tests validate end-to-end work order creation with idempotency verification. Production deploy: manual approval, Terraform apply, ECS rolling update. ECS autoscaling policy: pm-worker scales on celery_queue_length metric (scale out at >100 tasks, scale in at <20), min 2 tasks, max 10 tasks.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch custom metrics published by pm-worker: celery_queue_length (total pending tasks), cmms_work_order_success_rate (rolling 5-minute), cmms_rate_limit_hit_count (429 responses per 5 minutes), duplicate_prevention_count (idempotency key hits per hour), telemetry_ingestion_lag_minutes (minutes since last reading per vehicle, aggregated p95). CloudWatch Alarms: queue_length >500 for 10 minutes (PagerDuty), CMMS success rate <95% for 30 minutes (PagerDuty), any vehicle telemetry_ingestion_lag >120 minutes (email), pm-scheduler ECS task stopped (PagerDuty immediate).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate pm-scheduler (0.5 vCPU, 1GB, 1 task): ~$15/month. pm-worker autoscaling 2-10 tasks (0.5 vCPU, 1GB each): ~$30-150/month. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ (db.t3.medium): ~$80/month. ElastiCache Redis (cache.t3.micro): ~$25/month. ECR storage: ~$5/month. Total estimated: $155-275/month. Note: CloudWatch custom metric costs and ECS task startup time during autoscaling events are the main performance constraints during fleet-wide campaign bursts.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Terraform infrastructure, pm-scheduler and pm-worker ECS services, telemetry ingestion for 200 pilot vehicles, oil change and tire rotation interval evaluation, CMMS work order creation with idempotency, CloudWatch alarms, GitHub Actions CI/CD. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Full 2000-vehicle fleet rollout, brake_inspection, transmission_service, and annual_pm interval types, ECS autoscaling policy for campaign bursts, parts_inventory integration for work order parts reservation, daily fleet analytics metrics. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Telematics API direct integration replacing CSV feed, service_bays scheduling integration with CMMS, predictive interval adjustment based on fleet group historical service rates.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Celery task functions must be idempotent — check Redis idempotency key at task start before any external API call. cmms_client.py token bucket rate limiter must be initialized per-worker with Redis backend, not per-task, to share rate limit state across concurrent workers on the same Fargate task. Pydantic models for all CMMS API request and response payloads — never pass raw dicts to httpx. mypy strict mode enforced in CI. Integration tests against CMMS sandbox endpoint required before staging deploy.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis ElastiCache failure causes Celery broker unavailability and halts all work order creation. Mitigation: ElastiCache Multi-AZ with automatic failover; pm-scheduler continues evaluating but enqueues to dead-letter SQS queue during Redis outage. Risk: CMMS API vendor changes authentication scheme, breaking 30-day rotation Lambda. Mitigation: CMMS client abstracts auth header injection; rotation Lambda integration-tested in staging against sandbox. Risk: Fleet-wide maintenance campaign (e.g., 1500 vehicles due for annual PM simultaneously) overwhelms Celery queue and ECS autoscaling lag. Mitigation: pm-scheduler distributes tasks over 25-minute window with inter-task delay; autoscaling pre-warms workers based on scheduled campaign dates. Tradeoff: Celery Beat single-task scheduler is a single point of failure for hourly evaluation — ECS task restart latency is 30-60 seconds which is acceptable given hourly cadence.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Celery over AWS Step Functions for task orchestration — Celery provides richer retry semantics and rate limiting control needed for CMMS API management. Confirmed PostgreSQL over DynamoDB for vehicle and interval state — relational joins between maintenance_intervals and telemetry_readings make SQL significantly simpler than DynamoDB access patterns. Chose Redis idempotency keys over PostgreSQL unique constraint as the duplicate prevention primary mechanism — Redis check is sub-millisecond vs PostgreSQL constraint violation handling latency.

metadata.notes: Round 3 resolving critical Node.js/Celery contract violation (ISS-001) and CMMS rate limit spec (ISS-002); cost_and_scaling intentionally omits NAT Gateway egress charges.

project_goal.value: Performance-optimized static marketing site for a commercial vehicle leasing company showcasing fleet leasing packages, vehicle inventory, and financing calculators with fast global load times
project_goal.rationale: Current site is a legacy CMS with 8-12 second load times on mobile; losing leads to competitors with faster sites

target_users.value: Fleet managers, owner-operators, and procurement teams researching commercial vehicle leasing options
target_users.rationale: B2B decision makers who evaluate multiple leasing vendors before requesting a quote

project_class.rationale: Fully static with no server-side runtime; dynamic content handled client-side or at build time

capabilities.rationale: Static site with client-side analytics; no backend, database, or auth required

complexity_level.rationale: Static pages with client-side calculator widget; no server-side logic

risk_level.rationale: No user data, no auth, no payments — primary risk is content accuracy in financing disclosures

data_sensitivity.rationale: No user data collected or stored; GA4 data governed by Google's DPA

external_exposure.rationale: Public marketing site accessible to anyone globally

access_model.value: No authentication — fully public read access; contact form submissions sent directly to CRM via client-side JS
access_model.rationale: Public marketing content requires no login

feature_scope.value: Vehicle listings by category (heavy-duty truck, vocational, trailer), leasing package comparison pages, financing calculator widget, testimonials, branch location pages, contact form with CRM integration
feature_scope.rationale: Full marketing funnel from vehicle exploration to quote request

mvp_scope.value: Vehicle category landing pages, three leasing package tier pages, financing calculator widget, contact form, Google Analytics 4 integration
mvp_scope.rationale: Core conversion funnel required for launch; branch pages and testimonials in Phase 2

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via CloudFront; CSP headers blocking inline scripts; no user input stored server-side; financing calculator runs fully client-side with no data transmission
security_baseline.rationale: Standard static site security posture; CSP required to limit third-party script risk

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4.x with TypeScript; Tailwind CSS for styling; no client-side framework for static pages; React island for financing calculator widget
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro zero-JS by default maximizes static performance; React island scoped to calculator widget only

backend_stack.value: None — fully static; no server-side runtime
backend_stack.rationale: Static website hosted on S3 + CloudFront; no backend compute

data_platform.value: None — no database; vehicle inventory and leasing packages defined as Markdown/MDX content files in repository
data_platform.rationale: Static content management via Git; no CMS or database required for MVP

hosting_target.value: AWS S3 for static file hosting, CloudFront CDN with global edge locations, Route 53 for DNS, ACM for TLS
hosting_target.rationale: CloudFront provides sub-100ms global delivery with S3 origin; ACM provides free TLS certificate management

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data collected on site; GA4 collects anonymized page view events per Google DPA; contact form data forwarded to CRM and not stored on site infrastructure
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No server-side data storage eliminates privacy compliance complexity

future_scope.value: Vehicle inventory CMS integration (Contentful or Sanity) for marketing team self-service updates, dealer locator map, testimonials section, multi-language support for Canadian French market
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 after initial launch establishes baseline conversion metrics

reasoner_reviews.product: Astro is the correct choice for maximum performance on a static marketing site — zero JS by default means vehicle listing pages ship no JavaScript overhead. The React island scoped to the financing calculator is exactly the right pattern. Contact form via client-side CRM SDK avoids any server-side infrastructure.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: MDX content files for vehicle_listings and leasing_packages are appropriate for the MVP. The Astro content collections API provides type-safe access to MDX frontmatter for vehicle specs and pricing. Image optimization via Astro built-in image component with WebP conversion and srcset generation is critical for the vehicle photography-heavy pages to hit the 2s FCP target.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP headers via CloudFront response headers policy should block unsafe-inline scripts — this requires verifying that GA4 and CRM contact form SDK can operate within the CSP. GA4 can use nonce-based CSP or strict-dynamic. The TILA disclosure requirement means any page displaying monthly payment estimates must include APR, term, and required disclosures adjacent to the calculator output.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 2s FCP on 4G simulated requires: images in WebP with proper sizing, critical CSS inlined, fonts preloaded, no render-blocking scripts, CloudFront edge caching. Lighthouse CI gate in GitHub Actions will catch regressions before they reach production.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan lacked any specification of the financing calculator computation logic — how monthly payment is calculated, what inputs are required, and how TILA disclosures are rendered adjacent to output. This section must be addressed in round 2.

frontend.app_structure: Astro project with content collections: src/content/vehicles (MDX per vehicle category: heavy_duty_truck, vocational, trailer), src/content/packages (MDX per leasing tier: basic, standard, premium), src/content/testimonials. Page routes: src/pages/vehicles/[category].astro, src/pages/packages/[tier].astro, src/pages/calculator.astro, src/pages/contact.astro.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Vehicle Category Pages: vehicle listing cards with make/model/GVWR/payload, Astro image component for WebP-optimized photos. Leasing Package Pages: tier comparison table, included services list, monthly estimate range with TILA disclosure. Calculator Page: React island renders FinancingCalculator with inputs vehicle_value, down_payment, term_months (36/48/60), mileage_allowance; computes monthly payment client-side; displays APR disclosure per TILA. Contact Page: HTML form with Fetch API submission to CRM endpoint.
frontend.performance_notes: Astro build inlines critical CSS, defers non-critical stylesheets, generates WebP srcset for all vehicle images. Preload font declarations for fleet brand typeface. No JavaScript on vehicle listing and package pages — pure HTML/CSS. Calculator page ships React island (approx 45KB gzipped) only on /calculator route via Astro client:load directive.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: CloudFront response headers policy: Content-Security-Policy with script-src 'strict-dynamic' nonce-based for GA4 and CRM SDK; X-Frame-Options DENY; Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin; Permissions-Policy disabling camera/microphone/geolocation. No user input stored — contact form POSTs directly to CRM API endpoint.

security.auth_design: No authentication — fully public site
security.abuse_prevention: CloudFront WAF rate limiting on contact form POST endpoint to prevent spam submission; CAPTCHA via hCaptcha on contact form rendered client-side
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudFront access logs to S3 bucket for CDN performance analysis; no application-level audit logging required for static site

data.entities: vehicle_listings (MDX frontmatter: vehicle_id, category ENUM heavy_duty_truck/vocational/trailer, make, model, gvwr_lbs, payload_capacity_lbs, engine_options TEXT[], photo_hero, photo_gallery TEXT[], starting_lease_monthly, available BOOL). leasing_packages (MDX frontmatter: package_id, tier ENUM basic/standard/premium, monthly_rate_range, mileage_allowance_annual, included_services TEXT[], maintenance_covered BOOL, early_termination_fee). financing_options (MDX frontmatter: apr_range, available_terms INT[], min_down_payment_pct, max_vehicle_value, disclosure_text). testimonials (MDX frontmatter: testimonial_id, company_name, fleet_type, quote, author_title). branch_locations (MDX frontmatter: branch_id, city, state, address, phone, service_bay_count, accepts_walk_in BOOL).
data.retention_and_deletion: No user data stored on site infrastructure; MDX content files version-controlled in Git; GA4 data retention 14 months per GA4 default settings

devops.deployment_topology: S3 bucket (static asset storage, public read, versioning enabled), CloudFront distribution (HTTPS-only, Origin Access Control for S3, response headers policy, global edge caching), Route 53 alias record to CloudFront, ACM certificate in us-east-1 for CloudFront.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR, run astro check (TypeScript), astro build, Lighthouse CI against built output with FCP <2000ms and Performance score >90 gate, automated PR comment with Lighthouse report. On merge to main, sync built output to S3 via aws s3 sync --delete, create CloudFront invalidation for /* to flush stale assets.
devops.observability_stack: GA4 events: page_view (all pages), calculator_interaction (input changes and result renders), contact_form_submit (CTA conversion). CloudFront access logs aggregated in S3 analyzed via Athena for cache hit rate, error rate by page, and geographic traffic distribution.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 resolves ISS-001 by specifying the financing calculator monthly payment formula, required inputs, and TILA disclosure rendering. The standard amortization formula M = P*(r(1+r)^n)/((1+r)^n-1) is used with APR converted to monthly rate. TILA disclosures render directly beneath the monthly estimate output with APR, term, total cost, and the required statutory disclosure text pulled from financing_options MDX frontmatter.

fix_report.action_taken: Added financing calculator computation specification: monthly payment formula M = P*(r(1+r)^n)/((1+r)^n-1) where P = vehicle_value minus down_payment, r = APR/12, n = term_months. Added TILA disclosure placement: DisclosureBlock component renders immediately below monthly estimate output with APR, total financed, total cost, and statutory text from financing_options MDX disclosure_text field. Added financing_options MDX schema definition.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Calculator widget is TILA-compliant; APR and required disclosures appear adjacent to every displayed payment estimate; financing_options schema captures disclosure text for content team management.

target_output.title: Commercial Vehicle Leasing Marketing Site
target_output.executive_summary: A performance-optimized Astro 4.x static site for a commercial vehicle leasing company hosted on AWS CloudFront and S3. Vehicle listing pages, leasing package comparisons, and a client-side financing calculator with TILA-compliant disclosures are all generated at build time from MDX content files. Zero JavaScript on content pages; React island scoped to the financing calculator. Lighthouse CI gate enforces sub-2s FCP before every deployment. GA4 tracks calculator interactions and contact form conversions.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Astro 4.x site with no server-side runtime. MDX content collections define vehicle_listings, leasing_packages, financing_options, testimonials, and branch_locations as type-safe content schemas. Astro build generates HTML for each vehicle category, leasing tier, and branch location page with zero client-side JavaScript. The /calculator page uses Astro client:load directive to hydrate a React 18 island for the FinancingCalculator widget — no other pages ship JavaScript. Built output is deployed to S3 and served globally via CloudFront with HTTPS-only enforcement, security response headers, and full-page cache TTL of 24 hours. GitHub Actions CI runs astro check, astro build, and Lighthouse CI with FCP <2000ms gate before any merge to main.
target_output.technology_stack: Astro 4.x, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS v3, React 18 (calculator island only). Hosting: AWS S3 (static assets), CloudFront (CDN, HTTPS, response headers policy), Route 53, ACM. Build tool: Astro built-in Vite pipeline with image optimization. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI. Analytics: Google Analytics 4 via astro-google-analytics integration. Contact form: client-side POST to CRM API endpoint (HubSpot or Salesforce).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Vehicle Category Pages: heavy_duty_truck, vocational, and trailer landing pages with Astro content collection queries; vehicle listing cards showing make/model/GVWR/payload/starting lease monthly estimate; Astro Image component generates WebP srcset for vehicle photography. Leasing Package Pages: basic/standard/premium tier comparison with included services list and monthly rate range; TILA disclosure block showing APR range and required statutory text. Financing Calculator Page: React island accepts vehicle_value (slider 30K-300K), down_payment (dollar input), term_months (36/48/60 selector), mileage_allowance_annual (15K/20K/25K toggle); computes monthly payment using amortization formula M = P*(r(1+r)^n)/((1+r)^n-1) where P = vehicle_value minus down_payment, r = APR/12 from financing_options content, n = term_months; renders DisclosureBlock immediately below result showing APR, total financed, total interest, total cost, and statutory disclosure text from financing_options MDX disclosure_text field. Contact Page: HTML form with hCaptcha and Fetch API POST to CRM endpoint.
target_output.system_components: Astro build pipeline (content collections, image optimization, critical CSS inlining, TypeScript checking). React 18 FinancingCalculator island (amortization computation, DisclosureBlock component). MDX content files (vehicle_listings, leasing_packages, financing_options, testimonials, branch_locations). S3 bucket (static asset storage, versioned). CloudFront distribution (HTTPS-only, Origin Access Control, response headers policy with CSP and security headers, 24-hour TTL on HTML, 1-year TTL on hashed assets). Route 53 alias to CloudFront. ACM TLS certificate. GitHub Actions (CI with Lighthouse gate, S3 sync, CloudFront invalidation).
target_output.workflows: Content Update Workflow: marketing team edits MDX file in /content/vehicles/ via PR; GitHub Actions runs astro check to validate frontmatter schema, astro build to generate HTML, Lighthouse CI to validate FCP and Performance score; on PR approval and merge, GitHub Actions syncs built output to S3 and creates CloudFront /* invalidation; updated vehicle listing page live within 60 seconds. Financing Calculator Interaction Workflow: visitor on /calculator selects vehicle_value=150000, down_payment=30000, term_months=60, mileage_allowance_annual=20000; React FinancingCalculator computes P=120000, r=0.0699/12=0.005825, monthly_payment = 120000*(0.005825*(1.005825)^60)/((1.005825)^60-1) = $2,376; DisclosureBlock renders: 'Monthly payment $2,376. APR 6.99%. Total financed $120,000. Total cost $142,560. [statutory TILA text from financing_options MDX disclosure_text field].' GA4 event calculator_interaction fires on each result render with term and vehicle_value parameters.
target_output.data_model: vehicle_listings MDX frontmatter schema (vehicle_id string, category enum heavy_duty_truck|vocational|trailer, make string, model string, gvwr_lbs number, payload_capacity_lbs number, engine_options string[], photo_hero string, photo_gallery string[], starting_lease_monthly number, available boolean). leasing_packages MDX frontmatter schema (package_id string, tier enum basic|standard|premium, monthly_rate_range string, mileage_allowance_annual number, included_services string[], maintenance_covered boolean, early_termination_fee string). financing_options MDX frontmatter schema (option_id string, apr_range string, apr_representative number, available_terms number[], min_down_payment_pct number, max_vehicle_value number, disclosure_text string — contains full TILA-required statutory disclosure for use in DisclosureBlock). testimonials (testimonial_id string, company_name string, fleet_type string, quote string, author_title string). branch_locations (branch_id string, city string, state string, address string, phone string, service_bay_count number).
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Client-side interfaces: FinancingCalculator React component props: vehicleValueMin=30000, vehicleValueMax=300000, defaultTerm=60, availableTerms=[36,48,60], availableMileage=[15000,20000,25000], financingOptions (loaded at build time from MDX). DisclosureBlock props: apr number, totalFinanced number, totalCost number, disclosureText string (from financing_options MDX). Contact form Fetch API: POST to CRM endpoint URL (HubSpot or Salesforce Forms API) with fields: name, email, phone, fleet_size, inquiry_type; CRM endpoint configured via Astro environment variable at build time.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS-only enforced via CloudFront viewer protocol policy. CloudFront response headers policy: Content-Security-Policy (script-src 'strict-dynamic' with nonce for GA4 and CRM SDK; default-src 'self'; img-src 'self' data: https:); X-Frame-Options DENY; Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin; Strict-Transport-Security max-age=31536000 includeSubDomains. TILA compliance: every page displaying a monthly payment estimate (leasing_packages pages and /calculator) must render DisclosureBlock with APR, total financed, total cost, and disclosure_text from financing_options MDX — enforced by Astro layout components that require DisclosureBlock when showPaymentEstimate prop is true. hCaptcha on contact form prevents spam CRM submissions. No user data stored on site infrastructure.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions on PR: astro check (TypeScript and MDX schema validation), astro build, Lighthouse CI against local server with thresholds FCP <2000ms, LCP <3000ms, Performance >90, Accessibility >95; automated PR comment with Lighthouse report scores. On merge to main: astro build, aws s3 sync dist/ to s3://leasing-site-prod --delete (removes stale files), aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id XXXX --paths /* (flushes edge caches globally). CloudFront configuration: Origin Access Control prevents direct S3 URL access; 24-hour TTL on HTML pages, 1-year cache with content-hash filenames for CSS/JS/image assets. Rollback: S3 versioning enabled; rollback by restoring previous object versions and invalidating CloudFront.
target_output.observability: GA4 events: page_view (page title and URL), vehicle_listing_view (vehicle_id, category parameters), package_page_view (tier parameter), calculator_interaction (vehicle_value, term_months, monthly_payment_result parameters), contact_form_submit (inquiry_type parameter). CloudFront access logs stored in S3 and queryable via Athena: cache hit rate by page path, 4xx error rate by path, geographic request distribution. Lighthouse CI report stored as GitHub Actions artifact for every PR build — performance regression history maintained in CI artifacts.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: S3 storage for static assets (~500MB built output): approximately $0.01/month. CloudFront data transfer at 100K monthly visitors with average 2MB page weight: approximately $8-15/month. Route 53 hosted zone: $0.50/month. ACM: free. GA4: free. Total: under $20/month. CloudFront scales automatically to any global traffic volume with no configuration changes required. Lighthouse CI gate prevents performance regressions that would increase page weight and data transfer costs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Astro project setup with content collections, vehicle category pages for heavy_duty_truck, vocational, and trailer, three leasing package tier pages with DisclosureBlock, FinancingCalculator React island with TILA-compliant disclosure rendering, contact form with hCaptcha, GA4 integration, CloudFront deployment, GitHub Actions CI with Lighthouse gate. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Testimonials section with MDX content, branch location pages with map embed, Contentful CMS integration to replace MDX for vehicle inventory management, multi-language support stub for Canadian French market.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro content collection schemas are the source of truth for all MDX frontmatter — breaking schema changes require updating collection schema and all affected MDX files in the same PR. DisclosureBlock component must be used for every page or component that displays a monthly payment estimate — a TypeScript prop type enforcement pattern (showPaymentEstimate: true requires disclosureOptions prop) prevents TILA compliance gaps. Vehicle images committed to Git must be WebP format only — Astro build will not process raw camera JPEGs; content guidelines require images pre-processed to WebP. Lighthouse CI thresholds are not negotiable — FCP >2000ms fails the PR gate regardless of reason.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: financing_options MDX disclosure_text content becomes outdated as regulatory requirements change — TILA disclosures require legal review before updates. Mitigation: financing_options MDX changes require compliance team reviewer in GitHub CODEOWNERS file. Risk: contact form CRM SDK may conflict with CSP headers. Mitigation: CRM SDK nonce compatibility verified against Salesforce and HubSpot documentation before integration; fallback is to use a CRM API endpoint URL configured at build time (no third-party SDK). Risk: CloudFront 24-hour TTL means content updates take up to 24 hours without explicit invalidation. Mitigation: deployment pipeline always runs CloudFront /* invalidation on merge to main. Tradeoff: MDX files in Git for content management requires marketing team to learn basic Git PR workflow — justified by eliminating CMS infrastructure cost and complexity at MVP scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Astro over Next.js for zero-JS default on content pages — Next.js requires explicit server component patterns to achieve similar results. Confirmed MDX over Contentful for MVP to avoid external CMS dependency and subscription cost; Contentful integration scoped to Phase 2. Chose hCaptcha over reCAPTCHA for contact form to reduce Google data dependency. Confirmed TILA disclosure text stored in financing_options MDX frontmatter for content team management without code changes.

metadata.notes: Round 2 resolving financing calculator TILA compliance gap for commercial vehicle leasing static marketing site.

project.goal: Auto-generate and publish ML model library documentation from structured build artifacts
project.target_users: Automotive ML engineers and fleet data scientists
project.timeline: Continuous publishing on library release tags

frontend.theme: Custom Hugo theme with model card templates
frontend.diagrams: Mermaid SVG pre-rendering via mmdc CLI at build time

security.headers: CSP disabling inline scripts, X-Frame-Options DENY

data.source: JSON benchmark and model card outputs from model library CI

devops.ci: GitHub Actions triggered on library release tags
devops.environments: production only

confirmed_decisions.diagram_tool: Mermaid SVG at build time via mmdc

reasoner_reviews.product: The documentation site is the primary discovery surface for the automotive ML model library. Model card pages and benchmark tables are highest-value for evaluating fleet anomaly detection models; dataset schema viewer helps engineers assess compatibility before integration.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Hugo is the right choice: sub-100ms build times even with hundreds of model card pages generated from JSON templates. Pre-rendering Mermaid as SVGs at build time via mmdc eliminates the 3-second client-side Mermaid JS initialization penalty observed in round 1.
reasoner_reviews.security: Static site on Firebase Hosting has minimal attack surface. CSP headers must explicitly block eval() and inline scripts. No secrets should exist in the Hugo build context; Firebase deploy token must be stored only as a GitHub Actions encrypted secret.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Sub-2s load constraint is achievable with Firebase CDN and pre-compressed HTML/SVG assets. Round 1 lacked explicit caching header configuration for versioned benchmark JSON files.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan was thin on CI pipeline specifics for JSON artifact ingestion and did not document the mmdc CLI invocation order. Both gaps made the deployment_and_operations section insufficient for actual implementation.

frontend.app_structure: Hugo site with content/models/ for auto-generated model card .md files, data/benchmarks/ for JSON data files consumed by Hugo templates, and static/diagrams/ for pre-rendered SVGs from mmdc.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Model library index listing all released models filterable by vehicle domain (powertrain, chassis, ADAS). Per-model card page: architecture summary, training dataset schema table, benchmark metrics, pipeline diagram SVG. Benchmark comparison page with multi-model sortable table across F1/precision/recall/AUC-ROC. Release notes changelog.
frontend.performance_notes: Hugo build with --minify flag. Firebase Hosting serves gzip and brotli compressed assets. Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000 for versioned benchmark JSON files. Sub-resource integrity hashes on third-party CSS.
frontend.frontend_risks: Risk: Hugo template rendering errors silently produce empty pages if JSON schema changes upstream. Mitigation: CI validation step checks generated HTML for expected model card sections before deploying.

security.auth_design: No authentication required; public read-only site.
security.secrets_management: No secrets in Hugo build context. Firebase deploy token stored as GitHub Actions encrypted secret, never embedded in site content.
security.abuse_prevention: Firebase Hosting rate limiting and DDoS protection via GCP CDN layer. No forms or user input endpoints.
security.security_risks: Risk: Accidental inclusion of internal benchmark data not intended for public release. Mitigation: CI validation step checks model card JSON against a public-safe schema before hugo build runs.

data.entities: model_cards (model_id, name, vehicle_domain, version, training_dataset_schema_id, architecture_summary, benchmark_results_ref, published_at). benchmark_results (model_id, dataset_name, f1_score, precision, recall, auc_roc, eval_date). dataset_schemas (schema_id, dataset_name, signal_columns[], target_column, vehicle_domain, sample_rate_hz). pipeline_diagrams (diagram_id, model_id, mermaid_source_path, svg_output_path). release_notes (version, date, changes[]).
data.storage_design: All data stored as JSON files in the Git repository under data/ and content/ directories. Hugo consumes them as data files during build. No database.
data.retention_and_deletion: Content versioned via Git tags; old model card versions accessible via release tag checkouts but not published to the live site.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions workflow triggered on library release tag push. Steps: checkout repo, run ingest_artifacts.py to copy JSON to Hugo data/, run mmdc for SVG pre-rendering, run hugo --minify, run firebase deploy.
devops.ci_cd_design: Separate workflow for PR previews using Firebase Hosting preview channels. Release tag workflow deploys to production channel. Build validation step checks for empty model card HTML before deploy gate.
devops.observability_stack: Firebase Hosting analytics for CDN hit rates. GA4 events for model card views, benchmark table interactions, diagram gallery views. Build duration tracked in GitHub Actions.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: Model library JSON schema changes break Hugo templates silently. Mitigation: JSON schema validation step in CI before hugo build runs.

previous_plan.title: Automotive ML Library Documentation Site - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Hugo-based static documentation site auto-published to Firebase Hosting on each model library release, showcasing fleet anomaly detection model cards, benchmark tables, and pipeline architecture diagrams.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions deploys to Firebase Hosting on release tags. Hugo builds the site from model card markdown and benchmark JSON files. No server-side runtime.

target_output.title: Automotive ML Model Library Documentation Site
target_output.executive_summary: A Hugo-generated static documentation site for an automotive fleet anomaly detection ML model library, auto-published to Firebase Hosting on each library release tag. Model card pages, benchmark comparison tables, dataset schema viewers, and pre-rendered pipeline diagrams are generated from structured JSON build artifacts. No server-side runtime exists; all content is produced at CI build time and delivered via GCP CDN.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure static site architecture: Hugo reads JSON data files (model_cards, benchmark_results, dataset_schemas) from the repository's data/ directory and generates HTML via Go templates. Mermaid pipeline diagrams are pre-rendered to SVGs using mmdc CLI before Hugo runs, ensuring no client-side JavaScript rendering. GitHub Actions orchestrates artifact ingestion, SVG pre-rendering, Hugo build, and Firebase deploy in a single release pipeline. Firebase Hosting delivers all assets via GCP CDN with brotli compression and long-lived cache headers on versioned benchmark JSON files.
target_output.technology_stack: Static generator: Hugo 0.124 with custom Go template theme. Diagram tooling: Mermaid CLI (mmdc) for SVG pre-rendering from .mmd source files. Hosting: Firebase Hosting on GCP with global CDN. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with firebase-tools CLI. Analytics: Google Analytics 4 via Hugo partial template. Build validation: Python 3.12 script for JSON schema validation and HTML completeness checks.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Model card index page listing all released anomaly detection models filterable by vehicle domain (powertrain, chassis, ADAS). Per-model card page showing training architecture, dataset schema table, benchmark metrics (F1, precision, recall, AUC-ROC), and pipeline diagram SVG. Benchmark comparison table with sortable columns across all model versions. Dataset schema viewer rendering signal column definitions and target column metadata from dataset_schemas.json. Release notes changelog auto-generated from release_notes.json per library tag.
target_output.system_components: Hugo 0.124 static generator consuming data/model_cards/, data/benchmarks/, data/dataset_schemas/ JSON files. mmdc CLI converting content/diagrams/*.mmd to static/diagrams/*.svg before each build. GitHub Actions release pipeline with artifact-ingest, mmdc, hugo --minify, and firebase-deploy stages. Firebase Hosting CDN serving pre-compressed HTML, SVG, and JSON globally. Google Analytics 4 embedded via Hugo base template partial.
target_output.workflows: Model Library Release Publish: Library CI exports benchmark_results.json and model_cards/*.json to a release artifacts path. Documentation GitHub Action triggers on library release tag, runs ingest_artifacts.py to copy JSON into Hugo data/ and content/models/, runs 'mmdc -i content/diagrams/*.mmd -o static/diagrams/ --outputFormat svg --backgroundColor transparent' to produce all pipeline SVGs, validates output JSON against public schema with validate_public_schema.py, runs 'hugo --minify --baseURL https://ml-docs.fleet-platform.com', then runs 'firebase deploy --only hosting'. PR Preview workflow: deploys to Firebase Hosting preview channel using 'firebase hosting:channel:deploy pr-{PR_NUMBER}' for content review before merge.
target_output.data_model: model_cards (model_id, name, vehicle_domain ENUM powertrain/chassis/adas, version, training_dataset_schema_id, architecture_summary, benchmark_results_ref, published_at). benchmark_results (model_id, dataset_name, eval_date, f1_score, precision, recall, auc_roc, eval_dataset_size). dataset_schemas (schema_id, dataset_name, vehicle_domain, signal_columns[] each with name/unit/sample_rate_hz, target_column, capture_conditions). pipeline_diagrams (diagram_id, model_id, mermaid_source_path, svg_output_path). release_notes (version, release_date, changes[]). All entities stored as JSON files in the Git repository; no database.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Hugo pre-built page pattern: GET /models/{model_id}/index.html returns static model card, GET /benchmarks/index.html returns sortable benchmark table, GET /data/benchmarks/{version}.json returns versioned benchmark data with Cache-Control: public max-age=31536000, GET /diagrams/{model_id}-pipeline.svg returns pre-rendered pipeline SVG. Firebase Hosting rewrites redirect /models/ to /models/index.html.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Firebase Hosting; HTTP requests auto-redirected. CSP header: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://www.googletagmanager.com; img-src 'self' data:; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'. X-Frame-Options: DENY. Firebase deploy token stored exclusively as GitHub Actions encrypted secret; never embedded in site output. CI validation step runs validate_public_schema.py to confirm no internal benchmark fields present before deployment gate.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Release pipeline (GitHub Actions, trigger: library release tag push). Step 1: checkout documentation repo and download JSON artifact bundle from library release. Step 2: run ingest_artifacts.py copying model_cards/*.json to content/models/, benchmark_results.json to data/benchmarks/$(VERSION).json, dataset_schemas.json to data/. Step 3: run 'mmdc -i content/diagrams/*.mmd -o static/diagrams/ --outputFormat svg --backgroundColor transparent' to pre-render all pipeline architecture diagrams. Step 4: run validate_public_schema.py confirming no internal fields in published JSON. Step 5: run 'hugo --minify --baseURL https://ml-docs.fleet-platform.com'. Step 6: run 'firebase deploy --only hosting --project fleet-ml-docs'. PR preview pipeline: 'firebase hosting:channel:deploy pr-$PR_NUMBER'. Rollback: 'firebase hosting:rollback' to previous release version.
target_output.observability: Google Analytics 4 tracks model card page views, benchmark table sort interactions, diagram gallery navigation, and download link clicks as GA4 events via Hugo partial. Firebase Hosting console reports CDN cache hit rate, bandwidth, and geographic request distribution. Build duration tracked in GitHub Actions; failure alerts via GitHub Actions email notification. No server-side APM required.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Firebase Hosting Spark plan covers 10GB storage and 360MB/day transfer free tier. Estimated monthly cost under $5 for documentation traffic. Hugo build completes under 30 seconds for up to 500 model card pages. Firebase CDN scales globally to traffic spikes during model library conference announcements without configuration changes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (2 weeks): Configure Hugo theme with model card and benchmark templates. Implement ingest_artifacts.py. Set up GitHub Actions release pipeline with firebase deploy. Publish initial model cards for 3 existing anomaly detection models. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Add mmdc SVG pre-rendering step for pipeline diagrams. Implement benchmark comparison table. Add dataset schema viewer page template. Phase 3 (future): Interactive benchmark explorer with client-side filtering. Versioned API documentation from OpenAPI specs.
target_output.development_guidelines: Hugo template changes require local 'hugo server -D' preview before PR. JSON schema for model_cards and benchmark_results validated against JSON Schema v7 in CI. mmdc version pinned in package.json to prevent diagram rendering drift. Firebase deploy restricted to release tag workflows only; direct branch pushes to production are blocked.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Upstream model library JSON schema changes without updating ingest script produce empty model card pages. Mitigation: validate_public_schema.py runs before hugo build with CI failure on schema mismatch. Risk: Firebase Hosting free tier bandwidth exceeded during traffic spikes. Mitigation: paid plan upgrade path documented; CDN caching minimizes origin bandwidth. Tradeoff: Pre-rendered SVGs over client-side Mermaid eliminates 3s JS initialization but requires CI regeneration on diagram source changes.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Mermaid client-side rendering rejected in favor of mmdc SVG pre-rendering for consistent cross-browser display. Firebase Hosting selected over GCS plus Cloud CDN for simpler preview channel workflow. JSON schema validation added to CI after round 1 audit flagged accidental internal data exposure risk.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for automotive ML docs static site; fixes CI artifact ingestion and Mermaid SVG tooling gaps from round 1.

profile.domain: Insurance Claim Automation - Vehicle Damage Assessment

project_goal.value: Automate vehicle damage photo intake from field inspectors, extract structured damage annotations via computer vision, and submit insurance claims to carrier portals via API.
project_goal.rationale: Replaces manual adjuster photo review and claim data entry, reducing claim cycle time from 5 days to under 4 hours.

target_users.value: Field inspectors submitting damage photos, claims processors reviewing automation output, insurance carrier portal APIs receiving structured claims.
target_users.rationale: Three distinct actor types with different integration surfaces.

project_class.rationale: Backend automation pipeline with no end-user UI; integrates with inspector mobile intake and carrier portals.

capabilities.rationale: Requires FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL data store, carrier portal integrations, nightly reconciliation batch jobs, and computer vision LLM for damage annotation.

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-carrier API integration, vision model inference pipeline, idempotent claim submission, and GDPR-compliant EU data handling.

risk_level.rationale: Handles confidential vehicle damage photos and insurance policy data; incorrect claim submissions carry financial and regulatory risk.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vehicle damage photos linked to policy records and claimant identity are confidential business data.

external_exposure.rationale: Outbound integrations to insurance carrier portals via mTLS-authenticated APIs; inspector intake via partner mobile SDK.

access_model.value: Azure AD service principal authentication for carrier integrations; API key authentication for inspector intake webhook; internal service-to-service via managed identity.
access_model.rationale: Each integration surface requires distinct auth mechanism per carrier security requirements.

feature_scope.value: Inspector photo intake webhook, computer vision damage annotation pipeline, per-carrier claim form generation, carrier portal API submission, adjuster review queue, batch reconciliation of open claims.
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set covering end-to-end claim automation lifecycle.

mvp_scope.value: Photo intake and CV annotation pipeline with claim submission to two priority carriers (State Farm, Travelers) and nightly reconciliation batch job.
mvp_scope.rationale: Two-carrier MVP enables production validation before expanding to full carrier network.

security_baseline.value: mTLS for all carrier portal API calls, AES-256 encryption for vehicle photos at rest in Azure Blob Storage, Azure AD service principal rotation every 90 days, all claim submissions idempotent with unique submission_id.
security_baseline.rationale: Insurance carrier security requirements and internal confidential data handling policy.

frontend_stack.value: None; automation tool with no end-user UI. Adjuster review accessible via existing claims management system integration.
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation_tool project class; no frontend specialist needed.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI on Azure Container Apps. Celery for async task queue backed by Azure Service Bus.
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for intake webhook performance; Celery for durable async vision inference and claim submission tasks.

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. Azure Blob Storage for vehicle photo storage.
data_platform.rationale: Relational DB for claim state machine; Blob Storage for binary photo assets.

hosting_target.value: Azure Container Apps in East US 2 region for primary, West US 3 for DR.
hosting_target.rationale: Container Apps provides serverless scaling for variable claim volume; Azure-native for service principal and managed identity integration.

privacy_retention_policy.value: EU claimant photos and PII deleted 30 days after claim closure per GDPR Article 17. US claim records retained 7 years per state insurance regulations.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR right to erasure and US insurance data retention requirements.

future_scope.value: Expand to full carrier network (20+ carriers), add repair shop estimate integration, real-time claim status push notifications to inspector mobile app.
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 carrier expansion and downstream repair integration roadmap.

constraints.value: Vision inference must complete within 90 seconds per photo set. Carrier API rate limits: State Farm 60 req/min, Travelers 30 req/min. All EU claimant data must stay in Azure West Europe region.
constraints.rationale: SLA requirement from claims ops team and GDPR data residency constraint.

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor with Application Insights for FastAPI traces and Celery task metrics. Alerting on claim submission failure rate exceeding 2% or reconciliation job failures.
observability_baseline.rationale: Azure-native monitoring stack aligned with infrastructure.

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with carrier integration stubs for development; production carrier credentials gated behind environment promotion.
execution_preference.rationale: Carrier API sandbox environments enable dev/staging isolation.

compliance_context.value: State insurance regulations for electronic claim submission in all 50 US states; GDPR for EU claimant personal data and photos; carrier-specific EDI/API submission standards per carrier integration agreement.
compliance_context.rationale: High risk_level and confidential data_sensitivity require explicit compliance framing.

llm_integration.value: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o Vision for damage area identification and classification from photo sets. Structured output mode returns damage_annotations JSON with panel_id, damage_type, severity, repair_action. Model pinned to specific deployment version for audit reproducibility.
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o Vision provides multimodal damage classification without custom model training overhead.

project.goal: Automate vehicle damage photo intake, CV annotation, and carrier claim submission
project.target_users: Field inspectors, claims processors, carrier portal APIs

backend.framework: FastAPI with Celery task queue
backend.api: Webhook intake endpoint plus internal REST APIs for adjuster review

security.auth: Azure AD service principal for carrier APIs, API key for inspector intake webhook, mTLS for all carrier portal calls
security.encryption: AES-256 for photos at rest in Azure Blob, TLS 1.3 in transit

data.storage: Azure Blob Storage for vehicle photos
data.queue: Azure Service Bus for Celery task broker

devops.environments: dev, staging, production with carrier sandbox/production credentials per environment

constraints.gdpr_residency: EU data in Azure West Europe

open_questions.carrier_pagination: Confirm whether Travelers portal uses cursor or offset pagination for claim status queries

reasoner_reviews.product: The automation tool addresses a real bottleneck: manual adjuster review of damage photos takes 3-5 days and is the primary driver of claim cycle time. The two-carrier MVP (State Farm and Travelers) covers roughly 35% of claim volume, making it a meaningful production validation target before expanding to the full carrier network.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI with Celery on Azure Container Apps is well-matched: the intake webhook needs low-latency acknowledgment while CV inference can run asynchronously. Pinning the GPT-4o Vision deployment version is critical for audit trails; damage assessments must be reproducible. The idempotency key on claim submissions guards against duplicate submissions during network retries to carrier portals.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS is mandatory for carrier portal API calls per carrier integration agreements. Azure AD service principals should be scoped to minimum required permissions per carrier. Vehicle photos containing vehicle identification numbers must be treated as confidential even after claim closure until GDPR retention window expires. Audit logging of every CV annotation and claim submission is required for regulatory defensibility.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 90-second vision inference SLA per photo set is achievable with GPT-4o Vision for sets up to 10 photos. Carrier rate limits (60 rpm State Farm, 30 rpm Travelers) require per-carrier throttling in the submission queue. GDPR data residency means EU claim processing must route to a separate Azure West Europe deployment, not East US 2.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The batch reconciliation job design needs explicit idempotency handling: if a carrier returns a transient error, the job must not create duplicate claim submissions. The nightly schedule may create a 24-hour gap in claim status visibility; consider a 4-hour reconciliation cadence for priority claims.

backend.service_design: Three FastAPI service modules: IngestService (photo upload webhook, damage_assessment record creation), AnnotationService (Celery task triggering GPT-4o Vision, structured output parsing, annotation persistence), ClaimSubmissionService (per-carrier form builder, idempotent portal API submission with exponential backoff). AdjusterReviewService exposes internal REST endpoints for claims management system integration.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/intakes (inspector photo upload, returns assessment_id for polling). GET /v1/assessments/{id} (annotation results and claim status). POST /v1/claims/{id}/submit (manual re-submit trigger for adjuster). GET /v1/claims?status=pending_review (adjuster queue). All routes protected by API key middleware; carrier submission routes additionally require mTLS client cert validation.
backend.background_jobs: Celery tasks: annotate_damage_photos (fetches photos from Blob, invokes GPT-4o Vision, parses structured output, updates damage_assessments). submit_claim_to_carrier (carrier-specific form generation, portal API call with idempotency key, status update). reconcile_open_claims (nightly/4-hourly, polls carrier APIs for settlement updates, patches claim_submissions records).
backend.failure_handling: Celery tasks use max_retries=3 with exponential backoff (2^n * 30s). Vision inference timeout set to 120s with fallback to manual_review status. Carrier API 429 responses trigger per-carrier rate-limit backoff queue. Dead-letter queue in Azure Service Bus captures tasks exceeding max retries for manual investigation.
backend.backend_risks: Risk: GPT-4o Vision structured output returns unexpected damage type not in carrier form enum. Mitigation: validation layer maps CV output to carrier-specific damage codes with fallback to 'OTHER' and adjuster flag. Risk: Carrier portal API schema changes break claim submission. Mitigation: carrier integration tests run against sandbox on each deploy.

security.auth_design: Azure AD service principal per carrier with certificate-based auth. Inspector intake webhook authenticated via HMAC-SHA256 API key header. Internal Celery task workers use Azure Managed Identity for Blob Storage and Service Bus access.
security.authorization_model: Claims processors can read damage_assessments and trigger manual resubmits. Inspector intake is write-only via webhook. Carrier integration service principals have only claim-submission scope. No human user has direct database access; all access via API.
security.secrets_management: Carrier API credentials and mTLS certificates stored in Azure Key Vault. Celery broker connection string injected via Container Apps secrets. Service principal certificates rotated every 90 days via automated Key Vault rotation policy.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Structured audit log entry created for: photo intake received, CV annotation completed (includes model deployment version and prompt hash), claim submitted (includes carrier, submission_id, payload hash), reconciliation status change. Logs shipped to Azure Monitor Log Analytics workspace with 2-year retention.
security.security_risks: Risk: Vehicle photo URLs in Azure Blob exposed publicly due to misconfigured SAS token expiry. Mitigation: all photo access via short-lived (1-hour) SAS tokens generated server-side; direct public Blob access disabled.

data.entities: damage_assessments (id UUID PK, inspector_id, vehicle_vin, photo_set_blob_paths[], annotation_status ENUM pending/annotating/complete/failed/manual_review, created_at, completed_at). damage_annotations (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, panel_id, damage_type, severity ENUM minor/moderate/severe/total_loss, repair_action, confidence_score, cv_model_version). claim_submissions (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, carrier_id FK, submission_id_carrier, status ENUM pending/submitted/accepted/rejected/settled, submitted_at, last_reconciled_at, settlement_amount). insurance_carriers (id UUID PK, name, portal_api_base_url, auth_type, rate_limit_rpm, form_schema_version). policy_records (id UUID PK, vehicle_vin, policy_number, carrier_id FK, claimant_name_encrypted, claimant_jurisdiction ENUM us/eu). adjuster_reviews (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, reviewer_id, override_annotation_id, review_note, reviewed_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server for relational claim state. Azure Blob Storage container per claim with 7-year US retention lifecycle and 30-day EU deletion policy. Blob paths stored in damage_assessments.photo_set_blob_paths[].
data.retention_and_deletion: GDPR deletion job runs daily: identifies claim_submissions where claimant_jurisdiction=EU and last_reconciled_at > 30 days post-closure, deletes Blob photos, nullifies claimant_name_encrypted in policy_records. US records retained in Blob with lifecycle tier to Cool after 1 year, Archive after 3 years.

devops.deployment_topology: Azure Container Apps environment with three container apps: api (FastAPI), worker (Celery), scheduler (Celery Beat for reconciliation). Separate Container Apps environment in Azure West Europe for EU data residency. Azure Service Bus namespace shared between regions via geo-replication.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: build Docker image, run pytest with carrier sandbox integration tests, push to Azure Container Registry, deploy to dev environment, promote to staging on PR merge, promote to production on manual approval. Carrier credentials injected as Container Apps secrets per environment.
devops.observability_stack: Azure Application Insights SDK in FastAPI and Celery for distributed traces. Custom metrics: annotation_duration_seconds, claim_submission_success_rate per carrier, reconciliation_lag_hours. Azure Monitor alerts: submission failure rate >2%, worker queue depth >50, reconciliation job missed schedule.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: EU Container Apps environment diverges from US in configuration. Mitigation: shared Bicep IaC template with environment-specific parameter files; drift detection in CI.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an advanced automation pipeline on Azure for vehicle damage CV annotation and multi-carrier insurance claim submission. Key decisions: FastAPI with Celery on Container Apps for async vision inference without blocking intake webhook latency; GPT-4o Vision with pinned deployment version for audit-reproducible damage annotations; mTLS for all carrier portal API calls per integration agreements; separate Azure West Europe deployment for GDPR EU data residency; idempotency keys on all claim submissions to handle carrier portal network errors safely.
target_output.title: Vehicle Damage Assessment and Insurance Claim Automation Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A Python FastAPI automation tool deployed on Azure Container Apps that ingests vehicle damage photos from field inspectors, invokes GPT-4o Vision to extract structured damage annotations, generates carrier-specific claim forms, and submits claims to insurance carrier portals via mTLS-authenticated APIs. Celery handles async vision inference and claim submission tasks backed by Azure Service Bus. A nightly reconciliation batch job polls carriers for claim status updates. EU claimant data is processed in a dedicated Azure West Europe deployment for GDPR compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven automation pipeline: inspector mobile intake SDK sends a POST to the FastAPI intake webhook, which creates a damage_assessment record and enqueues an annotate_damage_photos Celery task to Azure Service Bus. The Celery worker fetches photos from Azure Blob Storage, invokes Azure OpenAI GPT-4o Vision with structured output mode, parses the damage_annotations JSON response, and persists results to PostgreSQL. A second Celery task submit_claim_to_carrier generates a carrier-specific claim form from the annotations and submits it to the carrier portal via mTLS-authenticated HTTPS, storing the carrier-returned submission_id. A Celery Beat scheduler triggers reconcile_open_claims every 4 hours for priority claims and nightly for standard claims, patching claim_submissions with settlement amounts. EU claim traffic routes to a separate Azure West Europe Container Apps environment with its own PostgreSQL instance and Blob Storage container for GDPR data residency.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.111, Celery 5.4 with Azure Service Bus broker. Vision AI: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o Vision with structured output mode, deployment version pinned per environment. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with read replica for reconciliation queries. Storage: Azure Blob Storage with lifecycle management policies. Hosting: Azure Container Apps (api, worker, scheduler apps). Security: Azure Key Vault for secrets, Azure AD service principals with cert-based auth, mTLS via Azure API Management for carrier calls. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Observability: Azure Application Insights, Azure Monitor.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Photo Intake Webhook: receives multipart POST from inspector mobile SDK with vehicle_vin, policy_number, and photo files; validates HMAC-SHA256 API key; creates damage_assessment record; returns assessment_id. CV Annotation Pipeline: Celery task downloads photos from Blob, sends to GPT-4o Vision with carrier-agnostic damage annotation prompt, parses structured output (panel_id, damage_type, severity, repair_action, confidence_score), persists damage_annotations. Carrier Claim Submission: per-carrier form builder maps annotations to carrier field schema; submits via carrier portal API with idempotency key; handles 429 rate limits with per-carrier backoff queue. Adjuster Review Queue: REST endpoint returns assessments in manual_review or rejected status with annotation details for human override. Batch Reconciliation: polls carrier APIs for open claim status, updates settlement_amount and status in claim_submissions.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI API container (intake webhook, adjuster review endpoints, health checks). Celery Worker container (annotation and submission tasks, Azure Service Bus consumer). Celery Beat Scheduler container (reconciliation job trigger every 4h and nightly). PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server (primary + read replica). Azure Blob Storage (photo storage, per-claim containers, lifecycle policies). Azure Service Bus (task queue, dead-letter queue for failed tasks). Azure OpenAI GPT-4o Vision deployment (damage annotation inference). Azure Key Vault (carrier credentials, mTLS certificates, service principal certs). Azure Container Registry (Docker images). Azure Application Insights (distributed tracing and metrics).
target_output.workflows: Inspector Photo Intake and Annotation: Inspector uploads 3-12 damage photos via mobile SDK POST /v1/intakes with vehicle_vin and policy_number. FastAPI validates HMAC-SHA256 key, stores photos to Azure Blob at claims/{assessment_id}/photos/, creates damage_assessment record with status=pending, enqueues annotate_damage_photos task, returns {assessment_id, status: 'queued'}. Celery worker dequeues task, fetches blob URLs, calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o Vision API with structured output schema, parses damage_annotations array (panel_id, damage_type, severity, repair_action), persists to DB, updates assessment status to complete, enqueues submit_claim_to_carrier. Carrier Claim Submission and Reconciliation: Submission task loads carrier form schema from insurance_carriers, maps damage_annotations to carrier fields, generates submission payload, calls carrier portal API with mTLS client cert and idempotency key=claim_submissions.id, stores submission_id_carrier on success, or retries on transient errors. Reconciliation task polls carrier GET /claims/{submission_id} endpoints, updates settlement_amount and status, triggers GDPR deletion job for EU closed claims.
target_output.data_model: damage_assessments (id UUID PK, inspector_id VARCHAR, vehicle_vin VARCHAR(17), photo_set_blob_paths TEXT[], annotation_status ENUM pending/annotating/complete/failed/manual_review, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). damage_annotations (id UUID PK, assessment_id UUID FK, panel_id VARCHAR, damage_type VARCHAR, severity ENUM minor/moderate/severe/total_loss, repair_action VARCHAR, confidence_score FLOAT, cv_model_version VARCHAR). claim_submissions (id UUID PK, assessment_id UUID FK, carrier_id UUID FK, submission_id_carrier VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/submitted/accepted/rejected/settled, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_reconciled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, settlement_amount NUMERIC(12,2)). insurance_carriers (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, portal_api_base_url VARCHAR, auth_type VARCHAR, rate_limit_rpm INT, form_schema_version VARCHAR). policy_records (id UUID PK, vehicle_vin VARCHAR(17), policy_number VARCHAR, carrier_id UUID FK, claimant_name_encrypted BYTEA, claimant_jurisdiction ENUM us/eu). adjuster_reviews (id UUID PK, assessment_id UUID FK, reviewer_id VARCHAR, review_note TEXT, reviewed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Internal REST API (FastAPI): POST /v1/intakes (multipart, API key auth, returns assessment_id). GET /v1/assessments/{id} (annotation results, claim status). GET /v1/claims?status=pending_review (adjuster queue, paginated). POST /v1/claims/{id}/resubmit (manual resubmit trigger). GET /v1/health (liveness probe). Carrier Portal Integrations (outbound, mTLS): State Farm POST /claims/v2/submit, GET /claims/v2/{id}/status. Travelers POST /api/claims/new, GET /api/claims/{ref}/status. All outbound calls go through Azure API Management with mTLS certificate injection from Key Vault.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS enforced for all carrier portal API calls via Azure API Management with Key Vault-managed client certificates. Inspector intake webhook authenticated via HMAC-SHA256 API key with 300-second timestamp tolerance. Vehicle photos stored in Azure Blob with private access only; all URLs are short-lived SAS tokens (1-hour expiry) generated server-side. Azure AD service principals per carrier with certificate-based authentication, rotated every 90 days via Key Vault rotation policy. Audit log entry created for each: photo intake, CV annotation (includes model_version and prompt_hash), claim submission (includes carrier, submission_id, payload SHA-256). GDPR: EU claimant photos and PII deleted within 30 days of claim closure; daily deletion job with verified audit record. State insurance compliance: all claim submissions include required electronic submission fields per state EDI standards.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps Pipeline stages: (1) build and push Docker images to Azure Container Registry; (2) run pytest unit and carrier sandbox integration tests; (3) deploy to dev Container Apps environment; (4) promote to staging on PR merge; (5) promote to production on manual approval gate. Three Container Apps per environment: api (min 1, max 10 replicas based on HTTP request queue), worker (min 2, max 20 replicas based on Service Bus queue depth), scheduler (min 1, max 1 singleton). EU data residency: separate Container Apps environment in Azure West Europe with region-isolated PostgreSQL Flexible Server and Blob Storage. Secrets injected via Container Apps secrets from Azure Key Vault references. Database migrations via Alembic run as Container Apps job before deploy. Rollback: Container Apps revision traffic split allows instant rollback to previous revision.
target_output.observability: Azure Application Insights SDK in FastAPI (request traces) and Celery (task start/complete/failure events). Custom metrics via Azure Monitor: annotation_duration_p95_seconds, claim_submission_success_rate_per_carrier, reconciliation_lag_hours, gdpr_deletion_job_success. Alerts: claim submission failure rate >2% over 15 min triggers PagerDuty P2; worker queue depth >100 triggers scale-out alert; reconciliation job missed schedule triggers P3.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Azure Container Apps scales worker replicas based on Service Bus queue depth (1 replica per 10 queued tasks). PostgreSQL read replica used exclusively for reconciliation queries to avoid contention with transactional writes. Estimated monthly cost: ~$800 for 2000 daily claim assessments (Container Apps, PostgreSQL Flexible Server, Blob Storage, Service Bus). GPT-4o Vision cost approximately $0.04 per 10-photo assessment at current pricing. Cost controls: Blob lifecycle policy tiers photos to Cool storage after 90 days.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Photo intake webhook, GPT-4o Vision annotation pipeline, State Farm and Travelers claim submission with mTLS, nightly reconciliation job, adjuster review queue REST endpoint, CI/CD pipeline with carrier sandbox tests. GDPR deletion job for EU claimants. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Expand to 5 additional carriers, per-carrier damage code mapping validation layer, 4-hour reconciliation cadence for priority claims, adjuster override workflow with annotation correction persistence. Phase 3 (future): Full 20-carrier network, repair shop estimate API integration, real-time claim status push via WebSocket to inspector mobile app.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Celery tasks must be idempotent: task_id used as idempotency key for both DB writes and carrier API submissions. GPT-4o Vision deployment version pinned in Azure OpenAI client config per environment; version upgrades require regression test on a 100-claim benchmark set before production promotion. Carrier integration code isolated in carrier_adapters/ module with a per-carrier adapter class implementing a common ClaimSubmitter interface. pytest fixtures use carrier sandbox API credentials from environment variables; never hardcode credentials in tests.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o Vision misclassifies damage severity (e.g., severe as moderate), causing claim undervaluation. Mitigation: confidence_score threshold gate routes low-confidence assessments to adjuster_reviews queue. Risk: Carrier portal API schema change breaks claim submission format. Mitigation: per-carrier integration tests run against sandbox on every deploy; form_schema_version tracked in insurance_carriers table. Risk: Azure OpenAI service outage blocks all annotation processing. Mitigation: graceful degradation to manual_review status with inspector notification; Celery tasks retry on service restoration. Tradeoff: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o Vision over custom fine-tuned CV model avoids 6-week model training cycle but introduces per-inference cost and vendor dependency.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o Vision selected over custom YOLOv8 fine-tuning to avoid training data labeling overhead for MVP timeline. Azure Service Bus chosen over Redis as Celery broker for durable task persistence and dead-letter queue support without additional infrastructure. Travelers pagination confirmed as cursor-based per carrier integration spec review.

metadata.notes: Strong first-pass for vehicle damage CV automation with multi-carrier insurance claim submission on Azure; includes GDPR EU data residency and mTLS carrier integration.

profile.domain: Automotive Engineering ML Workbench - CAN Bus Signal Analysis

project_goal.value: Provide automotive engineers with an interactive desktop workbench for importing CAN bus signal datasets, building feature sets, training ML models, and evaluating results with side-by-side experiment comparison.
project_goal.rationale: Replaces fragmented Jupyter notebook and CSV toolchain used by vehicle dynamics engineers for ML model development on test drive data.

target_users.value: Automotive ML engineers and vehicle dynamics researchers at OEM engineering facilities.
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in engineering team interviews.

project_class.rationale: Runs locally on engineer workstations with GPU; no cloud dependency for inference or data storage.

capabilities.rationale: Electron/React UI, local Python FastAPI backend, SQLite for experiment metadata, local ML training with GPU, experiment analytics dashboard.

complexity_level.rationale: Local GPU training pipeline, CAN bus signal parsing, ONNX export, and multi-experiment comparison require advanced architecture.

risk_level.rationale: OEM IP protection requirements for CAN bus test data and trained models; local-only deployment reduces attack surface.

data_sensitivity.rationale: CAN bus signal datasets and trained ML models constitute OEM proprietary IP.
