external_exposure.rationale: Desktop application runs entirely on local workstation with no network-facing services.

access_model.value: Local user profile per workstation; no network authentication. Model export restricted to OEM-approved storage paths via OS-level policy.
access_model.rationale: Air-gapped or restricted-network engineering environments; no external identity provider needed.

feature_scope.value: CAN bus log file import (.blf, .asc, .mf4 formats), visual signal selector for feature column definition, model training job configuration (classifier/regressor), local GPU training with loss curve monitoring, evaluation panel with confusion matrix/ROC/feature importance, experiment comparison, ONNX model export.
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for the ML workbench covering import-to-export workflow.

mvp_scope.value: CAN bus .blf/.mf4 import, signal feature selector, scikit-learn classifier training with local CPU/GPU, loss curve display, confusion matrix evaluation, single ONNX export.
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP covers the core import-train-evaluate loop for early engineer adoption.

security_baseline.value: Local SQLite database encrypted at rest via SQLCipher. CAN bus dataset files accessed read-only from source path; no cloud upload. Trained model ONNX exports written only to user-configured output directory.
security_baseline.rationale: OEM IP protection for test drive data and trained models.

frontend_stack.value: Electron 30 with React 18 and TypeScript for the desktop shell and UI.
frontend_stack.rationale: Electron provides cross-platform desktop shell with native file system access; React for complex UI panels.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI running as a local subprocess managed by the Electron main process. scikit-learn and PyTorch for model training.
backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem for ML libraries; FastAPI provides clean IPC interface over localhost.

data_platform.value: SQLite (via SQLCipher for encryption) for experiment metadata. Local file system for CAN bus datasets and trained model artifacts.
data_platform.rationale: Zero-dependency local storage appropriate for desktop application.

hosting_target.value: Local desktop distribution via signed installer (Windows MSI, macOS DMG). No cloud hosting.
hosting_target.rationale: Engineers install and run entirely on OEM workstations.

privacy_retention_policy.value: All experiment data stored locally on engineer workstation. No telemetry or usage data collected. OEM data governance policies apply to CAN bus dataset handling.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Local-only application; no data leaves the workstation.

future_scope.value: Deep learning model support (LSTM, Transformer), team experiment sharing via shared network drive, integration with OEM test data management system API.
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap items for expanded model architecture support.

constraints.value: Must run on Windows 10/11 and macOS 14+. Python subprocess must start within 5 seconds. Training on datasets up to 500MB must not freeze the Electron UI. ONNX export must be validated against onnxruntime before surfacing to engineer.
constraints.rationale: OEM workstation OS requirements and UX performance SLAs.

observability_baseline.value: Local log files in user app data directory. Training job progress events streamed via FastAPI SSE to Electron renderer. No external monitoring.
observability_baseline.rationale: Local-only app; no external observability infrastructure needed.

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with weekly engineer feedback sessions. Electron app auto-updates via electron-updater from internal artifact server.
execution_preference.rationale: Tight feedback loop with end users who are co-located with the engineering team.

compliance_context.value: ISO 26262 functional safety documentation standards for trained model outputs used in safety-relevant vehicle systems. OEM IP protection requirements for CAN bus test drive datasets.
compliance_context.rationale: data_sensitivity=confidential requires compliance_context per contract rules.

llm_integration.value: Local LLM integration is not used. AI capabilities are scikit-learn and PyTorch models trained locally by the engineer, not pre-trained LLMs.
llm_integration.rationale: ai_llm capability refers to local ML training pipeline, not an external LLM API.

project.goal: Desktop ML workbench for CAN bus signal-based model training and evaluation
project.target_users: Automotive ML engineers at OEM facilities

frontend.state: Zustand for experiment UI state, React Query for FastAPI data fetching
frontend.charts: Recharts for loss curves and ROC plots

backend.framework: FastAPI as local IPC server

security.access: Local user profile, read-only CAN bus file access

data.files: CAN bus log files: .blf, .asc, .mf4 via python-can and asammdf libraries

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for build and test
devops.packaging: electron-builder for MSI and DMG installers

constraints.ui: Training must not freeze Electron UI

open_questions.pytorch_gpu: Confirm CUDA version compatibility with OEM workstation GPU drivers

reasoner_reviews.product: The workbench addresses a real productivity gap for automotive ML engineers who currently stitch together Jupyter notebooks, pandas CSV transforms, and manual model training scripts. The import-train-evaluate-export loop is the right MVP scope; side-by-side experiment comparison is the highest-value differentiator versus the existing toolchain.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Running the Python FastAPI backend as a local subprocess managed by Electron main process is the right IPC pattern; it avoids bundling Python into the Electron package by using the system Python or a bundled miniforge environment. Training progress streamed via FastAPI SSE to the renderer eliminates polling. SQLCipher for SQLite provides OEM IP protection without cloud dependency.
reasoner_reviews.security: CAN bus test drive data constitutes OEM confidential IP. SQLCipher encryption key should be derived from the OS user keychain (Windows DPAPI or macOS Keychain), not a hardcoded value. ONNX export validation against onnxruntime before surfacing ensures engineers do not receive broken model artifacts.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 5-second Python subprocess startup is achievable with a pre-warmed process pool. Training on 500MB datasets without UI freeze requires running the PyTorch training loop in a separate process and streaming progress via SSE, not blocking the FastAPI event loop.

frontend.app_structure: Electron main process manages Python subprocess lifecycle and IPC via localhost FastAPI. Renderer process is a React 18 SPA with route-based panels: Import, Feature Selector, Training Config, Training Monitor, Evaluation, Experiment Comparison, Export.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Import Panel: file picker for .blf/.asc/.mf4 CAN log files, displays signal channel list from parsed header. Feature Selector: visual table of signal channels with checkboxes for feature columns and target column, configurable window size and step. Training Config: model type selector (RandomForest, SVM, GradientBoosting, PyTorch MLP), hyperparameter form, GPU/CPU device selector. Training Monitor: real-time loss curve from SSE stream, epoch counter, cancel button. Evaluation Panel: confusion matrix heatmap, ROC curve, feature importance bar chart, per-class metrics table. Experiment Comparison: side-by-side metric table across selected training_runs. Export: ONNX download with validation status badge.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for active experiment state (selected features, training config, current training_run_id). React Query for fetching evaluation_metrics and experiment_configs from FastAPI. SSE hook for streaming training progress events.
frontend.frontend_risks: Risk: Electron renderer freezes during large CAN log file parsing. Mitigation: file parsing runs in Python backend subprocess; renderer only receives parsed signal channel list via FastAPI response.

backend.service_design: FastAPI modules: DatasetService (CAN log parsing via python-can/asammdf, feature matrix construction), TrainingService (scikit-learn and PyTorch model training in subprocess with SSE progress stream), EvaluationService (confusion matrix, ROC, feature importance computation), ExportService (ONNX conversion and onnxruntime validation), ExperimentService (SQLite CRUD for training_runs and experiment_configs).
backend.api_patterns: POST /datasets/import (parse CAN log, return signal_channels). POST /features (save feature_definition). POST /training/start (launch training subprocess, return run_id). GET /training/{run_id}/progress (SSE stream of loss events). GET /evaluation/{run_id}/metrics (confusion matrix, ROC). POST /export/{run_id}/onnx (export and validate ONNX). GET /experiments (list training_runs with metrics summary).
backend.failure_handling: Training subprocess crash sends error event on SSE stream, updates training_run status to failed in SQLite. ONNX export validation failure returns 422 with onnxruntime error detail. CAN log parse errors return 400 with signal channel and byte offset of parse failure.
backend.backend_risks: Risk: PyTorch training loop blocks FastAPI event loop. Mitigation: training runs in a separate subprocess via multiprocessing; progress communicated via queue polled by SSE endpoint.

security.auth_design: No network authentication; FastAPI binds to 127.0.0.1 only, rejecting all non-localhost connections.
security.secrets_management: SQLCipher encryption key derived from OS user keychain: Windows DPAPI via win32crypt, macOS Keychain via keyring library. Key never written to disk or logged.
security.abuse_prevention: FastAPI bound to 127.0.0.1 only. Electron contextIsolation and nodeIntegration=false enforced to prevent renderer from directly accessing Node.js APIs.
security.security_risks: Risk: Trained ONNX model exported to a shared network path outside OEM-approved directories. Mitigation: export path validated against user-configured allowed_export_paths list stored in app settings.

data.entities: can_signal_datasets (id, name, file_path, file_format ENUM blf/asc/mf4, signal_channels_json, imported_at). feature_definitions (id, dataset_id FK, feature_columns[], target_column, window_size_samples, step_size_samples, created_at). training_runs (id, feature_definition_id FK, model_type, hyperparams_json, status ENUM pending/running/complete/failed, started_at, completed_at, model_artifact_path). evaluation_metrics (id, training_run_id FK, confusion_matrix_json, roc_auc, f1_macro, feature_importances_json, eval_dataset_fraction). experiment_configs (id, name, training_run_ids[], notes, created_at).
data.storage_design: SQLite database (encrypted via SQLCipher) at user app data path for all metadata. CAN bus log files and ONNX artifacts referenced by path on local file system; not copied into app data directory.
data.schema_notes: can_signal_datasets.signal_channels_json stores parsed channel metadata: [{name, unit, sample_rate_hz, min_val, max_val}]. hyperparams_json stores model-specific hyperparameters as a flat JSON object for flexibility across scikit-learn and PyTorch models.

devops.deployment_topology: Signed desktop installers: electron-builder produces Windows MSI (code-signed with OEM EV certificate) and macOS DMG (notarized via Apple Developer account). Python environment bundled as a miniforge conda environment in the app package to avoid system Python dependency.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: build Python backend (pytest), build Electron app (electron-builder), run smoke tests via Playwright against packaged app in headless Electron mode. Publish MSI and DMG to internal artifact server. electron-updater checks artifact server on app startup for new versions.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: PyTorch CUDA version incompatibility with OEM workstation GPU driver. Mitigation: bundle CPU-only PyTorch in installer by default; CUDA-enabled PyTorch downloaded on first GPU training attempt if compatible driver detected.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a local desktop ML workbench for automotive CAN bus signal analysis. The Electron plus local Python FastAPI IPC pattern isolates UI from training compute. SQLCipher protects OEM IP at rest. The intentional flaws in this plan include: technology_stack incorrectly describing a cloud-hosted Next.js frontend instead of Electron; data_model including a generic 'users' table irrelevant to a local desktop app with no auth; deployment_and_operations describing ECS Fargate cloud deployment instead of local installer distribution. These flaws should be caught by an auditor in the next round.
target_output.title: CAN Bus ML Workbench Desktop Application
target_output.executive_summary: A desktop application for automotive engineers providing an integrated workbench for CAN bus signal dataset import, feature engineering, ML model training with local GPU support, and evaluation with ONNX export. Built with Electron 30 and React 18 for the desktop shell, Python 3.12 FastAPI as a local IPC backend, and SQLite with SQLCipher for experiment metadata storage. Entirely local operation with no cloud dependencies protects OEM proprietary test drive data.
target_output.architecture_overview: Local two-process architecture: Electron main process spawns a Python 3.12 FastAPI subprocess bound to 127.0.0.1:8742 and manages its lifecycle. Electron renderer process is a React 18 SPA communicating with the local FastAPI server via HTTP and SSE for training progress streaming. CAN bus log files (.blf, .asc, .mf4) are parsed by the Python backend using python-can and asammdf libraries; parsed signal channels returned to the renderer for visual feature selection. ML training runs execute in a separate Python subprocess to avoid blocking the FastAPI event loop, with progress events communicated via multiprocessing.Queue polled by the SSE endpoint. SQLite with SQLCipher stores experiment metadata, feature definitions, and evaluation metrics. Trained models exported as ONNX and validated against onnxruntime before surfacing to the engineer.
target_output.technology_stack: FLAW: Desktop shell: Next.js 14 deployed on Vercel (incorrect - should be Electron 30 for local desktop). Python backend: FastAPI 0.111, scikit-learn 1.5, PyTorch 2.3, python-can, asammdf, onnxmltools, onnxruntime 1.18. Database: SQLite via SQLCipher (sqlcipher3 Python binding). UI: React 18 TypeScript, Zustand, React Query, Recharts for loss curves and ROC plots. Packaging: electron-builder for MSI and DMG. Distribution: internal artifact server with electron-updater.
target_output.functional_feature_map: CAN Bus Import: file picker for .blf, .asc, .mf4 formats; Python backend parses signal channel headers; renderer displays channel list with name, unit, and sample rate metadata. Feature Selector: checkboxes for feature columns and target column from parsed channels; configurable sliding window size and step; preview of feature matrix shape. Training Configuration: model type selector (RandomForest, GradientBoosting, SVM, PyTorch MLP); hyperparameter form per model type; GPU/CPU device selector. Training Monitor: real-time loss curve via SSE; epoch/iteration counter; estimated time remaining; cancel button. Evaluation Panel: confusion matrix heatmap; ROC curve with AUC; feature importance bar chart; per-class precision/recall/F1 table. Experiment Comparison: side-by-side metric table across multiple training_runs. ONNX Export: one-click export with onnxruntime validation status badge.
target_output.system_components: Electron 30 main process (Python subprocess manager, auto-updater, file system access). React 18 renderer SPA (UI panels, Zustand state, React Query, SSE training progress hook). Python 3.12 FastAPI server (127.0.0.1:8742, dataset/training/evaluation/export modules). Python training subprocess (scikit-learn and PyTorch training loop, multiprocessing.Queue progress stream). SQLite with SQLCipher (experiment metadata, feature definitions, evaluation metrics). Local file system (CAN bus log files read-only, ONNX model artifacts write to configured export path).
target_output.workflows: CAN Bus Import and Feature Engineering: Engineer opens Import Panel, selects a .mf4 CAN log file from local disk. Electron main process passes file path to FastAPI POST /datasets/import. Python backend reads signal channels via asammdf, returns channel list with metadata. Engineer checks feature channels and target column in Feature Selector, sets window_size=500 samples and step=50 samples, saves feature_definition to SQLite. Model Training and Evaluation: Engineer opens Training Config, selects RandomForest with n_estimators=200, clicks Train. FastAPI POST /training/start launches Python training subprocess, renderer subscribes to GET /training/{run_id}/progress SSE. Training subprocess builds feature matrices from CAN signal windows, fits model, emits loss/score events per fold. On completion, Evaluation Panel fetches confusion matrix, ROC AUC, and feature importance from GET /evaluation/{run_id}/metrics. Engineer exports model via POST /export/{run_id}/onnx; backend validates with onnxruntime.InferenceSession, returns ONNX file path.
target_output.data_model: FLAW: users (id, username, email, password_hash, created_at) - generic user table inappropriate for a local desktop app with no authentication. can_signal_datasets (id INTEGER PK, name TEXT, file_path TEXT, file_format TEXT, signal_channels_json TEXT, imported_at DATETIME). feature_definitions (id INTEGER PK, dataset_id INTEGER FK, feature_columns TEXT, target_column TEXT, window_size_samples INTEGER, step_size_samples INTEGER). training_runs (id INTEGER PK, feature_definition_id INTEGER FK, model_type TEXT, hyperparams_json TEXT, status TEXT, started_at DATETIME, completed_at DATETIME, model_artifact_path TEXT). evaluation_metrics (id INTEGER PK, training_run_id INTEGER FK, confusion_matrix_json TEXT, roc_auc REAL, f1_macro REAL, feature_importances_json TEXT). experiment_configs (id INTEGER PK, name TEXT, training_run_ids TEXT, notes TEXT, created_at DATETIME).
target_output.api_design: Local FastAPI REST and SSE API (127.0.0.1:8742): POST /datasets/import {file_path} returns {dataset_id, signal_channels[]}. POST /features {dataset_id, feature_columns, target_column, window_size, step_size} returns {feature_definition_id}. POST /training/start {feature_definition_id, model_type, hyperparams} returns {run_id}. GET /training/{run_id}/progress (SSE stream: {epoch, loss, val_score, status}). GET /evaluation/{run_id}/metrics returns {confusion_matrix, roc_auc, f1_macro, feature_importances}. POST /export/{run_id}/onnx returns {onnx_path, validation_status}. GET /experiments returns [{run_id, model_type, roc_auc, f1_macro, completed_at}].
target_output.security_and_compliance: FastAPI binds to 127.0.0.1 only; no network-facing port. SQLCipher encryption key derived from OS keychain (Windows DPAPI, macOS Keychain) via keyring library; never written to disk. Electron contextIsolation=true and nodeIntegration=false enforced in renderer BrowserWindow config. ONNX export path validated against user-configured allowed_export_paths before writing. ISO 26262 documentation: training_runs record model type, hyperparameters, dataset file path, and evaluation metrics to support functional safety traceability for any model used in safety-relevant vehicle systems.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: FLAW: Deploy to AWS ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1 using Docker containers (incorrect - this is a local desktop app, not a cloud service). CI/CD: GitHub Actions builds Python backend and Electron app, runs pytest and Playwright smoke tests against packaged Electron app in headless mode, publishes signed MSI and DMG to internal artifact server. electron-updater checks artifact server on startup for incremental updates. Python miniforge environment bundled in installer to eliminate system Python dependency.
target_output.observability: Local log files written to user app data directory: electron-main.log, python-backend.log, training-jobs.log. Training progress streamed to Electron renderer via SSE for real-time loss curve display. No external monitoring or telemetry; all observability is local to the engineer's workstation. Log rotation capped at 10MB per file with 3 rotations.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; all compute runs on engineer workstation. PyTorch GPU training on a mid-range NVIDIA GPU (RTX 3070) processes a 500MB CAN bus dataset (approx 5M signal samples) in under 10 minutes for a GradientBoosting model. electron-updater artifact server is a static file host on internal infrastructure with negligible cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): CAN bus .blf and .mf4 import, signal feature selector, scikit-learn RandomForest and GradientBoosting training with CPU/GPU, confusion matrix and ROC evaluation, single ONNX export with onnxruntime validation, SQLCipher SQLite metadata storage. Phase 2 (4 weeks): PyTorch MLP model support, real-time loss curve SSE streaming, experiment comparison panel, .asc format support, electron-updater auto-update. Phase 3 (future): LSTM and Transformer model support, team experiment sharing via shared network drive, OEM test data management system API integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python backend unit tests use pytest with fixture CAN log files; no real test drive data in CI. Electron renderer E2E tests use Playwright in headless Electron mode against packaged app. SQLCipher key derivation tested against mocked OS keychain to avoid test environment dependencies. Training subprocess must be killed cleanly on Electron app quit; main process registers app.on('before-quit') handler to terminate Python subprocess.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Python subprocess fails to start on engineer workstation due to bundled miniforge environment incompatibility with Windows Defender or macOS Gatekeeper. Mitigation: installer code-signed with OEM EV certificate (Windows) and notarized (macOS). Risk: PyTorch CUDA version incompatibility with OEM workstation GPU driver. Mitigation: CPU-only PyTorch bundled by default; CUDA version downloaded on first GPU training attempt. Tradeoff: Local FastAPI IPC over Electron IPC (ipcMain/ipcRenderer) for cleaner separation of Python backend from Electron architecture; trade-off is 1-2ms localhost HTTP overhead per request.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Electron selected over Tauri due to team React expertise and mature Node.js ecosystem for file system access. SQLCipher selected over application-level AES encryption for transparent SQLite encryption without schema changes. ONNX export format selected for compatibility with OEM downstream model deployment toolchain.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: technology_stack describes Next.js/Vercel instead of Electron, data_model includes irrelevant users table, deployment_and_operations describes ECS Fargate instead of local installer distribution.

profile.domain: National Fleet Analytics Platform - Telematics and Operations KPIs

project_goal.value: Aggregate telematics, fuel, maintenance, and driver scorecard data across a national trucking fleet to surface KPIs, industry benchmarks, and trend reports for fleet executives.
project_goal.rationale: Replaces 14 separate Excel-based KPI reports compiled manually by operations analysts each week.

target_users.value: Fleet executives, regional operations managers, and fleet administrators at a national trucking company with 4,000+ vehicles.
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user roles with different dashboard views and data access scopes.

project_class.rationale: Requires Next.js dashboard frontend and FastAPI analytics backend with ClickHouse for OLAP queries.

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, batch_jobs, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Dashboard UI, analytics API, ClickHouse OLAP, Okta auth, nightly KPI batch aggregation, and admin panel for org unit management.

complexity_level.rationale: Aggregates 50M+ daily telematics events across 4,000 vehicles; requires pre-computed materialized views for sub-second dashboard queries.

risk_level.rationale: Driver performance data is confidential operational data; SOC 2 required for enterprise trucking clients.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Driver scorecards, fuel costs, and maintenance records are confidential operational and commercial data.

external_exposure.rationale: Dashboard accessible only to authenticated fleet organization members via Okta SSO.

access_model.value: Okta OIDC SSO with RBAC: executive (org-wide read), regional_manager (regional scope read), fleet_admin (org management and report scheduling).
access_model.rationale: Three role tiers map to distinct data scopes: org-wide, regional, and administrative.

feature_scope.value: Executive KPI dashboard (cost per mile, idle time %, safety score, on-time delivery rate), driver scorecard views, fuel consumption trend charts, maintenance cost summaries, custom benchmark report builder, scheduled PDF exports, admin panel for org unit and user management.
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set covering executive, operational, and administrative use cases.

mvp_scope.value: Executive KPI dashboard with 6 core metrics, driver scorecard table, fuel trend chart, and nightly ClickHouse materialized view refresh for top 3 organizational units.
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP covers highest-value reporting for fleet executives within delivery timeline.

security_baseline.value: Okta OIDC SSO, JWT validation on all API routes, row-level security in ClickHouse views scoped to org unit, TLS 1.3 in transit, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager.
security_baseline.rationale: Enterprise security baseline for confidential fleet operational data.

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Recharts for time-series trend visualization.
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js SSR for fast initial dashboard load; Recharts for interactive trend charts.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI on AWS ECS Fargate.
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for high-throughput analytics query API; ClickHouse client for OLAP query execution.

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for OLAP analytics. Redis 7 for query result caching. PostgreSQL 16 for transactional data (user management, org units, report schedules).
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse handles columnar aggregation over 50M+ daily events; Redis caches materialized query results.

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1. ClickHouse on EC2 r6i instances. Redis on ElastiCache.
hosting_target.rationale: ECS Fargate for managed API containers; dedicated EC2 for ClickHouse to avoid shared compute contention.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Driver scorecard and telematics data retained 3 years then deleted. EU driver performance data (GDPR scope) deleted on driver offboarding request within 30 days.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 3-year retention supports trend analysis; GDPR erasure for EU drivers.

future_scope.value: Predictive maintenance alert engine using ML anomaly detection on telematics signals, driver coaching module with recommended actions, carrier benchmarking across multiple fleet organizations.
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 roadmap items dependent on data maturity.

constraints.value: Dashboard KPI queries must return in under 1 second for 12-month aggregations. Nightly batch must complete before 6 AM EST. Budget under $8,000/month.
constraints.rationale: Executive dashboard UX requirement and operational batch window constraint.

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for FastAPI request traces and ClickHouse query performance. Alerting on p99 API latency >2s, nightly batch job failures, and ClickHouse replication lag.
observability_baseline.rationale: Datadog is the corporate monitoring standard.

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints. ClickHouse schema changes via migration scripts in CI. Nightly batch jobs deployed as ECS scheduled tasks.
execution_preference.rationale: Structured sprint cadence with automated ClickHouse migrations.

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II required for enterprise trucking client contracts. GDPR for EU driver performance and location data.
compliance_context.rationale: data_sensitivity=confidential requires compliance_context per contract rules.

project.goal: Fleet analytics platform aggregating telematics, fuel, maintenance, and driver data for executive KPI dashboards
project.target_users: Fleet executives, regional managers, fleet admins

backend.framework: FastAPI
backend.db_client: clickhouse-driver for OLAP queries, asyncpg for PostgreSQL

security.auth: Okta OIDC SSO with JWT validation middleware
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 in transit, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager

data.cache: Redis 7 for query result caching (5-minute TTL)

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate for API, EC2 r6i for ClickHouse, ElastiCache for Redis
devops.batch: ECS Scheduled Tasks for nightly KPI aggregation

reasoner_reviews.product: The fleet analytics platform addresses a genuine pain point: 14 manual Excel reports consolidated into a single real-time dashboard saves approximately 40 analyst-hours per week. The benchmark report builder is the highest-value differentiator for fleet executives comparing their operation against industry percentiles. Round 2 improved ClickHouse materialized view strategy but the benchmark comparison module still lacks the peer-group scoping logic that makes it actionable.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 2 resolved the ClickHouse partitioning issue (ISS-001) by adding org_unit_id and period as partition keys on the kpi_summaries materialized view. ISS-002 (Redis cache invalidation on batch completion) remains partially addressed: the cache TTL was extended to 5 minutes but the batch job does not actively invalidate stale KPI caches after nightly refresh, meaning executives may see stale data for up to 5 minutes post-batch.
reasoner_reviews.security: Okta OIDC JWT validation is correctly placed in FastAPI middleware. Row-level security via ClickHouse user-defined scoped views per org_unit_id is the right approach but needs explicit documentation of how the JWT org_unit_id claim maps to ClickHouse view parameters. SOC 2 audit trail requires logging all dashboard data access events.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 1-second query latency constraint requires that all executive KPI dashboard queries hit pre-computed ClickHouse materialized views, not raw event tables. The benchmark report builder currently queries raw route_performance_metrics which at 50M events violates the latency SLA. Pre-computing benchmark percentile summaries in the nightly batch is required.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan improved ClickHouse partitioning and Redis TTL but still has ISS-002 unresolved: active cache invalidation after nightly batch is missing. The benchmark query path through raw tables will fail the 1-second SLA. The admin panel section is thin compared to the other sections.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 app router with route groups: (dashboard) for executive and regional KPI views, (reports) for benchmark builder and PDF export, (admin) for org unit and user management. Server components for initial KPI data fetch; client components for interactive chart filtering.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Executive Dashboard: 6 KPI cards (cost_per_mile, idle_time_pct, safety_score, on_time_delivery_pct, fuel_efficiency_mpg, maintenance_cost_per_vehicle), 90-day trend sparklines, regional breakdown bar chart. Driver Scorecards: sortable table by composite score, individual scorecard modal with sub-metric breakdown. Fuel Trend: 12-month consumption chart by vehicle class with anomaly highlight. Benchmark Report Builder: metric selector, peer group filter (vehicle_class, fleet_size_tier), date range, comparison output table, PDF export trigger. Admin Panel: org unit hierarchy editor, user role assignment, report schedule management.
frontend.state_management: React Query for server-side KPI data with 5-minute stale time aligned to Redis cache TTL. Zustand for benchmark report builder form state. URL query params for dashboard date range and org unit filter to enable shareable dashboard links.
frontend.performance_notes: Next.js ISR for static shell pages with revalidation every 5 minutes. ClickHouse KPI queries served via FastAPI with Redis cache. Large driver scorecard tables virtualized with react-virtual for 4,000-row rendering without DOM overhead.

backend.service_design: FastAPI route modules: KpiService (ClickHouse materialized view queries, Redis cache layer), DriverScorecardService (driver-level aggregations from scorecard materialized view), BenchmarkService (pre-computed percentile summaries from benchmark_summaries MV), ReportService (PDF generation via WeasyPrint from HTML templates), AdminService (PostgreSQL CRUD for org_units, users, report_schedules).
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/kpis/summary?org_unit_id=&period= (returns fleet_kpis from MV, Redis cached). GET /v1/drivers/scorecards?org_unit_id=&period= (driver scorecard aggregations). GET /v1/benchmarks?vehicle_class=&fleet_size_tier=&metric= (benchmark percentile summaries from pre-computed MV). POST /v1/reports/benchmark (async PDF generation, returns report_id). GET /v1/admin/org-units (org unit tree). All routes require Okta JWT; org_unit_id scope enforced in middleware.
backend.background_jobs: nightly_kpi_aggregation ECS Scheduled Task: runs at 2 AM EST, inserts pre-aggregated rows into kpi_summaries ClickHouse MV partitioned by org_unit_id and period. On completion, publishes cache invalidation event to Redis pub/sub channel fleet-kpis-updated, allowing FastAPI KpiService to flush matching cache keys immediately after batch.
backend.scaling_notes: FastAPI ECS Fargate auto-scales on CPU 60% target (2-20 replicas). ClickHouse EC2 r6i.2xlarge single-node for current scale; horizontal sharding planned at 200M daily events. Redis ElastiCache r7g.large with read replica for cache read offload.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC integration via next-auth in Next.js frontend; Okta-issued JWT validated in FastAPI middleware using Okta JWKS endpoint. JWT claims include sub, org_unit_ids[], and role.
security.authorization_model: RBAC in FastAPI middleware: executive role allows query on any org_unit_id in their JWT org_unit_ids claim. regional_manager scoped to their own org_unit_ids. fleet_admin can read all org units and manage users/schedules. ClickHouse queries parameterized with org_unit_id from JWT to enforce row-level data scope.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Structured audit log entry for every KPI query: user_id, org_unit_id, query_type, time_range, response_row_count, latency_ms. Logs shipped to Datadog Log Management with 1-year retention for SOC 2 audit evidence.
security.security_risks: Risk: JWT org_unit_ids claim not validated against PostgreSQL org_units table, allowing stale claim after user role change. Mitigation: FastAPI middleware validates org_unit_ids claim against live PostgreSQL lookup on each request, with Redis 1-minute TTL cache to avoid per-request DB hit.

data.entities: fleet_kpis (org_unit_id, vehicle_class, period DATE, cost_per_mile FLOAT, idle_time_pct FLOAT, safety_score FLOAT, on_time_delivery_pct FLOAT, fuel_efficiency_mpg FLOAT, maintenance_cost_per_vehicle FLOAT) - ClickHouse MV. driver_scorecards (driver_id, org_unit_id, period, composite_score, harsh_braking_count, speeding_minutes, idle_time_minutes, on_time_pct) - ClickHouse MV. fuel_consumption_records (vehicle_id, org_unit_id, event_date, fuel_liters, odometer_km, vehicle_class) - ClickHouse raw table. maintenance_cost_summaries (vehicle_id, org_unit_id, period, labor_cost, parts_cost, downtime_hours, work_order_type) - ClickHouse MV. benchmark_summaries (vehicle_class, fleet_size_tier, metric, period, p25, p50, p75, p90) - ClickHouse MV pre-computed nightly. organizational_units (id UUID PK, name, parent_id, region, vehicle_count) - PostgreSQL.
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree tables for raw telematics events partitioned by toYYYYMM(event_date). Materialized views pre-aggregate to kpi_summaries, driver_scorecards, and benchmark_summaries partitioned by org_unit_id and period for sub-second executive queries. PostgreSQL 16 for users, org_units, and report_schedules. Redis for 5-minute KPI query result cache.
data.analytics_events: Dashboard KPI query events tracked to Datadog for query pattern analysis. Benchmark report generation events tracked for feature usage analytics.
data.data_risks: Risk: ClickHouse materialized view refresh takes longer than nightly batch window at scale beyond 100M daily events. Mitigation: incremental MV refresh on event partitions rather than full recompute; partition pruning on toYYYYMM(event_date).

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate service for FastAPI (2-20 replicas, us-east-1 multi-AZ). ECS Fargate service for Next.js (2-10 replicas). EC2 r6i.2xlarge for ClickHouse with EBS gp3 volumes. ElastiCache r7g.large Redis cluster. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. ECS Scheduled Task for nightly_kpi_aggregation at 2 AM EST.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint and test (pytest for FastAPI, Jest for Next.js), build Docker images, push to ECR, deploy to staging via ECS rolling update, promote to production on manual approval. ClickHouse schema migrations as SQL scripts run as ECS one-off tasks before deploy.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM for FastAPI distributed traces and ClickHouse query durations. Datadog Dashboard: KPI query p99 latency, ClickHouse query count by MV vs raw table, Redis cache hit rate, nightly batch job duration. PagerDuty alerts for batch job failure, ClickHouse replication lag >5 min, API p99 >2s.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: ClickHouse EC2 instance maintenance window disrupts nightly batch. Mitigation: ECS Scheduled Task includes retry with 30-minute delay on ClickHouse connection failure.

ISS-001.title: ClickHouse materialized views not partitioned by org_unit_id
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 kpi_summaries MV had no partition key, causing full table scans on org-scoped queries and violating the 1-second latency SLA for large organizations.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner in round 1.
Added org_unit_id and period as partition keys to kpi_summaries MV.

ISS-002.title: Redis cache not invalidated after nightly batch completion
ISS-002.detail: Nightly batch refreshes ClickHouse MVs but does not invalidate Redis KPI cache, causing executives to see stale KPI data for up to 5 minutes after batch completes.

history.note: Identified by critic in round 1.
TTL shortened to 5 minutes but active cache invalidation not implemented.

ISS-003.title: Benchmark report queries raw tables, violating 1s latency SLA
ISS-003.detail: Benchmark report builder in round 2 plan queries raw route_performance_metrics table with 50M rows, which cannot meet the 1-second query SLA. Pre-computed benchmark_summaries MV is required.

history.note: Identified by constraints reviewer in round 2 audit.

focus_issues.title: Redis cache not invalidated after nightly batch completion
focus_issues.detail: Nightly batch must actively invalidate Redis KPI cache keys after ClickHouse MV refresh completes.

focus_issues.title: Benchmark report queries raw tables, violating 1s latency SLA
focus_issues.detail: Benchmark report builder must query pre-computed benchmark_summaries MV, not raw route_performance_metrics.

latest_recommendations: Add active Redis cache invalidation step in nightly_kpi_aggregation job after ClickHouse MV refresh
Route benchmark report builder queries to pre-computed benchmark_summaries ClickHouse MV instead of raw route_performance_metrics table

convergence_state.reason: Two high/medium issues unresolved; benchmark query path violates SLA.

previous_audits.summary: Strong domain-specific plan with ClickHouse analytics architecture, but materialized view partitioning strategy caused full table scans on org-scoped queries.
strengths: ClickHouse MergeTree with materialized views correctly identified for OLAP scale
Okta OIDC RBAC design is appropriate for multi-org fleet data
concerns: kpi_summaries MV partition strategy would cause full table scans
Cache invalidation after batch not addressed
blocking_issues: ISS-001: ClickHouse MV full table scan on org queries
recommendations: Partition kpi_summaries MV by org_unit_id and toYYYYMM(period)
Add Redis cache invalidation step to nightly batch job

issue_updates.title: ClickHouse MVs not partitioned by org_unit_id
issue_updates.detail: kpi_summaries MV used toYYYYMM(event_date) only, causing full scans on org-filtered dashboard queries

previous_audits.summary: ISS-001 resolved with correct partitioning. ISS-002 partially addressed by TTL reduction but active cache invalidation still missing. ISS-003 newly identified: benchmark queries hit raw tables.
strengths: ClickHouse partition key fix resolves full table scan issue
FastAPI JWT middleware with org_unit_id scoping is well-designed
concerns: Nightly batch does not actively flush Redis KPI caches
Benchmark report builder routes to raw route_performance_metrics table
blocking_issues: ISS-003: Benchmark query path will violate 1s SLA at 50M events
recommendations: Add Redis pub/sub cache invalidation event from nightly batch after MV refresh completes
Create benchmark_summaries ClickHouse MV pre-computing percentile distributions nightly

issue_updates.title: Redis cache not invalidated after batch
issue_updates.detail: Batch job reduced TTL to 5 minutes but does not publish cache flush event after MV refresh

issue_updates.title: Benchmark queries raw tables
issue_updates.detail: BenchmarkService queries SELECT from raw route_performance_metrics, estimated p99 >30s on 50M rows

previous_plan.title: National Fleet Analytics Platform - Round 2
previous_plan.executive_summary: A high-scale fleet analytics platform on AWS aggregating telematics and operational data into ClickHouse with pre-computed materialized views for sub-second executive KPI dashboards.
previous_plan.data_model: ClickHouse kpi_summaries MV partitioned by org_unit_id and toYYYYMM(period). driver_scorecards MV. fuel_consumption_records raw table. BenchmarkService queries raw route_performance_metrics (flagged in ISS-003).
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate for FastAPI and Next.js. EC2 r6i.2xlarge for ClickHouse. ECS Scheduled Task for nightly_kpi_aggregation at 2 AM EST. Redis TTL set to 5 minutes.
previous_plan.workflows: Nightly batch refreshes ClickHouse MVs. Redis TTL expires stale caches passively. Benchmark builder queries raw tables.

best_plan.title: National Fleet Analytics Platform - Round 2
best_plan.executive_summary: A high-scale fleet analytics platform on AWS aggregating telematics and operational data into ClickHouse with pre-computed materialized views for sub-second executive KPI dashboards.
best_plan.architecture_overview: ClickHouse MergeTree tables with materialized views partitioned by org_unit_id. FastAPI with Redis cache and Okta JWT middleware. ECS Fargate for containers.
best_plan.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC SSO. FastAPI JWT middleware scoped by org_unit_id. SOC 2 audit logging to Datadog.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 revision targeting ISS-002 (missing active Redis cache invalidation after nightly batch) and ISS-003 (benchmark report builder querying raw tables, violating 1s SLA). ISS-001 was resolved in round 2 with correct ClickHouse partitioning. I will add a Redis pub/sub cache invalidation step to the nightly batch job and introduce a pre-computed benchmark_summaries ClickHouse materialized view that the BenchmarkService queries instead of raw route_performance_metrics.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Redis pub/sub cache invalidation to nightly_kpi_aggregation job: after ClickHouse MV refresh completes, batch publishes a 'fleet-kpis-updated' event to Redis pub/sub; FastAPI KpiService subscribes and flushes all KPI cache keys matching the refreshed org_unit_id patterns immediately.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Executives see refreshed KPI data within seconds of batch completion rather than waiting up to 5 minutes for Redis TTL expiry.

fix_report.action_taken: Introduced benchmark_summaries ClickHouse materialized view pre-computing p25/p50/p75/p90 percentile distributions per vehicle_class, fleet_size_tier, and metric nightly. BenchmarkService now queries SELECT * FROM benchmark_summaries WHERE vehicle_class=? AND fleet_size_tier=? AND metric=?, returning sub-100ms responses regardless of raw event table size.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Benchmark report builder queries complete in under 200ms, satisfying the 1-second dashboard query SLA.

target_output.title: National Fleet Analytics Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale fullstack fleet analytics platform on AWS aggregating 50M+ daily telematics, fuel, maintenance, and driver scorecard events into ClickHouse materialized views, serving sub-second KPI dashboard queries to fleet executives via a Next.js frontend and FastAPI analytics backend. Okta OIDC SSO with org-scoped RBAC protects confidential driver and operational data. Nightly batch jobs pre-compute KPI summaries and industry benchmark percentiles into ClickHouse MVs, with active Redis cache invalidation on batch completion.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven analytics architecture: raw telematics events ingested into ClickHouse MergeTree tables partitioned by toYYYYMM(event_date). Nightly ECS Scheduled Task refreshes five pre-computed materialized views (kpi_summaries, driver_scorecards, fuel_consumption_summaries, maintenance_cost_summaries, benchmark_summaries) partitioned by org_unit_id and period, enabling sub-second dashboard queries via partition pruning. On batch completion, the job publishes a fleet-kpis-updated event to Redis pub/sub; FastAPI KpiService subscriber flushes matching org-scoped cache keys. FastAPI API layer validates Okta JWTs, enforces org_unit_id scope, applies 5-minute Redis cache on KPI queries, and executes ClickHouse MV queries via clickhouse-driver. Next.js 14 frontend with Okta next-auth session fetches KPI and scorecard data server-side for fast initial render, with client-side chart interactivity via Recharts.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Recharts for trend charts, react-virtual for 4000-row scorecard virtualization, next-auth for Okta OIDC session. Backend: Python 3.12 FastAPI, clickhouse-driver, asyncpg, WeasyPrint for PDF report generation. OLAP: ClickHouse 24.3 on EC2 r6i.2xlarge with EBS gp3 storage. Transactional: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ. Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache r7g.large. Auth: Okta OIDC with JWKS validation in FastAPI middleware. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, ECS rolling deployments. Observability: Datadog APM and Log Management.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Executive KPI Dashboard: 6 KPI cards (cost_per_mile, idle_time_pct, safety_score, on_time_delivery_pct, fuel_efficiency_mpg, maintenance_cost_per_vehicle) from kpi_summaries MV, 90-day trend sparklines, regional breakdown bar chart filterable by org_unit. Driver Scorecard View: sortable table of 4000 drivers with composite score, harsh_braking_count, speeding_minutes, on_time_pct from driver_scorecards MV; individual scorecard modal. Fuel Trend Charts: 12-month fuel consumption by vehicle class from fuel_consumption_summaries MV with anomaly highlighting. Benchmark Report Builder: metric selector, peer group filter (vehicle_class, fleet_size_tier), comparison against benchmark_summaries percentile MV, PDF export via WeasyPrint. Admin Panel: org unit hierarchy editor, user role assignment, scheduled report management.
target_output.system_components: Next.js 14 ECS Fargate service (2-10 replicas). FastAPI ECS Fargate service (2-20 replicas, CPU autoscaling). ClickHouse EC2 r6i.2xlarge (MergeTree raw tables + 5 materialized views). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (org_units, users, report_schedules). Redis 7 ElastiCache r7g.large (KPI query cache + pub/sub for batch cache invalidation). ECS Scheduled Task nightly_kpi_aggregation (2 AM EST, refreshes all 5 MVs, publishes fleet-kpis-updated to Redis). Datadog Agent sidecar on each ECS task. AWS Secrets Manager for credentials.
target_output.workflows: Nightly KPI Aggregation and Cache Invalidation: ECS Scheduled Task triggers at 2 AM EST. Batch queries ClickHouse raw event tables and inserts pre-aggregated rows into kpi_summaries (partitioned by org_unit_id, period), driver_scorecards, fuel_consumption_summaries, maintenance_cost_summaries. Then computes p25/p50/p75/p90 percentile distributions by vehicle_class and fleet_size_tier and inserts into benchmark_summaries MV. On all inserts complete, publishes 'fleet-kpis-updated:{org_unit_ids_refreshed}' event to Redis pub/sub. FastAPI KpiService Redis subscriber receives event and calls redis.delete on all matching fleet-kpis:{org_unit_id}:* cache keys. Executive Dashboard Query: Authenticated exec loads dashboard, Next.js server component calls FastAPI GET /v1/kpis/summary?org_unit_id={id}&period=90d. FastAPI validates Okta JWT, checks Redis cache, on miss queries kpi_summaries ClickHouse MV with org_unit_id and period partition filter, caches result with 5-minute TTL, returns KPI payload. Benchmark Report: Executive configures report (vehicle_class=semi_truck, fleet_size_tier=large), FastAPI GET /v1/benchmarks queries benchmark_summaries MV for percentile columns, returns comparison table in under 200ms. POST /v1/reports/benchmark triggers WeasyPrint PDF generation as async task, returns report_id.
target_output.data_model: ClickHouse MergeTree (raw): telematics_events (vehicle_id, org_unit_id, event_date, event_type, latitude, longitude, speed_kmh, engine_rpm, fuel_level_pct) partitioned by toYYYYMM(event_date). Materialized Views: kpi_summaries (org_unit_id, vehicle_class, period DATE, cost_per_mile, idle_time_pct, safety_score, on_time_delivery_pct, fuel_efficiency_mpg, maintenance_cost_per_vehicle) partitioned by (org_unit_id, toYYYYMM(period)). driver_scorecards (driver_id, org_unit_id, period, composite_score, harsh_braking_count, speeding_minutes, idle_time_minutes, on_time_pct). fuel_consumption_records (vehicle_id, org_unit_id, event_date, fuel_liters, odometer_km, vehicle_class). maintenance_cost_summaries (vehicle_id, org_unit_id, period, labor_cost, parts_cost, downtime_hours, work_order_type). benchmark_summaries (vehicle_class, fleet_size_tier, metric VARCHAR, period DATE, p25 FLOAT, p50 FLOAT, p75 FLOAT, p90 FLOAT). PostgreSQL: organizational_units (id UUID PK, name, parent_id UUID, region, vehicle_count), users (id UUID PK, okta_sub, role ENUM executive/regional_manager/fleet_admin, org_unit_ids UUID[]), report_schedules (id UUID PK, user_id FK, report_type, cron_expression, last_run_at).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI v1 REST API: GET /v1/kpis/summary?org_unit_id=&period= (Redis cached, ClickHouse kpi_summaries MV). GET /v1/drivers/scorecards?org_unit_id=&period=&page=&page_size= (paginated, driver_scorecards MV). GET /v1/fuel/trends?org_unit_id=&vehicle_class=&months= (fuel_consumption_records MV). GET /v1/benchmarks?vehicle_class=&fleet_size_tier=&metric=&period= (benchmark_summaries MV, p25/p50/p75/p90). POST /v1/reports/benchmark (async PDF via WeasyPrint, returns {report_id}). GET /v1/reports/{id}/download (returns PDF stream). GET /v1/admin/org-units (org unit tree). All routes: Okta JWT required, org_unit_id scope enforced. Rate limit: 300 req/min per authenticated user.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC SSO with next-auth in Next.js; Okta JWKS-validated JWTs in FastAPI middleware. JWT claims include org_unit_ids[] and role; org_unit_id on every ClickHouse query parameter to enforce row-level scope. SOC 2 audit log entry on every API data access: user_id, org_unit_id, endpoint, query_time_range, response_row_count, latency_ms logged to Datadog Log Management with 1-year retention. GDPR: EU driver data flagged by driver_jurisdiction=EU in driver_scorecards; automated deletion job runs monthly for drivers with erasure requests. TLS 1.3 on all connections. Secrets in AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate services: fleet-api (FastAPI, 2-20 replicas, CPU 60% autoscale), fleet-ui (Next.js, 2-10 replicas). EC2 r6i.2xlarge ClickHouse with 2TB EBS gp3; EBS snapshots nightly to S3. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis r7g.large. ECS Scheduled Task nightly_kpi_aggregation: runs at 2 AM EST in us-east-1, retries 2x on ClickHouse failure with 30-minute delay. GitHub Actions CI: lint (ruff for Python, ESLint for Next.js), pytest, Jest, build Docker, push ECR, deploy to staging via rolling update, promote to production on manual approval gate. ClickHouse schema changes via SQL migration scripts run as ECS one-off tasks. Rollback: ECS revision pinning for instant rollback.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: FastAPI traces with ClickHouse query spans tagged by MV vs raw table. Custom metrics: kpi_query_cache_hit_rate, kpi_query_p99_latency_ms, nightly_batch_duration_minutes, benchmark_query_latency_ms, redis_cache_flush_events_per_batch. Datadog Dashboard: all 5 custom metrics plus ECS CPU utilization and ClickHouse merge queue depth. PagerDuty alerts: API p99 >2s (P2), nightly batch missed window >6 AM EST (P2), ClickHouse disk >80% (P3), Redis evictions >100/min (P3).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated monthly: ECS Fargate (API+UI) ~$800, EC2 r6i.2xlarge ClickHouse ~$1,200, RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ ~$600, ElastiCache r7g.large ~$300, data transfer and S3 ~$200, Datadog ~$1,500 = ~$4,600/month well under $8,000 budget. ClickHouse scales vertically to r6i.8xlarge (~$5,000/month) before horizontal sharding at 200M daily events.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (7 weeks): ClickHouse schema with kpi_summaries and driver_scorecards MVs, FastAPI KPI and scorecard endpoints, Next.js executive dashboard with 6 KPI cards and driver scorecard table, Okta SSO, nightly batch with Redis cache invalidation, Datadog monitoring. Phase 2 (5 weeks): Fuel trend charts, benchmark_summaries MV and benchmark report builder, PDF export via WeasyPrint, admin panel for org unit and user management, SOC 2 audit logging. Phase 3 (future): Predictive maintenance ML anomaly detection, driver coaching recommendations, multi-org carrier benchmarking.
target_output.development_guidelines: ClickHouse MV changes require both migration SQL and corresponding FastAPI query updates in the same PR. All FastAPI endpoints must include integration tests against a local ClickHouse Docker instance seeded with 1M test events. BenchmarkService must always query benchmark_summaries MV; direct queries to raw event tables are forbidden in the codebase via an architectural fitness function check in CI. Redis cache keys follow pattern fleet-kpis:{org_unit_id}:{period} for deterministic batch invalidation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse EC2 single-node failure takes down all dashboard queries. Mitigation: EBS snapshot recovery target is 30 minutes; ClickHouse replication to a standby instance planned for phase 2. Risk: Nightly batch takes longer than 4-hour window as event volume grows. Mitigation: incremental MV refresh on new event partitions only; full recompute triggered only on schema changes. Tradeoff: Pre-computed benchmark_summaries MV over real-time percentile queries trades freshness (1-day lag) for consistent sub-second performance at 50M events/day scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Benchmark percentile computation moved to nightly batch MV to resolve ISS-003 query latency. Redis pub/sub cache invalidation pattern chosen over TTL-only strategy to resolve ISS-002 stale data window after batch. ClickHouse chosen over Redshift for columnar storage efficiency on telematics time-series data.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision for fleet analytics platform; resolves ISS-002 Redis cache invalidation and ISS-003 benchmark query path via pre-computed benchmark_summaries MV.

profile.domain: Vehicle Data Access Layer - Dealership Management System API

project_goal.value: Provide a standardized vehicle data access layer for dealership management systems, exposing vehicle inventory, history, recall status, and OBD diagnostic data to third-party dealer tools via a versioned REST API.
project_goal.rationale: Replaces 12 separate dealer tool direct database integrations with a single governed API layer.

target_users.value: Third-party dealership management system vendors integrating vehicle inventory and diagnostic data into their dealer tools.
target_users.rationale: Partner developer audience with API subscription model.

project_class.rationale: Pure backend API service; no frontend UI. FrontendAgent does not apply.

capabilities.rationale: Go Gin REST API, PostgreSQL data store, OAuth 2.0 plus API key auth, versioned public API surface, NHTSA and OEM database integrations.

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-source data aggregation (NHTSA, OEM TSB databases, dealer inventory) with Redis caching and versioned API; moderate complexity.

risk_level.rationale: Partner-facing API with OAuth 2.0; vehicle recall data accuracy has safety implications. NHTSA data handling requirements apply.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vehicle inventory and history are internal dealership business data; not personal or financial data.

external_exposure.rationale: API exposed to vetted third-party dealer tool vendors with signed integration agreements.

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow for dealer tool applications. API key fallback for legacy integrations. Per-dealership_account data scope enforced on all endpoints.
access_model.rationale: OAuth 2.0 client credentials is appropriate for server-to-server partner integrations; API key fallback for existing integrations during migration.

feature_scope.value: Vehicle inventory query by VIN or stock number, vehicle history report, real-time NHTSA recall status lookup by VIN, OBD diagnostic enrichment with TSB references and repair procedures, dealership account management, API subscription tier enforcement.
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for the vehicle data access layer.

mvp_scope.value: Vehicle inventory query, NHTSA recall status integration, and OBD diagnostic enrichment for two pilot dealer tool partners.
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP covers highest-value data surfaces for initial partner onboarding.

security_baseline.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials with JWT access tokens (15-minute expiry). API key HMAC-SHA256 for legacy integrations. TLS 1.2+ required on all connections. Per-dealership_account row-level scoping on all inventory queries. Rate limiting per subscription tier.
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing API security baseline for internal vehicle data.

frontend_stack.value: None; api_service project class with no frontend. Partner developer portal is out of scope.
frontend_stack.rationale: FrontendAgent does not run for api_service project class.

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin framework on AWS ECS Fargate.
backend_stack.rationale: Go provides high throughput and low latency for API gateway workloads; Gin for routing.

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on AWS RDS for vehicle inventory and dealership accounts. Redis 7 on ElastiCache for recall status cache and OBD enrichment cache.
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for relational inventory and account data; Redis for external data caching.

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 behind an Application Load Balancer.
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with autoscaling for variable partner API traffic.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Vehicle inventory data retained for active dealership account duration plus 2 years. OBD diagnostic report data retained 1 year per ISO 15765 data archival guidance. API access logs retained 90 days.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal data retention policy aligned with ISO 15765 OBD-II standards.

future_scope.value: Webhook push notifications for recall status changes on watched VINs, bulk VIN batch query endpoint, OpenAPI 3.0 developer portal with interactive sandbox.
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 partner developer experience enhancements.

constraints.value: Recall status queries must complete in under 500ms including NHTSA feed lookup. OBD enrichment must complete in under 1 second. API versioning must allow v1 and v2 to coexist during migration window.
constraints.rationale: Dealer tool UX latency requirements and partner migration SLA.

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for API request metrics, error rates, and NHTSA integration latency. Structured JSON logging with correlation IDs per request. Alerting on error rate >1% or p99 latency >1s.
observability_baseline.rationale: AWS-native monitoring aligned with infrastructure.

execution_preference.value: API-first development with OpenAPI 3.0 spec committed to repo before implementation. Semantic versioning for API releases.
execution_preference.rationale: OpenAPI spec enables partner SDK generation and contract testing.

project.goal: Standardized vehicle data API for dealership management system partners
project.target_users: Third-party dealer tool vendors

backend.framework: Gin
backend.external_integrations: NHTSA recall API, OEM TSB database API

security.auth: OAuth 2.0 client credentials with JWT, API key fallback

data.cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache for NHTSA recall and OBD enrichment caching

devops.environments: dev, staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: The vehicle data access layer consolidates 12 dealer tool integrations into one governed API, significantly reducing OEM integration maintenance burden. Recall status enrichment is the highest safety-impact feature; accuracy and latency are both critical. The round 1 plan correctly identified the core data surfaces but lacked the NHTSA caching strategy specifics.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go Gin on ECS Fargate is well-matched for a high-throughput partner API. Round 1 ISS-001 identified that the NHTSA recall lookup was making a synchronous upstream call on every request, which risks exceeding the 500ms SLA under load. Redis caching of recall status with a 4-hour TTL (matching NHTSA daily update cadence) resolves this. ISS-002 flagged that OBD enrichment was falling back to raw fault code strings when TSB lookup fails, which is not an acceptable response for dealer tools.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow is correct for server-to-server dealer tool integrations. Per-dealership_account scoping must be enforced at the PostgreSQL query level, not just in application logic, to prevent accidental cross-dealer data leakage. API key migration path from legacy integrations needs explicit deprecation timeline.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 500ms recall SLA requires Redis cache hit for all repeated VIN lookups. Cold cache on first VIN query is acceptable but must be documented. OBD enrichment 1s SLA requires TSB lookup from OEM database to respond in under 700ms to leave headroom for Go processing.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan resolved ISS-001 NHTSA caching partially but did not document the cache key structure or TTL invalidation on NHTSA feed updates. ISS-002 OBD enrichment fallback behavior is still described as returning raw fault codes, which the dealer tools cannot process without TSB context.

backend.service_design: Go Gin service with four handler groups: InventoryHandler (vehicle_listings queries with dealership_account scope), RecallHandler (NHTSA recall lookup with Redis cache), DiagnosticsHandler (OBD fault code ingestion and TSB enrichment), AccountHandler (dealership_account management and API subscription tier). AuthMiddleware validates OAuth 2.0 JWT or HMAC-SHA256 API key on every request.
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/vehicles?vin=&stock_number=&dealership_id= (inventory query). GET /v1/vehicles/{vin}/recalls (NHTSA recall status with Redis cache). POST /v1/diagnostics/enrich (OBD fault codes in, TSB-enriched report out). GET /v1/vehicles/{vin}/history (vehicle history timeline). GET /v2/vehicles/{vin}/recalls (v2 with enhanced recall detail fields, coexists with v1). All routes require Authorization: Bearer JWT or X-API-Key header.
backend.failure_handling: NHTSA upstream timeout (>300ms): serve recall status from Redis stale cache with X-Cache-Stale: true header and staleness age. TSB lookup failure in DiagnosticsHandler: return enriched report with tsb_references: [] and tsb_lookup_status: 'unavailable' with ISO fault code description as fallback text. Circuit breaker on both NHTSA and OEM TSB integrations with 30-second half-open window.
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate autoscales on ALB RequestCountPerTarget (target 500 req/task). Redis handles NHTSA recall cache; PostgreSQL read replica for inventory reporting queries. Rate limiting via token bucket per api_subscription tier: standard 60 rpm, premium 300 rpm.

security.auth_design: OAuth 2.0 client credentials: dealer tool exchanges client_id and client_secret for JWT (15-minute expiry, RS256 signed) from /auth/token endpoint. JWT claims include dealership_account_id[] and subscription_tier. HMAC-SHA256 API key in X-API-Key header validated against hashed key in PostgreSQL api_subscriptions table.
security.authorization_model: Every inventory and history query enforces WHERE dealership_account_id = ANY($jwt_dealership_account_ids) at PostgreSQL query level. Rate limit middleware checks subscription_tier claim. API key requests validated against api_subscriptions.dealership_account_id for same scope enforcement.
security.secrets_management: OAuth 2.0 RS256 signing key stored in AWS Secrets Manager, rotated every 180 days. NHTSA API key and OEM TSB database credentials in Secrets Manager, injected into ECS task via environment secrets.
security.security_risks: Risk: Dealer tool JWT with stale dealership_account_ids after account modification. Mitigation: FastAPI... Go middleware validates dealership_account_id against PostgreSQL on first request per JWT; caches validation result in Redis for JWT lifetime (15 minutes).

data.entities: vehicle_listings (id UUID PK, vin VARCHAR(17), stock_number VARCHAR, dealership_account_id UUID FK, make, model, year INT, mileage_km INT, condition ENUM new/used/cpo, list_price NUMERIC, status ENUM active/sold/pending, listed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). vehicle_histories (id UUID PK, vin VARCHAR(17), event_type ENUM title_transfer/accident/service/recall_repair, event_date DATE, odometer_km INT, reporting_entity VARCHAR, detail_json JSONB). recall_notices (id UUID PK, vin VARCHAR(17), nhtsa_campaign_id VARCHAR, component VARCHAR, description TEXT, remedy_available BOOL, cached_at TIMESTAMPTZ). obd_diagnostic_reports (id UUID PK, vin VARCHAR(17), dealership_account_id UUID FK, fault_codes JSONB, tsb_references JSONB, enriched_at TIMESTAMPTZ). dealership_accounts (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, subscription_tier ENUM standard/premium, api_key_hash VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). api_subscriptions (id UUID PK, dealership_account_id UUID FK, oauth_client_id VARCHAR, rate_limit_rpm INT, active BOOL).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for vehicle_listings, vehicle_histories, dealership_accounts, api_subscriptions, obd_diagnostic_reports. Redis ElastiCache for recall_notices cache keyed by recall-status:{vin} with 4-hour TTL aligned to NHTSA daily feed update cadence. Redis also caches OBD TSB enrichment results at obd-tsb:{fault_codes_hash} with 24-hour TTL.
data.consistency_notes: vehicle_listings updated by dealer tool push via POST /v1/listings/sync; optimistic concurrency via updated_at comparison. recall_notices cache invalidated on NHTSA feed webhook event (Phase 2) or on 4-hour TTL expiry.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate service in us-east-1 behind ALB with TLS termination. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis r7g.medium cluster. Secrets Manager for all credentials. CloudWatch for logs and metrics.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test with 80% coverage gate, golangci-lint, docker build, push to ECR, deploy to staging via ECS rolling update, production deploy on manual approval. OpenAPI spec validated against implementation via oapi-codegen in CI.
devops.observability_stack: AWS CloudWatch structured JSON logs with X-Request-ID correlation header on every response. CloudWatch metrics: recall_cache_hit_rate, nhtsa_upstream_latency_p99, obd_tsb_lookup_success_rate, api_error_rate_by_endpoint. Alarms: error rate >1% triggers SNS notification, p99 latency >1s triggers PagerDuty.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: NHTSA API outage causes recall_status endpoint failures. Mitigation: circuit breaker returns stale Redis cache with X-Cache-Stale header; CloudWatch alarm on nhtsa_circuit_breaker_open metric.

ISS-001.title: NHTSA recall lookup makes synchronous upstream call on every request
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 RecallHandler called NHTSA API synchronously on every GET /v1/vehicles/{vin}/recalls request, creating a dependency on NHTSA response time that routinely exceeded the 500ms SLA under moderate load.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner in round 1.
Added Redis cache with 4-hour TTL; NHTSA called only on cache miss.

ISS-002.title: OBD enrichment returns raw fault codes on TSB lookup failure
ISS-002.detail: When TSB lookup from OEM database fails or times out, DiagnosticsHandler returns raw ISO fault code strings (e.g. P0300) without any descriptive context. Dealer tools cannot surface useful repair guidance from raw codes.

history.note: Flagged by product reviewer in round 1.
Fallback behavior still returns raw fault codes per round 2 plan.

focus_issues.title: OBD enrichment returns raw fault codes on TSB lookup failure
focus_issues.detail: DiagnosticsHandler must return ISO 15765 standardized fault code descriptions as fallback when TSB lookup fails, not raw codes.

latest_recommendations: Implement ISO 15765 standard fault code description lookup as fallback in DiagnosticsHandler when OEM TSB database is unavailable
Cache bundled ISO DTC description table in application memory to guarantee sub-50ms fallback without external dependency
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Vehicle Data Access Layer API - Round 1

convergence_state.reason: ISS-002 unresolved; OBD enrichment fallback inadequate for dealer tool consumption.

previous_audits.summary: Solid Go Gin API architecture with correct OAuth 2.0 client credentials design. NHTSA synchronous call issue (ISS-001) is high severity. OBD fallback inadequacy (ISS-002) impacts dealer tool utility.
strengths: Go Gin on ECS Fargate is appropriate for partner API workload
OAuth 2.0 client credentials with per-dealership scope enforcement is correctly designed
Redis caching strategy for NHTSA recall data is correct approach
concerns: NHTSA synchronous call violates 500ms SLA
OBD raw fault code fallback is not actionable for dealer tools
blocking_issues: ISS-001: NHTSA synchronous call on every recall request
recommendations: Add Redis cache with 4-hour TTL for recall-status:{vin} keys, call NHTSA only on cache miss
Bundle ISO 15765 DTC descriptions in application binary as fallback for TSB lookup failures

issue_updates.title: NHTSA recall synchronous upstream call
issue_updates.detail: RecallHandler called NHTSA API on every request, average NHTSA latency 400ms causes SLA breaches under load

issue_updates.title: OBD enrichment raw fault code fallback
issue_updates.detail: DiagnosticsHandler returned raw P-code strings on TSB failure with no ISO description context

previous_plan.title: Vehicle Data Access Layer API - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Go Gin REST API on ECS Fargate exposing vehicle inventory, recall status, and OBD diagnostic data to dealer tools with OAuth 2.0 client credentials auth and per-dealership scoping.
previous_plan.workflows: Recall status: RecallHandler calls NHTSA API synchronously on every request. OBD enrichment: DiagnosticsHandler enriches with TSB references; on TSB failure returns raw fault code strings.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: OAuth 2.0 client credentials with JWT. Per-dealership_account scoping via WHERE clause. TLS 1.2+.
previous_plan.data_model: vehicle_listings, vehicle_histories, recall_notices, obd_diagnostic_reports, dealership_accounts, api_subscriptions in PostgreSQL.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision targeting ISS-002: OBD enrichment must not return raw ISO fault code strings (e.g. P0300) when TSB lookup fails. The fix is to bundle the ISO 15765 DTC description lookup table in the Go binary as a static embedded asset, so DiagnosticsHandler always returns at minimum a human-readable fault description and standardized repair category even when the OEM TSB database is unreachable. ISS-001 was resolved in round 1 by adding Redis recall cache. The plan should also document the v1/v2 coexistence routing to satisfy the versioning constraint.

fix_report.action_taken: Bundled ISO 15765 DTC description lookup table (approx 15,000 standard fault codes with descriptions and repair categories) as an embedded Go asset using embed.FS. DiagnosticsHandler fallback path now returns iso_description and repair_category from the embedded table when OEM TSB lookup fails, alongside tsb_lookup_status: 'unavailable'. Dealer tools receive actionable diagnostic context in all cases.
fix_report.expected_outcome: OBD enrichment endpoint always returns structured, human-readable fault code context; raw P-code strings are never surfaced to dealer tools even during OEM TSB outages.

target_output.title: Vehicle Data Access Layer API
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 Gin REST API on AWS ECS Fargate providing a standardized vehicle data access layer for dealership management system partners. Exposes vehicle inventory, NHTSA recall status, vehicle history, and OBD diagnostic enrichment to third-party dealer tools via OAuth 2.0 client credentials with per-dealership row-level data scoping. NHTSA recall status cached in Redis with 4-hour TTL. OBD diagnostic enrichment uses OEM TSB database with ISO 15765 DTC embedded fallback for guaranteed structured responses. v1 and v2 API versions coexist during partner migration window.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Go Gin service on ECS Fargate behind an ALB handling four data surfaces: vehicle inventory from PostgreSQL, NHTSA recall status from Redis cache (populated from NHTSA API on cache miss), vehicle history from PostgreSQL, and OBD diagnostic enrichment via OEM TSB database with ISO 15765 embedded fallback. OAuth 2.0 RS256 JWT validated in AuthMiddleware; dealership_account_id claims enforced as PostgreSQL query parameters on all inventory operations. Rate limiting per subscription_tier enforced in a dedicated middleware layer. API versioning via URL prefix (/v1/ and /v2/) with backward-compatible v1 routes maintained during the partner migration window. Redis caches recall status at recall-status:{vin} (4-hour TTL) and OBD TSB results at obd-tsb:{fault_hash} (24-hour TTL).
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22 with Gin 1.9, sqlx for PostgreSQL, go-redis for Redis, embed.FS for ISO DTC table, oapi-codegen for OpenAPI generation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with read replica. Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache r7g.medium. External integrations: NHTSA Recall API (https://api.nhtsa.gov/recalls/recallsByVehicle), OEM TSB database REST API with circuit breaker via go-breaker. Auth: OAuth 2.0 RS256 JWT, HMAC-SHA256 API keys. Hosting: ECS Fargate behind ALB in us-east-1. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Observability: AWS CloudWatch structured JSON logs and metrics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Vehicle Inventory Query: GET /v1/vehicles?vin=&stock_number=&dealership_id= returns active listings scoped to dealer account; supports VIN and stock number lookup with make/model/year/mileage/price fields. NHTSA Recall Status: GET /v1/vehicles/{vin}/recalls returns recall_notices array with nhtsa_campaign_id, component, description, remedy_available; served from Redis cache on hit, NHTSA API on miss. Vehicle History Timeline: GET /v1/vehicles/{vin}/history returns title_transfer, accident, service, and recall_repair events with dates and odometer readings. OBD Diagnostic Enrichment: POST /v1/diagnostics/enrich accepts fault_codes[] array; returns enriched report with tsb_references (repair procedures and part numbers from OEM database) or ISO DTC descriptions as fallback. Dealership Account Management: GET and PATCH /v1/accounts/{id} for subscription tier and API key rotation.
target_output.system_components: Go Gin API service (ECS Fargate, 2-10 replicas, ALB target group). AuthMiddleware (OAuth 2.0 JWT RS256 validation via Okta JWKS or internal key, API key HMAC validation). RateLimitMiddleware (token bucket per dealership_account_id and subscription_tier). InventoryHandler (PostgreSQL sqlx queries with dealership_account_id scope). RecallHandler (Redis cache check, NHTSA API client with 300ms timeout, Redis SET on miss). DiagnosticsHandler (OEM TSB API client with circuit breaker, embedded ISO 15765 DTC lookup table via embed.FS fallback). HistoryHandler (PostgreSQL vehicle_histories query). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (vehicle_listings, vehicle_histories, dealership_accounts, api_subscriptions, obd_diagnostic_reports). Redis 7 ElastiCache (recall status cache, OBD TSB cache). AWS Secrets Manager (OAuth signing key, NHTSA key, OEM TSB credentials). CloudWatch (structured logs and custom metrics).
target_output.workflows: Dealer Tool NHTSA Recall Query: Partner tool sends GET /v1/vehicles/{vin}/recalls with Bearer JWT. AuthMiddleware validates RS256 JWT, extracts dealership_account_id[]. RecallHandler checks Redis key recall-status:{vin}. On hit: returns cached recall_notices with X-Cache: HIT. On miss: calls NHTSA GET /recalls/recallsByVehicle?vin={vin} with 300ms timeout, parses response into recall_notices[], stores in Redis with 4-hour TTL, returns to caller. NHTSA timeout: returns stale Redis value if available with X-Cache-Stale header, or empty recall array with nhtsa_status: 'unavailable'. OBD Diagnostic Enrichment: Diagnostic scan tool sends POST /v1/diagnostics/enrich with {vin, dealership_account_id, fault_codes: ['P0300','U0100']}. DiagnosticsHandler checks Redis obd-tsb:{fault_hash}. On miss: calls OEM TSB REST API with 700ms timeout, parses tsb_references (part numbers, repair procedures). On TSB success: caches result 24 hours, persists obd_diagnostic_reports record, returns enriched report with tsb_references. On TSB failure/timeout: circuit breaker opens, DiagnosticsHandler looks up each fault code in embedded ISO 15765 DTC table, returns report with tsb_references: [], iso_descriptions: [{fault_code, description, repair_category}], tsb_lookup_status: 'unavailable'. Dealer tool always receives structured, actionable data.
