target_output.data_model: vehicle_listings (id UUID PK, vin VARCHAR(17) INDEXED, stock_number VARCHAR, dealership_account_id UUID FK, make VARCHAR, model VARCHAR, year SMALLINT, mileage_km INT, condition ENUM new/used/cpo, list_price NUMERIC(10,2), status ENUM active/sold/pending, listed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). vehicle_histories (id UUID PK, vin VARCHAR(17) INDEXED, event_type ENUM title_transfer/accident/service/recall_repair, event_date DATE, odometer_km INT, reporting_entity VARCHAR, detail_json JSONB). recall_notices (vin, nhtsa_campaign_id, component, description, remedy_available, cached_at) - Redis hash at recall-status:{vin}, 4-hour TTL. obd_diagnostic_reports (id UUID PK, vin VARCHAR(17), dealership_account_id UUID FK, fault_codes JSONB, tsb_references JSONB, iso_descriptions JSONB, tsb_lookup_status VARCHAR, enriched_at TIMESTAMPTZ). dealership_accounts (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, subscription_tier ENUM standard/premium, api_key_hash BYTEA, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). api_subscriptions (id UUID PK, dealership_account_id UUID FK, oauth_client_id VARCHAR, rate_limit_rpm INT, active BOOL).
target_output.api_design: REST API v1 and v2 on ECS Fargate (ALB path routing). GET /v1/vehicles?vin=&stock_number=&dealership_id=&page=&page_size= (inventory query, 20 results default, cursor pagination). GET /v1/vehicles/{vin}/recalls (recall status, Redis cached). GET /v2/vehicles/{vin}/recalls (v2 adds recall_risk_score and component_category fields alongside v1 fields). GET /v1/vehicles/{vin}/history (vehicle history events timeline). POST /v1/diagnostics/enrich body: {vin, fault_codes[]} returns {enriched_report, tsb_references[], iso_descriptions[], tsb_lookup_status}. GET /v1/accounts/{id} (dealership account details). POST /auth/token body: {client_id, client_secret, grant_type: client_credentials} returns {access_token, expires_in: 900}. All routes: TLS 1.2+, Authorization header required. Rate limits enforced per subscription_tier in response headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset).
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth 2.0 client credentials: dealer tools exchange client_id and client_secret for RS256 JWT (15-minute expiry) at POST /auth/token. AuthMiddleware validates signature via embedded public key (rotated via Secrets Manager). dealership_account_id[] claim enforced as WHERE dealership_account_id = ANY($1) parameter in all PostgreSQL inventory queries. HMAC-SHA256 API key in X-API-Key header validated against bcrypt hash in api_subscriptions for legacy integrations. NHTSA recall data handling: recall_notices served with X-Data-Source: NHTSA-API header; stale cache responses include staleness_seconds field per NHTSA recall data accuracy requirements. ISO 15765 compliance: OBD enrichment responses include obd_standard: 'ISO-15765-4' field. TLS 1.2 minimum enforced at ALB listener level. API access logs with correlation IDs retained 90 days in CloudWatch Logs.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate service (2-10 replicas, ALB RequestCountPerTarget autoscale at 500 req/task). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with read replica for history queries. ElastiCache Redis r7g.medium. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: golangci-lint, go test -race -coverprofile (80% gate), oapi-codegen schema validation, docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy to staging, production on manual approval. Database migrations via golang-migrate run as ECS one-off task before deploy. API v1 and v2 coexist on same service via Gin route groups gin.Group('/v1') and gin.Group('/v2'); v1 deprecated header added to all v1 responses during migration window. Secrets rotated every 180 days via Secrets Manager rotation Lambda.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch structured JSON logs: every request logged with request_id, dealership_account_id, endpoint, status_code, latency_ms, cache_status (HIT/MISS/STALE), nhtsa_upstream_latency_ms, tsb_lookup_status. Custom CloudWatch metrics: recall_cache_hit_rate, nhtsa_p99_latency_ms, obd_tsb_success_rate, obd_iso_fallback_rate, api_error_rate_4xx, api_error_rate_5xx. CloudWatch Alarms: error rate >1% (SNS email), p99 latency >1s (PagerDuty), NHTSA circuit breaker open >5 min (PagerDuty), obd_tsb_success_rate <90% (SNS warning).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated monthly: ECS Fargate ~$300, RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ ~$400, ElastiCache r7g.medium ~$150, data transfer ~$50 = ~$900/month at MVP scale. NHTSA recall cache hit rate expected >90% for active inventory VINs, minimizing external API costs. ECS autoscaling handles partner traffic spikes; PostgreSQL read replica offloads vehicle history queries.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (5 weeks): Vehicle inventory query with OAuth 2.0 and dealership scoping, NHTSA recall lookup with Redis cache, OBD diagnostic enrichment with ISO DTC fallback, /auth/token endpoint, CloudWatch observability. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Vehicle history endpoint, v2 recall endpoint with recall_risk_score, API key migration path for legacy integrations, rate limiting per subscription tier, OpenAPI developer portal. Phase 3 (future): Webhook push for recall status changes on watched VINs, bulk VIN batch endpoint, interactive sandbox environment.
target_output.development_guidelines: OpenAPI 3.0 spec committed to repo before implementation; oapi-codegen generates Go handler interfaces. All external API calls (NHTSA, OEM TSB) wrapped in circuit breaker with explicit fallback behavior documented in code. ISO 15765 DTC table embedded via Go embed.FS; table updates require PR with updated dtc_descriptions.json and regression test on a 500-code sample. PostgreSQL queries must include dealership_account_id as first WHERE parameter to ensure index use on composite index (dealership_account_id, vin).
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: NHTSA API returns inconsistent recall data during their weekly feed refresh window (Tuesday nights EST). Mitigation: 4-hour Redis TTL with stale-serve fallback avoids surfacing inconsistent data; X-Cache-Stale header allows dealer tools to display data freshness. Risk: OEM TSB database schema changes break enrichment parsing. Mitigation: TSB response parsed with permissive JSON unmarshaling; fallback to ISO DTC table on parse error. Tradeoff: Embedded ISO 15765 DTC table (~2MB binary size increase) over runtime database lookup eliminates TSB fallback network dependency at the cost of binary rebuild for DTC table updates.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISO 15765 DTC table embedded in Go binary via embed.FS to guarantee zero-latency fallback without runtime database dependency. v1/v2 coexistence implemented via Gin route groups on the same service, not separate deployments, to simplify operations during migration window. PostgreSQL chosen over DynamoDB for vehicle inventory due to complex dealership-scoped queries requiring relational filtering.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for vehicle data access layer API; resolves ISS-002 OBD enrichment fallback by embedding ISO 15765 DTC descriptions in Go binary.

project.goal: Static reference site for fleet ML model documentation and benchmark leaderboards
project.target_users: ML researchers and fleet engineers

frontend.styling: Custom CSS variables for fleet brand colors

backend.framework: Static site generation only

security.auth: None — public read access
security.transport: HTTPS enforced, CSP headers via Firebase Hosting configuration

data.db: None — JSON files in repository for benchmark tables

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for lint, build, and deploy
devops.environments: PR preview channels + production main branch

reasoner_reviews.product: The documentation site scope is well-defined: model cards, benchmark leaderboards from JSON, and reproducibility guides cover the full research reference use case. MDX in Docusaurus is the right choice for embedding code snippets and model performance tables alongside narrative text.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Docusaurus with Firebase Hosting is the correct minimal stack. The critical architectural decision is treating benchmark results as version-controlled JSON rather than a database, which gives researchers a full audit trail and allows leaderboard diffs in pull requests. Build pipeline should validate YAML front matter and JSON schema on every PR.
reasoner_reviews.security: Threat model for a public read-only static site is primarily content integrity. Protect the GitHub repository with branch protection, require PR reviews before merging to main, and enforce HTTPS with CSP headers on Firebase Hosting. No user authentication surface to protect.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Firebase Hosting free tier supports expected traffic for a research documentation site. Build under 5 minutes is achievable with Docusaurus. No runtime dependencies keep operational complexity at zero.

frontend.app_structure: Docusaurus site with docs/ for model documentation, leaderboards/ for benchmark tables auto-generated from JSON, guides/ for reproducibility walkthroughs, and blog/ for research announcements
frontend.pages_and_flows: Model Documentation pages with YAML front matter metadata, Benchmark Leaderboard pages rendering result tables from JSON data files, Dataset Cards for training data provenance, Reproducibility Guide pages with step-by-step shell commands and expected output hashes
frontend.performance_notes: Static HTML pre-rendered at build time; Docusaurus lazy-loads JS bundles per route; images served via Firebase CDN with WebP format; Algolia DocSearch for zero-latency search without server round trips
frontend.frontend_risks: Leaderboard tables must be auto-generated from JSON to prevent manual transcription errors; enforce via CI validation that regenerates tables and fails the build if output differs from committed HTML

security.auth_design: No authentication — fully public read access
security.secrets_management: No secrets in the static site; Firebase deploy token stored in GitHub Actions encrypted secret FIREBASE_TOKEN with read-only hosting scope
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Firebase Hosting access logs retained for 30 days; GA4 anonymized event logs for content engagement tracking
security.security_risks: Content defacement via compromised GitHub contributor account; mitigated by branch protection requiring 1 reviewer approval and signed commits enforcement

data.entities: model_documentation (slug, title, model_type ENUM classification/regression/reinforcement_learning, framework, primary_task, authors[], published_at, status), benchmark_leaderboards (benchmark_id, benchmark_name, metric, evaluation_dataset, results[] each with model_slug, score, params_millions, inference_ms, submitted_at), dataset_cards (dataset_id, name, source_url, size_gb, license SPDX, linked_model_slugs[]), reproducibility_guides (guide_id, model_slug, hardware_requirements, steps[], expected_output_hash SHA256)
data.storage_design: All entities stored as MDX files or JSON in the Git repository; no database; Docusaurus build pipeline reads JSON files to generate leaderboard table components at build time
data.retention_and_deletion: Content retained indefinitely in Git history; deprecated model entries marked with status: deprecated in YAML front matter rather than deleted

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub main branch triggers Firebase Hosting deploy via GitHub Actions; PR branches deploy to unique Firebase preview channel URLs for review
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions steps: (1) validate YAML front matter with ajv-cli against model-doc schema, (2) validate benchmark JSON against leaderboard schema, (3) bun run build Docusaurus, (4) firebase deploy --only hosting to production or PR preview channel
devops.environments: production (main branch, stable URL), PR previews (ephemeral Firebase channels expiring after 7 days)
devops.devops_risks: Docusaurus version upgrades can break MDX compatibility; pin to minor version and review upgrade PRs against full build verification

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a pure static site for fleet ML research documentation with no server-side runtime. Key decisions: Docusaurus for structured technical docs with MDX support, benchmark data as version-controlled JSON generating leaderboard tables at build time, Firebase Hosting for zero-ops CDN delivery. The architecture is intentionally minimal — complexity would be counterproductive for a content-first research reference site.
target_output.title: Fleet ML Research Documentation Site Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Docusaurus 3 static site serving as the canonical reference for driver behavior classification and route optimization ML models. Model documentation, benchmark leaderboard tables, dataset cards, and reproducibility guides are authored as MDX and version-controlled JSON, built by GitHub Actions, and served globally via Firebase Hosting CDN. No server-side runtime, no database, no authentication surface.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static architecture: Docusaurus 3 reads MDX content files and JSON benchmark data, generates pre-rendered HTML at build time, and GitHub Actions deploys the output to Firebase Hosting. Each pull request deploys to an ephemeral Firebase preview channel URL so reviewers can inspect rendered documentation before merging. The benchmark leaderboard tables are generated programmatically from structured JSON files committed alongside documentation, ensuring every published number is traceable to a specific git commit. GA4 with IP anonymization provides page engagement metrics without collecting PII.
target_output.technology_stack: Static Generator: Docusaurus 3 with React 18 and TypeScript. Content format: MDX for documentation and guides, schema-validated JSON files for benchmark results. Search: Algolia DocSearch crawling the Firebase Hosting domain. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with ajv-cli for schema validation, bun for builds. Hosting: Firebase Hosting with global CDN and automatic HTTPS. Analytics: Google Analytics 4 with anonymized IP measurement protocol.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Model Documentation: per-model pages with YAML front matter specifying model_type, task, framework, published_at, and authors. Benchmark Leaderboards: auto-generated tables from JSON files showing score, parameter count, inference latency, and submission date per model. Dataset Cards: provenance pages with source, size, license, and linked models. Reproducibility Guides: step-by-step instructions with hardware requirements, environment setup commands, and expected output hashes. Research Announcements: blog section for milestone publications.
target_output.system_components: GitHub Repository as single source of truth for all MDX content and JSON benchmark data. GitHub Actions CI pipeline for schema validation, Docusaurus build, and Firebase deploy. Firebase Hosting with global CDN serving pre-rendered HTML, JS bundles, and static assets. Firebase Preview Channels for PR review environments. Algolia DocSearch crawler indexing published documentation for site search. Google Analytics 4 for anonymized traffic measurement.
target_output.workflows: Documentation Publish: Researcher opens PR adding or updating an MDX model documentation file; GitHub Actions validates YAML front matter against the model-doc schema, builds the site, and deploys to a Firebase preview channel; reviewer inspects the rendered page at the preview URL and approves; merge to main triggers production deploy, making content live globally within 3 minutes. Benchmark Update: Researcher appends a new result object to the relevant benchmark JSON file; CI validates the entry against the leaderboard JSON schema (required: model_slug, score, params_millions, submitted_at), rebuilds leaderboard tables, and deploys; updated ranking is publicly visible immediately after merge.
target_output.data_model: model_documentation (slug STRING PK, title, model_type ENUM classification/regression/reinforcement_learning, framework, primary_task, authors[], published_at DATE, status ENUM active/deprecated) — stored as MDX front matter. benchmark_leaderboards (benchmark_id, benchmark_name, metric, evaluation_dataset, results[] each containing model_slug REF, score FLOAT, params_millions INT, inference_ms INT, submitted_at DATE) — stored as JSON files. dataset_cards (dataset_id, name, source_url, size_gb FLOAT, license SPDX STRING, linked_model_slugs[]) — stored as MDX. reproducibility_guides (guide_id, model_slug REF, hardware_requirements STRING, steps[], expected_output_hash SHA256) — stored as MDX.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Static asset endpoints via Firebase Hosting CDN: GET /docs/{model-slug} returns pre-rendered model documentation page, GET /leaderboards/{benchmark-id} returns benchmark leaderboard page, GET /datasets/{dataset-id} returns dataset card, GET /guides/{guide-id} returns reproducibility guide. All responses are pre-rendered HTML with zero dynamic server involvement. Search queries handled client-side by Algolia DocSearch JavaScript SDK.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced globally via Firebase Hosting automatic TLS provisioning. Content-Security-Policy header in firebase.json restricts script-src to self and Algolia CDN domains. GitHub branch protection on main requires at least 1 reviewer approval, preventing unauthorized content changes. Firebase deploy token scoped to hosting-only permissions stored as GitHub Actions encrypted secret. No user authentication surface, no session cookies. GA4 IP anonymization enabled to comply with EU analytics guidance for public-facing sites.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions workflow triggered on push to any branch: (1) bun install, (2) ajv-cli validate content/leaderboards/*.json against schemas/leaderboard.schema.json, (3) ajv-cli validate MDX front matter YAML against schemas/model-doc.schema.json, (4) bun run build invoking Docusaurus static export, (5) firebase deploy --only hosting --project fleet-ml-docs. PR trigger adds --channel-id pr-{NUMBER} for ephemeral preview. Main branch trigger deploys to production. Build time target under 3 minutes. Rollback: revert the PR commit and re-merge, triggering a fresh deploy.
target_output.observability: Google Analytics 4 custom events: page_view on all documentation pages, scroll_depth milestones on reproducibility guides, search_query events from Algolia DocSearch. Firebase Hosting dashboard tracks bandwidth consumption and CDN cache hit ratio. GitHub Actions workflow duration tracked per run to catch build regressions. Key signals: most-viewed model documentation pages identifying high-interest research, leaderboard page engagement rates, guide scroll completion indicating reproducibility success.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Firebase Hosting free tier provides 10 GB storage and 360 MB/day transfer, sufficient for research site traffic volumes. Algolia DocSearch is free for open documentation projects. GitHub Actions covers build minutes within free tier. Total cost: zero dollars at typical academic documentation traffic. Cost increases only if sustained viral traffic exceeds Firebase Spark plan limits, handled by upgrading to Blaze pay-as-you-go at approximately $0.026 per GB over limit.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (2 weeks): Docusaurus site structure and theming, model documentation pages for top 5 driver behavior classification models, one benchmark leaderboard auto-generated from JSON, GitHub Actions CI pipeline with schema validation and Firebase deploy to production and preview channels. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Dataset cards for all training datasets, reproducibility guides for top-3 models with expected output hashes, Algolia DocSearch integration, blog section. Phase 3 (ongoing): Route optimization model documentation, custom interactive leaderboard comparison components using Recharts, automated benchmark ingestion from CI experiment runs.
target_output.development_guidelines: All benchmark result entries must be added via PR — direct commits to main blocked by branch protection requiring at least 1 reviewer approval. YAML front matter validated against schema in CI before merge. MDX files must pass markdownlint rules enforced in pre-merge check. Benchmark JSON files sorted by score descending to keep diffs readable. Docusaurus version pinned to minor version; upgrades require team decision and full build verification before merging.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Manually entered benchmark numbers could be transcribed incorrectly. Mitigation: Leaderboards generated exclusively from JSON results file; humans never hand-edit leaderboard HTML, enforced by CI diff check. Risk: Algolia DocSearch crawler may index stale content after rapid updates. Mitigation: Manual Algolia re-crawl triggered via webhook after major content additions. Tradeoff: MDX-in-git over a headless CMS sacrifices non-technical contributor UX but gains full version history and CI validation on every change.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Docusaurus over Hugo or Jekyll for native React component support, enabling future interactive benchmark comparison widgets. Confirmed JSON-in-git over a database for benchmark data to provide git-diff visibility on every result submission. Selected Firebase Hosting over GitHub Pages for preview channel support enabling per-PR review workflows.

metadata.notes: Fleet ML research static documentation site with CI-driven benchmark leaderboards and reproducibility guides on Firebase Hosting.

project_goal.value: Ingest vehicle GPS tracking data with end-to-end encryption and data masking to protect driver location PII from unauthorized exposure
project_goal.rationale: Core business need driven by GDPR compliance requirements for fleet operators

target_users.value: Fleet operations engineers and security team members who access aggregated routing data, never raw driver PII
target_users.rationale: Internal consumers with role-based access to pseudonymized data

project_class.rationale: Ingestion, transformation, and storage pipeline for GPS telemetry with no user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: Pipeline needs data storage, batch processing, GPS device integration, and deployment

complexity_level.rationale: KMS key management, column-level encryption, GDPR pseudonymization, and real-time ingestion add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Driver location data is sensitive PII under GDPR; breach would trigger mandatory notification obligations

data_sensitivity.rationale: GPS tracks linked to individual drivers constitute personal data under GDPR Article 4

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline endpoints are within private VPC; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: IAM roles with least privilege; no direct database access for application services; KMS key policies restrict decryption to authorized service principals only
access_model.rationale: GDPR Article 32 mandates access controls proportionate to the risk

feature_scope.value: MQTT ingestion from GPS devices, driver identity tokenization via KMS, column-level PostgreSQL encryption for gps_track_points, immutable audit trail for all data access, nightly anomaly detection scan on access logs
feature_scope.rationale: Full security-hardened pipeline feature set

mvp_scope.value: MQTT ingestion with TLS, KMS-based driver tokenization, encrypted storage in PostgreSQL, basic audit logging to CloudWatch
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimal viable pipeline to replace unencrypted predecessor before compliance deadline

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 on all MQTT connections, KMS CMK encryption at rest, column-level encryption for driver_identity fields, IAM role-based access, VPC private subnets only
security_baseline.rationale: GDPR Article 32 minimum technical controls for personal data processing

frontend_stack.value: None — data pipeline with no user interface
frontend_stack.rationale: Internal data pipeline does not expose a frontend

backend_stack.value: Rust with Tokio async runtime for high-throughput MQTT ingestion and tokenization
backend_stack.rationale: Rust provides memory safety guarantees and throughput needed for GPS event processing

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with column-level encryption via pgcrypto extension
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL pgcrypto enables per-column AES encryption with KMS-managed key material

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in private VPC subnets in eu-west-1
hosting_target.rationale: EU data residency required for GDPR; private VPC eliminates public internet exposure

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw GPS track points retained for 90 days then deleted; aggregated route summaries retained for 2 years; driver identity tokens rotated annually; deletion verified via audit record
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR data minimization principle requires limiting retention to necessary duration

future_scope.value: Real-time geofence alerting, driver behavior scoring pipeline, GDPR data subject access request automation
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 capabilities building on the secure foundation

constraints.value: All processing in eu-west-1; no data transfer outside EU; pipeline must handle 10K GPS events per second at peak; no third-party data processors without DPA
constraints.rationale: GDPR data transfer restrictions and performance SLA from fleet operations

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for pipeline metrics and alarms; AWS Security Hub for compliance posture findings; structured JSON logs with correlation IDs to CloudWatch Logs
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; Security Hub provides automated GDPR-relevant findings

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with security review gates at each phase; no phase proceeds without sign-off from security team
execution_preference.rationale: High-risk personal data processing warrants formal security checkpoints

compliance_context.value: GDPR Articles 25 and 32 (data protection by design, encryption at rest and in transit), UK GDPR, CCPA for California-registered drivers; DPA required for all sub-processors

project.goal: Security-hardened GPS tracking data pipeline with driver PII protection
project.target_users: Fleet ops engineers and security team

backend.framework: Custom MQTT ingestion service and tokenization service

security.auth: IAM roles with KMS key policies
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 on MQTT, AES-256 column-level encryption, KMS CMK

data.object_store: S3 with SSE-KMS for audit exports

devops.environments: dev, staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: The pipeline scope is well-bounded for GDPR compliance: ingest, tokenize, store encrypted, audit. The critical missing element in the initial design is a documented data subject deletion workflow — GDPR Article 17 right to erasure must be architecturally supported, not bolted on later.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust with Tokio is the correct choice for 10K events/second MQTT ingestion with memory safety guarantees. The tokenization step must happen before any persistence — no plaintext driver identifiers should ever touch the database. The flawed aspect is that the proposed architecture writes raw GPS coordinates to a staging table before tokenization, creating a GDPR violation window.
reasoner_reviews.security: Column-level encryption with pgcrypto is sound but requires careful KMS key policy design. The audit trail implementation must be append-only — using PostgreSQL triggers is insufficient since a database admin could truncate the table. Recommend immutable audit export to S3 with object lock.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: eu-west-1 constraint is compatible with ECS Fargate and RDS PostgreSQL. 10K events/second requires Tokio worker thread pool sizing and connection pool tuning. The GDPR data transfer restriction means no CloudFront or any AWS global service that might cache data outside EU.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The staging table pattern before tokenization is architecturally dangerous for a GDPR-regulated pipeline. Tokenization must be synchronous and in-memory before any write operation. This is a design flaw that will block compliance sign-off.

backend.service_design: Two Rust services: ingest_service (MQTT subscriber with TLS, in-memory tokenization, batch write to PostgreSQL) and audit_scanner (nightly batch job scanning access logs against policy rules)
backend.api_patterns: Internal only: ingest_service writes via parameterized PostgreSQL COPY for bulk inserts; no HTTP API surface exposed
backend.failure_handling: MQTT connection drop triggers exponential backoff reconnect; failed tokenization events routed to dead-letter S3 bucket with alert; database connection pool exhaustion returns NACK to MQTT broker
backend.scaling_notes: Horizontal scaling via ECS task count; each ingest_service task maintains independent MQTT subscriber connection; PostgreSQL connection pool shared via PgBouncer sidecar
backend.backend_risks: KMS API rate limits could throttle tokenization at sustained 10K events/second; design requires batched KMS GenerateDataKey calls with local key caching per session

security.auth_design: ECS task IAM roles with KMS decrypt permissions scoped to specific CMK ARNs; no long-lived credentials; RDS access via IAM authentication token
security.authorization_model: Service-level IAM roles only; no human access to production database allowed except via Break Glass procedure with dual-approval and automatic audit alert
security.secrets_management: KMS CMK for data encryption keys; RDS IAM auth token for database connection; no secrets in environment variables or container images
security.privacy_controls: Driver identity replaced with KMS-generated pseudonymous token before any persistence; re-identification requires KMS decrypt permission which is restricted to authorized services only
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All data access logged to immutable audit_events table; nightly export to S3 bucket with Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode preventing deletion for 7 years; Security Hub findings monitored for anomalous IAM activity
security.security_risks: Staging table containing plaintext driver data before tokenization is a critical GDPR violation risk — must be eliminated from the design

data.entities: gps_track_points (id UUID, driver_token BYTEA encrypted, vehicle_id, latitude_enc BYTEA, longitude_enc BYTEA, speed_kmh, timestamp, ingested_at), driver_identities (driver_token BYTEA PK, kms_key_id, created_at, rotated_at, gdpr_deletion_requested_at), data_access_policies (policy_id, principal_arn, allowed_operations[], resource_pattern, effective_from, expires_at), encryption_key_metadata (key_alias, kms_key_arn, created_at, rotation_due_at, status ENUM active/rotating/retired), audit_events (id UUID, principal, action, resource_type, resource_id, timestamp, source_ip, outcome ENUM allowed/denied), masking_rules (rule_id, field_path, masking_strategy ENUM tokenize/redact/generalize, applicable_roles[])
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS in eu-west-1 private subnet; latitude_enc and longitude_enc columns encrypted with pgcrypto using per-row data keys wrapped by KMS CMK; driver_token column uses KMS-generated pseudonymous identifier; no plaintext PII in any table
data.retention_and_deletion: Automated Airflow DAG deletes gps_track_points older than 90 days nightly; GDPR erasure request sets gdpr_deletion_requested_at on driver_identities and triggers cascade deletion job within 72 hours; deletion completion logged to audit_events

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate tasks in private subnets with no public IP; MQTT broker (EMQX on ECS) in private subnet receiving from GPS devices via AWS PrivateLink; RDS PostgreSQL in isolated subnet; all traffic via VPC endpoints only
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: cargo clippy, cargo test, cargo build --release, Docker push to ECR, ECS rolling deploy with health check validation; deployment blocked if Security Hub has active critical findings
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch custom metrics for events_per_second, tokenization_latency_ms, kms_api_calls_per_minute, dead_letter_queue_depth; Security Hub automated standards for CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark
devops.devops_risks: ECS task replacement during rolling deploy must drain existing MQTT connections gracefully; requires SIGTERM handler with 30-second drain window in ingest_service

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a GDPR-compliant GPS tracking data pipeline in Rust on AWS ECS Fargate. The critical architectural flaw to acknowledge: the initial design included a staging table with plaintext driver data before tokenization. This is a GDPR Article 25 violation — tokenization must be synchronous and in-memory before any database write. The plan intentionally preserves this flaw as a flawed training example. Other design decisions: Rust with Tokio for 10K events/second throughput, KMS CMK for key material, pgcrypto for column-level encryption, append-only audit export to S3 with Object Lock.
target_output.title: Security-Hardened Fleet GPS Tracking Data Pipeline Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Rust-based MQTT ingestion pipeline processing 10K GPS events per second with KMS-managed driver pseudonymization and PostgreSQL column-level encryption for GDPR compliance. GPS track points are tokenized in memory before any persistence, stored in encrypted columns, and all access logged to an immutable audit trail exported to S3 with Object Lock. Note: the initial design contains a staging table flaw where plaintext GPS data is persisted before tokenization, which violates GDPR Article 25 data-protection-by-design requirements.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven pipeline: GPS devices publish to EMQX MQTT broker over TLS 1.3 within a private VPC; the Rust ingest_service subscribes, performs in-memory driver tokenization via AWS KMS GenerateDataKey, encrypts latitude and longitude fields, and bulk-writes to PostgreSQL 16 via COPY. A staging_raw table briefly holds plaintext GPS events before the tokenization step writes to gps_track_points — this staging pattern is architecturally incorrect for GDPR compliance and creates a PII exposure window. The audit_scanner batch service runs nightly, comparing data access events against masking_rules and alerting Security Hub on anomalous bulk exports or out-of-hours access patterns.
target_output.technology_stack: Ingestion Runtime: Rust 1.78 with Tokio async runtime, rumqttc MQTT client, aws-sdk-kms for tokenization. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS with pgcrypto extension for column-level AES-256 encryption. Key Management: AWS KMS with CMK in eu-west-1. Batch Jobs: Rust binary as ECS scheduled task for audit scanning and retention deletion. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, RDS in isolated subnets, VPC endpoints for KMS and S3, PgBouncer for connection pooling. Monitoring: CloudWatch custom metrics, AWS Security Hub.
target_output.functional_feature_map: MQTT Ingestion: TLS 1.3 connection from GPS devices to EMQX broker, subscriber pool in ingest_service consuming events at up to 10K/second. Driver Tokenization: KMS GenerateDataKey produces per-session data key; driver_id field replaced with BYTEA token before persistence. Column Encryption: latitude_enc and longitude_enc stored as pgcrypto-encrypted BYTEA; decryption requires KMS decrypt IAM permission. Audit Trail: every SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE on gps_track_points logged to audit_events; nightly S3 export with Object Lock COMPLIANCE 7-year retention. Retention Enforcement: nightly Airflow DAG deletes gps_track_points older than 90 days and processes GDPR erasure queue.
target_output.system_components: EMQX MQTT Broker on ECS Fargate in private subnet handling device connections. Rust ingest_service ECS tasks with PgBouncer sidecar for connection pooling. AWS KMS CMK for driver tokenization key material with per-region key in eu-west-1. RDS PostgreSQL 16 with pgcrypto in isolated private subnet. S3 bucket with Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode for immutable audit archive. Rust audit_scanner ECS scheduled task for anomaly detection. AWS Security Hub for compliance posture monitoring. CloudWatch for pipeline metrics and operational alarms.
target_output.workflows: GPS Event Ingestion: GPS device publishes JSON event to EMQX broker; ingest_service receives via MQTT subscriber; driver_id extracted and passed to KMS tokenization call; result token written to staging_raw table alongside plaintext coordinates (FLAWED: this staging step persists PII before encryption); a second step encrypts coordinates and writes to gps_track_points; staging_raw row deleted — this two-step pattern creates a GDPR violation window. Audit Anomaly Scan: audit_scanner ECS task triggered nightly at 02:00 UTC; loads audit_events from the past 24 hours; joins against data_access_policies to identify accesses that exceed normal query volume thresholds or occur outside declared business hours; anomalous events published as Security Hub findings with severity HIGH.
target_output.data_model: gps_track_points (id UUID PK, driver_token BYTEA NOT NULL, vehicle_id UUID FK, latitude_enc BYTEA, longitude_enc BYTEA, speed_kmh SMALLINT, event_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ). staging_raw (id UUID PK, driver_id VARCHAR plaintext, latitude DECIMAL, longitude DECIMAL, speed_kmh SMALLINT, received_at TIMESTAMPTZ) — GDPR risk: contains plaintext PII. driver_identities (driver_token BYTEA PK, kms_key_id VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, rotated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, gdpr_deletion_requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ). data_access_policies (policy_id UUID PK, principal_arn VARCHAR, allowed_operations TEXT[], resource_pattern VARCHAR, effective_from DATE, expires_at DATE). encryption_key_metadata (key_alias VARCHAR PK, kms_key_arn VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, rotation_due_at DATE, status ENUM). audit_events (id UUID PK, principal VARCHAR, action VARCHAR, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id UUID, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, source_ip INET, outcome ENUM allowed/denied). masking_rules (rule_id UUID PK, field_path VARCHAR, masking_strategy ENUM tokenize/redact/generalize, applicable_roles TEXT[]).
target_output.api_design: No external HTTP API. Internal interfaces: ingest_service exposes health check GET /health on port 8080 for ECS task health checks only. PostgreSQL COPY protocol for bulk insert of gps_track_points batches. KMS API calls: GenerateDataKey for tokenization, Decrypt for authorized access. Internal admin: POST /admin/erasure-request {driver_token} triggers GDPR deletion job (accessible only from VPN). CloudWatch PutMetricData for custom pipeline metrics from ingest_service.
target_output.security_and_compliance: GDPR Article 25 design flaw: staging_raw table persists plaintext driver_id and coordinates before tokenization; this violates data-protection-by-design and must be corrected in revision (tokenization must be in-memory before any write). GDPR Article 32 controls: TLS 1.3 on all MQTT connections, AES-256 column-level encryption via pgcrypto, KMS CMK key rotation annually, IAM least-privilege roles. Audit logging: all gps_track_points access logged to audit_events with append-only S3 export using Object Lock COMPLIANCE 7-year retention for GDPR accountability. Break Glass procedure requires dual approval and triggers automatic Security Hub alert. UK GDPR and CCPA compliance via same technical controls.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate tasks in eu-west-1 private subnets only; no public IP assigned to any task. RDS in isolated subnet accessible only via VPC security group from ingest_service task IAM role. EMQX broker in private subnet with PrivateLink endpoint for GPS devices. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: cargo clippy (zero warnings gate), cargo test (unit + integration), Docker build and push to ECR, ECS rolling deploy with 30-second SIGTERM drain window. Deployment blocked if Security Hub has active critical or high findings. Secrets: no environment variable secrets; RDS IAM auth token for DB connection; KMS CMK referenced by ARN in task role policy. Environments: dev (single AZ, synthetic GPS data), staging (multi-AZ, replicated from production schema), production (multi-AZ, eu-west-1a/1b, ECS auto-scaling 3-12 tasks on CPU 70% threshold).
target_output.observability: CloudWatch custom metrics namespace FleetGPS: events_ingested_per_second (target 10K at peak), tokenization_latency_p99_ms (alert >50ms), kms_api_calls_per_minute (alert if approaching KMS quota), dead_letter_queue_depth (alert >0). Security Hub findings dashboard for CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark compliance score. CloudWatch Logs Insights queries for failed MQTT authentication attempts. Alarm: dead_letter_queue_depth > 0 triggers PagerDuty P1 alert to on-call engineer within 5 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: 3-12 tasks auto-scaling on CPU 70%, estimated $180/month at baseline. RDS PostgreSQL db.r6g.xlarge multi-AZ: $320/month. KMS API calls at 10K events/second with batched GenerateDataKey: approximately 1M calls/day at $0.03/10K = $3/day. S3 Object Lock audit archive: $0.023/GB/month. Total estimated $600-800/month. Scaling ceiling: 20 ECS tasks handles 80K events/second; beyond that requires EMQX cluster sharding.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): MQTT ingestion service with TLS, KMS tokenization (with acknowledged staging_raw flaw to be fixed in Phase 2), PostgreSQL encrypted storage, basic audit logging to CloudWatch, ECS Fargate deployment in eu-west-1. Security sign-off required before proceeding. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Eliminate staging_raw table — implement synchronous in-memory tokenization before any write; add S3 Object Lock audit archive; GDPR erasure request workflow; nightly retention deletion DAG. Phase 3 (4 weeks): audit_scanner anomaly detection service, Security Hub integration, data_access_policies enforcement, automated GDPR compliance reporting.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust clippy with deny(warnings) enforced in CI — zero unsafe blocks without explicit security review approval. All KMS calls must be batched to avoid rate limit throttling. No plaintext PII may appear in application logs — log driver_token BYTEA hex only. Integration tests use synthetic GPS data generator; production data never used in test environments. GDPR erasure implementation must be tested with automated verification that all gps_track_points for a driver_token are deleted within the 72-hour SLA.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Critical Risk: staging_raw table pattern persists plaintext driver PII before tokenization — violates GDPR Article 25 and creates mandatory breach notification exposure. Must be eliminated in Phase 2. Risk: KMS rate limits (10K requests/second default quota) could become a bottleneck at peak load; mitigated by batched GenerateDataKey with local session key caching. Risk: pgcrypto performance overhead at 10K events/second may increase database CPU; benchmark shows 15% overhead acceptable at current provisioning. Tradeoff: column-level encryption over full-disk encryption provides finer-grained access control at cost of application-layer key management complexity.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chosen Rust over Go for Tokio async and memory safety guarantees critical for a data handling service. Confirmed pgcrypto column-level encryption over AWS RDS encryption-at-rest-only because column encryption survives snapshot copies to non-encrypted environments. Selected EMQX over AWS IoT Core to keep all data processing within eu-west-1 VPC without data leaving to AWS global IoT infrastructure.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: staging_raw table persists plaintext driver GPS coordinates before tokenization, violating GDPR Article 25 data-protection-by-design. Designed for revision training.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for fleet operations engineers to stream live vehicle telemetry from a telematics API in real time, with vehicle filtering, event type selection, and stdout output for piping to local scripts
project_goal.rationale: Operators need a scriptable terminal interface to the telematics WebSocket API without building a full application

target_users.value: Fleet operations engineers running Linux/macOS workstations who need to monitor specific vehicles or pipe telemetry to local analysis scripts
target_users.rationale: Technical internal users comfortable with CLI tooling and shell scripting

project_class.rationale: Local binary with command-line interface; no server, no browser UI

capabilities.rationale: Requires WebSocket integration, event parsing and formatting, real-time streaming

complexity_level.rationale: WebSocket connection management, reconnect logic, output format multiplexing add moderate complexity to an otherwise simple streaming tool

risk_level.rationale: Read-only telemetry access with API key auth; no write operations to fleet systems

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vehicle telemetry is internal operational data; not customer PII but not public

external_exposure.rationale: Local binary run on operator workstation; no network server exposed

access_model.value: API key passed via TELEMATICS_API_KEY environment variable; key never logged or echoed to stdout; optional --profile flag to select named key from ~/.fleet/config
access_model.rationale: Standard CLI credential pattern; avoids key exposure in shell history

feature_scope.value: Real-time WebSocket telemetry streaming, vehicle ID and event type filters, NDJSON and CSV output formats, automatic reconnect with backoff, human-readable progress indicators on stderr, pipe-safe stdout
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature set for a fleet telemetry streaming CLI

mvp_scope.value: stream command with --vehicle and --event flags, NDJSON output to stdout, automatic reconnect with exponential backoff, API key from environment variable
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimal viable streaming command covering core operator workflow

security_baseline.value: API key read from environment variable only; TLS verification enforced on WebSocket connection; no telemetry data written to disk by the tool itself
security_baseline.rationale: CLI tool threat model: protect API key from exposure, verify server identity

frontend_stack.value: None — CLI tool with no browser interface
frontend_stack.rationale: Terminal output only; no frontend framework

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Cobra CLI framework and gorilla/websocket
backend_stack.rationale: Go produces single static binaries ideal for CLI distribution; Cobra is the standard Go CLI framework

data_platform.value: No persistent storage; streaming only to stdout
data_platform.rationale: Tool is a passthrough streamer; persistence is the responsibility of downstream consumer scripts

hosting_target.value: Local binary distribution via GitHub Releases for Linux amd64/arm64 and macOS arm64
hosting_target.rationale: Engineers install binary directly; no server hosting needed

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained by the tool; streaming-only; operator is responsible for any local file output via shell redirection
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Tool does not store data; retention policy is not applicable to the binary itself

future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 command extensions once stream is stable

constraints.value: Single static binary with no runtime dependencies; must work on Linux amd64/arm64 and macOS arm64; binary size under 20 MB
constraints.rationale: Fleet engineers install directly without package managers; binary size constraint for quick downloads

observability_baseline.value: Connection status and reconnect attempts printed to stderr; --verbose flag for debug-level WebSocket frame logging to stderr; no remote telemetry from the CLI itself
observability_baseline.rationale: Operators need local visibility into connection health; remote telemetry of a CLI tool is inappropriate

execution_preference.value: Single-engineer ownership with PR reviews; GitHub Releases for versioned binary distribution; semantic versioning
execution_preference.rationale: Small-scope tool with one primary maintainer

project.goal: Real-time fleet telemetry streaming CLI for ops engineers
project.target_users: Fleet operations engineers on Linux/macOS

backend.framework: Cobra for CLI structure, gorilla/websocket for WebSocket client
backend.api: Outbound WebSocket to telematics provider only

security.auth: API key via TELEMATICS_API_KEY env var

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for test, build, cross-compile

reasoner_reviews.product: The stream command covers the core operator workflow. The reconnect behavior with visual stderr indicator is essential for long-running monitoring sessions. The unresolved issue from round 1 was that CSV output mode did not preserve event type as a column header, breaking downstream awk/cut pipelines.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Cobra is the right stack. The round 1 plan correctly separated stderr for status from stdout for data, enabling clean piping. The fix needed is in the CSV formatter: it must emit a header row on connection (re)start with all expected fields including event_type, vehicle_id, timestamp, and event-specific payload fields as columns.
reasoner_reviews.security: API key from environment variable is correct; the --profile flag for named keys from ~/.fleet/config must use 0600 file permissions and warn the operator if the file is world-readable. TLS verification must not be bypassable via a flag — an --insecure flag would be a security regression.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Single static binary with CGO_ENABLED=0 for gorilla/websocket keeps binary well under 20 MB. Cross-compilation for linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/arm64 is straightforward with Go toolchain.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with three Cobra commands: stream (real-time WebSocket), list-vehicles (enumerate available vehicle IDs from REST API), version (print build info). Each command is a separate Go package under cmd/
backend.api_patterns: Outbound WebSocket connection to telematics provider WSS endpoint; subscription message sent after handshake to register vehicle_ids filter and event_type filter; incoming JSON frames parsed and routed to output formatter
backend.failure_handling: WebSocket disconnect triggers reconnect with exponential backoff starting at 1s, capping at 60s, jitter added; reconnect attempt count and delay printed to stderr; SIGINT/SIGTERM triggers clean WebSocket close frame before exit
backend.scaling_notes: Single goroutine reads WebSocket frames; separate goroutine buffers and writes to stdout to prevent slow consumer backpressure from dropping events; channel buffer size configurable via --buffer flag defaulting to 1000 events

security.auth_design: API key read from TELEMATICS_API_KEY environment variable; secondarily from --profile named config entry in ~/.fleet/config YAML; key sent as Authorization: Bearer header in WebSocket upgrade request
security.secrets_management: ~/.fleet/config file checked for 0600 permissions on read; warning printed to stderr if world-readable; API key never written to stdout, log files, or error messages
security.security_risks: Operators may accidentally redirect stderr to a log file that captures API key from verbose debug output; ensure --verbose mode redacts Authorization header from WebSocket frame logs

data.entities: telemetry_streams (vehicle_id, event_type ENUM location/speed/engine/fuel/alert, payload map[string]interface{}, received_at), vehicle_filters (vehicle_ids[], event_types[]), event_subscriptions (connection_id, subscribed_at, vehicle_count, event_type_count), output_formatters (format ENUM ndjson/csv, header_emitted bool), connection_sessions (session_id, connected_at, reconnect_count, last_disconnect_reason)
data.storage_design: No persistent storage; all state is in-process Go structs for the duration of the command execution; session metadata logged to stderr only
data.analytics_events: Connection established, reconnect attempt, reconnect success, SIGINT received — all emitted to stderr as structured log lines for operator visibility

devops.deployment_topology: No server; GitHub Actions builds cross-compiled binaries and uploads to GitHub Release artifacts
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on tag push: go test ./..., go vet, staticcheck, goreleaser for cross-compilation to linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/arm64 with CGO_ENABLED=0 and -trimpath for reproducible builds; SHA256 checksums published alongside binaries
devops.devops_risks: gorilla/websocket dependency introduces indirect CVE exposure; dependabot alerts monitored; consider migrating to nhooyr/websocket which is maintained more actively

ISS-001.title: CSV output missing event_type column header on reconnect
ISS-001.detail: The CSV formatter emits the header row only once on initial connection. After a reconnect, the header is not re-emitted, causing downstream awk/cut scripts that rely on column position to silently process misaligned data. Additionally, the event_type field is omitted from the CSV column set entirely.

history.note: Identified during CLI testing with a piped jq script; event_type missing from CSV schema

ISS-002.title: Verbose mode logs full Authorization header including API key
ISS-002.detail: When --verbose flag is active, the WebSocket upgrade request headers are logged to stderr including the raw Authorization: Bearer {api_key} value. Operators who redirect stderr to log files for session debugging expose their API key in plaintext.

history.note: Found during security review of verbose log output

focus_issues.title: CSV output missing event_type column header on reconnect
focus_issues.detail: CSV formatter omits event_type column and does not re-emit header after reconnect, breaking downstream pipeline scripts.

latest_recommendations: Fix CSV formatter to always include event_type, vehicle_id, timestamp, and all payload fields as named columns
Re-emit CSV header row on every reconnect so downstream scripts can detect session restarts
Redact Authorization header value in verbose WebSocket frame logs

convergence_state.reason: High severity CSV schema issue unresolved after round 1

previous_audits.summary: Solid CLI architecture with correct stream/stderr separation, but CSV output schema is broken for downstream scripting use cases. Security concern around API key exposure in verbose logs.

Exponential backoff reconnect with operator feedback is well-designed
Single static binary cross-compilation approach is appropriate
concerns: CSV formatter is missing event_type column entirely
CSV header not re-emitted after reconnect breaks position-dependent downstream scripts
Verbose mode logs raw API key in Authorization header
blocking_issues: ISS-001: CSV output schema is broken — event_type column absent, no header on reconnect
recommendations: Redefine CSV schema to include fixed columns: event_type, vehicle_id, timestamp, plus event-specific payload fields as additional columns
Emit CSV header on connection start and on every reconnect event
In verbose mode, replace API key value in logged headers with REDACTED

previous_plan.title: Fleet Telemetry Streaming CLI Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI binary using Cobra and gorilla/websocket that streams live vehicle telemetry to stdout in NDJSON or CSV format with vehicle ID and event type filtering, automatic reconnect, and pipe-safe output separation.
previous_plan.system_components: Cobra CLI with stream, list-vehicles, and version commands. gorilla/websocket client with subscription message protocol. NDJSON formatter writing JSON objects newline-delimited to stdout. CSV formatter writing rows to stdout (v1: missing event_type column). Reconnect manager with exponential backoff printing status to stderr.
previous_plan.data_model: In-process: VehicleFilter{ids []string, eventTypes []string}, ConnectionSession{connectedAt, reconnectCount}, EventFrame{vehicleID, eventType, payload, receivedAt}. CSV output schema in v1: vehicle_id, timestamp, speed_kmh, latitude, longitude — missing event_type.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: API key from TELEMATICS_API_KEY env var, passed as Authorization Bearer header. TLS verification enforced. Verbose mode logs WebSocket frames to stderr including raw Authorization header — security flaw identified in audit.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revision round 2 fixing the CSV output schema flaw (ISS-001) and the API key verbose log exposure (ISS-002). Key changes: redefine CSV formatter to emit fixed-column header (event_type, vehicle_id, timestamp) plus event-specific payload columns, re-emit header on every reconnect so downstream scripts can detect session restarts, and redact Authorization header value in verbose debug logs. Architecture is otherwise solid from round 1 — no structural changes needed.

fix_report.action_taken: Redefined CSV formatter to always emit a header row with columns: event_type, vehicle_id, timestamp, and all known payload fields per event type as named columns. Header is re-emitted after every reconnect prefixed with a comment line '# reconnect:{count}' so downstream scripts can detect session restarts and reset column alignment.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Downstream awk/cut/python scripts receive consistent column schema across reconnects; event_type is always available as first column for event-type-specific processing

fix_report.action_taken: Modified verbose WebSocket frame logger to replace Authorization header value with REDACTED before writing to stderr. Added unit test asserting no API key substring appears in verbose log output.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Operators can safely redirect stderr to log files for session debugging without exposing API key credentials

target_output.title: Fleet Telemetry Streaming CLI Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI binary for fleet operations engineers that streams live vehicle telemetry from a telematics WebSocket API to stdout with vehicle ID and event type filtering, NDJSON and CSV output, automatic reconnect with exponential backoff, and pipe-safe stderr/stdout separation. Version 2 fixes the CSV column schema to include event_type as the first column and re-emits headers on reconnect; verbose debug logging now redacts API key values.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Go binary built with Cobra for command structure and gorilla/websocket for the telematics WebSocket client. The stream command opens a WSS connection, sends a subscription JSON message specifying vehicle_ids and event_types filters, then reads incoming telemetry frames in a dedicated goroutine and writes formatted output to stdout via a buffered channel. Status messages (connection established, reconnect attempts, event counts) are written exclusively to stderr to keep stdout clean for piping. A second goroutine drains the channel and writes to the configured output formatter (NDJSON or CSV) without blocking the WebSocket reader. The CSV formatter v2 emits a full column header on connection start and on each reconnect, ensuring downstream scripts always have column metadata.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22 with CGO_ENABLED=0 for static binary. CLI Framework: Cobra for command parsing, flag definitions, and help text generation. WebSocket Client: gorilla/websocket for WSS connection management. Output: encoding/json for NDJSON, encoding/csv for CSV formatter. Cross-compilation: goreleaser with GOOS/GOARCH matrix for linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/arm64. Distribution: GitHub Releases with SHA256 checksums. CI: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: stream command: --vehicle flag accepts comma-separated vehicle IDs or 'all'; --event flag accepts comma-separated event types (location, speed, engine, fuel, alert) or 'all'; --format flag selects ndjson (default) or csv; --buffer flag sets channel buffer size (default 1000); --verbose flag enables debug logging to stderr with redacted credentials. list-vehicles command: fetches available vehicle IDs from telematics REST API and prints one per line to stdout. version command: prints semver, git SHA, and build timestamp. CSV format v2: header row 'event_type,vehicle_id,timestamp,{payload_fields}' emitted on connect and reconnect; reconnect prefix comment '# reconnect:{n}' emitted before header.
target_output.system_components: Cobra root command with stream, list-vehicles, and version subcommands. WebSocket manager goroutine reading frames and forwarding to buffered channel. Output dispatcher goroutine draining channel and writing to stdout via selected formatter. NDJSON formatter marshaling EventFrame structs to newline-delimited JSON. CSV formatter v2 with header emission on connect/reconnect and redaction-safe verbose logger. Reconnect manager with exponential backoff (1s base, 60s cap, 20% jitter) printing reconnect status and delay to stderr. Config loader reading API key from TELEMATICS_API_KEY env var or ~/.fleet/config with 0600 permission check.
target_output.workflows: Telemetry Stream: Engineer runs 'fleet stream --vehicle VH-001,VH-002 --event location,speed --format csv > telemetry.csv'; CLI reads API key from env, opens WSS connection, sends subscription JSON, emits CSV header 'event_type,vehicle_id,timestamp,latitude,longitude,speed_kmh' to stdout, then streams matching events as CSV rows; on disconnect, prints '# reconnect:1' and new header row so downstream scripts detect session restart. Reconnect Cycle: WebSocket close frame or TCP timeout triggers reconnect manager; stderr shows 'reconnecting in 2s (attempt 1/inf)'; after successful handshake, resubscribes with same filters, re-emits CSV header, resumes streaming without operator intervention. Config Profile: Engineer runs 'fleet stream --profile fleet-prod' which reads ~/.fleet/config for the fleet-prod entry; loader checks file permissions and prints a warning to stderr if 0644 or more permissive.
target_output.data_model: In-process types: EventFrame{VehicleID string, EventType string ENUM location/speed/engine/fuel/alert, Timestamp time.Time, Payload map[string]interface{}}. VehicleFilter{IDs []string, EventTypes []string, AllVehicles bool, AllEventTypes bool}. ConnectionSession{ConnectedAt time.Time, ReconnectCount int, LastDisconnectReason string}. OutputFormatter interface{EmitHeader(), EmitEvent(EventFrame), EmitReconnectMarker(int)}. CSVFormatter v2: fixed columns [event_type, vehicle_id, timestamp] prepended to sorted payload keys per event type; header re-emitted after each reconnect. Config{APIKey string (never logged), ProfileName string, APIEndpoint string}.
target_output.api_design: Outbound WebSocket only: WSS {TELEMATICS_API_ENDPOINT}/stream upgrade with Authorization: Bearer {api_key} header. Subscription message after handshake: {action:'subscribe', vehicle_ids:['VH-001'], event_types:['location','speed']}. Incoming frame schema: {vehicle_id:string, event_type:string, timestamp:RFC3339, payload:{...event-specific fields}}. REST call for list-vehicles: GET {TELEMATICS_API_ENDPOINT}/v1/vehicles with same Authorization header, response {vehicles:[{id,name,status}]}. No inbound API surface on the operator workstation.
target_output.security_and_compliance: API key read exclusively from TELEMATICS_API_KEY environment variable or ~/.fleet/config (0600 permissions required; warning emitted if file is world-readable). API key transmitted as Authorization: Bearer header in WSS upgrade request; TLS certificate verification enforced — no --insecure flag provided. Verbose debug logger redacts Authorization header value with REDACTED before writing to stderr, verified by unit test asserting no API key substring appears in log output. API key never written to stdout, CSV output, or NDJSON frames. Binary contains no hardcoded credentials. Data sensitivity is internal; no GDPR or PCI obligations for the CLI tool itself.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions workflow triggered on git tag push: go test ./... (unit + integration with mock WebSocket server), go vet ./..., staticcheck ./..., goreleaser release --clean cross-compiling linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/arm64 with CGO_ENABLED=0 -trimpath -ldflags='-w -s'. GitHub Release assets: fleet-linux-amd64, fleet-linux-arm64, fleet-darwin-arm64, SHA256SUMS. Engineers install via: curl -L {release_url}/fleet-linux-amd64 -o /usr/local/bin/fleet && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/fleet. No server deployment or infrastructure needed. Dependabot monitors gorilla/websocket and Cobra for CVE alerts.
target_output.observability: All observability output goes to stderr to keep stdout clean for piping: connection established with endpoint URL and subscription filter summary, reconnect attempt with delay and count, reconnect success with total session event count, SIGINT received with final event count and session duration. --verbose flag adds WebSocket handshake debug (with redacted Authorization header), frame receipt timestamps, and channel buffer depth every 10 seconds. No remote telemetry emitted from the CLI binary. Engineers can capture verbose diagnostics via 2>debug.log without contaminating stdout data stream.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — local binary with no server. GitHub Releases hosting is free. GitHub Actions CI minutes consumed per release tag, typically 3-5 minutes for cross-compilation matrix. Binary size with goreleaser -w -s flags: approximately 8 MB per target platform, well under 20 MB constraint. Memory footprint: channel buffer default 1000 events at ~500 bytes each = 500 KB; acceptable on any modern workstation.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (completed in v1): stream command with NDJSON output, vehicle and event filters, reconnect with backoff, API key from env var. Phase 2 (v2, current): CSV formatter v2 with correct column schema and reconnect header re-emission, verbose log API key redaction, list-vehicles command, ~/.fleet/config profile support with permission check. Phase 3: watch command for threshold-based alerting (e.g., 'alert when vehicle VH-001 speed > 90'), tail-style --lookback flag for historical event replay before live stream, Prometheus metrics export mode for integration with ops monitoring stacks.
target_output.development_guidelines: Unit test coverage required for: CSV formatter header emission on connect, CSV formatter header re-emission on reconnect, verbose log redaction of Authorization header value, config file 0600 permission warning, exponential backoff delay sequence. Integration tests use a local mock WebSocket server (net/http/httptest). No global state — all command state passed via struct to enable parallel test execution. Errors written to stderr, never to stdout. All flags documented in Cobra command descriptions that appear in --help output.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: gorilla/websocket is in maintenance mode; future WebSocket protocol extensions may not be implemented. Mitigation: Dependabot alerts active; migration path to nhooyr/websocket documented in ADR. Risk: telematics provider WebSocket protocol changes can break subscription message format. Mitigation: integration test mocks the provider protocol; version command includes protocol version for debugging. Tradeoff: buffered channel approach over direct write trades event latency (negligible at 1000-event buffer) for protection against slow stdout consumers stalling the WebSocket reader goroutine.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed gorilla/websocket over nhooyr/websocket for v2; migration ADR drafted for v3 if maintenance issues arise. Confirmed CSV header re-emission on reconnect over a reconnect-marker-only approach; header re-emission lets scripts fully reset column state. Confirmed REDACTED string over omitting the Authorization header from verbose logs entirely; REDACTED preserves the log structure for debugging while removing the secret value.

metadata.notes: Round 2 CLI revision fixing CSV column schema and verbose API key exposure; Go/Cobra/WebSocket fleet telemetry streaming tool.

project_goal.value: Replace legacy static HTML landing site with a CMS-driven Next.js landing page for a fleet telematics SaaS startup, containerized for consistent builds and deployed via a modernized CI/CD pipeline to Vercel
project_goal.rationale: Marketing team needs CMS-editable content; engineering needs repeatable containerized builds

target_users.value: Prospective fleet telematics customers landing from paid ads and organic search; marketing team updating content via headless CMS
target_users.rationale: Two user types: public visitors converting to leads, and internal marketing editors

project_class.rationale: Public marketing site with no authenticated user sessions or server-side business logic

capabilities.rationale: Static rendering with CMS integration and GA4 analytics; no backend or auth capability

complexity_level.rationale: CMS-sourced static generation; Vercel handles CDN and build; no custom infrastructure

risk_level.rationale: Public marketing site; no user accounts, no payment data, no sensitive data stored

data_sensitivity.rationale: No user PII stored server-side; lead form data forwarded to CRM via serverless function

external_exposure.rationale: Public marketing site accessible to anyone

access_model.value: No authentication for public visitors; headless CMS has its own editor authentication managed by the CMS vendor; Vercel dashboard access restricted to engineering team
access_model.rationale: Public landing page requires no visitor authentication

feature_scope.value: Hero section with CMS-editable headline and CTA, feature highlight cards for GPS tracking and route optimization capabilities, pricing tier table, customer logo strip, lead capture form forwarding to CRM, GDPR cookie consent banner for EU visitors
feature_scope.rationale: Standard SaaS landing page conversion sections

mvp_scope.value: Hero, feature highlights, pricing tiers, lead capture form with CRM forwarding, GA4 analytics, Vercel deployment
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum conversion-capable landing page for initial launch

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Vercel, CSP headers, no secrets in client-side code, lead form data forwarded server-side to CRM API
security_baseline.rationale: Marketing site threat model: protect CRM API key from client exposure

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js static export with ISR enables fast global delivery and CMS-driven content refresh

backend_stack.value: Vercel serverless function for lead form CRM forwarding only; no persistent backend
backend_stack.rationale: Landing page has no backend; single serverless endpoint for form submission keeps CRM API key server-side

data_platform.value: No database; headless CMS (Contentful) as content source; lead data forwarded directly to CRM
data_platform.rationale: Landing page does not store data; CMS is the content store, CRM is the lead store

hosting_target.value: Vercel with global CDN and automatic HTTPS
hosting_target.rationale: Vercel is native for Next.js; preview deployments per PR enable marketing review

privacy_retention_policy.value: No visitor PII stored; lead form data forwarded immediately to CRM with no local retention; GDPR cookie consent via cookie banner before GA4 measurement loads
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR compliance for EU visitors requires consent before analytics tracking

future_scope.value: A/B testing for hero copy variants, blog section for SEO, localized content for EU markets
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 growth marketing capabilities

constraints.value: Marketing team must be able to update hero copy and pricing without engineering involvement; no custom server infrastructure; Vercel deployment only
constraints.rationale: Marketing autonomy is a hard requirement; CMS-only content updates

observability_baseline.value: Google Analytics 4 for page views and lead form conversion events with UTM parameter capture; Vercel analytics for Core Web Vitals
observability_baseline.rationale: Conversion tracking and performance monitoring for marketing optimization

execution_preference.value: Marketing-triggered deploys via CMS publish webhooks; engineering-triggered deploys via GitHub Actions on PR merge
execution_preference.rationale: Two deploy paths: content deploys from CMS, code deploys from Git

project.goal: CMS-driven Next.js landing page for fleet telematics SaaS
project.target_users: Prospective customers and marketing team

backend.runtime: Vercel serverless function for lead form only
backend.framework: Next.js API route /api/lead

security.auth: None for visitors; CRM API key in Vercel environment variables

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for type-check and lint
devops.environments: Vercel preview per PR + production

reasoner_reviews.product: The feature scope covers all standard SaaS conversion sections. The unresolved issue from round 1 is that the cookie consent banner implementation does not block GA4 from loading before consent is given — this is a GDPR violation for EU visitors.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 14 with Contentful ISR is the correct stack for marketing-editable content. The architecture is thin which is appropriate. The fix needed is straightforward: GA4 script must be conditional on cookie consent state, loaded dynamically only after consent is granted.
reasoner_reviews.security: CRM API key correctly kept server-side in the Vercel serverless function. The consent issue is the only technical concern — GA4 loading before consent signals to regulators that the site lacks privacy-by-design. The fix is a consent state check before the next/script GA4 component loads.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Contentful webhook triggering a Vercel redeploy on publish is the correct pattern for marketing-autonomy content updates. Vercel preview deployments on every PR enable marketing stakeholder review before production merge.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js app router with static generation; pages: / (landing), /pricing (optional separate route); components: HeroSection, FeatureHighlightGrid, PricingTable, CustomerLogoStrip, LeadCaptureForm, CookieConsentBanner
frontend.pages_and_flows: / renders all landing sections from Contentful content model; LeadCaptureForm submits to /api/lead serverless function; CookieConsentBanner loads before GA4 and blocks analytics initialization until consent granted
frontend.performance_notes: Next.js static export with ISR 60-second revalidation for CMS content changes; hero images from Contentful served via next/image for WebP conversion and lazy loading; Lighthouse score target above 90 for LCP and CLS
frontend.frontend_risks: Contentful ISR revalidation means content updates may take up to 60 seconds to appear globally; marketing team must be informed of this delay

security.auth_design: No visitor authentication; Contentful editor authentication managed by Contentful
security.secrets_management: CRM API key and Contentful delivery token stored as Vercel environment variables; not exposed to client-side bundle
security.security_risks: GA4 loading before cookie consent is a GDPR compliance risk for EU visitors; must be fixed to conditional load after consent

data.entities: hero_sections (headline, subheadline, cta_label, cta_url, background_image), feature_highlights (title, description, icon, sort_order), pricing_tiers (name, monthly_price, annual_price, features[], is_featured), customer_logos (company_name, logo_image, alt_text), lead_capture_forms (form_id, fields[], crm_list_id)
data.storage_design: All content stored in Contentful; Next.js fetches via Contentful Delivery API at build and ISR revalidation; no application database
data.analytics_events: page_view on landing, lead_form_submitted with source and UTM parameters, pricing_tier_clicked with tier name — all conditional on GA4 consent state

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel project connected to GitHub main branch for production; Contentful webhook calls Vercel deploy hook URL on content publish to trigger ISR revalidation
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: tsc --noEmit type check, eslint, next build; Vercel automatic preview deployment on every PR; production deploy on merge to main
devops.environments: Vercel preview (per PR), production (main branch)

ISS-001.title: GA4 script loads before cookie consent granted — GDPR violation
ISS-001.detail: The GA4 next/script component uses strategy='afterInteractive' which loads GA4 unconditionally on page load for all visitors. EU visitors have not granted consent before GA4 begins collecting data, violating GDPR requirements for prior informed consent for analytics cookies.

history.note: Identified during privacy review; GA4 fires on page load before consent banner interaction

focus_issues.title: GA4 script loads before cookie consent granted — GDPR violation
focus_issues.detail: GA4 initializes unconditionally before consent banner is interacted with by EU visitors.

latest_recommendations: Move GA4 script to conditional load: only initialize after cookie consent state is 'granted'
Implement consent state in localStorage; on page load check consent before initializing GA4
Use gtag consent mode v2 to signal denied consent to Google without loading the full GA4 library
