project.goal: Nightly ML risk scoring of fleet vehicles and carrier API submission
project.target_users: Data engineering team and carrier partner API consumers

backend.framework: Prefect 2 for orchestration, httpx for carrier API calls
backend.model_serving: batch inference via scikit-learn + XGBoost loaded from S3

security.auth: IAM roles for pipeline tasks; API keys per carrier in Secrets Manager

data.model_store: S3 bucket with versioned model artifacts
data.feature_store: 30-day telematics feature snapshots in S3 Parquet

devops.environments: dev, production

open_questions.adverse_action: Confirm FCRA adverse action notification owner — pipeline or downstream carrier?

confirmed_decisions.model_format: scikit-learn + XGBoost pickle with S3 versioning

reasoner_reviews.product: The nightly scoring pipeline cleanly separates telematics feature extraction, ML inference, and carrier submission into distinct Prefect tasks, allowing independent retries at each stage without reprocessing upstream data.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Loading versioned XGBoost models from S3 at flow startup provides model rollback capability without redeployment. Carrier fan-out should be parallelized with Prefect task mapping to fit within the 6-hour window as carrier count grows.
reasoner_reviews.security: API keys for carrier APIs must be fetched from Secrets Manager at runtime — never baked into container images. FCRA adverse action obligations should be documented with Legal: the pipeline produces scores but if carriers use them to deny coverage, disclosure obligations may fall on the fleet operator.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Carrier batch size limits of 500 vehicles per request require chunking logic for fleets over 500 vehicles. The 6-hour window is feasible for up to 50,000 vehicles at current latencies but should be validated in load testing.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Risk tier labels written to the database must be immutable audit records — the schema should prevent overwriting prior scoring run results to support regulatory audit trails.

backend.service_design: Prefect flow with four tasks: extract_telematics_features (reads 30-day Parquet snapshots from S3), run_inference (loads XGBoost model from S3, returns risk_tier and confidence_interval per vehicle), batch_carrier_submissions (chunks vehicles by carrier and submits via httpx), log_receipts (writes carrier acknowledgments to PostgreSQL)
backend.api_patterns: Internal Prefect task interface only; no external API surface. Carrier integrations use per-carrier httpx client classes with retry and timeout configuration.
backend.background_jobs: Prefect ConcurrencyLimit controls parallel carrier submission tasks; exponential backoff on carrier API 429/503 responses
backend.failure_handling: Each Prefect task retries independently; failed carrier submissions flagged in carrier_submissions table with status=failed for manual re-run via CLI trigger

security.auth_design: ECS task execution role with least-privilege S3 and RDS access; Secrets Manager GetSecretValue permission scoped to carrier API key ARNs only
security.secrets_management: Carrier API keys fetched via boto3 Secrets Manager at task startup, never logged or serialized to Prefect artifacts
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every scoring_run and carrier_submission record is append-only with created_at timestamp; CloudWatch logs capture task-level flow execution with redacted payloads

data.entities: vehicle_risk_profiles (vehicle_id, scoring_run_id, risk_tier ENUM standard/elevated/high_risk/excluded, confidence_score, feature_snapshot_s3_key, scored_at), scoring_runs (id, model_version, flow_run_id, started_at, completed_at, vehicle_count, status ENUM pending/running/completed/failed), carrier_submissions (id, scoring_run_id, carrier_id, batch_index, vehicle_count, submitted_at, acknowledgment_code, status ENUM submitted/acknowledged/failed), ml_model_versions (id, model_s3_key, framework_version, trained_at, promoted_at, deprecated_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ for scoring and submission records; S3 for model artifacts and 30-day telematics feature Parquet snapshots with lifecycle policy to Glacier after 90 days
data.retention_and_deletion: Scoring run records retained 3 years; telematics feature snapshots purged after 90 days per privacy policy

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate scheduled task triggered nightly at 01:00 UTC; Prefect agent runs as ECS task pulling flow deployments from Prefect Cloud; model and feature S3 buckets in same region as ECS cluster
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: run pytest with mock carrier fixtures, build Docker image, push to ECR, update ECS task definition. Prefect deployment updated via prefect deployment build on main merge.
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboards on Prometheus metrics exported from Prefect flow; CloudWatch log group per flow run; PagerDuty webhook on flow failure alert
devops.rollback_strategy: Model rollback by promoting prior ml_model_versions record to active and redeploying ECS task definition; carrier submission errors trigger manual re-run Prefect deployment

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a nightly ML insurance risk scoring pipeline using Prefect on ECS Fargate. Key decisions: immutable append-only scoring records for FCRA audit trail, per-carrier httpx client classes to handle schema differences, XGBoost model versioning via S3 rather than a model serving endpoint to keep latency predictable in batch mode. FCRA adverse action notification responsibility must be surfaced as an open question resolved by Legal before carrier go-live.
target_output.title: Fleet Insurance Risk Scoring Automation Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A nightly Prefect-orchestrated automation tool on AWS ECS Fargate that extracts 30-day telematics features per fleet vehicle, runs batch XGBoost risk classification to assign insurance risk tiers with confidence scores, and submits scored results to insurance carrier REST APIs with retry logic and acknowledgment logging. Compliance controls address FCRA scoring audit requirements and GDPR for EU fleet data.
target_output.architecture_overview: Headless pipeline architecture: ECS Fargate scheduled task starts a Prefect agent nightly at 01:00 UTC. The flow sequentially extracts telematics feature Parquet snapshots from S3, loads the active versioned XGBoost model artifact from the S3 model registry, runs batch inference producing risk_tier and confidence_score per vehicle, then fans out carrier submission tasks in parallel via Prefect task mapping. Each carrier receives vehicle batches of up to 500 via httpx REST calls. Acknowledgment codes and submission status are persisted to PostgreSQL 16 on RDS. All carrier communication occurs over TLS 1.3 with API keys fetched at runtime from Secrets Manager.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Prefect 2.x deployed to Prefect Cloud with ECS Fargate agent. Inference: Python 3.12, scikit-learn 1.4, XGBoost 2.0, model artifacts in S3. Storage: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ, S3 Parquet for feature snapshots. Carrier integration: httpx 0.27 with per-carrier client classes. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ECR, Secrets Manager, CloudWatch, S3 with lifecycle policies. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Docker.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Telematics Feature Extraction: reads 30-day vehicle GPS, speed, braking, idle, and DTC event Parquet files from S3 and engineers per-vehicle feature vectors. Batch Risk Inference: loads versioned XGBoost model, applies scikit-learn preprocessing pipeline, scores each vehicle with risk_tier (standard/elevated/high_risk/excluded) and confidence_interval. Carrier Submission: chunks vehicles by carrier assignment, formats payloads per carrier API schema, submits with retry on 429/503. Acknowledgment Logging: persists carrier acknowledgment codes and reconciliation status. Failure Alerting: PagerDuty notification on any task failure exceeding retry limit.
target_output.system_components: Prefect Cloud (flow run scheduling and observability), ECS Fargate task (Prefect agent + flow execution container), S3 model registry bucket (versioned XGBoost artifacts), S3 feature snapshot bucket (30-day Parquet files with Glacier lifecycle), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (scoring_runs, vehicle_risk_profiles, carrier_submissions tables), Secrets Manager (per-carrier API keys), ECR (container images), CloudWatch Logs (task execution logs), Grafana + Prometheus (flow-level metrics dashboard), PagerDuty (failure escalation).
target_output.workflows: Nightly Scoring Flow: Prefect agent picks up scheduled deployment at 01:00 UTC, spins up ECS task. extract_telematics_features task reads S3 Parquet for all active fleet vehicles and returns feature DataFrame. run_inference task loads active model version from S3, applies preprocessing, scores vehicles, writes vehicle_risk_profiles rows. batch_carrier_submissions task maps over carriers, chunks vehicles into 500-vehicle batches, submits via per-carrier httpx client, logs carrier_submissions rows with acknowledgment codes. On failure at any task, Prefect retries up to 3 times before marking run failed and triggering PagerDuty. Model Rollback: engineer promotes prior ml_model_versions record via CLI, redeploys ECS task definition pointing to prior model S3 key, triggers manual flow run to re-score affected vehicles.
target_output.data_model: vehicle_risk_profiles (id UUID PK, vehicle_id FK, scoring_run_id FK, risk_tier ENUM standard/elevated/high_risk/excluded, confidence_score NUMERIC, feature_snapshot_s3_key TEXT, scored_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ) — append-only, no updates. scoring_runs (id UUID PK, model_version_id FK, prefect_flow_run_id TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, vehicle_count INT, status ENUM pending/running/completed/failed). carrier_submissions (id UUID PK, scoring_run_id FK, carrier_id TEXT, batch_index INT, vehicle_count INT, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, acknowledgment_code TEXT, status ENUM submitted/acknowledged/failed, error_detail TEXT). ml_model_versions (id UUID PK, model_s3_key TEXT, xgboost_version TEXT, sklearn_version TEXT, trained_at DATE, promoted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, deprecated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Internal Prefect task interface — no external HTTP API surface. Carrier clients expose per-carrier classes: CarrierAClient.submit_batch(vehicles: list[VehicleScore]) -> BatchAcknowledgment, CarrierBClient.submit_batch(...). Manual re-run endpoint: POST /runs/trigger on Prefect Cloud API (internal). Health check: GET /api/health on Prefect agent (internal). No partner-facing inbound API surface — all communication is outbound to carrier endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: FCRA: all vehicle_risk_profiles records are append-only with model_version reference for adverse action audit trail; Legal owns disclosure obligations for carrier use of scores. GDPR: EU fleet vehicle data pseudonymized before inclusion in feature snapshots — vehicle_id replaces driver PII in Parquet files. API key security: carrier keys fetched from Secrets Manager at Prefect task startup, never logged or emitted to Prefect artifact store. IAM: ECS task role scoped to specific S3 key prefixes and Secrets Manager ARNs. TLS 1.3 enforced on all outbound carrier API calls. Network: ECS tasks run in private VPC subnets with NAT gateway for carrier API egress.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate scheduled task definition runs nightly at 01:00 UTC via EventBridge rule. GitHub Actions CI: pytest with mocked carrier fixtures, build Docker image, push ECR, update ECS task definition revision. Prefect deployment updated via prefect deployment build on main branch merge. Environments: dev (on-demand ECS run with sandbox carrier endpoints), production (nightly scheduled). Model deployment: S3 upload of new model artifact + UPDATE to ml_model_versions table + ECS task definition redeploy. Rollback: revert ml_model_versions promotion and redeploy prior task definition revision. Secrets rotation: Secrets Manager auto-rotation triggers re-fetch on next flow run.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboard on Prometheus metrics: vehicles_scored_total, carrier_submission_success_rate per carrier, submission_latency_seconds P95, model_inference_duration_seconds. CloudWatch log group /prefect/scoring-flow with structured JSON logs per task. Prefect Cloud UI for flow run history and task-level retry counts. PagerDuty alert on: flow run failed (any task exceeding retry limit), carrier submission success rate <95%, scoring_run.vehicle_count deviation >10% from prior run (potential feature extraction issue).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: ~$12/night for 2 vCPU/8 GB task running 4 hours. RDS PostgreSQL db.t3.medium: ~$60/month. S3 Parquet storage: ~$20/month for 30-day retention. Total MVP: ~$100/month. Scaling path: Prefect task mapping parallelizes carrier submissions; ECS task size increases for larger fleets; S3 Parquet partitioning by fleet_id enables selective feature extraction for large fleet counts.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Prefect flow with telematics feature extraction from existing S3 Parquet dumps, XGBoost model integration with initial risk model version, two carrier API integrations (Carrier A + B), PostgreSQL schema with append-only scoring records, GitHub Actions CI, PagerDuty alerting. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Carrier fan-out expanded to 5 carriers, model versioning UI via Prefect parameter override, FCRA audit report export (scoring run + vehicle_risk_profiles CSV per carrier). Phase 3 (4 weeks): Multi-model ensemble scoring, mid-term risk adjustment submission on telematics anomaly events, Grafana SLA dashboard.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Prefect task functions independently unit-testable with mocked S3 and carrier clients. Carrier client classes implement abstract BaseCarrierClient interface for testability and future carrier additions. vehicle_risk_profiles table enforces no-UPDATE constraint via PostgreSQL rule — only INSERTs allowed. Model S3 keys follow versioning pattern: s3://models/risk-scoring/v{semver}/model.pkl. Secrets never passed as environment variables — always fetched via boto3 at task entry.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: XGBoost model loaded from S3 adds 10-30 seconds to flow startup. Mitigation: cache model artifact in ECS task ephemeral storage for flow duration; do not reload per-vehicle. Risk: carrier API downtime during nightly window delays submissions. Mitigation: Prefect retry with backoff; failed submissions flagged for manual re-run; SLA impact tracked in carrier_submissions table. Risk: FCRA adverse action notification responsibility unclear between pipeline operator and carrier. Mitigation: Legal sign-off required before carrier go-live; document in compliance runbook. Tradeoff: append-only scoring records prevent correction of scoring errors; invalid runs must be superseded by a new scoring run rather than updated.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: FCRA adverse action notification owner assigned to Legal for sign-off before carrier go-live. XGBoost batch inference chosen over real-time model server (SageMaker endpoint) for cost and auditability — nightly batch pattern does not require sub-second latency. Parquet chosen over direct database query for telematics features to decouple scoring pipeline from live telematics database load.

metadata.notes: Nightly ML insurance risk scoring pipeline with FCRA compliance considerations and multi-carrier fan-out.

project_goal.value: Replace legacy SFTP batch file exchange with a real-time REST API for DMV and dealership title transfer, lien filing, and plate assignment integrations
project_goal.rationale: Legacy nightly SFTP batch causes 24-hour title status lag and creates reconciliation failures for dealership DMS systems

target_users.value: Dealership management systems and DMV integration brokers consuming the API programmatically
target_users.rationale: B2B API consumed by dealer DMS software and state DMV integration brokers under signed data agreements

project_class.rationale: Pure REST API with no browser frontend; partner systems consume API directly

capabilities.rationale: API gateway, persistent title records, OAuth-secured partner access, DMV state integration layer

complexity_level.rationale: Real-time DMV state API integration, idempotent filing workflows, multi-state regulatory variance in field requirements

risk_level.rationale: Processes personal vehicle ownership records and credit-related lien data under FCRA and state DMV laws

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores owner PII including SSN, vehicle registration details, and lienholder data subject to GDPR and FCRA

external_exposure.rationale: API consumed by external dealership DMS vendors and DMV integration brokers under signed partner data agreements

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials per partner tenant with scoped permissions for title:submit, title:read, lien:submit, lien:read, plate:submit
access_model.rationale: Keycloak client credentials enforce per-partner scope isolation to prevent lien-read-only clients from submitting filings

feature_scope.value: Title transfer filing, lien creation and release, plate assignment and reassignment, DMV submission status polling, webhook event delivery for title and lien status changes
feature_scope.rationale: Core titling operations required by all dealership DMS integrations

mvp_scope.value: Title transfer and lien filing endpoints with DMV submission and webhook status for the top 5 states by transaction volume (CA, TX, FL, NY, IL)
mvp_scope.rationale: Top 5 states cover 80% of transaction volume with initial partner cohort

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3, OAuth 2.0 with Keycloak, AES-256-GCM field encryption for SSN and EIN, per-client API rate limiting, immutable FCRA audit log
security_baseline.rationale: FCRA and state DMV data handling requirements mandate field-level encryption and permissible-purpose access logging

frontend_stack.value: none - API service only, no browser UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Partner systems consume REST API directly; no user-facing frontend in scope

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.2 on Azure Kubernetes Service
backend_stack.rationale: Team Java expertise, Spring Integration adapter pattern for DMV API variance, AKS for managed Kubernetes orchestration

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security for transactional records; Redis 7 for idempotency key deduplication and DMV status caching
data_platform.rationale: ACID transactions for title records; Redis sub-millisecond idempotency checks prevent duplicate DMV filings on DMS retries

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) in East US 2 region
hosting_target.rationale: AKS managed Kubernetes with Azure AD integration and US data residency for state DMV compliance

privacy_retention_policy.value: Title records retained 10 years per state archival mandates; PII access logs retained 3 years for FCRA dispute window; lien records until release plus 7 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: State DMV archival statutes and FCRA dispute period requirements

future_scope.value: Expand to all 50 states, add consumer-facing digital title wallet API, support EV title with battery certification and range certification fields
future_scope.rationale: Full national coverage and consumer title access in Year 2 roadmap

constraints.value: Multi-state DMV variance absorbed by adapter layer without per-state code branches in domain logic; idempotent submissions for DMS retry scenarios; lien status reflected within 60 seconds
constraints.rationale: DMS systems retry on timeout creating duplicate filing risk; DMV APIs have differing required fields per state

observability_baseline.value: New Relic APM, structured JSON logging with correlation IDs, per-state DMV submission success rate, lien reflection latency p95 alert at 55 seconds
observability_baseline.rationale: Partner SLA compliance requires real-time DMV pipeline health visibility

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with state-by-state DMV integration rollouts gated by DMV sandbox E2E test pass
execution_preference.rationale: Each state DMV integration requires dedicated E2E testing against DMV sandbox before production enablement

compliance_context.value: FCRA for credit-related lien data including field-level permissible-purpose logging; state DMV electronic title regulations; GDPR for EU-resident vehicle owners; CCPA for California owner data
compliance_context.rationale: risk_level high and data_sensitivity personal both trigger compliance_context requirement

project.goal: Real-time title transfer and lien filing API replacing SFTP batch exchange
project.target_users: Dealership DMS vendors and DMV integration brokers

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3.2 with Spring Integration for DMV adapters
backend.api: RESTful OpenAPI 3.1 with versioned /v1/ prefix and X-Idempotency-Key header enforcement

security.auth: Keycloak 23 OAuth 2.0 client credentials with per-scope enforcement
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 in transit; AES-256-GCM field encryption for SSN and EIN via Azure Key Vault
security.compliance: FCRA permissible-purpose logging, state e-title regulations, GDPR, CCPA

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security per partner_tenant_id
data.cache: Redis 7 for idempotency keys (TTL 72h) and DMV polling cache (TTL 30s)

devops.ci: Azure DevOps Pipelines with WireMock DMV sandbox E2E gate
devops.environments: dev, staging (DMV sandbox), production

constraints.idempotency: DMS retries must not create duplicate DMV filings
constraints.lien_sla: Lien status reflected within 60 seconds via webhook

open_questions.multi_state_routing: State routing strategy for differing DMV API schemas resolved by Strategy pattern adapter

reasoner_reviews.product: Replacing SFTP batch with real-time REST eliminates the 24-hour title status lag that causes dealer DMS reconciliation failures. The 5-state MVP prioritizes CA, TX, FL, NY, IL which represent 80% of transaction volume and gives a clear adapter pattern template for national expansion.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Spring Integration adapter pattern is the right abstraction for DMV API variance - each state DMV exposes different field requirements and authentication schemes, and the adapter isolates this from the Title and Lien domain model. The idempotency layer via Redis is non-negotiable since dealership DMS systems will retry on any timeout, and duplicate DMV filings require expensive manual correction with state agencies.
reasoner_reviews.security: FCRA compliance requires field-level access logging - the permissible purpose must be captured per data field accessed, not just per API request. Keycloak scope enforcement must hard-block title:read clients from exercising lien:submit endpoints. SSN and lienholder EIN must be AES-256 encrypted before PostgreSQL persistence with key references stored in Azure Key Vault.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The sub-60-second lien SLA cannot be met with synchronous DMV round-trips since state DMV APIs have variable latency. The API must return a transaction_id immediately and deliver status asynchronously via webhook with idempotent retry logic for non-2xx webhook delivery responses.

backend.service_design: Domain modules: TitleModule (transfer workflows and state machine), LienModule (filing, release, and status), PlateModule (assignment per state inventory rules), DMVAdapterModule (Strategy pattern with AbstractDmvAdapter base and per-state implementations for CA, TX, FL, NY, IL), WebhookModule (async event delivery via Azure Service Bus)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful OpenAPI 3.1; POST /v1/titles/transfers (202 + transaction_id), GET /v1/transactions/:id, POST /v1/liens, DELETE /v1/liens/:id (lien release), GET /v1/titles/:vin, POST /v1/webhooks/subscriptions; X-Idempotency-Key checked against Redis before processing any state-mutating request
backend.failure_handling: DMV adapter timeouts trigger async retry with exponential backoff (1s, 5s, 30s, 2min, 10min); after 5 attempts, submission moves to dead_letter_submissions table with NEEDS_MANUAL_REVIEW status; webhook delivery retried 3 times at 1/5/30 minute intervals before marking as failed

security.auth_design: Keycloak 23 with OAuth 2.0 client credentials grant; each partner tenant has dedicated client_id with assigned scope set; tokens validated at AKS ingress via Keycloak JWT public key; token TTL 1 hour with refresh disabled for server-to-server clients
security.authorization_model: PostgreSQL row-level security policies filter all queries by partner_tenant_id extracted from JWT claim; Spring Security @PreAuthorize annotations enforce scope per controller method; title:read insufficient for title:submit
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault for PostgreSQL credentials, Redis auth token, per-state DMV API keys, and per-partner webhook signing secrets; AKS Secrets Store CSI Driver mounts Key Vault references as pod environment variables; secret rotation triggers rolling pod restart via Azure Event Grid
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only audit_events table captures partner_id, action, resource_type, resource_id, fields_accessed TEXT[], ip_address, x_access_purpose, and timestamp for every title, lien, and plate access; FCRA requires fields_accessed to name specific data elements (owner_ssn, owner_name, lien_amount)

data.entities: title_records (id UUID PK, vin CHAR(17), state_code CHAR(2), owner_name_encrypted BYTEA, owner_ssn_encrypted BYTEA, partner_tenant_id UUID, status ENUM pending/submitted/issued/rejected, dmv_transaction_ref VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), lien_records (id UUID PK, title_id UUID FK, lienholder_name VARCHAR, lienholder_ein_encrypted BYTEA, lien_amount NUMERIC(12,2), filing_date DATE, release_date DATE, status ENUM active/released/rejected), plate_assignments (id UUID PK, title_id UUID FK, plate_number VARCHAR, plate_type ENUM standard/vanity/fleet, state_code CHAR(2), assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ), dmv_submissions (id UUID PK, title_id UUID FK, state_code CHAR(2), submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, response_code VARCHAR, retry_count INT), audit_events (id UUID PK, partner_id UUID, fields_accessed TEXT[], ip_address INET, x_access_purpose VARCHAR, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server with zone redundancy; row-level security policy: CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON title_records USING (partner_tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::UUID); Redis for idempotency keys (TTL 72h) and dmv_status polling cache (TTL 30s)
data.migration_notes: Legacy SFTP batch files imported via one-time Python ETL job; VIN-to-title_record mapping seeded before API cutover; idempotency keys pre-populated for in-flight legacy transactions to prevent re-submission

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster with 3 node pools: system (2 nodes, m4.xlarge equivalent), api-workers (3-8 nodes HPA on CPU 70% and request queue depth), dmv-adapters (2-6 nodes HPA on per-state submission queue depth in Azure Service Bus); separate Deployment per functional domain
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps: Maven build, Checkstyle, SpotBugs, JUnit unit tests, Spring Boot integration tests against WireMock DMV sandbox mocks, Docker image push to ACR, Helm chart deploy to AKS via rolling update; staging deployment gates on all 5 DMV sandbox E2E tests passing
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous release on readinessProbe failure; Flyway database migrations are versioned and reversible; partner webhook subscriptions preserved across rollbacks via PostgreSQL persistence

target_output.thinking_summary: Building a real-time vehicle title and lien filing API to replace SFTP batch processing. Key decisions: Spring Integration Strategy pattern absorbs multi-state DMV API variance without polluting domain models; Redis idempotency layer prevents duplicate DMV filings on DMS retries; async filing with Azure Service Bus webhook delivery meets the sub-60-second lien SLA; FCRA field-level audit logging captures exact data elements accessed per permissible purpose.
target_output.title: Vehicle Registration and Titling REST API - DMV Modernization Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Java 21 Spring Boot 3 REST API deployed on Azure Kubernetes Service that replaces nightly SFTP batch title processing with real-time title transfer, lien filing, and plate assignment operations. Dealership DMS systems and DMV brokers authenticate via Keycloak OAuth 2.0 client credentials and call scoped endpoints. Per-state DMV adapters normalize submissions to each state DMV API. Status events are delivered asynchronously via HMAC-signed webhooks. FCRA-compliant field-level audit logging tracks every access to owner PII and lien data with permissible purpose.
target_output.architecture_overview: API-first service on AKS with a Spring Boot 3 core handling inbound partner requests authenticated by Keycloak JWT validation at the NGINX ingress layer. Title, lien, and plate domain modules implement business workflows independently. A DMVAdapterModule implements the Strategy pattern with one AbstractDmvAdapter subclass per state DMV API, isolating field mapping and authentication quirks from the domain model. Redis handles idempotency key deduplication before any state-mutating operation. PostgreSQL 16 persists all records with Azure Key Vault-managed AES-256-GCM encryption for SSN and EIN fields and row-level security enforcing partner tenant isolation. Webhook events publish to Azure Service Bus and a dedicated webhook worker delivers signed payloads to partner endpoints with exponential retry.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Java 21 with virtual threads, Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Integration, Spring Security with Keycloak JWT validation, springdoc-openapi for OpenAPI 3.1 spec generation. Data: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server with row-level security, Redis 7 Azure Cache for idempotency and polling cache. Async: Azure Service Bus for webhook event queue. Auth: Keycloak 23 OAuth 2.0. Infrastructure: AKS East US 2, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault with Secrets Store CSI Driver. Observability: New Relic APM Java agent, Logback structured JSON, Azure Monitor for cluster metrics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Title Transfer Filing: POST /v1/titles/transfers validates VIN format and state eligibility, creates title_record with status=pending, submits to state DMV adapter, returns 202 with transaction_id. Lien Filing and Release: POST /v1/liens creates lien_record encrypted with AES-256-GCM, files to DMV, triggers lien.created webhook within 60s; DELETE /v1/liens/:id files lien release and delivers lien.released webhook. Plate Assignment: POST /v1/plates/assign links plate to active title per state plate inventory rules. Status Polling: GET /v1/transactions/:id returns current DMV submission status from Redis cache or live DMV query. Webhook Subscriptions: POST /v1/webhooks/subscriptions registers HTTPS endpoint and event type filter per partner tenant.
target_output.system_components: AKS NGINX Ingress (TLS termination, Keycloak JWT validation middleware), Spring Boot API Deployment (3-8 replicas, HPA CPU 70%), DMV Adapter Worker Deployment (2-6 replicas per state queue depth), Keycloak Identity Provider (AKS-deployed HA), PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server (zone redundant, read replica for audit queries), Redis 7 Azure Cache Standard tier (idempotency keys and DMV status cache), Azure Service Bus (webhook event queue with dead-letter), Webhook Delivery Worker (Spring Integration outbound, 1-3 replicas), Azure Key Vault (SSN/EIN encryption keys, DMV API credentials, webhook signing secrets), Azure Container Registry.
target_output.workflows: Title Transfer: Dealership DMS sends POST /v1/titles/transfers with VIN, seller_id, buyer details, and X-Idempotency-Key header. API checks Redis for existing key - hit returns cached 202 response. Miss: validates VIN format and state eligibility, encrypts owner SSN via Key Vault AES-256-GCM, inserts title_record with status=pending, writes idempotency key to Redis TTL 72h, enqueues DMV submission to Azure Service Bus. DMV Adapter Worker picks up job, routes to state-specific adapter (CaDmvAdapter, TxDmvAdapter, etc.), maps fields to state DMV schema, calls state DMV API, updates title_record with response and dmv_transaction_ref, publishes title.submitted event to Service Bus. Webhook Worker reads event, resolves partner webhook_subscriptions, signs payload with HMAC-SHA256 using per-partner secret from Key Vault, sends HTTPS POST to partner endpoint, logs delivery receipt. Lien Filing: Finance company DMS sends POST /v1/liens with title_id, lienholder_name, lienholder_ein, lien_amount. API encrypts EIN, inserts lien_record status=active, submits to DMV adapter. DMV reflects lien and adapter updates lien_record; webhook delivers lien.created event within 60-second SLA window.
target_output.data_model: title_records (id UUID PK, vin CHAR(17) NOT NULL, state_code CHAR(2), owner_name_encrypted BYTEA, owner_ssn_encrypted BYTEA, partner_tenant_id UUID NOT NULL, status ENUM pending/submitted/issued/rejected/cancelled, dmv_transaction_ref VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). lien_records (id UUID PK, title_id UUID FK REFERENCES title_records, lienholder_name VARCHAR NOT NULL, lienholder_ein_encrypted BYTEA, lien_amount NUMERIC(12,2), filing_date DATE, release_date DATE, status ENUM active/released/rejected). plate_assignments (id UUID PK, title_id UUID FK, plate_number VARCHAR, plate_type ENUM standard/vanity/fleet, state_code CHAR(2), assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ). dmv_submissions (id UUID PK, title_id UUID FK, state_code CHAR(2), submission_hash VARCHAR, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, response_code VARCHAR, retry_count INT DEFAULT 0). audit_events (id UUID PK, partner_id UUID, action VARCHAR, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id UUID, fields_accessed TEXT[], ip_address INET, x_access_purpose VARCHAR, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). Row-level security on title_records, lien_records, plate_assignments enforced by partner_tenant_id.
target_output.api_design: OpenAPI 3.1 spec at /v1/openapi.json. POST /v1/titles/transfers - submit title transfer (scope: title:submit; returns 202 + {transaction_id, status: 'pending'}). GET /v1/transactions/:id - poll DMV submission status (scope: title:read). POST /v1/liens - file new lien (scope: lien:submit; body: {title_id, lienholder_name, lienholder_ein, lien_amount}; returns 202 + {lien_id}). DELETE /v1/liens/:id - file lien release (scope: lien:submit). GET /v1/titles/:vin - retrieve current title record fields (scope: title:read; FCRA: requires X-Access-Purpose header). POST /v1/webhooks/subscriptions - register callback URL and event_types array (scope: title:read). GET /v1/plates/availability?state=TX - check plate inventory. X-Idempotency-Key required on all POST mutations. Rate limit: 500 req/min per client via Redis token bucket enforced at ingress.
target_output.security_and_compliance: FCRA field-level controls: every GET /v1/titles/:vin call writes to audit_events with fields_accessed=['owner_name','owner_ssn'] and x_access_purpose from required header; FCRA permissible purposes (credit transaction, account review, employment) enforced via allowed-values validation. Keycloak OAuth scopes: title:submit required for title transfer POST; lien:read insufficient for lien:submit; Keycloak introspection blocks mismatched scope attempts. PostgreSQL RLS: all queries filtered by SET LOCAL app.tenant_id = :partner_id before execution, preventing cross-tenant record leakage. SSN and EIN fields encrypted with AES-256-GCM using Azure Key Vault managed keys before PostgreSQL persistence; keys rotated annually via Key Vault rotation policy. Webhook payloads signed with HMAC-SHA256 using per-partner secrets stored in Key Vault; partners verify X-Webhook-Signature header before processing. GDPR right-to-erasure: owner PII pseudonymized in title_records upon verified erasure request within archival retention constraints; erasure logged to audit_events.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS cluster East US 2 with three node pools: system (2 Standard_D4s_v3, always-on), api-workers (3-8 Standard_D4s_v3, HPA on CPU 70% and /actuator/metrics/service.bus.queue.depth), dmv-adapter-workers (2-6 Standard_D4s_v3, HPA on per-state Service Bus queue depth). Namespaces: dev, staging, production with Kubernetes NetworkPolicy restricting cross-namespace API traffic. Azure DevOps CI: Maven build, Checkstyle strict, SpotBugs, JUnit 5 unit tests, Spring integration tests with WireMock DMV sandbox for all 5 states, Docker build, ACR push, Helm chart upgrade --atomic on AKS. Staging deployment gates require all 5 state DMV WireMock E2E suites passing with 100% title transfer and lien scenarios. Secrets: Secrets Store CSI Driver mounts Azure Key Vault references; no secrets in container images or ConfigMaps. Flyway manages schema migrations with version-locked reversible V-scripts; migration applied via AKS one-off Job before rolling deployment.
target_output.observability: New Relic APM Java agent on all pods with distributed trace propagation via W3C Trace Context headers through Service Bus message attributes. Custom metrics: dmv_submission_success_rate per state_code (alert < 95%), lien_reflection_latency_p95 (alert > 55s, critical > 60s), webhook_delivery_success_rate (alert < 98%), idempotency_cache_hit_rate, partner_request_rate per client_id. Structured JSON logs via Logback with correlation_id, partner_id, state_code, operation_type, transaction_id fields. Azure Monitor for AKS node CPU, memory, and Service Bus queue depth. PagerDuty escalations: DMV adapter error rate > 5% per state, lien p95 > 55s, PostgreSQL connection pool > 80% utilization.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $3,100/month for AKS cluster at baseline 5 nodes across pools, Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server zone-redundant ($420/month), Azure Cache for Redis Standard C2 ($200/month), Azure Service Bus Standard ($50/month). HPA prevents over-provisioning during off-peak hours (evenings, weekends). DMV adapter workers use spot node pool (interruption-tolerant via retry queue) reducing adapter compute cost by ~40%. PostgreSQL read replica added for audit query workload when audit reporting feature ships in Phase 2.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (16 weeks): 5-state DMV adapters (CA, TX, FL, NY, IL), POST /v1/titles/transfers and POST /v1/liens endpoints, Keycloak OAuth 2.0, PostgreSQL with RLS and AES-256-GCM field encryption, Redis idempotency, Azure Service Bus webhook delivery, New Relic APM, Azure DevOps CI/CD, FCRA audit logging. Phase 2 (12 weeks): expand to 20 states, DELETE /v1/liens/:id lien release, POST /v1/plates/assign, FCRA audit reporting API for compliance team, partner developer portal with sandbox environment. Phase 3 (ongoing): all 50 states, consumer digital title wallet API, EV battery certification title fields, AAMVA DLExchange cross-state validation integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 virtual threads for DMV adapter I/O to avoid blocking platform threads on slow state API calls. All AbstractDmvAdapter subclasses must implement submitTitle(), submitLien(), submitLienRelease(), and getTransactionStatus() contract methods. SpotBugs and Checkstyle run in CI gate; zero high-severity findings permitted. JUnit 5 with WireMock for all DMV adapter unit tests; 85% line coverage required on TitleModule and LienModule. PRs touching encryption code paths or FCRA audit logging require two senior engineer approvals. Feature flags via Azure App Configuration for per-state production enablement.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: state DMV sandbox environments do not accurately reflect production API behavior, causing integration failures at production launch. Mitigation: maintain internal WireMock mocks with production-equivalent schemas; shadow-traffic test each state with real transactions before GA. Risk: Redis TTL of 72 hours insufficient for DMS retry windows spanning extended weekends plus holiday closures. Mitigation: add persistent idempotency_requests table as fallback after Redis miss. Tradeoff: async filing with webhook delivery means partners cannot receive a synchronous DMV confirmation - mitigated by transaction_id polling endpoint available until webhook arrives. Tradeoff: per-state adapter subclasses increase codebase size but are far preferable to a single adapter with state-specific flag branches that would make unit testing and DMV schema changes error-prone.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Async webhook delivery chosen over synchronous DMV confirmation to meet sub-60-second lien SLA without blocking Spring threads on variable DMV API latency. Keycloak chosen over Azure AD for granular per-scope token enforcement required for FCRA permissible-purpose field separation. PostgreSQL RLS chosen over application-layer tenant filtering to guarantee isolation even in the presence of application-layer bugs in multi-tenant query construction.

metadata.notes: Vehicle titling API replacing SFTP batch; FCRA field-level compliance; async DMV filing with webhook delivery; Spring Integration state adapter pattern.

project_goal.value: Replace legacy SOAP-based DMS integration with a modern async Python SDK exposing typed clients for inventory, repair order, and customer data operations
project_goal.rationale: Legacy SOAP integration is brittle and untested, blocking new DMS provider onboarding

target_users.value: Python application developers integrating dealership back-office systems with external services
target_users.rationale: SDK consumers are internal and partner developers building on top of DMS data

project_class.rationale: Published Python package with no hosted runtime; consumer applications own deployment

capabilities.rationale: Async HTTP client, typed data models, and DMS provider adapters; no UI or auth surface

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-provider adapter pattern, SOAP compatibility shim, and async pagination logic

risk_level.rationale: Handles dealership customer and vehicle data; SDK misuse could leak records to unauthorized callers

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vehicle inventory and repair order data is proprietary dealership operational data

external_exposure.rationale: SDK published to PyPI and used by partner integration developers

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials per DMS tenant; SDK manages token lifecycle and refresh
access_model.rationale: DMS APIs require per-dealer OAuth tokens; SDK abstracts token refresh from consumer

feature_scope.value: Async clients for vehicle inventory, repair orders, customer records, service appointments, parts orders; SOAP compatibility shim for legacy field name mapping; pagination helpers; typed Pydantic dataclasses for all entities
feature_scope.rationale: Full DMS entity coverage for dealer integrations

mvp_scope.value: Inventory and repair order clients with SOAP shim, OAuth token management, paginated list methods, typed Pydantic models for core entities
mvp_scope.rationale: Unblocks highest-volume integration workflows first

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 enforced for all DMS API calls; OAuth tokens never cached to disk; credentials passed via environment variables, not constructor arguments
security_baseline.rationale: SDK must not introduce credential persistence vulnerabilities in consumer apps

frontend_stack.value: None — Python library with no UI surface
frontend_stack.rationale: SDK published as PyPI package; no frontend components

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with httpx for async HTTP
backend_stack.rationale: httpx provides async/sync dual-mode HTTP with HTTP/2 support

data_platform.value: None — SDK is stateless; no embedded database
data_platform.rationale: State management is the consumer application responsibility

hosting_target.value: PyPI package registry
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python package distribution; consumers install via pip or poetry

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK does not persist data; consumer applications are responsible for retention of data retrieved via SDK
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Library pattern — no embedded storage

future_scope.value: Additional DMS provider adapters (CDK Global, Reynolds and Reynolds), GraphQL client variant, async event streaming for real-time RO updates
future_scope.rationale: Planned expansion after MVP DMS provider coverage

constraints.value: Must support Python 3.10+, minimal transitive dependencies (httpx and pydantic only), no vendor lock-in to single DMS provider
constraints.rationale: Broad consumer compatibility and maintainability requirements

observability_baseline.value: Structured logging via stdlib logging module; optional request/response trace hook for consumer debugging; no telemetry emitted by default
observability_baseline.rationale: Library must not impose observability dependencies on consumer apps

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with CHANGELOG; breaking changes only on major version bumps
execution_preference.rationale: Partner developers need API stability guarantees

compliance_context.value: DMS vendor data use agreements restrict SDK from caching or re-exporting raw DMS records; GDPR applies to customer PII retrieved via SDK for EU dealerships
compliance_context.rationale: DMS vendor agreements and GDPR create data handling obligations for SDK consumers

project.goal: Async Python SDK replacing legacy SOAP DMS integration
project.target_users: Python developers integrating with dealership management systems

backend.framework: httpx 0.27 async HTTP client, Pydantic v2 for data models
backend.soap_shim: custom SOAP response parser, no zeep dependency

security.auth: OAuth 2.0 client_credentials grant; in-memory token cache with expiry-aware refresh

data.entities: Pydantic dataclasses: VehicleInventory, RepairOrder, CustomerRecord, ServiceAppointment, PartsOrder
data.pagination: AsyncPageIterator helper returning typed entity pages

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with pytest, mypy strict, ruff, PyPI publish on version tag

reasoner_reviews.product: The SOAP compatibility shim is the highest-risk component — field name mapping rules must be driven by a configurable mapping table rather than hardcoded to allow per-provider overrides without SDK version bumps.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Pydantic v2 dataclasses provide fast validation and clean serialization. Async-first design with sync wrapper via asyncio.run() avoids duplicating client logic across two implementations.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth tokens must be cached only in-memory using a thread-safe structure keyed by tenant and scopes. The SDK must never write tokens to disk, log token values, or include them in exception tracebacks. CustomerRecord repr must mask email and phone fields.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Keeping dependencies to httpx and pydantic only is achievable. Custom SOAP parsing using stdlib xml.etree.ElementTree avoids adding lxml or zeep.

backend.service_design: Three-layer package: dms_sdk.providers (BaseDMSProvider ABC plus concrete adapters loaded with DMSProviderConfig from YAML), dms_sdk.clients (InventoryClient, RepairOrderClient, CustomerClient, AppointmentClient, PartsClient), dms_sdk.models (Pydantic v2 frozen dataclasses for all DMS entities).
backend.api_patterns: Async-first: await client.list_vehicles(page_size=50) returns AsyncPageIterator[VehicleInventory]. Sync wrapper DMSClientSync wraps async methods via asyncio.run(). All mutating operations return typed response objects.
backend.failure_handling: httpx.TimeoutException and httpx.HTTPStatusError raised to consumer with provider context. 401 response triggers one automatic token refresh before raising AuthExpiredError.

security.auth_design: OAuth2TokenManager handles client_credentials grant, caches token and expiry in-memory dict, refreshes proactively 60 seconds before expiry. Token value never serialized or logged.
security.secrets_management: Credentials accepted via DMSCredentials dataclass initialized from environment variables DMS_CLIENT_ID and DMS_CLIENT_SECRET; raw credential values excluded from dataclass __repr__.
security.security_risks: Consumers may inadvertently log full response objects containing customer PII; SDK provides sanitized __repr__ on CustomerRecord and ServiceAppointment, and documentation warns against debug logging in production.

data.entities: VehicleInventory (vin, stock_number, year, make, model, mileage, status, list_price, cost_basis), RepairOrder (ro_number, vin, customer_id, opened_at, closed_at, status, line_items, labor_total, parts_total), CustomerRecord (customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, phone, gdpr_consent_date) with PII-masked repr, ServiceAppointment (appt_id, customer_id, vin, scheduled_at, advisor_id, services_requested), PartsOrder (po_number, ro_number, parts, ordered_at, received_at, status).
data.schema_notes: All entities use Pydantic v2 model_config frozen=True. SOAP field names mapped via DMSProviderConfig.field_mappings dict loaded from bundled YAML per provider.
data.migration_notes: SoapCompatShim.from_soap_response(xml_element, provider_config, entity_type) resolves legacy XML field names to Pydantic model fields via provider YAML mapping, enabling entity-by-entity REST migration without changing downstream attribute access.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR — ruff, mypy --strict, pytest with respx mocks. On version tag — build sdist + wheel, publish to PyPI via OIDC trusted publishing. Integration test job on main branch uses DMS sandbox credentials from Actions secrets.
devops.rollback_strategy: PyPI yanked release for security issues; consumers pin to last known good version in requirements files. CHANGELOG documents breaking changes per semver policy.

ISS-001.title: SOAP shim uses hardcoded field mapping constants blocking multi-provider support
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described SoapFieldMapper using module-level constant dicts INVENTORY_FIELD_MAP and RO_FIELD_MAP with no per-provider override path, preventing new DMS provider onboarding without SDK code changes.

history.note: Identified in architect_reasoner review — hardcoded constants block roadmap

ISS-002.title: CustomerRecord default repr emits raw email and phone fields
ISS-002.detail: Pydantic default __repr__ includes email and phone verbatim in log output and exception messages, violating DMS data use agreement and GDPR guidelines.

history.note: Raised in security specialist review

previous_audits.summary: Clean async SDK architecture with good provider abstraction, but SOAP shim is inflexible and CustomerRecord PII repr is a compliance gap.
strengths: Clean three-layer package structure with provider/client/model separation
OAuth2TokenManager in-memory caching with proactive refresh is well-designed
Pydantic v2 frozen models prevent post-fetch mutation
concerns: SOAP field mappings hardcoded — cannot onboard new DMS provider without releasing a new SDK version
CustomerRecord __repr__ emits raw email and phone to any logging consumer

Override __repr__ on all PII-bearing models to redact sensitive fields

previous_plan.title: DMS Async Python SDK Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Async Python SDK replacing SOAP DMS integration with typed httpx clients for vehicle inventory, repair orders, and customer records.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Three-layer package: providers with SoapFieldMapper using hardcoded field maps, clients wrapping provider adapters, Pydantic v2 frozen models with default repr.
previous_plan.data_model: VehicleInventory, RepairOrder, CustomerRecord with Pydantic v2. SOAP parsing via SoapFieldMapper with hardcoded INVENTORY_FIELD_MAP and RO_FIELD_MAP module constants.
previous_plan.development_guidelines: All client methods unit-testable with respx mock transport. Public API fully typed and passing mypy --strict.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising DMS SDK to fix ISS-001 by replacing hardcoded SOAP field maps with a configurable DMSProviderConfig loaded from bundled YAML per provider, and ISS-002 by adding custom PII-masked __repr__ to CustomerRecord and ServiceAppointment. The YAML-driven config approach also unlocks the multi-provider roadmap without requiring SDK code changes per new DMS vendor.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced hardcoded SoapFieldMapper module constants with DMSProviderConfig dataclass containing field_mappings dict loaded from bundled YAML files per provider (e.g., providers/automate.yaml, providers/dealer_socket.yaml). SoapFieldMapper now accepts DMSProviderConfig at init and resolves field names dynamically per entity type.
fix_report.expected_outcome: New DMS providers onboarded by adding a YAML config file without Python code changes; existing adapters use bundled YAML seamlessly.

fix_report.action_taken: Added model_config with custom __repr__ override on CustomerRecord and ServiceAppointment masking email and phone: repr format CustomerRecord(customer_id=C-1234, email=[REDACTED], phone=[REDACTED]). Added pytest assertion verifying repr output does not contain raw email or phone values.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CustomerRecord repr safe for log output and exception messages; DMS data use agreement and GDPR repr requirements satisfied.

target_output.title: DMS Async Python SDK — Configurable Provider Adapters
target_output.executive_summary: An async Python SDK published on PyPI that modernizes dealership DMS integration by replacing SOAP envelope construction with typed httpx clients for vehicle inventory, repair orders, customer records, service appointments, and parts orders. Provider adapters use YAML-configurable field mappings enabling multi-DMS support without code changes, and all customer-facing models emit PII-masked repr output compliant with DMS data use agreements.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-layer package: dms_sdk.providers contains BaseDMSProvider ABC and concrete adapter classes (AutomateDMSProvider, DealerSocketProvider) initialized with DMSProviderConfig loaded from bundled YAML specifying DMS base URL, OAuth token endpoint, and entity field mappings. dms_sdk.clients contains InventoryClient, RepairOrderClient, CustomerClient, AppointmentClient, and PartsClient accepting a BaseDMSProvider instance and using it for URL construction and SOAP field resolution. dms_sdk.models contains Pydantic v2 frozen dataclasses for all DMS entities with PII-safe repr on customer-bearing models. SoapCompatShim parses legacy XML using the provider field_mapping config, enabling gradual REST migration per entity without requiring simultaneous cutover.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 (supports 3.10+), httpx 0.27 async HTTP with HTTP/2, Pydantic v2 frozen dataclasses with custom repr, PyYAML for provider config loading (bundled in package), xml.etree.ElementTree (stdlib) for SOAP XML parsing. Dev dependencies: pytest, pytest-asyncio, respx for HTTP mocking, mypy strict, ruff. Distribution: PyPI via GitHub Actions OIDC trusted publishing.
target_output.functional_feature_map: InventoryClient: list_vehicles(filters, page_size) returning AsyncPageIterator[VehicleInventory], get_vehicle(vin). RepairOrderClient: list_repair_orders(status, since) returning AsyncPageIterator[RepairOrder], get_repair_order(ro_number). CustomerClient: get_customer(customer_id) returning CustomerRecord with PII-masked repr, search_customers(email) returning list[CustomerRecord]. AppointmentClient: list_appointments(date_range) returning AsyncPageIterator[ServiceAppointment]. SoapCompatShim: from_soap_response(xml_element, provider_config, entity_type) returning typed entity using configurable field mappings from YAML. OAuth2TokenManager: transparent client_credentials token refresh with in-memory cache keyed by tenant and scopes.
target_output.system_components: DMSProviderConfig dataclass (base_url, token_endpoint, field_mappings per entity, loaded from bundled YAML). BaseDMSProvider ABC with abstract resolve_url and get_config methods. Concrete adapters: AutomateDMSProvider (providers/automate.yaml), DealerSocketProvider (providers/dealer_socket.yaml). Entity clients: InventoryClient, RepairOrderClient, CustomerClient, AppointmentClient, PartsClient. AsyncPageIterator generic lazy page iterator. OAuth2TokenManager in-memory token cache with proactive refresh at T-60s. SoapCompatShim XML parser using provider field_mapping config.
target_output.workflows: Inventory Fetch: Developer instantiates AutomateDMSProvider(DMSCredentials.from_env()), passes to InventoryClient. await client.list_vehicles(status='available') opens async iterator; OAuth2TokenManager resolves in-memory cached token or fetches new one via client_credentials, attaches Bearer header; httpx GET to provider base_url/inventory?page=1; response validated into VehicleInventory Pydantic models yielded lazily. SOAP Migration: Developer calls SoapCompatShim.from_soap_response(xml_element, provider.get_config(), 'repair_order'); shim reads provider YAML field_mapping to translate legacy XML field names (e.g., 'RoNum' to 'ro_number') and constructs typed RepairOrder dataclass transparently.
target_output.data_model: VehicleInventory (vin: str, stock_number: str, year: int, make: str, model: str, trim: str, mileage: int, status: Literal['available','sold','in_transit'], list_price: Decimal, cost_basis: Decimal, location_code: str) — frozen Pydantic model. RepairOrder (ro_number: str, vin: str, customer_id: str, opened_at: datetime, closed_at: datetime | None, status: Literal['open','in_progress','closed'], line_items: list[ROLineItem], labor_total: Decimal, parts_total: Decimal). CustomerRecord (customer_id: str, first_name: str, last_name: str, email: str, phone: str, gdpr_consent_date: date | None) with custom __repr__ masking email and phone fields. DMSProviderConfig (provider_id: str, base_url: str, token_endpoint: str, field_mappings: dict[str, dict[str, str]]) loaded from YAML — example: providers/automate.yaml defines field_mappings.vehicle_inventory.stockNum = 'stock_number'.
target_output.api_design: Public SDK API (no HTTP server surface). InventoryClient.list_vehicles(status, page_size=50) -> AsyncPageIterator[VehicleInventory]. InventoryClient.get_vehicle(vin: str) -> VehicleInventory. RepairOrderClient.list_repair_orders(status, since, page_size=50) -> AsyncPageIterator[RepairOrder]. CustomerClient.get_customer(customer_id: str) -> CustomerRecord. SoapCompatShim.from_soap_response(xml_element, config, entity_type) -> BaseModel. OAuth2TokenManager.get_token(provider_config) -> str (async, cached). All methods fully typed; mypy strict compliance required.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth tokens cached in-memory dict keyed by (provider_id, scopes); never written to disk or emitted in log lines. TLS 1.3 enforced via httpx ssl_context; SSL verification always enabled — no public API option to disable. CustomerRecord and ServiceAppointment custom __repr__ masks email and phone: CustomerRecord(customer_id=C-1234, email=[REDACTED], phone=[REDACTED]) — unit tested with assertion repr(record) does not contain raw email value. DMSCredentials repr excludes client_secret field. GDPR: SDK documentation guidance for EU dealerships notes that CustomerRecord contains personal data subject to GDPR Article 5 retention limits owned by consumer application.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: on PR — ruff lint, mypy --strict, pytest -x with respx HTTP mocks for all DMS endpoints. On version tag vX.Y.Z — python -m build generates sdist and wheel, published to PyPI via OIDC trusted publishing with environment protection requiring tag pattern. Integration test job on main branch: live DMS sandbox credentials from Actions secrets, validates InventoryClient and SoapCompatShim against live sandbox responses. Provider YAML config files bundled in package via pyproject.toml package-data.
target_output.observability: SDK emits structured log records via logging.getLogger('dms_sdk') at DEBUG for request lifecycle and INFO for token refresh events. No telemetry or metrics emitted — library pattern. Optional on_request and on_response hooks on DMSProvider for consumer-owned tracing. Documentation examples show how to attach a logging.StreamHandler to capture SDK diagnostics in consumer app logs without SDK imposing a handler.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost — stateless PyPI package; no server infrastructure. Consumer application owns all infrastructure. AsyncPageIterator yields entities lazily — memory bounded to one page regardless of total inventory size. httpx connection pool amortizes TLS handshake across paginated requests to same DMS provider.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): InventoryClient and RepairOrderClient with AutomateDMSProvider, DMSProviderConfig YAML loading, SoapCompatShim with configurable field mappings, OAuth2TokenManager, Pydantic v2 models with PII-masked repr on CustomerRecord, GitHub Actions CI with respx mocks and PyPI trusted publishing. Phase 2 (3 weeks): CustomerClient, AppointmentClient, PartsClient; DealerSocketProvider YAML config; full async pagination with AsyncPageIterator. Phase 3 (3 weeks): CDK Global provider adapter, DMSClientSync wrapper, optional dms-sdk-testing companion package with pre-recorded respx fixtures.
target_output.development_guidelines: All client methods unit-tested with respx mock transport — zero live DMS calls in pytest suite. Provider YAML files validated against JSON Schema at DMSProviderConfig load time. PII-masked repr asserted in unit tests: assert raw_email not in repr(customer_record). Public methods fully typed and passing mypy --strict. No new dependencies beyond httpx and pydantic without explicit team review. Breaking changes documented in CHANGELOG.md with migration guide before major version increment.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DMS provider updates REST schema breaking field mappings in provider YAML. Mitigation: CI integration test validates YAML mappings against sandbox responses; YAML versioning with provider_schema_version field. Risk: SOAP shim coverage gaps for uncommon entity fields. Mitigation: SoapCompatShim logs WARNING with field name when encountering unmapped XML attributes, prompting YAML additions. Tradeoff: YAML-driven field mapping adds config maintenance overhead vs. hardcoded — justified by multi-provider onboarding without SDK code releases. Tradeoff: async-first API with asyncio.run() sync wrapper may conflict with consumer apps running an event loop — documented with alternative thread executor pattern.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: SOAP parsing uses stdlib xml.etree.ElementTree eliminating zeep and lxml dependencies. DMSProviderConfig loaded from YAML bundled in package — consumers do not supply config files unless adding a new provider. OAuth token refresh proactive at T-60s before expiry to prevent mid-pagination expiry during large inventory fetches.

metadata.notes: Python SDK modernizing SOAP DMS integration with YAML-configurable provider adapters and PII-safe model repr.

project_goal.value: Driver coaching mobile app using on-device ML to analyze driving behavior in real time and deliver personalized safety scores and coaching nudges
project_goal.rationale: Fleet operators need measurable safety improvement with driver buy-in; on-device inference enables real-time coaching without connectivity dependency

target_users.value: Commercial fleet drivers using the app during trips, and fleet safety managers reviewing aggregate coaching data
target_users.rationale: Dual audience: drivers need real-time feedback while driving; managers need fleet-level reporting

project_class.rationale: Primary interaction surface is a mobile app running during trips; on-device ML inference is a core capability

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, analytics, realtime
capabilities.rationale: Flutter UI, FastAPI backend, on-device TFLite model, Firebase Auth, PostgreSQL analytics, real-time trip upload

complexity_level.rationale: On-device ML inference with model versioning, offline buffering, GDPR consent for behavioral monitoring, leaderboard gamification

risk_level.rationale: Behavioral monitoring data is personal and subject to employer disclosure requirements; no financial or health data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Driving behavior scores, GPS trip data, and harsh-event logs constitute personal data under GDPR and California driver privacy laws

external_exposure.rationale: App requires Firebase Auth login; no public-facing endpoints

access_model.value: Firebase Auth with custom claims for driver vs fleet_manager roles; fleet_manager role gates access to aggregate coaching dashboards and all-driver score exports
access_model.rationale: Drivers can only access their own trip data; managers access fleet-level aggregates with consent scope restrictions

feature_scope.value: On-device real-time harsh event detection (braking, cornering, acceleration), trip safety score calculation, in-app coaching nudges, post-trip behavior upload, fleet leaderboard (opt-in), safety trend charts, fleet manager dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: End-to-end coaching loop from detection through gamified improvement

mvp_scope.value: Real-time on-device harsh event detection, post-trip score upload, per-driver coaching history, fleet manager aggregate view with no leaderboard or gamification in MVP
mvp_scope.rationale: Leaderboard requires opt-in consent framework deferred to Phase 2

security_baseline.value: Firebase Auth with ID token validation on all API calls, TLS 1.3, GDPR consent records for behavioral data, driver opt-in for leaderboard participation
security_baseline.rationale: Employer monitoring disclosure laws require explicit consent for behavioral scoring and leaderboard sharing

frontend_stack.value: Flutter 3 with Dart; TFLite plugin for on-device inference; Riverpod for state management
frontend_stack.rationale: Flutter enables single codebase for iOS and Android; TFLite runs efficiently on mobile GPU for accelerometer classification

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI on GKE Autopilot
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async handlers for trip upload and score recalculation; GKE Autopilot for managed scaling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for driver scorecards and trip records; Redis 7 for leaderboard rankings and session state
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for relational trip and score data; Redis sorted sets for real-time leaderboard ranking queries

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in us-central1 with global Cloud CDN for static assets and model binary distribution
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot eliminates node pool management; Cloud CDN delivers TFLite model updates globally with low latency

privacy_retention_policy.value: Trip behavior events retained 24 months; safety scores retained 36 months; raw GPS coordinates anonymized after 90 days; leaderboard participation data deleted immediately on opt-out
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR data minimization and retention limits for behavioral monitoring data; California driver privacy law storage restrictions

future_scope.value: Peer coaching challenges between drivers, insurance telematics export for UBI programs, predictive fatigue detection using trip pattern analysis
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 differentiation features leveraging accumulated behavioral dataset

constraints.value: On-device inference must run on mid-range Android phones (Snapdragon 665 or equivalent); app must function fully offline during trips with buffered upload on reconnect; TFLite model binary under 15MB
constraints.rationale: Fleet drivers often operate in areas with poor cellular coverage; mid-range hardware constraint reflects fleet-issued device specs

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus on GKE, Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crash reporting, per-driver trip upload success rate, model inference latency p95 on device
observability_baseline.rationale: Mobile crash visibility and trip data completeness are primary reliability KPIs

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; mobile releases via Firebase App Distribution to beta fleet before Play Store/App Store release
execution_preference.rationale: Beta fleet testing catches device-specific inference issues before public release

compliance_context.value: GDPR Articles 6 and 9 for employee behavioral monitoring data; California Labor Code Section 3551 employer monitoring disclosure; state-level driver privacy laws; employer monitoring disclosure requirements before app installation
compliance_context.rationale: data_sensitivity personal and capabilities includes ai_llm trigger compliance_context requirement

llm_integration.value: TFLite binary classification model (harsh_event_classifier_v2.tflite) bundled in app assets for on-device inference on accelerometer and GPS features; model updates distributed via Cloud Storage signed URLs and applied on next app foreground without store update
llm_integration.rationale: capabilities includes ai_llm; on-device inference required for offline operation and sub-100ms event detection latency

project.goal: Real-time on-device driver behavior coaching with post-trip score backend
project.target_users: Fleet drivers and safety managers
project.timeline: MVP in 14 weeks; Phase 2 leaderboard in 8 additional weeks

frontend.state: Riverpod 2 for async state and trip session management
frontend.ml: TFLite 2.14 plugin for on-device harsh event classification

backend.framework: FastAPI with async SQLAlchemy
backend.api: REST with Firebase ID token validation middleware

security.auth: Firebase Auth with custom claims (driver/fleet_manager)
security.encryption: TLS 1.3; GPS coordinates hashed after 90 days via Cloud Scheduler job
security.compliance: GDPR consent records, employer monitoring disclosure pre-enrollment

data.cache: Redis 7 for leaderboard sorted sets and trip session state

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for backend; Fastlane for mobile
devops.environments: dev, staging, production; Firebase App Distribution for beta

constraints.offline: Full trip recording offline; upload on reconnect

reasoner_reviews.product: On-device inference is the right architectural choice for this use case - driving often occurs in areas with spotty cellular coverage, and a coaching app that requires connectivity to detect harsh braking would be unusable. The opt-in leaderboard is a strong engagement mechanic for Phase 2 but deferring it to avoid the consent complexity in MVP is the right call.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: TFLite with Riverpod's StreamProvider is a clean integration pattern - the accelerometer stream feeds the inference engine and emits harsh_event objects that the UI observes reactively. The model update distribution via Cloud Storage signed URLs rather than app store releases is important for rapid iteration on model performance without store review delays. The offline trip buffer needs a size limit and eviction policy to handle drivers who forget to open the app for days.
reasoner_reviews.security: GDPR requires a lawful basis for processing behavioral monitoring data - legitimate interest alone is insufficient for employment monitoring in EU; explicit consent is the safest basis and must be captured before any trip recording begins. The leaderboard opt-in must be granular: a driver can consent to scoring but not leaderboard participation. Firebase custom claims should encode both role and gdpr_consent_version to invalidate sessions when consent policy updates.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: TFLite model under 15MB is achievable with quantization (INT8) but requires post-training quantization and on-device accuracy validation on the target Snapdragon 665 chipset. The 90-day GPS anonymization window aligns with GDPR data minimization but the anonymization job must use consistent GPS grid-snapping rather than deletion to preserve route analytics without individual tracking.

frontend.app_structure: Flutter app with feature-based structure: /trip (recording, real-time detection, score display), /coaching (history, trend charts, nudges), /leaderboard (opt-in Phase 2), /profile (consent management, opt-out). Background service using flutter_background_service for continuous accelerometer sampling during trips
frontend.pages_and_flows: Trip Start screen with pre-trip GDPR consent reminder, Active Trip screen with real-time harsh event indicators and running score, Trip Summary screen with event breakdown and coaching tip, Coaching History screen with 30/60/90-day score trends, Fleet Manager Dashboard (web view via Flutter WebView for complex charts)
frontend.state_management: Riverpod 2: TripSessionNotifier manages accelerometer stream, TFLite inference, and offline event buffer; CoachingHistoryProvider fetches paginated trip records from backend; LeaderboardProvider polls Redis-backed ranking API
frontend.performance_notes: TFLite inference runs on Flutter Isolate to avoid blocking UI thread; accelerometer sampled at 50Hz during trips; harsh event detector uses 2-second sliding window with 0.5s step for braking/cornering classification; model cold-start time < 200ms on Snapdragon 665

backend.service_design: FastAPI routers: /trips (upload, list), /scores (driver safety score history), /coaching (personalized tip generation), /leaderboard (fleet ranking, opt-in management), /admin (fleet manager aggregate views). Async SQLAlchemy with connection pool sized to GKE pod count
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/trips/upload (accepts compressed NDJSON event batch), GET /v1/drivers/:id/scores?days=30, GET /v1/drivers/:id/coaching-tips, POST /v1/drivers/:id/leaderboard/consent, GET /v1/fleets/:id/scorecard-summary
backend.llm_integration_design: TFLite harsh_event_classifier_v2 runs entirely on-device; model metadata (version, checksum, min_app_version) stored in Cloud Firestore; app checks version on foreground and downloads updated model from Cloud Storage signed URL if newer version available; backend validates model_version in trip uploads for score calibration consistency
backend.failure_handling: Trip upload endpoint idempotent on trip_id; duplicate uploads return 200 with existing score rather than reprocessing; offline buffer in SQLite on device evicts oldest trips when buffer exceeds 500MB; failed backend uploads retry with exponential backoff up to 6 attempts across 48 hours

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth with email/password and optional SSO via SAML for enterprise fleets; Firebase ID tokens validated in FastAPI middleware; custom claims: {role: driver|fleet_manager, fleet_id, gdpr_consent_version, leaderboard_opted_in}
security.authorization_model: Drivers can only read/write their own driver_id-scoped resources; fleet_manager role required for /v1/fleets/:id/ endpoints; FastAPI dependency checks fleet_id claim matches path parameter fleet_id to prevent cross-fleet data access
security.privacy_controls: GDPR consent captured and versioned in driver_consent_records table before first trip; consent withdrawal API deletes all behavioral data within 30 days; GPS coordinates grid-snapped to 0.01-degree precision after 90 days by scheduled Cloud SQL function; leaderboard data deleted within 24 hours of opt-out

data.entities: drivers (id UUID PK, firebase_uid, fleet_id UUID FK, display_name, gdpr_consent_version, leaderboard_opted_in BOOL, created_at), driving_sessions (id UUID PK, driver_id UUID FK, started_at, ended_at, distance_km, duration_min, model_version VARCHAR), behavior_events (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, event_type ENUM harsh_brake/harsh_corner/harsh_accel, severity ENUM mild/moderate/severe, occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ, lat_grid NUMERIC(6,4), lng_grid NUMERIC(7,4)), safety_scores (id UUID PK, driver_id UUID FK, session_id UUID FK, composite_score NUMERIC(4,2), brake_score NUMERIC(4,2), cornering_score NUMERIC(4,2), accel_score NUMERIC(4,2), scored_at TIMESTAMPTZ), coaching_messages (id UUID PK, driver_id UUID FK, content TEXT, triggered_by_event_type VARCHAR, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, acknowledged BOOL)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL with read replica for fleet manager dashboard queries; Redis 7 sorted set fleet:{fleet_id}:leaderboard for real-time ranking; Cloud Storage bucket for TFLite model binary distribution and trip export archives
data.analytics_events: trip_completed (session_id, score, event_count, model_version), harsh_event_detected (session_id, event_type, severity), coaching_tip_acknowledged (driver_id, message_id), leaderboard_rank_changed (driver_id, old_rank, new_rank) - published to Pub/Sub for fleet analytics pipeline

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot us-central1; FastAPI pods 2-8 replicas via HPA on CPU 60%; Redis via Cloud Memorystore; Cloud SQL with private IP; Cloud Armor WAF on GKE Ingress; Artifact Registry for container images
devops.ci_cd_design: Backend: GitHub Actions - pytest with 85% coverage gate, mypy type check, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling deploy. Mobile: Fastlane - Flutter test, flutter build appbundle, sign with Fastlane Match certificates, distribute to Firebase App Distribution beta fleet before promoting to Play Store/App Store
devops.observability_stack: Grafana + Prometheus via kube-prometheus-stack on GKE; custom metrics: trip_upload_success_rate, score_calculation_latency_p95, leaderboard_update_latency; Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crash symbolication; Cloud Logging for FastAPI structured logs

ISS-001.title: TFLite model cold-start exceeds 200ms on low-end devices in preliminary testing
ISS-001.detail: Initial integration testing on Snapdragon 665 devices shows TFLite interpreter initialization at 340-450ms, blocking the Trip Start screen until the model is ready. Drivers may start driving before the app signals readiness.

history.note: Identified during device lab testing on Android 11 Snapdragon 665 test device

ISS-002.title: GDPR consent flow does not capture consent version in Firebase custom claim
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan described consent capture in driver_consent_records table but did not specify that the gdpr_consent_version custom claim in Firebase tokens must be updated on consent or policy change. Sessions with stale consent versions must be invalidated when the data processing policy changes.

history.note: Raised by security reviewer - consent version not propagated to JWT custom claims

ISS-003.title: Offline trip buffer eviction policy unspecified - risk of data loss on low-storage devices
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 architecture mentioned SQLite offline buffer but did not define maximum size, eviction strategy (FIFO vs score-weighted), or driver notification when buffer is full. Fleet-issued devices with limited storage may silently drop older trips.

history.note: Raised by architect reviewer - offline buffer behavior not specified

focus_issues.title: GDPR consent flow does not capture consent version in Firebase custom claim
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan described consent capture in driver_consent_records table but did not specify that the gdpr_consent_version custom claim in Firebase tokens must be updated on consent or policy change. Sessions with stale consent versions must be invalidated when the data processing policy changes.

focus_issues.title: TFLite model cold-start exceeds 200ms on low-end devices in preliminary testing
