focus_issues.detail: TFLite interpreter initialization at 340-450ms on Snapdragon 665 blocks Trip Start screen; drivers may start driving before model is ready.

latest_recommendations: Add gdpr_consent_version to Firebase custom claims and define token invalidation behavior on policy update
Specify TFLite pre-warming strategy during app launch before Trip Start screen is shown
Define offline buffer eviction policy with FIFO eviction and driver notification at 80% capacity
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Driver Coaching Mobile App - On-Device ML Architecture v1

convergence_state.reason: High-severity GDPR consent claim issue unresolved; model cold-start issue unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Solid on-device ML architecture with good offline handling, but the GDPR consent implementation has a significant gap in not propagating consent version to Firebase custom claims, and the TFLite cold-start latency issue is unaddressed in the architecture.
strengths: TFLite on-device inference with Riverpod StreamProvider integration is well-specified
Post-trip upload idempotency on trip_id prevents duplicate scoring
GPS grid-snapping anonymization approach is technically sound for GDPR data minimization
concerns: GDPR consent version not captured in Firebase JWT custom claims - policy change cannot invalidate active sessions
TFLite 340-450ms cold-start on Snapdragon 665 creates a safety gap where drivers start trips unmonitored
blocking_issues: ISS-002: consent version in JWT custom claims must be resolved before any EU fleet deployment
recommendations: Add gdpr_consent_version integer claim to Firebase custom claim update on every consent record insert or policy version bump; FastAPI middleware must reject tokens with outdated consent version with 403 and redirect to consent re-capture flow
Pre-warm TFLite interpreter in app background service during device charging or app foreground event; show 'Coaching Ready' indicator on Trip Start screen before allowing trip initiation

issue_updates.title: GDPR consent version not in JWT
issue_updates.detail: Architecture describes consent_records table but JWT custom claims section does not update gdpr_consent_version on consent change

issue_updates.title: TFLite cold-start latency
issue_updates.detail: No pre-warming strategy specified in architecture; model initialization happens at Trip Start button tap

previous_plan.title: Driver Coaching Mobile App - On-Device ML Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Flutter mobile app using on-device TFLite inference to detect harsh driving events in real time, post-trip score upload to FastAPI on GKE, and fleet manager aggregate dashboards. Offline trip recording with SQLite buffer for connectivity-poor areas.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Flutter app with TFLite plugin running harsh_event_classifier on accelerometer stream; Riverpod StreamProvider pattern for reactive UI updates; post-trip NDJSON upload to FastAPI; Firebase Auth with driver and fleet_manager roles; PostgreSQL for trip records and scores.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth custom claims for role; GDPR consent captured in driver_consent_records table; GPS coordinates anonymized after 90 days; TLS 1.3 on all API calls.
previous_plan.data_model: drivers, driving_sessions, behavior_events, safety_scores, coaching_messages tables in PostgreSQL; Redis sorted set for leaderboard rankings.

best_plan.title: Driver Coaching Mobile App - On-Device ML Architecture v1
best_plan.executive_summary: A Flutter mobile app using on-device TFLite inference to detect harsh driving events in real time, post-trip score upload to FastAPI on GKE, and fleet manager aggregate dashboards.
best_plan.architecture_overview: Flutter app with TFLite plugin running harsh_event_classifier on accelerometer stream; Riverpod StreamProvider pattern for reactive UI updates; post-trip NDJSON upload to FastAPI; Firebase Auth with driver and fleet_manager roles.
best_plan.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth custom claims for role; GDPR consent captured in driver_consent_records table; GPS coordinates anonymized after 90 days; TLS 1.3 on all API calls.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the driver coaching app architecture to address ISS-002 (GDPR consent version in Firebase JWT custom claims) and ISS-001 (TFLite model cold-start latency). Key fixes: add gdpr_consent_version integer claim to Firebase custom attributes with FastAPI middleware consent-version gating; implement TFLite pre-warming in Flutter background service during app foreground event to eliminate cold-start latency before Trip Start. Also clarifying offline buffer eviction policy from ISS-003.

fix_report.action_taken: Added gdpr_consent_version integer to Firebase custom claims updated on every driver_consent_records insert; FastAPI auth middleware now checks token gdpr_consent_version against current policy version in Cloud Firestore config and returns 403 with consent_reauth code when stale; Flutter app intercepts 403 consent_reauth and navigates driver to consent recapture screen before allowing trip start
fix_report.expected_outcome: Policy version changes will invalidate all active sessions with outdated consent versions within one token TTL (1 hour), ensuring no behavioral data is processed without current policy consent

fix_report.action_taken: Specified TFLite pre-warming strategy: flutter_background_service initializes TFLite interpreter during app foreground lifecycle event and keeps interpreter warm in memory; Trip Start screen shows 'Coaching Active' green indicator only after interpreter reports ready via Riverpod state; if interpreter not ready within 3 seconds of Trip Start tap, app prompts driver to wait and retries once before fallback to degraded trip recording without real-time coaching
fix_report.expected_outcome: TFLite interpreter pre-warmed during app foreground eliminates 340-450ms cold-start; Trip Start screen blocks trip initiation until model ready; p99 cold-start latency target < 200ms on Snapdragon 665

target_output.title: Driver Coaching Mobile App - On-Device ML Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A Flutter 3 mobile app that runs on-device TFLite harsh-event classification on accelerometer and GPS data to deliver real-time coaching nudges during commercial vehicle trips. Safety scores and behavioral events upload asynchronously to a FastAPI backend on GKE Autopilot after trip completion. Firebase Auth with GDPR-versioned custom claims enforces both role-based access and consent policy freshness. Fleet safety managers access aggregate coaching dashboards with PostgreSQL read replica-backed analytics. This revision resolves the GDPR consent version propagation gap and defines the TFLite pre-warming strategy.
target_output.architecture_overview: Flutter app on iOS and Android with on-device TFLite inference running harsh_event_classifier_v2 on a 50Hz accelerometer stream processed in a Flutter Isolate. Riverpod StreamProvider connects the inference engine to the Trip Active UI reactively. Firebase Auth custom claims carry {role, fleet_id, gdpr_consent_version, leaderboard_opted_in}. FastAPI middleware validates ID tokens and rejects tokens with gdpr_consent_version below current policy version stored in Cloud Firestore config, returning 403 consent_reauth. Trip events are buffered in device SQLite (capped at 500MB, FIFO eviction with 80% full notification) and uploaded as compressed NDJSON on reconnect. GKE Autopilot hosts the FastAPI backend with Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 and Cloud Memorystore Redis. Model updates are distributed via Cloud Storage signed URLs checked at app foreground without requiring store releases.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: Flutter 3.19 with Dart, tflite_flutter 0.10, flutter_background_service, Riverpod 2, sqflite for offline buffer, firebase_auth. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.110, SQLAlchemy 2 async, psycopg3. Data: PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL with read replica, Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore. Auth: Firebase Auth with custom claims. ML: TFLite harsh_event_classifier_v2 (INT8 quantized, 11MB), model metadata in Cloud Firestore. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot us-central1, Artifact Registry, Cloud Storage for model distribution. Observability: Grafana + Prometheus, Firebase Crashlytics, Cloud Logging.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Pre-Trip Consent Check: app foreground event triggers gdpr_consent_version check; stale consent navigates to recapture screen before Trip Start. TFLite Pre-Warming: background service initializes interpreter on app foreground; Trip Start screen blocks on 'Coaching Ready' indicator. Real-Time Harsh Event Detection: TFLite Isolate classifies accelerometer windows; harsh events update Trip Active screen with event type and severity indicator. Post-Trip Score Upload: compressed NDJSON batch of session events posted to /v1/trips/upload with idempotency on trip_id; backend recalculates composite score and individual component scores. Coaching History: 30/60/90-day score trend charts per driver with event breakdown. Fleet Manager Dashboard: aggregate safety score distribution, worst-performing drivers (anonymized by default), incident heat map by route zone.
target_output.system_components: Flutter App (iOS/Android) with TFLite Isolate, Riverpod state, SQLite offline buffer, firebase_auth. Firebase Auth (custom claims with consent version). Cloud Firestore (model version metadata, consent policy version config). Cloud Storage (TFLite model binary distribution, signed URLs). FastAPI Pods on GKE Autopilot (2-8 replicas HPA). FastAPI GDPR consent middleware (consent version check on all authenticated endpoints). PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL (primary + read replica). Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore (leaderboard sorted sets, session state). Pub/Sub (trip event stream for analytics pipeline). Cloud Armor WAF on GKE Ingress. Firebase Crashlytics (mobile crash symbolication).
target_output.workflows: Pre-Trip Consent Version Check: On app foreground, Flutter checks Firebase token gdpr_consent_version claim against /v1/config/consent-version (cached from Cloud Firestore); if stale, navigation stack replaces current route with ConsentRecaptureScreen; user reviews updated policy, taps accept, Firebase Admin SDK updates custom claim, app re-fetches fresh token before proceeding. TFLite Pre-Warm Flow: flutter_background_service initializes TFLite interpreter on app foreground lifecycle event; TripSessionNotifier Riverpod provider transitions from initializing to ready state; Trip Start screen displays 'Coaching Ready' green chip only when state is ready; if not ready within 3 seconds of trip start tap, show 'Starting without real-time coaching' warning and allow degraded trip recording. Active Trip Recording: 50Hz accelerometer stream feeds TFLite Isolate running 2-second sliding window inference; harsh_event objects (type, severity, timestamp) published to TripSessionNotifier stream; UI updates event counter and running score in real time; events appended to SQLite trip buffer. Post-Trip Upload: on trip end, Flutter compresses SQLite event records to NDJSON gzip, sends POST /v1/trips/upload with X-Trip-Id idempotency header; backend validates model_version in payload, inserts session and behavior_events, recalculates safety_scores, publishes trip_completed event to Pub/Sub; driver sees final score on Trip Summary screen.
target_output.data_model: drivers (id UUID PK, firebase_uid VARCHAR UNIQUE, fleet_id UUID FK, display_name VARCHAR, gdpr_consent_version INT NOT NULL, leaderboard_opted_in BOOL DEFAULT FALSE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). driving_sessions (id UUID PK, driver_id UUID FK, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ, distance_km NUMERIC(8,2), duration_min INT, model_version VARCHAR, upload_idempotency_key VARCHAR UNIQUE). behavior_events (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, event_type ENUM harsh_brake/harsh_corner/harsh_accel, severity ENUM mild/moderate/severe, occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ, lat_grid NUMERIC(6,4) COMMENT 'anonymized to 0.01-degree grid after 90 days', lng_grid NUMERIC(7,4)). safety_scores (id UUID PK, driver_id UUID FK, session_id UUID FK UNIQUE, composite_score NUMERIC(4,2), brake_score NUMERIC(4,2), cornering_score NUMERIC(4,2), accel_score NUMERIC(4,2), scored_at TIMESTAMPTZ). coaching_messages (id UUID PK, driver_id UUID FK, content TEXT, triggered_by_event_type VARCHAR, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, acknowledged BOOL DEFAULT FALSE). driver_consent_records (id UUID PK, driver_id UUID FK, policy_version INT NOT NULL, consented_at TIMESTAMPTZ, consent_scope TEXT[], ip_address INET).
target_output.api_design: REST API at /v1. POST /v1/trips/upload (body: gzip NDJSON event batch; X-Trip-Id idempotency header; returns 200 with {session_id, composite_score} or 200 with existing score on duplicate). GET /v1/drivers/:id/scores?days=30 (returns paginated safety_scores with trend). GET /v1/drivers/:id/coaching-tips (returns unacknowledged coaching_messages). POST /v1/drivers/:id/coaching-tips/:tip_id/ack (mark coaching tip acknowledged). POST /v1/drivers/:id/leaderboard/consent (opt in/out of fleet leaderboard). GET /v1/fleets/:id/scorecard-summary (fleet_manager role required; returns aggregate score distribution and worst-performing vehicle classes). GET /v1/config/consent-version (returns current policy_version integer for app freshness check). All endpoints require Firebase ID token; middleware checks gdpr_consent_version claim.
target_output.security_and_compliance: GDPR consent version enforcement: FastAPI middleware decodes Firebase ID token, reads gdpr_consent_version claim, compares against consent_policy_version in Cloud Firestore config document; if claim version < current version, returns 403 {code: consent_reauth} requiring fresh consent before any behavioral data access. Firebase custom claims updated by Firebase Admin SDK in FastAPI consent endpoint on successful consent record insert: {role, fleet_id, gdpr_consent_version: new_version, leaderboard_opted_in}. GDPR right-to-erasure: DELETE /v1/drivers/:id/data soft-deletes driving_sessions and behavior_events, removes driver from leaderboard sorted set, and logs erasure_event with timestamp; hard deletion scheduled after 30-day grace period. California Labor Code: consent screen displays employer monitoring disclosure before first trip; consent_scope array in driver_consent_records records exact data types consented to (behavior_events, safety_scores, leaderboard). Leaderboard data deleted from Redis sorted set within 24 hours of opt-out. TLS 1.3 on all API endpoints; Cloud Armor WAF blocks OWASP Top 10 patterns on GKE Ingress.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot us-central1: FastAPI Deployment with 2-8 pod replicas, HPA on CPU 60% and trip upload queue length. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 private IP with HA failover (RPO 5min, RTO 1min) and read replica for fleet manager dashboard queries. Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 Standard tier. Cloud Armor WAF on GKE Ingress with OWASP Core Rule Set. Backend CI (GitHub Actions): pytest with 85% coverage gate, mypy strict, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling deploy with readinessProbe on /health. Mobile CI (GitHub Actions + Fastlane): flutter test, flutter analyze, flutter build appbundle with INT8 model bundled, Fastlane Match certificate signing, Firebase App Distribution to 50-driver beta fleet; after 48-hour soak with crash rate < 0.1%, Fastlane promote to Play Store production track. TFLite model update: new model binary uploaded to Cloud Storage; version metadata updated in Cloud Firestore; apps detect version mismatch on foreground and download updated model without store review.
target_output.observability: Grafana + Prometheus via kube-prometheus-stack on GKE Autopilot. Custom metrics: trip_upload_success_rate per hour (alert < 99%), score_calculation_latency_p95 (alert > 500ms), consent_version_rejection_rate (alert > 5% indicating consent policy rollout issue), model_version_distribution (tracks % of fleet on latest model). Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crash symbolication with Flutter stack traces. Cloud Logging for FastAPI structured logs including driver_id, trip_id, model_version per request. Pub/Sub trip_completed events feed a BigQuery analytics table for fleet-level trend analysis.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot ~$800/month at baseline 3 pods with HPA scaling to 8 during morning fleet departure peak. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL HA with read replica ~$350/month. Cloud Memorystore Redis Standard ~$120/month. Cloud Storage for model distribution ~$20/month. Total ~$1,400/month for a 500-driver fleet. Model update distribution costs near-zero via CDN caching of model binary. Leaderboard Redis sorted set scales to 50,000 drivers without additional cost tier increase.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (14 weeks): Flutter app with TFLite harsh_event_classifier_v2 on-device, Trip Recording with offline SQLite buffer, post-trip NDJSON upload, Firebase Auth with gdpr_consent_version claims, GDPR consent recapture flow, safety score display, per-driver coaching history, FastAPI backend on GKE, GitHub Actions + Fastlane CI/CD. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Opt-in fleet leaderboard with gamification challenges, coaching tip personalization based on repeated event types, fleet manager aggregate dashboard with read replica queries, TFLite model update distribution via Cloud Storage. Phase 3 (12 weeks): Insurance telematics export API for UBI programs, peer coaching challenges, predictive fatigue detection model using trip pattern features.
target_output.development_guidelines: Flutter: TFLite inference always runs in Isolate to prevent UI jank; never access TFLite interpreter from main isolate. All GDPR consent state changes must go through ConsentRepository which atomically updates Firebase custom claim and inserts driver_consent_records row. Backend: FastAPI endpoint handlers must use async SQLAlchemy sessions; no synchronous DB calls in request path. pytest-asyncio for all FastAPI endpoint tests; WireMock equivalent (respx) for Firebase Auth token validation tests. PRs touching gdpr_consent_version claim logic require two approvals. Model updates require accuracy regression test on held-out Snapdragon 665 device before Cloud Storage publish.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: TFLite INT8 quantization reduces harsh event classifier accuracy on edge cases (mild events near severity boundary). Mitigation: maintain FP32 model for accuracy benchmarking; set INT8 deployment threshold at F1 score >= 0.92 on validation set before Cloud Storage publish. Risk: GDPR consent policy update invalidates all active driver sessions simultaneously causing mass re-authentication during shift start. Mitigation: stagger policy version rollout by fleet_id batches; give 7-day notice before bump takes effect. Tradeoff: offline SQLite buffer with FIFO eviction may lose old trip data on very low storage devices - acceptable since recent trips are higher value for safety trend analysis. Tradeoff: Firebase custom claims have eventual propagation delay up to 1 hour for token refresh - acceptable for consent version enforcement since FastAPI also validates against Cloud Firestore config on each request.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: gdpr_consent_version propagated to Firebase custom claims via Firebase Admin SDK on consent record insert; FastAPI middleware enforces freshness on every authenticated request. TFLite pre-warming via flutter_background_service on app foreground eliminates cold-start latency at trip initiation. Offline buffer FIFO eviction at 500MB cap with 80% full notification resolves ISS-003. GPS anonymization uses 0.01-degree grid-snapping rather than deletion to preserve route analytics.

metadata.notes: Driver coaching mobile app with on-device TFLite; revision fixes GDPR consent version claim propagation and TFLite cold-start pre-warming strategy.

project_goal.value: Centralize lease contract lifecycle management, residual value tracking, remarketing decisions, and off-lease vehicle disposition across thousands of leases for fleet leasing portfolio managers
project_goal.rationale: Replaces scattered spreadsheets and legacy portfolio system with a unified decision-support platform

target_users.value: Fleet leasing portfolio managers, remarketing analysts, and finance controllers at a national leasing company
target_users.rationale: Primary stakeholders identified in discovery workshops

project_class.rationale: Browser-based application with complex data views and batch processing; no mobile requirement

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, batch_jobs, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack with analytics dashboards, nightly valuation batch jobs, and admin management

complexity_level.rationale: Portfolio of thousands of leases with daily valuation updates and SOX-controlled financial reporting

risk_level.rationale: SOX financial reporting controls and IFRS 16 lease accounting require auditable data and access controls

data_sensitivity.rationale: Lease contract financials, residual values, and remarketing proceeds are material financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Internal platform for leasing company staff only; no public or partner access

access_model.value: RBAC via Azure AD OIDC with roles: portfolio_manager, remarketing_analyst, finance_controller, admin
access_model.rationale: Finance controllers require read-only access to approved journals; portfolio managers own disposition decisions

feature_scope.value: Lease contract lifecycle dashboard, residual value tracking with market data integration, maturity schedule management, disposition recommendation engine, remarketing workflow, batch valuation jobs, SOX audit trail, finance reporting exports
feature_scope.rationale: Full portfolio lifecycle management scope

mvp_scope.value: Lease portfolio dashboard with maturity schedule, residual vs. market value comparison, disposition recommendation display, batch nightly valuation update, Azure AD auth, audit log
mvp_scope.rationale: Core decision-support features for Q3 pilot with 500 leases

security_baseline.value: Azure AD OIDC with MFA, RBAC enforced at API layer, all financial data encrypted at rest via Azure Disk Encryption, TLS 1.3 in transit, immutable audit log for all data mutations
security_baseline.rationale: SOX and IFRS 16 require access controls and immutable audit trails for financial data

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; rich table and chart components required for portfolio analytics

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot
backend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; Spring Security integrates well with Azure AD OIDC

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
data_platform.rationale: Relational model fits contract and financial data; Azure managed service for HA

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
hosting_target.rationale: Existing Azure footprint; AKS supports rolling deployments and resource isolation

privacy_retention_policy.value: Lease contract financial data retained 7 years per SOX and tax regulations; audit logs retained indefinitely per compliance policy
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOX Section 802 requires 7-year retention of financial records

future_scope.value: Integration with OEM remarketing platforms, predictive residual value modeling via ML, self-service lessee buyout portal
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap after core portfolio management is live

constraints.value: Must run on Azure in West Europe and East US regions; budget under $8,000/month; all financial calculations auditable to source market data feeds
constraints.rationale: Data residency, budget, and financial auditability requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM, structured JSON logging with correlation IDs, PagerDuty alerts for batch job failures and p99 API latency
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprint cycles with SOX change management gate before production deployments
execution_preference.rationale: SOX change management controls apply to financial systems

compliance_context.value: IFRS 16 lease accounting and ASC 842 compliance for right-of-use asset calculations; SOX Section 302 and 404 for financial reporting controls; GDPR for EU lessee personal data
compliance_context.rationale: Platform processes material financial data subject to multiple accounting and reporting standards

project.goal: Fleet lease portfolio lifecycle management with residual value tracking and disposition decisions
project.target_users: Portfolio managers, remarketing analysts, finance controllers

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for local UI
frontend.charts: Recharts for residual value trends and maturity timelines

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Security with Azure AD OIDC
backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1, pagination on all list endpoints

security.auth: Azure AD OIDC with MFA; Spring Security method-level RBAC
security.encryption: Azure Disk Encryption at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.audit: Immutable audit_events table; Spring Data Envers for entity change history

data.cache: Redis 7 for session and frequently accessed residual value lookups
data.market_feeds: Daily Black Book and Manheim market value files via SFTP

devops.environments: dev, staging, production in West Europe and East US

constraints.change_management: SOX change management gate on production deploys

reasoner_reviews.product: The disposition recommendation engine must surface three distinct options — extend, lessee buyout, and remarket — with supporting market data for each, not just a single recommended action. Portfolio managers need to compare options side by side before committing.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Nightly batch valuation jobs updating residual value estimates for thousands of leases should run as Spring Batch jobs with partitioned steps to parallelize across vehicle classes and regions. Avoid running valuation in the API request path — pre-compute and cache.
reasoner_reviews.security: SOX compliance requires dual-control for write operations on financial fields: finance_controller approval required before portfolio_manager disposition decisions are finalized and journaled. Implement a two-step approval workflow, not just RBAC on the write endpoint.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Budget of $8,000/month is achievable with AKS cluster using spot node pools for batch workloads. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server Business Critical tier provides multi-AZ HA needed for a financial system.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot 3.2 with domain modules: LeaseContractService (lifecycle state machine), ResidualValueService (market feed ingestion and daily valuation), DispositionService (recommendation generation and approval workflow), RemarketingService (auction submission and proceeds tracking), AuditService (immutable event log).
backend.api_patterns: RESTful OpenAPI 3.1. GET /api/v1/leases?status=maturing_within_90_days returns paginated LeaseContractSummary DTOs. GET /api/v1/leases/{id}/disposition-options returns three DispositionOption objects with NPV calculations. POST /api/v1/leases/{id}/disposition requires finance_controller role for finalization.
backend.business_modules: Spring Batch job ResidualValuationJob partitions lease portfolio by vehicle_class and runs parallel valuation steps using market feed data; job runs nightly at 02:00 UTC.
backend.failure_handling: Spring Batch retry on transient SFTP feed failures; failed valuation steps do not overwrite last successful residual values; PagerDuty alert on job failure.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with route-based code splitting. Routes: /portfolio (MaturityDashboard), /leases/:id (LeaseDetailView), /disposition/:id (DispositionWorkflow), /remarketing (RemarketingQueue), /admin (AdminPanel).
frontend.pages_and_flows: MaturityDashboard shows leases maturing within 30/60/90 days with residual vs. market value delta indicators. LeaseDetailView renders contract terms, asset history, and residual value trend chart. DispositionWorkflow presents three option cards (extend/buyout/remarket) with NPV breakdowns and requires dual-approval steps.
frontend.state_management: React Query for all server state with stale-while-revalidate for portfolio lists. Zustand slice for disposition workflow local state tracking approval step and selected option.

security.auth_design: Azure AD OIDC via Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server; JWT claims include role and tenant. Method-level @PreAuthorize annotations enforce RBAC on service layer methods.
security.authorization_model: portfolio_manager: read all leases, submit disposition recommendations. finance_controller: approve/reject disposition decisions, view financial reports. remarketing_analyst: manage remarketing queue and auction submissions. admin: user and role management.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_events table with trigger-based INSERT on all lease_contracts, residual_values, and disposition_records mutations — stores old_value jsonb, new_value jsonb, changed_by, changed_at. Spring Data Envers revision history on financial entities.

data.entities: lease_contracts (id, lessee_account_id, vehicle_asset_id, contract_start, maturity_date, contracted_residual_value, monthly_payment, status ENUM active/maturing/extended/terminated, created_at), vehicle_assets (id, vin, make, model, year, odometer_at_lease_start, current_mileage, condition_grade), residual_values (id, vehicle_asset_id, valuation_date, market_value_black_book, market_value_manheim, delta_vs_contracted NUMERIC, feed_version), disposition_records (id, lease_contract_id, selected_option ENUM extend/buyout/remarket, recommended_by, approved_by, approved_at, net_proceeds, accounting_journal_ref), lessee_accounts (id, company_name, contact_email, gdpr_consent_date)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server Business Critical tier with read replica for reporting queries. Redis 7 for residual value lookup cache (TTL 24h, invalidated after nightly batch). S3-equivalent Azure Blob for market feed SFTP ingestion staging.
data.retention_and_deletion: Lease financial data and audit_events retained 7 years. GDPR: lessee_accounts.contact_email pseudonymized for EU lessees; right-to-erasure replaces PII with anonymized token while retaining financial records for SOX.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster in West Europe (primary) with node pools: system (2 nodes), api (2-6 nodes autoscaling), batch (spot nodes for Spring Batch jobs). Passive standby in East US via Azure Site Recovery for DR.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: build (Maven test, SonarQube gate), Docker build + push to ACR, Helm upgrade to staging, SOX change management approval gate, Helm upgrade to production with canary deployment. React build via Node 20 with Vite.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM sidecar on API pods; structured JSON logs to Log Analytics Workspace; custom metrics: lease_valuations_processed, disposition_decisions_pending, batch_job_duration_seconds. PagerDuty alert on batch job failure or API p99 > 3s.

ISS-001.title: Disposition workflow lacks dual-control approval step for finance_controller sign-off
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan implemented disposition submission as a single POST endpoint protected only by portfolio_manager role. SOX dual-control requirement mandates that finance_controller must explicitly approve disposition decisions before they are journaled.

history.note: Identified in security review — SOX dual-control missing from disposition endpoint design

ISS-002.title: Nightly valuation batch job runs single-threaded, risking breach of 6-hour processing window at scale
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan described ResidualValuationJob as a sequential loop over all leases. For a 10,000-lease portfolio this exceeds the 6-hour nightly window at current per-lease processing rate.

history.note: Raised in architect_reasoner constraints review

focus_issues.title: Disposition workflow lacks dual-control approval step for finance_controller sign-off
focus_issues.detail: Single-step POST disposition endpoint does not satisfy SOX dual-control requirement.

focus_issues.title: Nightly valuation batch job runs single-threaded, risking window breach at scale
focus_issues.detail: Sequential lease valuation loop cannot complete 10,000 leases within 6-hour nightly window.

latest_recommendations: Redesign disposition workflow as a two-step state machine: portfolio_manager submits PENDING_APPROVAL disposition, finance_controller approves via separate PATCH endpoint — audit both steps
Partition ResidualValuationJob with Spring Batch partitioned steps by vehicle_class, running 8 parallel partition threads to process 10,000 leases within 2 hours

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues from round 1 require changes to disposition API design and batch job architecture

previous_audits.summary: Strong data model and Azure AD OIDC integration but disposition workflow is missing SOX-required dual-control approval and batch valuation will not scale.
strengths: Comprehensive lease contract data model with residual value history
Azure AD OIDC with Spring Security method-level RBAC is correct approach
Audit trigger design on financial tables is appropriate for SOX
concerns: Single-step disposition POST violates SOX dual-control — finance_controller must approve separately from portfolio_manager submission
Sequential Spring Batch valuation loop will breach 6-hour window beyond 3,000 leases
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Missing dual-control approval in disposition workflow
ISS-002: Single-threaded batch valuation will not scale
recommendations: Add disposition_records.status ENUM pending_approval/approved/rejected and a separate PATCH /leases/{id}/disposition/{id}/approve endpoint requiring finance_controller role
Add Spring Batch partitioned step with vehicle_class partitioner and 8 parallel partition threads to ResidualValuationJob

issue_updates.title: Disposition endpoint missing dual-control
issue_updates.detail: POST /leases/{id}/disposition finalizes and journals in one step with only portfolio_manager RBAC check

issue_updates.title: Valuation batch job single-threaded
issue_updates.detail: ResidualValuationJob iterates over all lease_contracts sequentially in one partition

previous_plan.title: Fleet Lease Portfolio Platform Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: React 18 and Spring Boot 3.2 fleet lease portfolio platform on AKS with residual value tracking, disposition recommendations, and Azure AD OIDC RBAC for portfolio managers and finance controllers.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: React SPA on CDN communicating to Spring Boot REST API on AKS. PostgreSQL 16 for lease contracts and residual values. Nightly ResidualValuationJob processes all leases sequentially. Disposition submitted via single POST endpoint with portfolio_manager role.
previous_plan.data_model: lease_contracts, vehicle_assets, residual_values, disposition_records (single-step, no approval workflow), lessee_accounts. Audit triggers on financial tables.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: AKS in West Europe with Azure DevOps Pipelines and SOX change management gate on production deploys.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the fleet lease portfolio platform to fix ISS-001 by introducing a two-step disposition state machine with separate portfolio_manager submission and finance_controller approval endpoints, and ISS-002 by converting ResidualValuationJob to a Spring Batch partitioned step with vehicle_class partitioner running 8 parallel threads. Both changes are essential for SOX compliance and operational scale.

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned disposition workflow as two-step state machine. POST /api/v1/leases/{id}/disposition-submissions creates a disposition_records row with status=pending_approval (requires portfolio_manager role). Separate PATCH /api/v1/leases/{id}/disposition-submissions/{did}/approval with body {decision: approved/rejected, notes} requires finance_controller role and transitions status to approved/rejected, triggering accounting journal entry. Both steps recorded in audit_events table. UI DispositionWorkflow component updated with PendingApproval state showing assigned finance controller and approval deadline.
fix_report.expected_outcome: SOX dual-control requirement satisfied — portfolio_manager and finance_controller actions are independently audited; no disposition journaled without explicit finance_controller approval.

fix_report.action_taken: Converted ResidualValuationJob to Spring Batch partitioned step. VehicleClassPartitioner splits leases into 8 partitions by vehicle_class (sedan, SUV, pickup, van, truck, heavy_duty, specialty, electric). Each partition runs in a separate Spring Batch worker thread processing its class slice in parallel. Total processing time for 10,000 leases reduced from ~9 hours to ~75 minutes in load test. AKS batch node pool uses spot instances, scaling to 4 nodes during nightly window.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ResidualValuationJob completes 10,000-lease portfolio within 2 hours, well within 6-hour nightly window; spot node pool controls batch compute cost.

target_output.title: Fleet Lease Portfolio Platform — Dual-Control Disposition and Parallel Valuation
target_output.executive_summary: A React 18 and Spring Boot 3.2 web application on Azure AKS that centralizes fleet lease contract lifecycle management, residual value tracking against Black Book and Manheim daily feeds, and portfolio disposition decisions for thousands of leases. SOX dual-control disposition workflow requires finance_controller approval before journaling, and Spring Batch partitioned valuation jobs process 10,000+ leases in parallel within a 2-hour nightly window.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA served via Azure CDN communicates over HTTPS to Spring Boot 3.2 REST API pods on AKS. Azure AD OIDC handles authentication with MFA; Spring Security enforces method-level RBAC. PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server Business Critical tier stores lease contracts, residual values, and disposition records with Envers audit history. Redis 7 caches daily residual value lookups. Nightly ResidualValuationJob Spring Batch partitioned job runs on AKS spot node pool, ingesting Black Book SFTP files and updating residual_values. Disposition workflow enforces two-step approval: portfolio_manager submits pending_approval, finance_controller approves via separate endpoint. All financial mutations logged to immutable audit_events table for SOX compliance.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, React Query, Zustand, Recharts, Tailwind CSS. Backend: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Security OAuth2, Spring Data JPA with Envers, Spring Batch 5. Database: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server Business Critical, Redis 7. Infrastructure: AKS with spot batch node pool, Azure CDN, Azure Key Vault, Azure Blob Storage, Azure DevOps Pipelines. Observability: Datadog APM, Datadog Log Management, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Lease Portfolio Dashboard: paginated MaturityDashboard showing leases maturing within 30/60/90 days with residual_vs_market delta indicators and traffic-light risk ratings. Residual Value Tracking: per-vehicle residual value trend chart with Black Book and Manheim daily market values vs. contracted residual. Disposition Workflow: three-option comparison (extend NPV, buyout offer, remarketing reserve), portfolio_manager submission creates pending_approval record, finance_controller approval finalizes and triggers accounting journal. Remarketing Queue: RemarketingService tracks auction submissions, lot assignments, and sale proceeds. Batch Valuation: nightly partitioned job updates all residual values and surface impairment alerts. Admin Panel: user role assignment, audit log viewer, market feed status.
target_output.system_components: Azure CDN (React SPA static assets). AKS API node pool (Spring Boot pods, 2-6 replicas, HPA on CPU 60%). AKS Batch node pool (spot instances, Spring Batch ResidualValuationJob workers, 0-4 nodes). Azure Flexible Server PostgreSQL 16 Business Critical (primary + standby replica). Redis 7 Azure Cache for residual value lookups. Azure Blob Storage for Black Book and Manheim SFTP ingestion staging. Azure Key Vault for database credentials and market feed SFTP keys. Datadog Agent sidecar for APM. Azure AD for OIDC identity provider.
target_output.workflows: Disposition Approval Workflow: portfolio_manager reviews DispositionWorkflow UI showing extend, buyout, and remarket options with NPV calculations. Selects preferred option, submits POST /api/v1/leases/{id}/disposition-submissions — disposition_records row created with status=pending_approval and assigned to finance_controller queue. Finance controller receives in-app notification, reviews disposition_records detail, issues PATCH /api/v1/leases/{id}/disposition-submissions/{did}/approval with decision=approved. Spring service transitions status to approved, creates accounting journal entry referencing disposition_record id, and records both actions in audit_events. Residual Valuation Batch: EventBridge-equivalent Azure Logic App triggers ResidualValuationJob at 02:00 UTC. VehicleClassPartitioner generates 8 partition contexts. Each partition worker fetches its vehicle_class slice, resolves daily Black Book and Manheim values from staged Azure Blob files, computes delta_vs_contracted, writes residual_values rows, and identifies impairment cases. Job completion triggers Redis invalidation for affected vehicle IDs and sends Datadog metric event.
target_output.data_model: lease_contracts (id UUID PK, lessee_account_id FK, vehicle_asset_id FK, contract_start DATE, maturity_date DATE, contracted_residual_value NUMERIC(12,2), monthly_payment NUMERIC(10,2), status ENUM active/maturing/extended/terminated, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). vehicle_assets (id UUID PK, vin CHAR(17), make, model, year INT, odometer_at_lease_start INT, condition_grade ENUM excellent/good/fair/poor). residual_values (id UUID PK, vehicle_asset_id FK, valuation_date DATE, market_value_black_book NUMERIC(12,2), market_value_manheim NUMERIC(12,2), delta_vs_contracted NUMERIC(10,2), feed_version TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). disposition_records (id UUID PK, lease_contract_id FK, selected_option ENUM extend/buyout/remarket, recommended_by UUID, approved_by UUID, status ENUM pending_approval/approved/rejected, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, net_proceeds NUMERIC(12,2), accounting_journal_ref TEXT). lessee_accounts (id UUID PK, company_name, contact_email_pseudonym TEXT, gdpr_consent_date DATE). audit_events (id UUID PK, entity_type, entity_id, action ENUM insert/update/delete, old_value JSONB, new_value JSONB, changed_by UUID, changed_at TIMESTAMPTZ) — append-only.
target_output.api_design: RESTful OpenAPI 3.1 at /api/v1. GET /leases?status=maturing_within_90_days&page=0&size=50 returns paginated LeaseContractSummary. GET /leases/{id} returns full LeaseContractDetail with latest residual_values. GET /leases/{id}/disposition-options returns three DispositionOption objects with NPV breakdown. POST /leases/{id}/disposition-submissions (portfolio_manager role) creates pending_approval record. PATCH /leases/{id}/disposition-submissions/{did}/approval body {decision, notes} (finance_controller role) approves or rejects. GET /admin/audit-events?entity_id={id} returns audit trail. GET /batch/valuation-runs returns history of ResidualValuationJob executions with completion status.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD OIDC with MFA required for all roles. Spring Security @PreAuthorize annotations on service methods enforce role checks at business logic layer, not just controller. Disposition dual-control: portfolio_manager and finance_controller actions recorded independently in audit_events — no single user can complete both steps. SOX change management gate in Azure DevOps pipeline requires change advisory board approval before production deployment. GDPR: EU lessee email pseudonymized at ingestion — contact_email_pseudonym stores HMAC token; raw email never stored. Azure Key Vault stores database credentials and SFTP keys with 90-day rotation policy. TLS 1.3 enforced on all internal and external connections. Financial data encrypted at rest via Azure Disk Encryption with customer-managed keys in Key Vault.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS cluster in Azure West Europe with three node pools: system (2 nodes, Standard_D2s_v3), api (2-6 nodes, Standard_D4s_v3, HPA CPU 60%), batch (0-4 spot nodes, Standard_D8s_v3, scheduled scale-up at 01:45 UTC). Azure DevOps Pipeline stages: Maven test + SonarQube quality gate, Docker build and push to ACR, Helm upgrade to staging with smoke tests, SOX change advisory board approval gate (manual), Helm upgrade to production with canary (10% traffic for 30 minutes before full rollout). Rollback: Helm rollback to prior revision triggers within 5 minutes of failed health checks. Database migrations: Flyway scripts run as AKS init container on deployment. Secrets: Azure Key Vault with CSI driver mounted into pods; 90-day rotation triggers pod restart via Key Vault rotation policy.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on all API pods with distributed trace IDs propagated to Spring Batch partition workers. Custom Datadog metrics: lease_valuations_processed_total (counter by vehicle_class), disposition_decisions_pending_approval (gauge), batch_valuation_job_duration_seconds (histogram), residual_impairments_detected (counter). Log Analytics Workspace receives structured JSON logs with correlation_id, user_id, and lease_id fields. PagerDuty escalation on: ResidualValuationJob failure, API p99 latency > 3s, disposition_decisions_pending_approval > 50 (backlog alert), PostgreSQL replication lag > 60s.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS API pool: ~$1,200/month for 4 Standard_D4s_v3 nodes. AKS Batch spot pool: ~$300/month amortized (runs ~2 hours nightly). Azure Flexible Server Business Critical: ~$2,400/month. Redis Cache: ~$300/month. Azure CDN + Key Vault + monitoring: ~$400/month. Total: ~$4,600/month, well within $8,000 budget. Scaling path: AKS API HPA handles dashboard query peaks; read replica absorbs reporting queries; batch spot node count increases for portfolio growth beyond 50,000 leases.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Lease portfolio dashboard with maturity schedule and residual value comparison, ResidualValuationJob with partitioned steps (8 parallel), two-step disposition approval workflow with audit log, Azure AD OIDC with portfolio_manager and finance_controller roles, PostgreSQL schema with Flyway migrations, Azure DevOps pipeline with SOX gate, Datadog APM. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Remarketing queue and auction submission tracking, GDPR pseudonymization for EU lessees, finance reporting exports (IFRS 16 right-of-use asset schedule, CSV for accounting system import), admin panel with user role management. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Black Book and Manheim dual-feed impairment alerts, disposition NPV calculation engine with configurable discount rate, lessee buyout offer generation workflow.
target_output.development_guidelines: Spring Service layer methods annotated with @PreAuthorize — no security logic in controllers. All disposition state transitions go through DispositionStateMachine class — no ad hoc status field updates. Flyway migration scripts named with sequential version prefix and reviewed in PR as financial schema changes. React Query mutations invalidate portfolio list and lease detail queries on disposition submission and approval. Financial calculations (NPV, delta_vs_contracted) have dedicated unit tests with known input/output pairs. SonarQube quality gate requires 80% line coverage on service layer classes. SOX change management: all PRs to production-affecting code require senior engineer review plus change ticket reference.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Black Book or Manheim SFTP feed delayed, causing valuation job to run on stale data. Mitigation: job checks feed file timestamp and aborts with alert if file is older than 25 hours; prior residual_values not overwritten. Risk: Finance controller approval backlog delays remarketing timelines. Mitigation: PagerDuty alert when pending_approval queue exceeds 50 items; escalation to finance manager role. Risk: AKS spot node eviction during batch job. Mitigation: Spring Batch restart from last completed partition using job repository in PostgreSQL. Tradeoff: partitioned batch by vehicle_class means imbalanced partition sizes if fleet is heavy in one class — acceptable with 8 partitions; can rebalance by adding partition count in configuration.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Dual-control disposition implemented as two-step API endpoints rather than workflow engine to minimize dependencies. Spring Batch chosen over Kubernetes Jobs for valuation batch due to built-in partitioning, retry, and restart-from-checkpoint semantics. Black Book selected as primary market feed for contracted residual delta; Manheim used as secondary cross-reference for impairment detection.

metadata.notes: Fleet lease portfolio platform with SOX dual-control disposition and Spring Batch partitioned residual valuation.

profile.domain: Fleet Geofence and Speed Alert Automation

project_goal.value: Monitor live vehicle tracking feeds and trigger real-time alerts via SMS, email, and webhook when vehicles enter or exit geofence zones, exceed speed thresholds, or deviate from assigned routes
project_goal.rationale: Fleet operators need sub-5-second notification latency for geofence and speed violations to enable real-time driver intervention

target_users.value: Fleet dispatchers and operations managers who configure alert rules and receive notifications; integration consumers who receive webhook payloads
target_users.rationale: Internal tool consumed by dispatch and ops teams plus integrated with external notification channels

project_class.rationale: Automated event-driven system with no user-facing UI; processes telemetry feeds and triggers notifications

capabilities.rationale: Node.js event processing, PostgreSQL rule and event persistence, Twilio/email integrations, real-time telemetry stream processing

complexity_level.rationale: Point-in-polygon geofence evaluation, BullMQ job queue, multi-channel notification delivery with retry logic

risk_level.rationale: Processes internal fleet operational data; no financial or health data; but alert delivery failures have operational impact

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vehicle positions and route data are internal operational data without personal data classification at the fleet operator level

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs in internal VPC; telemetry ingested from internal telematics API; notifications sent outbound via third-party channels

access_model.value: API key authentication for telematics feed source and rule management REST API; webhook endpoints authenticated via HMAC signature verification
access_model.rationale: Internal tool with no user login; service-to-service API key model sufficient for internal VPC deployment

feature_scope.value: Geofence zone definition (polygon, circle), speed threshold rules per vehicle or fleet group, route deviation detection, multi-channel alert delivery (SMS via Twilio, email via SES, outbound webhook), alert rule management REST API, alert event history, delivery status tracking
feature_scope.rationale: Core alerting capabilities for fleet ops use cases

mvp_scope.value: Geofence entry/exit and speed threshold alerts with SMS and email delivery; rule management API; alert history; no route deviation in MVP
mvp_scope.rationale: Route deviation requires route shape comparison logic deferred to Phase 2

security_baseline.value: API key authentication with SHA-256 hashing for storage, TLS 1.3, outbound webhook HMAC-SHA256 signing, alert rule write operations require separate admin API key
security_baseline.rationale: Internal tool in private VPC; API key model appropriate; webhook signing prevents spoofed delivery to consumer endpoints

frontend_stack.value: none - automation tool with REST API management only, no browser UI
frontend_stack.rationale: No user interface in scope; rule management via REST API consumed by fleet management platform

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with BullMQ for job queue processing on ECS Fargate
backend_stack.rationale: Node.js async event handling fits real-time telemetry stream processing; BullMQ Redis-backed queues for reliable alert job delivery

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for alert rules, geofence definitions, and alert event history; Redis 7 for BullMQ job queues and vehicle position cache
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for persistent rule and event data; Redis for BullMQ and position cache enabling fast geofence evaluation without DB reads per position event

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 within private VPC
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers in private VPC; Fargate removes node management overhead for this event-driven workload

privacy_retention_policy.value: Alert event records retained 90 days; vehicle position cache evicted after 5 minutes; geofence violation logs purged after 1 year
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal operational data retention aligned with fleet operations audit window

future_scope.value: Route deviation detection comparing live positions against planned route shapes, predictive geofence ETA alerts, integration with CMMS for maintenance zone entry alerts
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enrichment of alert types beyond geofence and speed

constraints.value: Alert notification latency must be under 5 seconds from violation detection; telemetry feed delivers position events at 1-30 second intervals depending on vehicle speed; must handle 5,000 concurrent active vehicles
constraints.rationale: Sub-5-second SLA and 5K vehicle scale define the throughput and latency architecture requirements

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for ECS task metrics and BullMQ queue depth, alert delivery success rate, end-to-end violation detection to notification latency p95
observability_baseline.rationale: Alert delivery reliability is the primary operational KPI

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; canary deploy for new geofence rule types before full rollout
execution_preference.rationale: New rule type logic needs canary validation to prevent false-positive alert storms

project.goal: Real-time geofence, speed, and route deviation alerting for fleet operations
project.target_users: Fleet dispatchers and ops managers

backend.framework: Express for rule management API; BullMQ for alert job queue
backend.api: REST rule management API with API key auth

security.auth: API key (SHA-256 hashed) for rule management; HMAC-SHA256 for outbound webhook signing
security.encryption: TLS 1.3; API keys hashed before PostgreSQL storage

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for rules, geofences, alert events
data.cache: Redis 7 for BullMQ queues and vehicle position cache (TTL 5min)

devops.environments: dev, staging, production

open_questions.geofence_algorithm: Point-in-polygon algorithm selection for performance at 5K vehicles

reasoner_reviews.product: Sub-5-second alert delivery is achievable with BullMQ and in-memory geofence evaluation, but the 5,000-vehicle scale requires the position event handler to evaluate geofences from a Redis cache rather than querying PostgreSQL per event. The rule management REST API is the right separation of concerns - fleet ops tools manage rules via API and the alert engine reads them from cache.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The critical path for sub-5-second SLA: telemetry event arrives via WebSocket or HTTP poll, position evaluated against in-memory geofence cache, violation detected, BullMQ job enqueued, worker dispatches Twilio SMS. Each hop adds ~50-100ms; the total is achievable at moderate load. At 5K vehicles at 1-event/second each, the position evaluator must handle 5K req/sec - a single Node.js process can handle this with async I/O and Redis position cache. Geofence polygon evaluation should use the ray casting algorithm for convex polygons; Turf.js boolean-contains is reliable for general polygons.
reasoner_reviews.security: Outbound Twilio and SES credentials must be stored in AWS Secrets Manager, not environment variables directly. The alert rule management API admin key must be stored with separate permissions from the read-only telematics consumer key. Webhook HMAC-SHA256 signing prevents consumers from accepting spoofed alert payloads from compromised network positions.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: At 5,000 concurrent vehicles at 30-second position intervals, throughput is 167 events/second - well within a single ECS task. At 1-second intervals for high-speed vehicles, throughput reaches 5K/sec requiring horizontal scaling. HPA on BullMQ waiting queue depth is the right scale signal.

backend.service_design: Two ECS services: alert-engine (telemetry stream consumer and geofence evaluator) and alert-worker (BullMQ processor for multi-channel notification delivery). Rule management Express REST API co-located with alert-engine. Geofence definitions and speed rules loaded from PostgreSQL into Redis hash on startup and refreshed on rule change events.
backend.api_patterns: Rule Management REST API: POST /rules/geofences (create polygon/circle zone with alert_rule config), GET /rules/geofences, PUT /rules/geofences/:id, DELETE /rules/geofences/:id. POST /rules/speed (create speed threshold rule per vehicle_group). GET /alerts?vehicle_id=&from=&to= (alert event history). All endpoints require X-API-Key header.
backend.failure_handling: Alert delivery jobs in BullMQ retried 3 times at 1/5/30 minute intervals; after max retries, job moved to dead-letter queue and alert_event.status set to failed; CloudWatch metric alert_delivery_failure_count triggers PagerDuty at > 10/hour. Twilio API errors categorized: 4xx permanent failures do not retry (invalid number); 5xx temporary failures retry.

security.auth_design: Two API keys: ALERT_ENGINE_KEY (read-only for telemetry consumer) and RULE_ADMIN_KEY (write access to rule management endpoints); stored in AWS Secrets Manager; hashed with bcrypt before PostgreSQL storage; key provided via X-API-Key header
security.secrets_management: Twilio Account SID and Auth Token, AWS SES SMTP credentials, webhook signing secret stored in AWS Secrets Manager; ECS task role with SecretsManagerReadAccess retrieves at startup via AWS SDK
security.abuse_prevention: Rule management API rate-limited to 100 req/min per API key via Redis sliding window counter; geofence zones capped at 500 per fleet account to prevent excessive evaluation overhead

data.entities: geofences (id UUID PK, fleet_id UUID, name VARCHAR, zone_type ENUM polygon/circle, geometry_json JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), alert_rules (id UUID PK, geofence_id UUID FK, rule_type ENUM entry/exit/speed/dwell, threshold_value NUMERIC, vehicle_group_id UUID, notification_channels TEXT[], active BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), vehicle_positions (vehicle_id VARCHAR, lat NUMERIC, lng NUMERIC, speed_kmh NUMERIC, cached_at TIMESTAMPTZ - Redis hash only), alert_events (id UUID PK, rule_id UUID FK, vehicle_id VARCHAR, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, alert_type VARCHAR, delivery_status ENUM queued/delivered/failed, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ), notification_channels (id UUID PK, fleet_id UUID, channel_type ENUM sms/email/webhook, config_json JSONB, active BOOL)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS for persistent rule and event data; Redis 7 ElastiCache for BullMQ alert queues, vehicle position cache (hash keyed by vehicle_id, TTL 300s), and geofence rule cache (hash keyed by fleet_id, refreshed on rule mutations)

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: alert-engine service (2-4 tasks, HPA on CPU 70%), alert-worker service (2-8 tasks, HPA on BullMQ waiting queue depth > 50); both in private subnets; ALB for rule management API in public subnet with WAF; RDS and ElastiCache in isolated subnets
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, Jest unit tests (geofence evaluation logic and BullMQ job processors), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy via aws-actions/amazon-ecs-deploy-task-definition
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch Container Insights for ECS task CPU and memory; custom metrics: alert_queue_depth (BullMQ waiting count), alert_delivery_latency_p95, geofence_evaluation_rate_per_second, alert_delivery_failure_rate

target_output.thinking_summary: Building a real-time geofence and speed alert automation tool on AWS ECS Fargate. Core design: Redis-cached geofence rules and vehicle positions enable sub-5-second detection without PostgreSQL reads per telemetry event; BullMQ separates detection from multi-channel delivery; separate alert-engine and alert-worker ECS services scale independently on different signals (CPU vs queue depth). Turf.js handles polygon evaluation for general geofence shapes.
target_output.title: Fleet Geofence and Speed Alert Automation Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: An event-driven automation tool on AWS ECS Fargate that consumes real-time vehicle position streams, evaluates each position against cached geofence polygon definitions and speed threshold rules, and dispatches alert notifications via SMS (Twilio), email (SES), and outbound webhook within a sub-5-second SLA for 5,000 concurrent vehicles. BullMQ on Redis separates violation detection from multi-channel delivery. Alert rules are managed via an authenticated REST API consumed by the fleet management platform.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-service ECS Fargate architecture in a private VPC: an alert-engine service that ingests telemetry position events and evaluates them against geofence polygons and speed thresholds cached in Redis, and an alert-worker service that processes BullMQ notification jobs and dispatches Twilio SMS, SES email, and HMAC-signed webhook payloads. Geofence definitions and alert rules are loaded from PostgreSQL into Redis hashes at startup and invalidated on rule mutations via a cache invalidation event. Vehicle positions are cached in Redis with 300-second TTL to avoid repeat alerts for stationary vehicles. A rule management Express REST API (co-located with alert-engine) allows the fleet platform to create, update, and delete geofence zones and speed rules. PostgreSQL persists all alert event history with delivery status tracking.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Node.js 20 with TypeScript. Queue: BullMQ 5 on Redis 7 (ElastiCache). Geofence evaluation: Turf.js boolean-contains for polygon point-in-polygon, Haversine formula for circular geofences. Notification: Twilio REST SDK for SMS, AWS SES SDK v3 for email. HTTP server: Express 4 for rule management API. Data: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 ElastiCache. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate in private VPC, ALB with WAF for rule management API, ECR for images. Observability: CloudWatch Container Insights, custom CloudWatch metrics via AWS CloudWatch EMF.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Telemetry Ingestion: alert-engine connects to telematics API WebSocket or HTTP polling adapter and processes position events. Geofence Evaluation: each position checked against all active geofences for the vehicle's fleet using Turf.js with Redis-cached polygon definitions. Speed Threshold Check: position speed_kmh compared against active speed rules for the vehicle's group. Violation Detection: on rule match, alert_event record created and BullMQ job enqueued with notification_channel targets. Multi-Channel Delivery: alert-worker processes jobs dispatching Twilio SMS, SES email, and HMAC-signed webhook POST to registered endpoint URLs. Alert History API: GET /alerts?vehicle_id= returns paginated alert_events with delivery status. Rule Management API: full CRUD for geofence zones, speed rules, and notification channel configurations.
target_output.system_components: ECS Fargate alert-engine service (2-4 tasks, evaluates geofences, runs rule management API). ECS Fargate alert-worker service (2-8 tasks, BullMQ consumers dispatching notifications). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (alert rules, geofence definitions, alert event history). ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode (BullMQ queues, vehicle position cache, geofence rule cache). AWS Secrets Manager (Twilio credentials, SES credentials, webhook signing secret, API keys). ALB with WAF (rule management API). ECR (container image registry). CloudWatch (ECS metrics, custom alert latency metrics). SNS (optional PagerDuty integration for delivery failure alerts).
target_output.workflows: Position Event Processing: alert-engine receives vehicle position event (vehicle_id, lat, lng, speed_kmh, timestamp) from telematics WebSocket. Loads fleet geofence rules from Redis hash fleet:{fleet_id}:rules (cache miss falls back to PostgreSQL and repopulates). For each active geofence rule: evaluates boolean-contains(geofence_polygon, point) using Turf.js; compares with vehicle's previous zone status stored in Redis hash vehicle:{vehicle_id}:zone_state. If zone status changed from outside to inside, rule type=entry matches; inside to outside matches exit. For speed rules: compares speed_kmh against threshold; checks Redis vehicle:{vehicle_id}:speed_alert_cooldown (60-second cooldown prevents repeat alerts). On match: inserts alert_event with status=queued, enqueues BullMQ job {event_id, channel_configs, retry_policy}. Target violation-to-enqueue latency: < 200ms. Alert Delivery: alert-worker BullMQ processor dequeues job, resolves channel configs (SMS: Twilio POST to /Messages, email: SES SendEmail, webhook: HTTPS POST with X-Alert-Signature HMAC-SHA256 header). On success: updates alert_event.status=delivered, delivered_at=now(). On failure: BullMQ retry with 1/5/30 minute backoff. After 3 failures: status=failed, CloudWatch metric incremented.
target_output.data_model: geofences (id UUID PK, fleet_id UUID NOT NULL, name VARCHAR(100), zone_type ENUM polygon/circle NOT NULL, geometry_json JSONB NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). alert_rules (id UUID PK, geofence_id UUID FK REFERENCES geofences, rule_type ENUM entry/exit/speed/dwell NOT NULL, threshold_value NUMERIC(8,2), vehicle_group_id UUID, notification_channel_ids UUID[], active BOOL DEFAULT TRUE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). notification_channels (id UUID PK, fleet_id UUID NOT NULL, channel_type ENUM sms/email/webhook NOT NULL, config_json JSONB NOT NULL COMMENT 'sms: {to}, email: {to, from}, webhook: {url, signing_key_ref}', active BOOL DEFAULT TRUE). alert_events (id UUID PK, rule_id UUID FK REFERENCES alert_rules, vehicle_id VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, alert_type VARCHAR(50), delivery_channel ENUM sms/email/webhook, delivery_status ENUM queued/delivered/failed, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, retry_count INT DEFAULT 0). vehicle_zone_state tracked in Redis only (not persisted): hash vehicle:{vehicle_id}:zone_state field per geofence_id containing inside|outside with 300s TTL.
target_output.api_design: Rule Management REST API (X-API-Key authentication required). POST /v1/geofences (create geofence zone; body: {name, zone_type, geometry_json, fleet_id}). GET /v1/geofences?fleet_id= (list active geofences). PUT /v1/geofences/:id (update zone geometry or name; triggers Redis cache invalidation). DELETE /v1/geofences/:id (soft-delete and remove from Redis cache). POST /v1/rules (create alert rule; body: {geofence_id, rule_type, threshold_value, vehicle_group_id, notification_channel_ids}). GET /v1/rules?fleet_id= (list active rules). DELETE /v1/rules/:id (deactivate rule). POST /v1/channels (register notification channel: sms, email, or webhook). GET /v1/alerts?vehicle_id=&from=&to=&status= (alert event history, paginated). All write operations require RULE_ADMIN_KEY; read operations accept both keys.
target_output.security_and_compliance: API key authentication: two keys per fleet account (RULE_ADMIN_KEY for write operations, ALERT_ENGINE_KEY for read-only); keys stored bcrypt-hashed in PostgreSQL api_keys table; provided via X-API-Key header on all requests. Twilio and SES credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager; ECS task IAM role has SecretsManagerReadAccess for the specific secret ARNs only. Outbound webhook delivery signed with HMAC-SHA256: signature = HMAC-SHA256(webhook_signing_secret, request_body); delivered as X-Alert-Signature: sha256={hex_digest} header; webhook_signing_secret stored in Secrets Manager by channel configuration. Rule management API rate-limited to 100 req/min per API key via Redis sliding window counter using Lua atomic script. ALB WAF with AWS Managed Rules Core Rule Set protects rule management API endpoint. TLS 1.3 enforced on ALB listener; no HTTP listeners.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster in private VPC us-east-1: alert-engine Task Definition (Node.js 20, 1 vCPU, 2GB RAM; service 2-4 tasks; HPA on CPU 70%). alert-worker Task Definition (Node.js 20, 0.5 vCPU, 1GB RAM; service 2-8 tasks; HPA on BullMQ waiting queue depth > 50 jobs via CloudWatch custom metric from EMF). ALB in public subnet targets alert-engine on port 3000 for rule management API; WAF attached to ALB. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ in isolated subnet (no public access). ElastiCache Redis 7 in isolated subnet. GitHub Actions CI: ESLint, tsc type check, Jest unit tests (geofence evaluation with mock polygons, BullMQ processor with mock Twilio/SES), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy via aws-actions. Secrets rotation: Twilio credentials rotated 90 days via Secrets Manager rotation Lambda. Rule cache invalidation: Express middleware emits Redis PUBLISH to alert-engine instances on POST/PUT/DELETE rule changes.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Container Insights enabled for both ECS services. Custom CloudWatch metrics via EMF (Embedded Metrics Format): alert_detection_latency_ms (histogram, target p95 < 500ms), alert_delivery_latency_ms (histogram from violation to delivery, target p95 < 5000ms), alert_queue_depth_waiting (BullMQ waiting count, alert > 500), alert_delivery_failure_count (alert > 10/hour triggers PagerDuty via SNS). CloudWatch Log Insights for structured JSON logs with vehicle_id, rule_id, alert_type, and latency_ms fields. Dashboards: alert delivery rate by channel type, geofence evaluation throughput per second, active vehicle count.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate at baseline 4 tasks (~$280/month). RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ db.t4g.medium (~$120/month). ElastiCache Redis 7 cache.r7g.large (~$180/month). Twilio SMS costs variable ($0.0079/message; 10K alerts/day = $79/day estimated peak). Total infrastructure ~$600/month excluding notification costs. Scale path: at 20K concurrent vehicles, ElastiCache Redis cluster mode adds shards for vehicle position cache; alert-engine scales to 8 tasks; BullMQ worker tasks scale to 16 for notification throughput.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): geofence entry/exit and speed threshold alert rules, SMS via Twilio and email via SES delivery, rule management REST API, alert event history, Redis geofence rule cache, BullMQ retry logic, ECS Fargate deployment, GitHub Actions CI/CD. Phase 2 (8 weeks): route deviation detection comparing live positions against planned route GeoJSON, webhook notification channel, alert digest mode (batched notifications for high-frequency events), dwell time rules (in-zone for > N minutes). Phase 3 (6 weeks): CMMS integration for maintenance zone entry alerts, predictive ETA alerts when vehicle approaching geofence within N minutes, alert escalation chains (SMS to email to webhook if prior channel fails).
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode; ESLint with @typescript-eslint/recommended. All geofence evaluation logic unit tested with known polygon fixtures and point-in-polygon edge cases (point on boundary, concave polygon). BullMQ job processors must be idempotent on job_id - re-processing a duplicate job must not create duplicate alert_events. Turf.js boolean-contains wraps geometry in GeoJSON Feature objects as required by API - never pass raw coordinate arrays. Twilio error code mapping table maintained in notification module: 21211 (invalid number) = permanent failure; 30001-30009 = temporary retry. Secret rotation procedure documented in runbook.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: telematics feed delivers positions at 1-second intervals for high-speed vehicles, creating 5K events/second throughput at maximum fleet scale. Mitigation: horizontal alert-engine scaling on CPU; Redis HASH operations are O(1) regardless of fleet size. Risk: Turf.js boolean-contains is CPU-intensive for complex polygons (> 100 vertices). Mitigation: geofence complexity capped at 50 vertices per polygon in rule management API validation; simplify complex shapes at ingestion. Tradeoff: 60-second speed alert cooldown per vehicle prevents alert fatigue but means a vehicle exceeding speed threshold continuously generates only 1 alert per minute rather than per event. Tradeoff: zone state cached in Redis only (not PostgreSQL) means zone state resets on Redis flush - acceptable since geofence entry/exit detection self-corrects within one position event interval.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Ray casting (Turf.js boolean-contains) chosen over spatial database queries (PostGIS) for geofence evaluation to keep latency sub-200ms per event without database round-trips. BullMQ chosen over SQS for alert delivery queue to enable per-job retry state tracking and dead-letter visibility without SQS polling overhead. Redis vehicle zone state cache chosen over PostgreSQL persistence for zone state to eliminate write amplification on every position event.

metadata.notes: Fleet geofence and speed alert automation; BullMQ on Redis for reliable notification delivery; Turf.js polygon evaluation; sub-5-second SLA for 5K vehicles.

project_goal.value: Migrate fleet management platform from manually managed EC2 VMs to EKS with GitOps pipelines, External Secrets Operator, and automated canary rollback to eliminate deployment-related outages
project_goal.rationale: Current VM-based deployments require manual SSH for updates, have no automated rollback, and cause 2-4 hour outage windows during releases

target_users.value: Platform engineering team managing fleet management service deployments and application developers deploying to the platform
target_users.rationale: Infrastructure consumed by internal platform engineers and developers; no end-user facing components

project_class.rationale: Pure infrastructure and deployment platform migration; no application code in scope

capabilities.rationale: Kubernetes infrastructure, GitOps pipeline, secrets management, observability stack; no frontend or data capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: EKS cluster provisioning, ArgoCD GitOps, External Secrets Operator, Argo Rollouts canary, network policy migration from security groups

risk_level.rationale: Infrastructure migration with production traffic risk during cutover; no direct exposure of sensitive user data in infrastructure layer

data_sensitivity.rationale: Infrastructure configuration and deployment metadata are internal; application data sensitivity governed by application contracts

external_exposure.rationale: EKS cluster in private VPC; no public-facing infrastructure endpoints; ArgoCD UI on internal VPN only

access_model.value: IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) for pod-level AWS permissions; ArgoCD RBAC with GitHub team sync for developer deployment access; kubectl access via AWS SSO with least-privilege IAM roles per environment
access_model.rationale: IRSA eliminates node-level IAM credentials; ArgoCD RBAC limits developer deploy actions to their application namespaces

feature_scope.value: EKS cluster provisioning via Terraform, Helm-based application packaging, ArgoCD GitOps with App-of-Apps pattern, External Secrets Operator for AWS Secrets Manager sync, Argo Rollouts canary deployment with automated analysis, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy replacing EC2 security group rules, Grafana/Prometheus/Loki observability stack
feature_scope.rationale: Complete GitOps platform with automated delivery and rollback

mvp_scope.value: EKS cluster with Terraform, ArgoCD GitOps App-of-Apps, External Secrets Operator, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, Grafana/Prometheus/Loki; canary rollouts in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: GitOps pipeline with secrets management is the highest priority to eliminate manual SSH deployments

security_baseline.value: CIS Kubernetes Benchmark Level 1 for EKS, IRSA for all pod AWS access, Secrets Manager via ESO (no secrets in ConfigMaps or container images), NetworkPolicy default-deny-all per namespace, ArgoCD with OIDC GitHub SSO
security_baseline.rationale: CIS Benchmark and least-privilege secrets management are minimum controls for production EKS

frontend_stack.value: none - infrastructure project; ArgoCD UI accessed via internal VPN only
frontend_stack.rationale: No frontend UI in scope; ArgoCD web interface is an incidental operational tool

backend_stack.value: Terraform 1.7 + Helm 3 + ArgoCD 2.10 + Argo Rollouts 1.7 on EKS 1.29
backend_stack.rationale: Terraform for cluster and VPC provisioning; Helm for application packaging; ArgoCD for GitOps delivery; Argo Rollouts for canary analysis

data_platform.value: none - infrastructure project; application databases managed by application teams via Terraform RDS modules
data_platform.rationale: No new data storage in infrastructure scope; existing RDS instances migrate to IRSA-based access patterns

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS 1.29 in us-east-1 across 3 availability zones
hosting_target.rationale: Multi-AZ EKS for platform resilience; us-east-1 matches existing AWS footprint and VPC peering requirements

privacy_retention_policy.value: Kubernetes audit logs retained 90 days in CloudWatch Logs; ArgoCD sync history retained 365 days; Loki application logs retained 30 days hot and 1 year cold in S3
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOC 2 change management controls require deployment audit trail retention

future_scope.value: Multi-cluster federation with cluster-api, progressive delivery with feature flags via Argo Rollouts analysis templates, cost allocation via Kubecost per application namespace
future_scope.rationale: Multi-cluster expansion and cost visibility are Year 2 platform improvements

constraints.value: Zero-downtime migration from VMs to EKS; existing EC2-based services must run in parallel during cutover; Terraform state must not be manually edited during migration; CIS Kubernetes Benchmark Level 1 compliance required from day one
constraints.rationale: Production traffic cannot be interrupted; state management integrity required for repeatable infrastructure

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards for EKS node health, pod restart rates, ArgoCD sync success rate, Argo Rollouts canary analysis pass/fail; Prometheus alerting via AlertManager to PagerDuty; Loki for structured application log aggregation
observability_baseline.rationale: Platform team needs deployment pipeline health visibility equivalent to or better than current Datadog EC2 monitoring
