execution_preference.value: Infrastructure changes via PRs to GitOps repo; no manual kubectl apply to production; Terraform changes require plan review PR before apply
execution_preference.rationale: All infrastructure changes must be auditable and reversible via GitOps

project.goal: VM to EKS migration with GitOps, ESO, and canary rollback
project.target_users: Platform engineers and application developers
project.timeline: EKS cluster and GitOps MVP in 12 weeks; full VM migration in 20 weeks

backend.framework: Terraform 1.7 + Helm 3 + ArgoCD 2.10 + Argo Rollouts 1.7
backend.api: ArgoCD API and kubectl for deployment operations

security.auth: IRSA per service account, ArgoCD OIDC GitHub SSO, AWS SSO kubectl access
security.encryption: Secrets Manager via ESO - no secrets in cluster; etcd encryption at rest enabled on EKS

data.db: N/A - application databases in scope of application teams

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for Terraform plan/apply and Helm chart lint/test
devops.environments: dev, staging, production - separate EKS namespaces with NetworkPolicy isolation

constraints.zero_downtime: VM and EKS run parallel during cutover
constraints.state: Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB locking

confirmed_decisions.secrets: External Secrets Operator + AWS Secrets Manager

reasoner_reviews.product: The App-of-Apps ArgoCD pattern is the correct approach for managing the full fleet management platform's microservices as a single GitOps unit. The critical path is the External Secrets Operator integration - eliminating secrets from ConfigMaps and image environment variables is the security improvement that unblocks production cutover.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: CIS Benchmark Level 1 compliance requires specific EKS settings that Terraform must encode from day one: etcd encryption via KMS, API server endpoint private-only, audit logging to CloudWatch, node security group restricting 443 to VPC CIDR only. The Argo Rollouts canary analysis templates should reference Prometheus metrics for application error rate and latency - generic CPU/memory analysis is insufficient for fleet management service canary validation.
reasoner_reviews.security: IRSA requires explicit service account annotations per pod; the Terraform modules must create ServiceAccount resources alongside the IAM role and trust policy. Default-deny NetworkPolicy must be applied before any application pods are deployed to namespaces - retroactive NetworkPolicy application to running namespaces risks connection disruption. Kubernetes etcd encryption at rest must be configured in the EKS cluster resource, not added post-creation.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The zero-downtime VM migration requires a specific traffic cutover sequence: 1) deploy application to EKS, 2) add EKS pods to existing ALB target group alongside VM instances (weighted routing), 3) gradually shift traffic to EKS pods, 4) drain VMs when EKS pods pass canary analysis. This sequence must be documented as the standard cutover runbook for each service.

backend.service_design: Terraform modules: vpc-eks (VPC, subnets, NAT, EKS cluster), eks-addons (CoreDNS, kube-proxy, VPC CNI, EBS CSI driver), argocd (ArgoCD Helm install with OIDC GitHub config), argo-rollouts, external-secrets-operator, observability (kube-prometheus-stack, Loki, Grafana). GitOps repository structure: /infra (Terraform), /platform (ArgoCD App-of-Apps YAML), /apps/{service-name}/helm (Helm charts per service)
backend.api_patterns: ArgoCD App-of-Apps pattern: root Application CR points to /platform directory; each service has an Application CR with automated sync and prune enabled. Helm chart values per environment in /apps/{service}/values-{env}.yaml. Argo Rollouts Rollout CR replaces Deployment for services requiring canary analysis.
backend.failure_handling: ArgoCD automated sync with self-heal enabled: drift from GitOps repo state triggers re-sync within 3 minutes. Argo Rollouts canary rollback: analysis template queries Prometheus fleet_api_error_rate_5m; if > 1% during canary step, Rollout automatically rolls back to stable revision. Terraform apply failures: state lock prevents concurrent applies; plan must be re-run after any manual infrastructure change.

security.auth_design: ArgoCD with OIDC: GitHub Teams SSO maps team memberships to ArgoCD Projects and RBAC roles; developers get read access to their app namespaces and sync permission for their Application CRs; platform engineers get full admin. AWS SSO EKS access: IAM Identity Center permission sets map to EKS aws-auth ConfigMap groups with least-privilege ClusterRole bindings.
security.secrets_management: External Secrets Operator with ClusterSecretStore pointing to AWS Secrets Manager in same region; ExternalSecret CR per application syncs named secret paths to Kubernetes Secrets; sync interval 1 hour with immediate refresh on manual trigger; rotation events via Secrets Manager rotation Lambda publish to EventBridge, triggering ESO sync and pod rolling restart via Reloader controller
security.authorization_model: IRSA: each application ServiceAccount annotated with eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn pointing to IAM role with minimum required policy (e.g., fleet-api ServiceAccount gets only S3 read on fleet-documents bucket); no node-level instance profiles for application workloads

devops.deployment_topology: EKS 1.29 cluster in us-east-1 with 3 node groups: system (3 nodes On-Demand m5.large for system workloads), app-general (3-12 nodes On-Demand/Spot mixed m5.xlarge, HPA), app-memory (2-6 nodes On-Demand r5.xlarge for memory-intensive services). Separate namespaces: argocd, monitoring, external-secrets, and one namespace per fleet management service with NetworkPolicy default-deny-all.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Terraform workflow (terraform fmt, validate, plan on PR; apply on merge to main for non-production; production apply requires manual approval gate in GitHub Environments). Helm workflow: helm lint, helm template | kubeval, helm test in CI. ArgoCD handles all cluster-side deployment; GitHub Actions never runs kubectl apply against cluster.
devops.rollback_strategy: Application rollback: ArgoCD UI or CLI argocd app rollback {app-name} {history-id} reverts to previous Git commit sync. Argo Rollouts automatic rollback: abort on failed canary analysis. Infrastructure rollback: Terraform state allows targeted resource replacement without full cluster destroy. EKS version rollback: not possible in-place; blue-green cluster upgrade strategy for major version upgrades.

ISS-001.title: NetworkPolicy default-deny applied after application pods deployed in staging - caused connection outages during Round 1 test
ISS-001.detail: In Round 1, default-deny NetworkPolicy was applied to the fleet-api namespace after pods were already running, interrupting existing TCP connections from ingress controller to pods. Order of operations must enforce NetworkPolicy creation before pod scheduling.

history.note: Staging outage during NetworkPolicy application
Terraform module now creates NetworkPolicy resource before Helm chart deploy via explicit depends_on; namespace bootstrap module applies default-deny before any ArgoCD Application CR is created

ISS-002.title: Argo Rollouts canary analysis template uses generic CPU metric instead of fleet-specific application error rate
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 canary analysis templates reference cpu_utilization_percent as the success metric. CPU is an unreliable canary signal for fleet management services - a canary with correct CPU but elevated 5xx error rates would pass analysis. Fleet-specific Prometheus metrics (fleet_api_http_errors_total, dispatch_order_processing_failures_total) must be used.

history.note: Initial analysis templates used CPU metric as placeholder
CPU metrics still used; fleet-specific metrics not yet instrumented in canary templates

ISS-003.title: VM traffic cutover runbook not defined - risk of production traffic split without documented rollback procedure
ISS-003.detail: The architecture specifies EKS parallel deployment alongside VMs but does not define the ALB weighted target group routing sequence, the traffic split percentages, or the rollback triggers for reverting to 100% VM traffic during cutover.

history.note: Cutover sequence not specified
Still no cutover runbook in architecture

focus_issues.detail: Canary analysis templates reference cpu_utilization_percent; fleet-specific error metrics (fleet_api_http_errors_total) must be used to catch functional regressions during canary steps.

focus_issues.title: VM traffic cutover runbook not defined - risk of production traffic without documented rollback procedure
focus_issues.detail: ALB weighted routing sequence, traffic split percentages, and VM rollback triggers not specified in architecture.

latest_recommendations: Replace CPU-based canary analysis metric with fleet_api_http_error_rate_5m and dispatch_order_processing_failure_rate Prometheus queries in Argo Rollouts AnalysisTemplate

Add ALB weighted target group cutover runbook section to deployment_and_operations: 0/100 VM initial state, 10/90, 50/50, 90/10 with 15-minute soak at each step, 100/0 EKS on canary analysis pass, immediate 100/0 VM revert trigger on error rate spike

previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: EKS 1.29 multi-AZ cluster; Terraform modules for VPC, cluster, addons, ArgoCD, ESO, observability; GitHub Actions for Terraform plan/apply; ArgoCD handles cluster deployment; Argo Rollouts canary using cpu_utilization_percent analysis metric.

target_output.thinking_summary: Third revision of the EKS GitOps migration architecture. ISS-001 (NetworkPolicy ordering) was resolved in Round 2 via Terraform depends_on in the namespace bootstrap module. This round must resolve ISS-002 (canary analysis must use fleet-specific HTTP error rate PromQL, not CPU) and ISS-003 (VM traffic cutover runbook with ALB weighted routing sequence and rollback triggers must be specified in deployment_and_operations).

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced cpu_utilization_percent metric in Argo Rollouts AnalysisTemplate with two fleet-specific PromQL queries: (1) fleet API error rate: rate(fleet_api_http_requests_total{status=~'5..'}[5m]) / rate(fleet_api_http_requests_total[5m]) with successCondition 'result[0] < 0.01'; (2) dispatch order processing failure rate: rate(dispatch_order_processing_failures_total[5m]) with successCondition 'result[0] < 0.005'. Canary step now waits 5 minutes before analysis to allow metric collection window to stabilize.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Canary analysis will detect functional regressions (elevated 5xx errors, dispatch failures) and automatically roll back to stable revision within 5-10 minutes, preventing bad releases from reaching 100% traffic

fix_report.action_taken: Added VM traffic cutover runbook to deployment_and_operations: ALB weighted target group routing sequence with 0/100 VM start, four weighted steps (10/90, 30/70, 70/30, 100/0) with 15-minute soak at each step; rollback trigger defined as fleet_api_http_error_rate_5m > 0.5% or manual PagerDuty acknowledgment; each soak step monitored in Grafana fleet-migration dashboard; full cutover script using AWS CLI aws elbv2 modify-listener-rule for automated weight updates
fix_report.expected_outcome: Documented cutover procedure with automated rollback trigger enables zero-downtime VM migration with a clear abort path if EKS pods exhibit errors during traffic ramp

target_output.title: Fleet Platform VM-to-EKS Migration - GitOps Architecture v3
target_output.executive_summary: A Terraform-provisioned EKS 1.29 multi-AZ cluster replacing the fleet management platform's manually managed EC2 VMs with GitOps-driven deployment via ArgoCD App-of-Apps, fleet-specific canary analysis via Argo Rollouts using HTTP error rate PromQL metrics, and External Secrets Operator syncing secrets from AWS Secrets Manager. This revision resolves the canary analysis metric gap (ISS-002) and adds the VM traffic cutover runbook with ALB weighted routing and automated rollback trigger (ISS-003). NetworkPolicy bootstrap ordering was fixed in Round 2 (ISS-001 resolved).
target_output.architecture_overview: EKS 1.29 cluster provisioned via Terraform in us-east-1 across 3 AZs with three node groups (system, app-general, app-memory). ArgoCD 2.10 manages the full fleet management platform application fleet via App-of-Apps pattern - a root Application CR syncs the /platform GitOps directory, which contains Application CRs for each fleet service. External Secrets Operator ClusterSecretStore syncs named AWS Secrets Manager paths to Kubernetes Secrets; Reloader controller watches ESO-managed secrets and triggers rolling pod restarts on rotation. Argo Rollouts Rollout CRs replace Deployment resources for fleet services; canary AnalysisTemplate queries fleet_api_http_error_rate_5m and dispatch_order_processing_failure_rate Prometheus metrics to gate promotion. Kubernetes NetworkPolicy default-deny-all is applied by the namespace bootstrap Terraform module before any ArgoCD Application CR is synced to the namespace. VM-to-EKS traffic migration uses ALB weighted target group routing with a documented 4-step cutover runbook.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure as Code: Terraform 1.7 with S3 remote state and DynamoDB locking. Container orchestration: EKS 1.29, Kubernetes 1.29 with VPC CNI, EBS CSI driver. GitOps: ArgoCD 2.10 with App-of-Apps pattern, OIDC GitHub SSO. Progressive delivery: Argo Rollouts 1.7 with Prometheus AnalysisTemplate. Secrets: External Secrets Operator 0.10, AWS Secrets Manager, Reloader controller for pod restart on secret rotation. Packaging: Helm 3 with values overlay per environment. Observability: kube-prometheus-stack (Prometheus Operator, Grafana), Loki + Promtail, AlertManager. Network: AWS VPC CNI, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy default-deny, AWS Load Balancer Controller.
target_output.functional_feature_map: EKS Cluster Provisioning: Terraform vpc-eks module creates VPC, 3 private app subnets, 3 public NAT subnets, EKS cluster with etcd KMS encryption, private API endpoint, and CloudWatch audit logging. GitOps Application Delivery: ArgoCD App-of-Apps syncs fleet service Helm charts with per-environment values; automated sync with self-heal reverts manual kubectl changes within 3 minutes. Secrets Sync: ESO ClusterSecretStore syncs Secrets Manager paths to Kubernetes Secrets; rotation events trigger immediate re-sync and Reloader rolling restart. Canary Deployment: Argo Rollouts Rollout CR for each fleet service; canary steps weight 20/80 for 5 minutes with AnalysisRun querying fleet_api_http_error_rate_5m; automatic rollback on failure condition. VM Cutover: 4-step ALB weighted routing migration with documented rollback trigger at each soak step.
target_output.system_components: EKS 1.29 Cluster (3 node groups: system m5.large x3, app-general m5.xlarge 3-12, app-memory r5.xlarge 2-6). ArgoCD 2.10 (argocd namespace, HA mode with Redis). Argo Rollouts 1.7 (argo-rollouts namespace, analysis controller). External Secrets Operator 0.10 (external-secrets namespace, ClusterSecretStore). Reloader Controller (stakater/reloader, watches ESO secrets). kube-prometheus-stack (Prometheus 2.51, Grafana 10.4, AlertManager 0.27 in monitoring namespace). Loki 2.9 + Promtail DaemonSet (monitoring namespace, S3 compactor for cold logs). AWS Load Balancer Controller (kube-system namespace, IRSA for ALB provisioning). Terraform S3 backend with DynamoDB state lock. GitHub Actions runners (Terraform plan/apply, Helm lint/test). AWS Secrets Manager (application secrets). CloudWatch Logs (Kubernetes audit log stream).
target_output.workflows: GitOps Deployment Flow: developer opens PR updating /apps/{service}/helm/values-production.yaml; GitHub Actions runs helm lint and helm template | kubeval; on merge to main, ArgoCD detects diff within 3 minutes and creates ApplicationSet sync; if service uses Argo Rollouts, sync triggers Rollout with canary steps: 20% canary weight for 5 minutes, AnalysisRun queries fleet_api_http_error_rate_5m and dispatch_order_processing_failure_rate; if both succeed (error_rate < 1%, failure_rate < 0.5%), Rollout promotes to 100% stable; if either fails, Rollout aborts and reverts to previous stable revision. Secret Rotation Flow: Secrets Manager rotation Lambda rotates secret and updates secret value; EventBridge rule fires on SecretRotationSucceeded event; ESO ClusterSecretStore sync triggered via annotation; Kubernetes Secret updated; Reloader detects Secret update annotation and performs rolling restart of pods consuming the secret; no manual pod restarts required. VM Cutover Flow: cutover begins with fleet service running on both EC2 (100%) and EKS (0%); platform engineer runs cutover script: Step 1: aws elbv2 modify-listener-rule sets EKS target group weight to 10, VM to 90; 15-minute soak monitoring fleet-migration Grafana dashboard; if fleet_api_error_rate_5m < 0.5%, proceed to Step 2: 30/70; Step 3: 70/30; Step 4: 100/0 EKS; rollback trigger at any step: if error rate > 0.5% or PagerDuty alert fires, script sets weights back to 0/100 VM and pages on-call; after 100% EKS soak for 24 hours, Terraform decommissions EC2 instances and removes VM target group.
target_output.data_model: No application data model - infrastructure project. Infrastructure state: Terraform S3 state file per module (vpc-eks, eks-addons, argocd, observability) with DynamoDB lock table. ArgoCD Application CR in GitOps repo: spec.source.repoURL, spec.source.targetRevision, spec.source.helm.valueFiles per environment, spec.syncPolicy.automated with prune and selfHeal. Argo Rollouts Rollout CR: spec.strategy.canary.steps [{setWeight: 20}, {pause: {duration: '5m'}}, {analysis: {templates: [{templateName: fleet-error-rate-analysis}]}}], spec.strategy.canary.analysis.templates referencing AnalysisTemplate with fleet_api_http_error_rate_5m PromQL. ESO ExternalSecret CR: spec.refreshInterval 1h, spec.secretStoreRef fleet-secret-store, spec.data mapping Secrets Manager secret paths to Kubernetes Secret keys.
target_output.api_design: No public-facing REST API. ArgoCD API (internal VPN only): argocd app sync {app-name}, argocd app rollback {app-name} {history-id}, argocd app get {app-name} --refresh. Argo Rollouts kubectl plugin: kubectl argo rollouts get rollout {name}, kubectl argo rollouts abort {name}. Terraform automation via GitHub Actions: terraform plan on PR (output posted as PR comment), terraform apply on merge with manual approval gate in GitHub Environments for production. Cutover script interface: fleet-cutover.sh --service fleet-api --step [1|2|3|4|rollback] --region us-east-1.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CIS Kubernetes Benchmark Level 1 controls: EKS API endpoint private-only (no public endpoint); etcd encryption at rest via AWS KMS key managed in Terraform; Kubernetes audit logging streamed to CloudWatch Logs /aws/eks/{cluster}/cluster; node security groups restrict inbound 443 to VPC CIDR only; privileged container admission blocked via OPA Gatekeeper policy (no privileged: true in pod specs). IRSA: every fleet service ServiceAccount has eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn annotation pointing to minimum-privilege IAM role; no EC2 instance profile grants application-level AWS permissions. ESO secrets management: no Kubernetes Secret created by human - all secrets created by ESO from Secrets Manager; rotation via EventBridge triggers ESO re-sync and Reloader rolling restart within 5 minutes of rotation. ArgoCD OIDC: GitHub Teams membership maps to ArgoCD Project roles; developers cannot sync Applications outside their team's Project; platform engineers hold argocd-admin role. NetworkPolicy: namespace bootstrap Terraform module applies default-deny-all NetworkPolicy before first ArgoCD sync; explicit allow policies created for each service's required ingress and egress paths.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS Cluster: Terraform vpc-eks module creates EKS 1.29 cluster, 3 node groups, VPC CNI, EBS CSI, AWS Load Balancer Controller, etcd KMS encryption, private API endpoint, CloudWatch audit log retention 90 days. GitOps repo structure: /infra/terraform (VPC, EKS, addons), /platform (ArgoCD App-of-Apps), /apps/{service}/helm/{chart} + values-{env}.yaml. GitHub Actions Terraform workflow: on PR - terraform init, fmt, validate, plan (output posted as comment); on merge to main (non-production) - terraform apply auto-approved; production apply requires GitHub Environments manual approval. ArgoCD auto-sync with 3-minute poll interval and self-heal. Argo Rollouts canary AnalysisTemplate fleet-error-rate-analysis: provider Prometheus URL kube-prometheus-stack:9090, metric fleet_api_error_rate: PromQL rate(fleet_api_http_requests_total{status=~'5..'}[5m]) / rate(fleet_api_http_requests_total[5m]), successCondition 'result[0] < 0.01', failureLimit 1; metric dispatch_failure_rate: PromQL rate(dispatch_order_processing_failures_total[5m]), successCondition 'result[0] < 0.005', failureLimit 1. VM Cutover Runbook: Step 0: deploy service to EKS, validate pods healthy, 0% traffic. Step 1: aws elbv2 modify-listener-rule --listener-rule-arn {arn} --actions (EKS weight 10, VM weight 90); soak 15 min; check fleet-migration Grafana dashboard for fleet_api_error_rate_5m < 0.5%. Step 2: 30/70; soak 15 min. Step 3: 70/30; soak 15 min. Step 4: 100/0 EKS; soak 24 hours. Rollback: any error_rate > 0.5% or PagerDuty alert triggers fleet-cutover.sh --step rollback which sets 0/100 VM immediately. After 24-hour Step 4 soak: Terraform taint EC2 instances and terraform apply to decommission.
target_output.observability: kube-prometheus-stack: Prometheus scrapes fleet service /metrics endpoints via ServiceMonitor CRs; Grafana dashboards: EKS Node Health (CPU, memory, disk per node group), Pod Restart Rate (alert > 5 restarts in 10 minutes), Argo Rollouts Canary Progress (canary weight, analysis pass/fail status), Fleet Migration Traffic Split (ALB weighted routing per target group, error rate per weight step). Loki + Promtail DaemonSet collects structured JSON logs from all pods; Grafana Loki datasource for log exploration; S3 compactor archives logs older than 30 days at $0.023/GB. AlertManager rules: fleet_api_error_rate_5m > 1% → PagerDuty critical; pod_restart_rate > 5/10min → PagerDuty warning; argocd_app_sync_status != Synced for > 10min → Slack #platform-alerts; argo_rollout_analysis_failed → PagerDuty with rollout name and failed metric. CloudWatch Logs: Kubernetes audit log stream for compliance; 90-day retention.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EKS cluster baseline 3 node groups: system 3x m5.large ($0.096/hr = $207/month), app-general 3x m5.xlarge On-Demand baseline + Spot for scale-out ($400-800/month variable), app-memory 2x r5.xlarge ($340/month). Loki S3 logs storage ~$30/month at 500GB/month. Grafana/Prometheus on system node group. Total platform infrastructure ~$1,000-1,500/month; application workload cost governed by each service's HPA and node group. Cost optimization: Spot instances for app-general node group (fleet services are stateless, interruption-tolerant); Karpenter provisioner can replace fixed node groups in Phase 2 for more granular bin-packing.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (12 weeks): EKS 1.29 cluster Terraform, ArgoCD App-of-Apps, ESO + Reloader secret rotation, NetworkPolicy bootstrap, kube-prometheus-stack, Loki, IRSA for all fleet services, GitHub Actions Terraform pipeline, first 3 fleet services migrated to EKS via canary with CPU metric (interim). Phase 2 (8 weeks): Argo Rollouts AnalysisTemplate with fleet_api_error_rate_5m and dispatch_failure_rate metrics (resolving ISS-002), VM cutover runbook execution for all remaining services using ALB weighted routing (resolving ISS-003), Gatekeeper OPA policies for CIS Benchmark admission control. Phase 3 (ongoing): Karpenter replacing fixed node groups for cost optimization, multi-cluster federation via cluster-api, Kubecost for namespace-level cost allocation.
target_output.development_guidelines: All cluster changes via GitOps PR - no manual kubectl apply to production cluster permitted. Terraform module changes require plan review by second platform engineer before production apply. Each fleet service Helm chart must include ServiceMonitor CR for Prometheus metric scraping and HorizontalPodAutoscaler resource. AnalysisTemplate metric queries must be validated against staging Prometheus before production canary rollout. ESO ExternalSecret CRs must map minimum required secret keys only; no whole-secret imports. Node selector and tolerations required on all workloads to prevent system pool contamination.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Argo Rollouts canary analysis 5-minute window may not capture intermittent errors in fleet dispatch workflows that occur at 15-minute intervals. Mitigation: extend analysis window to 15 minutes for dispatch service specifically; add canary step that holds 20% for 15 minutes before analysis runs. Risk: ESO sync delay up to 1 hour may cause pods to run with stale secret values between rotations. Mitigation: Reloader forces rolling restart within 5 minutes of ESO sync completing; combined worst-case latency is 65 minutes which is acceptable for API credential rotation. Tradeoff: private EKS API endpoint requires VPN or bastion for kubectl access, adding operational friction compared to public endpoint - justified by CIS Benchmark requirement and reduced attack surface. Tradeoff: Argo Rollouts Rollout CR cannot be upgraded to a standard Deployment without downtime - service teams must plan Rollout-to-Deployment migration if they want to remove progressive delivery.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Fleet-specific Prometheus error rate metrics (fleet_api_http_error_rate_5m, dispatch_order_processing_failure_rate) now specified in canary AnalysisTemplate replacing CPU placeholder - resolves ISS-002. ALB weighted routing cutover sequence with 4 steps (10/90, 30/70, 70/30, 100/0 EKS), 15-minute soak windows, and error-rate rollback trigger now documented in deployment_and_operations - resolves ISS-003. NetworkPolicy bootstrap ordering resolved in Round 2 via Terraform depends_on - ISS-001 closed.

metadata.notes: Fleet management VM-to-EKS migration; Round 3 fixes canary analysis metrics and VM cutover runbook; ISS-001 NetworkPolicy ordering resolved in Round 2.

project_goal.value: Public-facing vehicle fleet status page displaying real-time service availability, incident status, and live GPS coverage maps for a shared vehicle rental network using static hosting with client-side polling
project_goal.rationale: Renters and potential customers need a public status page to check zone availability and incidents before attempting to rent; static hosting eliminates server infrastructure for this read-only page

target_users.value: Vehicle rental customers checking service availability before starting a rental, and operations staff monitoring the public status page output
target_users.rationale: Public unauthenticated audience; no login required for read-only status information

project_class.rationale: Entire page shell served as static HTML/CSS/JS from CDN; dynamic data injected via client-side polling of a public read-only JSON API

capabilities.rationale: Astro/Svelte frontend with GA4 analytics; no backend runtime, no auth, no database in static site scope

complexity_level.rationale: Static page with client-side polling; no SSR, no database, no authentication

risk_level.rationale: Public read-only page with no user data collection; no PII, no financial data, no auth flow

data_sensitivity.rationale: Page displays aggregate fleet availability counts and incident summaries only; no individual vehicle tracking or user data

external_exposure.rationale: Fully public status page; no authentication; intended for all internet users to access

access_model.value: no authentication required; fully public read-only page
access_model.rationale: Fleet status page is a public communications tool requiring zero friction

feature_scope.value: Fleet zone availability counts per service zone, incident banners with affected zones and estimated resolution time, GPS coverage map with zone polygons, 30-second polling for data refresh, operational history timeline (last 24 hours), subscribe to incident updates via email link
feature_scope.rationale: Core status page functionality matching industry standard (similar to Atlassian Statuspage)

mvp_scope.value: Zone availability counts, active incident banners, 30-second client-side polling, coverage map with zone polygons; email subscription link and history timeline deferred to Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Core real-time status with coverage map covers primary customer use case

security_baseline.value: Firebase Hosting HTTPS with HSTS; Content-Security-Policy header allowing only Firebase CDN and GA4 origins; no user-submitted data; no cookies except GA4 analytics
security_baseline.rationale: Static site with no sensitive data; HTTPS and CSP sufficient for low-risk public page

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with Svelte components for interactive islands (coverage map, availability counters, incident banners)
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro static rendering with Svelte islands for interactive polling components; minimal JavaScript shipped by default

backend_stack.value: none - static site only; reads from external public JSON API endpoint provided by fleet operations platform
backend_stack.rationale: No server-side backend in this project scope; status data served by existing fleet operations REST API

data_platform.value: none - no database; static assets and client-side data only
data_platform.rationale: static_website project class has no data platform; all dynamic data polled client-side from read-only JSON API

hosting_target.value: Firebase Hosting with global CDN
hosting_target.rationale: Firebase Hosting provides global CDN, HTTPS, and custom domain configuration with no server management

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data collected on the static page; GA4 session analytics retained per GA4 default policy; no cookies set by the page itself
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No PII collected; GA4 analytics governed by Google's data processing terms

future_scope.value: Email incident notification subscription, 24-hour uptime history timeline, embeddable status widget for partner apps
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 engagement features for returning users and partner integrations

constraints.value: No server-side runtime or database on Firebase Hosting; page must load in under 2 seconds on mobile LTE; polling interval 30 seconds; coverage map must render zone polygons without a paid tile service
constraints.rationale: static_website constraint; mobile performance requirement for customer use case; cost constraint on map tiles

observability_baseline.value: Google Analytics 4 for page views, unique visitors, coverage map interaction events; Firebase Hosting analytics for CDN cache hit rate and bandwidth
observability_baseline.rationale: GA4 provides sufficient insight for a low-risk public status page

execution_preference.value: Feature branch PRs with GitHub Actions preview deploy to Firebase Hosting preview channel before merge
execution_preference.rationale: Preview deploys allow visual review of layout and data polling behavior before production merge

project.goal: Public static fleet status page with real-time data via client-side polling
project.target_users: Vehicle rental customers and operations staff
project.timeline: MVP in 4 weeks; Phase 2 in 3 additional weeks

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with mobile-first responsive design

security.auth: none - public page
security.headers: HTTPS HSTS, CSP header via Firebase Hosting rewrites config

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for Astro build, Firebase preview deploy on PR, production deploy on merge to main
devops.environments: preview channels per PR, production channel

constraints.map_tiles: OpenStreetMap free tiles via Leaflet (no paid Mapbox)

reasoner_reviews.product: Astro with Svelte islands is an excellent fit for this status page - the page shell (header, footer, coverage map SVG) is rendered at build time as static HTML, and the dynamic availability counts and incident banners are client-side Svelte components that poll independently. This means a CDN cache miss does not affect data freshness since the dynamic parts are always fetched client-side.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The 30-second polling pattern with a Svelte writable store is simple and reliable for this use case. The critical implementation detail is that the polling setInterval must be cleaned up in the Svelte onDestroy lifecycle to prevent memory leaks on navigation. For the coverage map, Leaflet.js with pre-rendered GeoJSON zone polygons embedded at build time is the right choice - the zone boundaries change infrequently and can be baked into the static build, while only availability counts are polled.
reasoner_reviews.security: For a public read-only static page the security surface is minimal, but the Content-Security-Policy header should be set via Firebase Hosting headers config to restrict script sources to self, GA4 CDN, and the status JSON API origin only. No inline scripts in Astro components - all JavaScript must be in module script blocks to comply with a strict CSP.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: OpenStreetMap tiles served through Leaflet's default tile server have a usage policy - at significant traffic volume (> 1M tile requests/day) OSM may throttle. Consider pre-rendering zone coverage areas as SVG polygons on a plain background map at build time to eliminate tile dependency entirely for the zone overlay, falling back to OSM only for the base map.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 project with /src/pages/index.astro as single page, /src/components/Svelte for interactive islands: AvailabilityCounter.svelte (polls /api/status every 30s, displays zone vehicle counts), IncidentBanner.svelte (polls /api/incidents, shows active incidents with affected zones), CoverageMap.svelte (Leaflet map with GeoJSON zone polygons loaded at build time; colors zones by availability tier), StatusHistory.svelte (Phase 2 - 24h history chart).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single-page status site: Hero section with overall service status badge (Operational/Degraded/Outage), Availability Grid showing vehicle counts per zone with color-coded availability tiers (green/yellow/red), Coverage Map with interactive Leaflet zones, Active Incidents section with estimated resolution times, Footer with subscribe link. All sections accessible without JavaScript; Svelte islands add real-time updates.
frontend.performance_notes: Astro partial hydration: only AvailabilityCounter, IncidentBanner, and CoverageMap components are client:load hydrated; static content (header, zone names, footer) is pure HTML; Tailwind CSS purged to < 15KB; Leaflet.js lazy-loaded only after coverage map viewport intersection; total JS bundle < 80KB; target LCP < 2s on mobile LTE via Firebase CDN.

devops.deployment_topology: Firebase Hosting project with production channel (status.fleetco.com) and PR preview channels (status-pr-{number}.web.app); Firebase Hosting rewrites config sets HTTPS HSTS and CSP headers for all routes; CDN cache 1-hour for HTML with stale-while-revalidate; assets (CSS, JS, images) cache 1 year immutable.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR - npm run build (Astro static build), firebase deploy --only hosting:preview --channel pr-{number}, post preview URL to PR comment. On merge to main - npm run build, firebase deploy --only hosting:production. No server-side deploy steps; pure static build pipeline.
devops.observability_stack: GA4 for page engagement (zone map clicks, incident banner impressions, polling latency visible via Navigation Timing API custom event); Firebase Hosting analytics for CDN request volume, cache hit rate, bandwidth by region; no backend monitoring needed for static hosting.

ISS-001.title: Coverage map uses paid Mapbox GL tiles in Round 1 - violates no-paid-tile-service constraint
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 architecture specified Mapbox GL JS with Mapbox tiles for the coverage map, which violates the explicit constraint against paid map tile services. Must use Leaflet.js with OpenStreetMap tiles or pre-rendered SVG zone polygons.

history.note: Architecture specified Mapbox GL JS - violates no-paid-tile constraint
Replaced with Leaflet.js + OpenStreetMap tiles; zone polygons pre-rendered as GeoJSON at build time

ISS-002.title: Polling setInterval not cleaned up in Svelte component - memory leak on page navigation
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 AvailabilityCounter.svelte used setInterval for 30-second polling but did not include an onDestroy cleanup. In Astro with client:load islands, navigating away and back creates multiple overlapping polling intervals accumulating over the session.

history.note: setInterval not cleaned up in onDestroy lifecycle

focus_issues.title: Polling setInterval not cleaned up in Svelte component - memory leak on page navigation
focus_issues.detail: AvailabilityCounter.svelte setInterval not cleaned up in onDestroy; multiple overlapping intervals accumulate in single-page navigation sessions.

latest_recommendations: Add onDestroy clearInterval call to all Svelte polling components; use a Svelte writable store with derived polling state to manage a single interval per component lifecycle

Firebase Hosting with CDN is appropriate for global public availability

Add onDestroy(() => clearInterval(pollHandle)) in AvailabilityCounter.svelte and IncidentBanner.svelte; use Svelte stores for shared polling state

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the fleet status page static site to resolve ISS-001 (Mapbox replaced with Leaflet.js + OpenStreetMap in Round 1 resolution) and ISS-002 (Svelte polling setInterval must be cleaned up in onDestroy to prevent memory leaks). Key fix: document the Svelte polling pattern with writable store and onDestroy clearInterval; confirm Leaflet.js with pre-built GeoJSON zone polygons in the architecture. Static site constraint (no server runtime) must be maintained.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified Svelte polling lifecycle pattern in functional_feature_map and workflows: each polling island uses a writable store initialized in onMount with setInterval and cleans up the handle in onDestroy via clearInterval; AvailabilityCounter.svelte and IncidentBanner.svelte both implement this pattern; a shared statusApi.js module exports a createPollingStore(url, intervalMs) factory function that encapsulates the interval lifecycle, preventing duplicate implementations
fix_report.expected_outcome: No memory leak from overlapping polling intervals; each component instance creates exactly one interval and cleans it up on destroy; API call rate remains one request per 30 seconds per component regardless of navigation behavior

target_output.title: Fleet Status Page - Static Site Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A public-facing static status page for a shared vehicle rental network built with Astro 4 and Svelte interactive islands, hosted on Firebase Hosting global CDN. The page shell is pre-rendered at build time as static HTML; AvailabilityCounter and IncidentBanner Svelte islands poll a public read-only JSON API endpoint every 30 seconds using a lifecycle-safe polling store pattern with onDestroy cleanup. The coverage map renders zone polygons via Leaflet.js with OpenStreetMap tiles and build-time embedded GeoJSON fleet zone definitions. This revision resolves the Mapbox paid-tile constraint violation (ISS-001, resolved in Round 1) and the Svelte polling setInterval memory leak (ISS-002).
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static architecture with no server-side runtime in scope. Astro 4 generates a static HTML site at build time from component templates and embedded GeoJSON data files. The built output is deployed to Firebase Hosting which serves it globally via CDN with HTTPS and HSTS headers. Two Svelte islands are hydrated client:load for interactivity: AvailabilityCounter (polls /api/status every 30 seconds for zone vehicle counts) and IncidentBanner (polls /api/incidents for active incidents and affected zones). Both islands use a shared createPollingStore utility that manages setInterval lifecycle in onMount and clearInterval in onDestroy to prevent polling accumulation. The coverage map renders using Leaflet.js with OpenStreetMap free tiles; fleet zone GeoJSON polygons are embedded in the Astro build as a static JSON import and rendered as Leaflet GeoJSON layers without tile API cost.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend framework: Astro 4.6 with @astrojs/svelte integration. Svelte 4 for interactive polling islands. Tailwind CSS 3.4 purged to < 15KB. Leaflet.js 1.9 for coverage map with OpenStreetMap tile attribution. GA4 gtag.js for analytics. Build: Vite via Astro. Hosting: Firebase Hosting with global CDN, HTTPS, custom domain support. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with firebase-tools for preview channel and production deploy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Static Shell (rendered at build time): page header with overall service status badge, zone grid layout, coverage map container, footer with incident subscription mailto link. AvailabilityCounter Island (client:load Svelte): calls createPollingStore('/api/status', 30000); store value updates zone availability count badges and colors zones green (> 5 vehicles), yellow (1-5), red (0) without page reload; onDestroy cleanup prevents interval accumulation. IncidentBanner Island (client:load Svelte): calls createPollingStore('/api/incidents', 30000); renders active incident banners with incident title, affected zones list, and estimated resolution time; banner dismisses when incident.resolved_at is populated in API response. CoverageMap Island (client:load with client:visible): Leaflet map initialized on viewport intersection; zone_polygons.json embedded at build time provides GeoJSON for all fleet zones; zone polygon fill color updated by AvailabilityCounter store subscription without re-polling map data.
target_output.system_components: Firebase Hosting global CDN (static HTML/CSS/JS delivery, HTTPS HSTS, CSP headers). Astro 4 build pipeline (generates /dist static output at CI time). Svelte 4 polling islands (AvailabilityCounter, IncidentBanner with createPollingStore lifecycle management). Leaflet.js coverage map (OpenStreetMap tiles, build-time GeoJSON zone polygons). fleet_zones.json (static file generated by fleet operations team and committed to repo; rebuilt into /dist/assets at build time). External fleet operations JSON API (read-only public endpoints /api/status and /api/incidents; out of scope for this project). GA4 gtag.js (analytics, non-blocking async load). GitHub Actions CI/CD (Astro build, Firebase preview channel deploy, production deploy).
target_output.workflows: Build and Deploy: developer edits Astro components or updates fleet_zones.json; PR opens GitHub Actions workflow: npm ci, npm run build (Astro generates /dist), firebase deploy --only hosting:preview --channel pr-{pr_number}; preview URL posted to PR comment for visual review. On merge to main: npm ci, npm run build, firebase deploy --only hosting:production; CDN cache invalidated automatically by Firebase deploy. Client-Side Polling: browser loads status.fleetco.com; Firebase CDN serves HTML shell in < 100ms from nearest PoP; page parses and hydrates AvailabilityCounter and IncidentBanner Svelte islands via client:load; each island calls createPollingStore(url, 30000) which executes: fetch(url) immediately, sets interval for 30-second repeats, stores clearInterval handle in returned Svelte writable; Svelte onDestroy registers clearInterval(handle) cleanup; store value reactively updates availability badges and incident banners. Zone Geometry Update: fleet operations team updates fleet_zones.json in repo; PR review; merge triggers Astro rebuild and CDN deploy; zone polygon changes visible on coverage map globally within 5 minutes of merge. Incident Publication: operations team updates incident status via their internal dashboard which writes to /api/incidents; status page reflects new incident within one 30-second polling cycle; incident banner disappears automatically when resolved_at field is populated.
target_output.data_model: No database - static site. Build-time data: fleet_zones.json - array of zone objects {id, name, polygon_geojson (GeoJSON Polygon feature), display_color, status_page_label} committed to repository and embedded in Astro build output as /dist/assets/fleet-zones.json. Runtime polled data (external API, not in scope): GET /api/status returns {zones: [{zone_id, available_vehicles, total_vehicles, status: operational|limited|unavailable}], last_updated: ISO8601}. GET /api/incidents returns {incidents: [{id, title, affected_zones: [zone_id], severity: minor|major|critical, started_at, estimated_resolution, resolved_at|null}]}. No data persisted by the static site itself.
target_output.api_design: No API routes in this static website project. Client-side consumption of external fleet operations API: GET /api/status (polled every 30s by AvailabilityCounter island; response drives zone count badges and color tiers). GET /api/incidents (polled every 30s by IncidentBanner island; response drives incident banner display). Both endpoints are read-only, unauthenticated, and CORS-enabled for the status page domain. Fleet zone GeoJSON served from static file /assets/fleet-zones.json built into Firebase Hosting (not polled - loaded once at Leaflet map init).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase Hosting headers config (firebase.json headers array): Strict-Transport-Security max-age=31536000 includeSubDomains preload. Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://www.googletagmanager.com; img-src 'self' https://*.tile.openstreetmap.org data:; connect-src 'self' https://api.fleetco.com https://www.google-analytics.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'. X-Frame-Options: DENY. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. No cookies set by the status page itself (GA4 uses first-party cookies under fleetco.com domain per GA4 configuration). No user input forms on the page; email subscription is a mailto: link with no data submission to the status site. GDPR: GA4 configured with IP anonymization enabled; cookie banner displayed on first visit for EU visitors via Cookiebot lightweight script (no-consent mode disables GA4 measurement until consent).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Firebase Hosting project fleet-status-page with two hosting channels: production (status.fleetco.com, custom domain with SSL provisioned by Firebase) and PR preview (auto-named status-pr-{number}.web.app). GitHub Actions workflow: on PR open/update - checkout, node 20, npm ci, npm run build, firebase deploy --only hosting:preview (channel pr-{pr_number}) using FIREBASE_TOKEN secret, post preview URL to PR comment via actions/github-script. On merge to main - checkout, node 20, npm ci, npm run build, firebase deploy --only hosting:production. Build artifact /dist is pure static output (HTML, CSS, JS, JSON, images); no Node.js process runs post-deploy. Cache headers: index.html Cache-Control max-age=0 must-revalidate (always fetch fresh HTML shell); /assets/*.js /assets/*.css Cache-Control max-age=31536000 immutable (hashed filenames via Vite). Firebase CDN global edge nodes serve content with < 50ms TTFB for > 95% of world population. Fleet zone updates: operations team opens PR to update fleet_zones.json; reviewed by ops lead; merged to main triggers rebuild and deploy within 3 minutes.
target_output.observability: GA4 custom events: zone_map_interaction (fires on Leaflet zone polygon click with zone_id and available_vehicles), incident_banner_impression (fires when IncidentBanner renders with incident_id), polling_error (fires when fetch to /api/status or /api/incidents returns non-200 with error_code). GA4 standard metrics: page views, session duration, device category breakdown (tracking mobile vs desktop customer usage). Firebase Hosting analytics: total requests per day, CDN cache hit rate (target > 95%), bandwidth by region, top referrers. No PagerDuty or server alerting needed - static hosting has no server to monitor. If /api/status becomes unavailable, polling_error GA4 event provides visibility; the page degrades gracefully showing last-known availability counts with a 'Data may be outdated' badge.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Firebase Hosting Spark (free) tier sufficient for < 10GB storage and 360MB/day transfer; paid Blaze plan activates above thresholds at $0.026/GB storage and $0.15/GB egress. Expected cost near-zero for a fleet status page (estimated 10,000 page views/day at 200KB/load = 2GB/day egress = ~$0.30/day on Blaze). OpenStreetMap tiles served directly from OSM CDN at no cost for typical status page traffic volumes. Build minutes on GitHub Actions free tier (< 10 builds/day). Total cost: $0-10/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): Astro 4 static site with AvailabilityCounter and IncidentBanner Svelte islands using createPollingStore with onDestroy cleanup, Leaflet coverage map with build-time GeoJSON zone polygons, Firebase Hosting deploy, GA4, GitHub Actions CI/CD with preview channels, Firebase Hosting CSP headers. Phase 2 (3 weeks): 24-hour operational history timeline chart (Chart.js loaded from static JSON history file updated by scheduled job), email incident subscription link connecting to Mailchimp campaign, embeddable status widget as an iframe-safe variant page (/widget) for partner app integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: All dynamic client-side behavior must use the createPollingStore(url, intervalMs) factory from /src/lib/statusApi.js - no inline setInterval calls in Svelte components. createPollingStore must: (1) call fetch on initialization, (2) schedule repeat fetch via setInterval, (3) expose {subscribe, cleanup} interface where cleanup calls clearInterval. Svelte islands must call cleanup in onDestroy. No inline script tags in Astro templates - all JS in module script blocks or Svelte components only (CSP compliance). Fleet zone GeoJSON polygon complexity capped at 50 vertices per zone for Leaflet rendering performance. PR preview deploy required before merge to main; visual review of map rendering on mobile viewport (375px width) required in PR checklist.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OpenStreetMap tile server has acceptable use policy; at high traffic (> 1M tile requests/day) OSM may throttle or block the domain. Mitigation: pre-render zone coverage polygons as CSS-colored SVG overlays on a plain background at build time; OSM tiles used only for base map context which is less critical than zone availability data. Risk: 30-second polling from a high-traffic status page during a major fleet incident may create thousands of concurrent requests to /api/status. Mitigation: fleet operations API team should configure CloudFront caching with 25-second TTL on /api/status to absorb polling spikes from the status page. Tradeoff: Astro static build means zone polygon changes require a full site rebuild and deploy (3-5 minutes) rather than being dynamic - acceptable since zone boundaries change rarely (quarterly). Tradeoff: client-side polling rather than SSE or WebSocket keeps the architecture purely static (no server required) at the cost of 30-second data staleness - acceptable for a status page where sub-minute freshness is sufficient.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Leaflet.js with OpenStreetMap tiles chosen over Mapbox GL JS to comply with no-paid-tile-service constraint (ISS-001, resolved Round 1). createPollingStore factory pattern with onDestroy cleanup resolves ISS-002 Svelte polling memory leak. Zone GeoJSON embedded at build time (not polled) eliminates map data staleness and API rate concerns for zone geometry.

metadata.notes: Fleet rental network public status page; Astro + Svelte static site; Round 2 fixes Svelte polling setInterval memory leak with onDestroy cleanup pattern; Mapbox replaced by Leaflet in Round 1.

project_goal.value: Desktop application for fleet procurement managers to analyze TCO across vehicle acquisition scenarios, comparing leasing vs. purchasing, fuel costs, and depreciation curves to minimize fleet operating costs.
project_goal.rationale: Core business need: replace spreadsheet-based TCO modelling with a dedicated tool that enforces consistent assumptions and produces shareable PDF reports.

target_users.value: Fleet procurement managers and finance analysts at commercial fleet operators
target_users.rationale: Primary stakeholders identified in discovery sessions who currently rely on Excel for TCO comparisons.

project_class.rationale: Offline-capable local tool required; sensitive fleet cost data must not leave the corporate network, and rich chart rendering favors a native desktop runtime.

capabilities.rationale: Rich UI for scenario configuration and chart rendering, Rust command layer for computations, SQLite persistence, and analytics output via PDF export.

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-year TCO modelling with depreciation curves and fuel projections is non-trivial but bounded in scope; no external integrations or auth services required.

risk_level.rationale: Data is internal fleet cost estimates; no PII, no external exposure, and no financial transaction processing.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores procurement scenarios and cost projections that are commercially sensitive but not regulated personal or financial data.

external_exposure.rationale: App operates entirely offline on a procurement manager's workstation; no inbound network surface.

access_model.value: Local user profile per workstation; no multi-user authentication required
access_model.rationale: Single-user desktop tool; access control is managed at the OS level.

feature_scope.value: Scenario builder for lease vs. purchase comparison, multi-year TCO projection engine, depreciation curve configurator, fuel cost forecasting with price inputs, side-by-side cost breakdown charts, PDF export with assumption tables
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set identified during stakeholder workshops with procurement and finance teams.

mvp_scope.value: Scenario builder with lease vs. purchase comparison, TCO projection for up to 7 years, basic depreciation and fuel cost inputs, chart rendering, and PDF export
mvp_scope.rationale: Core decision-support flow sufficient for pilot with three regional fleet procurement managers.

security_baseline.value: Local SQLite database encrypted with SQLCipher; app binary signed and notarized for macOS and Windows; no network connections; audit log of scenario exports written to local log file
security_baseline.rationale: Protects commercially sensitive fleet cost models from unauthorized access on shared workstations.

frontend_stack.value: Tauri 2.0 with React 18 and TypeScript; Recharts for TCO visualizations; Tailwind CSS for layout
frontend_stack.rationale: Tauri provides a lightweight native shell with small bundle size; React and Recharts are familiar to the frontend team and support the required chart types.

backend_stack.value: Rust with Tauri commands for TCO computation engine and SQLite I/O via rusqlite
backend_stack.rationale: Rust ensures computation correctness for financial modelling and provides fast multi-year NPV and depreciation calculations without external dependencies.

data_platform.value: SQLite 3.45 with SQLCipher encryption for local scenario persistence
data_platform.rationale: Embedded database appropriate for single-user desktop tool; no server infrastructure required.

hosting_target.value: Local desktop distribution via signed installers for macOS (ARM/x86) and Windows 10+
hosting_target.rationale: No cloud hosting; distributed via internal software portal as a signed .dmg and .msi.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Scenarios stored locally indefinitely until manually deleted by the user; no telemetry or usage data transmitted off-device
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No personal data collected; internal cost models remain on the user's workstation.

future_scope.value: Fleet-wide scenario aggregation via shared network drive export, integration with OEM MSRP feeds for automated vehicle pricing inputs, multi-user collaboration mode
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap items pending adoption feedback from pilot users.

constraints.value: Must run fully offline; installer size under 50 MB; macOS 12+ and Windows 10+ compatibility; no cloud services or telemetry
constraints.rationale: Procurement managers operate in environments with restricted internet access and strict software approval processes.

observability_baseline.value: Local structured log file (JSON lines) capturing scenario creation, export events, and computation errors; no remote monitoring
observability_baseline.rationale: Offline-only tool; remote APM is not applicable. Local logs support debugging on user machines.

execution_preference.value: Iterative desktop releases with semantic versioning; internal distribution via signed installer packages
execution_preference.rationale: Desktop app lifecycle with infrequent but well-tested releases distributed through the internal software portal.

project.goal: TCO analysis desktop tool for fleet procurement decisions
project.target_users: Fleet procurement managers and finance analysts

frontend.charts: Recharts for line, bar, and area TCO visualizations

backend.framework: Tauri IPC bridge exposing TCO engine commands
backend.computation: rusqlite for SQLite I/O; custom Rust modules for NPV, depreciation, and fuel cost calculations

security.code_signing: Apple Developer ID notarization (macOS), Authenticode signing (Windows)
security.network: No outbound connections; Tauri allowlist restricts all network access

data.persistence: Scenario and model data stored in user-scoped app data directory

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for multi-platform builds (macOS ARM/x86, Windows x64)
devops.distribution: Signed .dmg and .msi via internal software portal
devops.environments: dev (local), release (signed installers)

open_questions.depreciation_methods: Does the app need to support multiple depreciation methods (straight-line, declining balance, MACRS)?

reasoner_reviews.product: The TCO scenario builder addresses a genuine pain point: procurement managers currently maintain fragile Excel models that diverge across teams. Standardizing assumptions in a shared tool will improve decision consistency. The PDF export is critical for finance committee presentations and must reproduce charts faithfully.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Tauri 2.0 with a Rust computation backend is the right call for this workload. Depreciation curve and NPV calculations benefit from Rust's numeric precision and performance, especially for fleet-wide batch scenario comparisons. The IPC boundary between React and Rust should be kept thin: pass scenario parameters in, receive serialized result structs out.
reasoner_reviews.security: SQLCipher protects locally stored procurement models from access by other users on shared workstations. Code signing and notarization are non-negotiable for enterprise IT deployment approvals. No network allowlist entries should be permitted in the Tauri config.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 50 MB installer constraint is achievable with Tauri's lightweight webview approach versus Electron. GitHub Actions matrix builds across macOS ARM, macOS x86, and Windows x64 add CI complexity but are required for mixed-platform procurement teams.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The previous plan lacked specificity on the depreciation model engine: it did not define which depreciation methods are supported (straight-line vs. declining balance) or how fuel price escalation rates are parameterized. These gaps must be addressed in this revision to prevent scope ambiguity during implementation.

frontend.app_structure: Single Tauri window with a React SPA; routes: /scenarios (list), /scenarios/new (builder), /scenarios/:id (detail with charts), /scenarios/:id/compare (side-by-side), /settings (depreciation presets). React Router v6 for navigation.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Scenario Builder: step-through form collecting vehicle specs (MSRP, category, powertrain), acquisition method (lease terms or purchase financing), annual mileage, fuel type and price inputs, and analysis horizon (1-7 years). Comparison View: side-by-side Recharts area charts for cumulative TCO, bar charts for cost component breakdown (depreciation, fuel, maintenance, financing). Export: triggers Rust PDF generation command with current scenario state.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store holds active scenario draft with partial validation; React Query (Tauri adapter) for async Rust command invocations. Chart data derived via useMemo from scenario result structs returned by Rust.
frontend.performance_notes: Recharts renders up to 7-year monthly data points (84 points per series) smoothly. Memoize expensive chart dataset transformations. Lazy-load comparison view component to reduce initial render time.
frontend.frontend_risks: PDF chart fidelity risk: Recharts SVG renders correctly in the webview but may not reproduce identically in PDF if using a headless renderer. Mitigation: use canvas-based chart snapshots via html2canvas before passing to the Rust PDF builder.

backend.service_design: Tauri command handlers organized into modules: `scenario_commands` (CRUD for scenarios), `tco_engine` (NPV, depreciation, fuel projection calculations), `pdf_commands` (report generation via printpdf crate), `db_commands` (SQLCipher connection management).
backend.business_modules: TCO Engine: supports straight-line and double-declining-balance depreciation; fuel cost projection with configurable annual escalation rate (default 3%); NPV calculation using user-supplied discount rate; lease vs. purchase cost stream comparison over 1-84 month horizon. Scenario persistence: full scenario parameter set and computed results serialized to SQLite JSON columns.
backend.failure_handling: Rust commands return Result types; serialization errors and SQLite constraint violations are mapped to typed error enums and surfaced in the React UI as user-friendly messages. Computation panics are caught at the Tauri command boundary and logged to the local log file.
backend.backend_risks: Numeric precision risk for currency calculations: use Rust `rust_decimal` crate for all monetary arithmetic to avoid floating-point rounding errors in multi-year TCO projections.

security.auth_design: No authentication service; access control delegated to OS user session. App data directory scoped to the OS user profile.
security.secrets_management: SQLCipher passphrase derived from a machine-specific key (hardware UUID + salt) stored in the OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager) via the `keyring` Rust crate.
security.security_risks: Risk: SQLCipher key exposure if the OS keychain is compromised. Mitigation: document that the tool is not suitable for classified procurement data; internal sensitivity classification is sufficient for current use case.

data.entities: vehicle_scenarios (id, name, created_at, updated_at, parameters_json, results_json), tco_models (id, scenario_id, model_type ENUM straight_line/double_declining, horizon_months, discount_rate, created_at), depreciation_curves (id, scenario_id, month, book_value, depreciation_amount), fuel_cost_projections (id, scenario_id, year, projected_price_per_gallon, annual_fuel_cost), lease_terms (id, scenario_id, monthly_payment, term_months, residual_value, money_factor), procurement_decisions (id, scenario_id, recommendation ENUM lease/purchase/extend, rationale, decided_at)
data.storage_design: Single SQLite database file per user in the OS app data directory (macOS: ~/Library/Application Support/fleet-tco, Windows: %APPDATA%/fleet-tco). SQLCipher encryption applied at database open. WAL journal mode for write performance.
data.schema_notes: parameters_json and results_json columns store the full scenario snapshot as JSON for portability; normalized tables (depreciation_curves, fuel_cost_projections) are derived on computation and used for chart queries.
data.data_risks: Risk: database file corruption on unexpected process termination. Mitigation: WAL mode ensures durability; add SQLite integrity_check on app startup and warn user if corruption detected.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions matrix: jobs for macos-14 (ARM), macos-13 (x86), and windows-latest (x64). Each job: checkout, install Rust toolchain, install Node deps, run `bun run tauri build`, sign and notarize.
devops.ci_cd_design: On tag push (v*): build all three targets in parallel, upload signed installers to GitHub Releases. On PR: build macOS ARM only for rapid feedback. Code signing secrets (Apple Developer ID cert, Windows Authenticode cert) stored in GitHub Actions encrypted secrets.
devops.observability_stack: Local JSON-lines log file at {app_data}/logs/fleet-tco.log. Log entries include: scenario creation, computation duration, export events, and Rust errors. Log rotation at 10 MB. No remote telemetry.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: Apple notarization round-trip adds 5-15 minutes to CI pipeline. Mitigation: run notarization only on tag builds, not PR builds.
